<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2023]**

**Q1. Define a transducer. Differentiate between a transducer and an inverse transducer with one example each. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **transducer** is a device that converts energy from one form to another. In instrumentation, it specifically converts a non-electrical physical quantity (measurand) into a measurable electrical signal.

| Aspect | Transducer | Inverse Transducer |
|---|---|---|
| Function | Non-electrical ŌåÆ Electrical | Electrical ŌåÆ Non-electrical (or electrical) |
| Example | Thermocouple (heat ŌåÆ mV) | Piezoelectric crystal (V ŌåÆ mechanical vibration) |
| Application | Measurement / Sensing | Actuation / Control |

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]**

**Q2. List any six general features of an ideal transducer. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Any six of the following):**

1. **High accuracy** and precision
2. **Linearity** between input and output
3. **High sensitivity** to measurand, low sensitivity to other variables
4. **Low hysteresis** error
5. **High repeatability** and reproducibility
6. **Wide dynamic range** and good frequency response
7. **Good environmental resistance** (temperature, humidity, vibration)
8. **Compact, rugged, and economical** with long-term stability

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) ŌĆö KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**

#### ŌŁÉ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Define a transducer and explain the general classification of transducers based on (i) energy source, (ii) output signal type, and (iii) operating principle. Give two examples for each category. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the static and dynamic characteristics of a transducer. Derive the expression for sensitivity and total static error. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Definition and Classification (7 Marks):**

A **transducer** is a device that converts energy from one form to another. In process instrumentation, it converts a non-electrical physical quantity into an electrical signal.

**Classification (i) Based on Energy Source:**
- **Active (Self-Generating) Transducers:** Generate their own electrical output without external power. Examples: Thermocouple (heat ŌåÆ mV), Piezoelectric crystal (force ŌåÆ charge), Photovoltaic cell (light ŌåÆ V).
- **Passive (Externally Energised) Transducers:** Require external AC/DC excitation; measurand modulates an electrical parameter (R, L, C). Examples: RTD (temperature ŌåÆ resistance), Strain gauge (force ŌåÆ resistance), LVDT (displacement ŌåÆ induced voltage).

**Classification (ii) Based on Output Signal Type:**
- **Analog Transducers:** Produce a continuous output proportional to measurand. Examples: Thermocouple, Strain gauge, LVDT.
- **Digital Transducers:** Produce a discrete (pulsed or coded) output. Examples: Shaft encoder, digital flow meter with frequency output, vibrating-wire pressure sensor.

**Classification (iii) Based on Operating Principle:**
- **Resistive:** RTD, thermistor, strain gauge, potentiometer, LDR.
- **Inductive:** LVDT, RVDT, eddy current probe.
- **Capacitive:** Differential capacitor, condenser microphone, capacitive level sensor.
- **Electromagnetic / Electromagnetic Induction:** Tachogenerator, electromagnetic flow meter.
- **Piezoelectric:** Quartz crystal, piezo accelerometer.
- **Photoelectric / Optical:** Photodiode, phototransistor, LDR, pyrometer.
- **Ultrasonic:** Ultrasonic flow meter, ultrasonic level sensor.
- **Electrochemical:** pH electrode, dissolved-oxygen probe.

**(b) Static & Dynamic Characteristics with Derivation (7 Marks):**

*Block Diagram (text representation):*

```
            ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
   Input x  Ōöé  SENSING  ŌåÆ  TRANSDUCTION  ŌåÆ  OUTPUT y   Ōöé  Output y
 ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢ČŌöé   ELEMENT     ELEMENT          (V/mA)    Ōöé ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢Č
            ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś
                  Ōöé             Ōöé              Ōöé
                  Ō¢╝             Ō¢╝              Ō¢╝
              STATIC       DYNAMIC      ENVIRONMENTAL
            Charact.      Charact.       Robustness
            (Accuracy,   (Speed, BW,    (Temp, Hum,
             Lin.,       Phase,         Vibration,
             Hyst.,      Fidelity)      EMI/RFI)
             Drift)
```

**Static Characteristics (3 of 7 marks):**
- *Accuracy:* Closeness of output to true value.
- *Linearity:* Output proportional to input.
- *Sensitivity:* $S = \Delta y / \Delta x$.
- *Hysteresis:* Difference between ascending and descending outputs.
- *Repeatability:* Same input ŌåÆ same output under identical conditions.
- *Drift:* Output change with time at constant input.
- *Resolution & Threshold:* Smallest detectable input change / minimum detectable input.

**Dynamic Characteristics (2 of 7 marks):**
- *Speed of response / Rise time:* How fast output follows input.
- *Bandwidth:* Range of input frequencies accurately reproduced.
- *Dynamic error / Phase shift:* Time delay in step/sinusoidal response.
- *Fidelity:* Faithful reproduction of input waveform.

**Derivation of Sensitivity and Total Error (2 of 7 marks):**

The ideal linear model is:

$$y = S \cdot x + b$$

At input endpoints $x_{\min}$ and $x_{\max}$:

$$S = \frac{y_{\text{FS}} - y_0}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}} = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$$

Total static error is the sum of all individual error components:

$$e_{\text{total}} = \pm \left( e_{\text{nonlin}} + e_h + e_r + e_{\text{drift}} + e_{\text{cal}} \right)$$

with individual error defined as:

$$e_{\text{nonlin}} = \pm \frac{\vert y_a - y_l \vert_{\max}}{y_{\text{FSO}}} \times 100\%$$

$$e_h = \frac{\vert y_{\text{up}} - y_{\text{down}} \vert_{\max}}{y_{\text{FSO}}} \times 100\%$$

---

#### ŌŁÉ Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice

**(a)** Differentiate between active and passive transducers with suitable examples. Explain the working principle of a thermocouple as an active transducer, with a neat sketch of the thermoelectric circuit. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A thermistor is used to measure temperature in the range 0 to 150 ┬░C. The resistance varies from 5520 ╬® at 0 ┬░C to 75 ╬® at 150 ┬░C following the relation $R_T = R_0 \, e^{B(1/T - 1/T_0)}$. Determine:
   (i) The sensitivity at $T = 50\;┬░\text{C}$ in $\Omega/┬░\text{C}$.
   (ii) The non-linearity if a straight line is fitted between the endpoint values.
   (iii) Comment on the suitability of this transducer for a linear temperature indicator. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Active vs Passive Transducers and Thermocouple Principle (7 Marks):**

**Differentiation Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Active Transducer | Passive Transducer |
|---|---|---|
| Energy Source | Self-generating | Requires external power |
| Working Principle | Direct energy conversion | Parameter modulation (R, L, C) |
| External Excitation | Not required | Required (AC or DC) |
| Output Signal | Small (mV, nA, pC) | Proportional to excitation |
| Examples | Thermocouple, Piezo, Tachogenerator | RTD, LVDT, Strain Gauge |
| Application | Direct measurement, robust | Bridge-based measurement |

**Thermocouple Working Principle (4 marks):**

A thermocouple consists of two dissimilar metals (e.g., Chromel-Alumel for Type K, Iron-Constantan for Type J) joined at one end called the **hot junction** (or measuring junction). The other ends are kept at a known reference temperature ŌĆö the **cold junction** (or reference junction).

*Thermoelectric Effect:* When the hot junction is heated, an EMF is generated due to the **Seebeck effect**. The magnitude of EMF is:

$$\varepsilon = \int_{T_{\text{cold}}}^{T_{\text{hot}}} \left( S_A(T) - S_B(T) \right) dT$$

where $S_A$ and $S_B$ are the **Seebeck coefficients** of the two metals (in V/┬░C).

For small temperature differences, this is approximated as:

$$\varepsilon \approx (S_A - S_B) \cdot (T_{\text{hot}} - T_{\text{cold}})$$

A complete thermocouple circuit therefore requires **cold-junction compensation (CJC)** ŌĆö usually an ice bath at 0 ┬░C or an electronic compensation circuit in modern transmitters.

**Sketch (text representation):**

```
        HOT JUNCTION (T_hot)
              Ō¢▓
              Ōöé
         ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌö┤ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
         Ōöé         Ōöé
       Metal A    Metal B
     (Chromel)  (Alumel)
         Ōöé         Ōöé
         Ōöé         Ōöé
         ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌö¼ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś
              Ōöé
              Ō¢╝
     COLD JUNCTION (T_cold = 0 ┬░C, ice bath)
     V_out = mV signal to indicator / transmitter
```

**(b) Thermistor Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $R_T = R_0 \, e^{B(1/T - 1/T_0)}$ with $R_0 = 5520 \; \Omega$ at $T_0 = 0 \; ┬░C = 273.15 \; K$ and $R_{150} = 75 \; \Omega$ at $T = 150 \; ┬░C = 423.15 \; K$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Find the constant B (1 mark):**

$$75 = 5520 \cdot e^{B(1/423.15 - 1/273.15)}$$

$$\frac{75}{5520} = e^{B(-0.002364 + 0.003662)} = e^{B(0.001298)}$$

$$\ln(0.013587) = B \cdot (0.001298)$$

$$-4.300 = B \cdot (0.001298) \;\Rightarrow\; B = -3313.6 \; K$$

(Take $B = 3313.6 \; K$ as the magnitude.)

**Step 2 ŌĆö Resistance at 50 ┬░C (T = 323.15 K) (1 mark):**

$$R_{50} = 5520 \cdot e^{3313.6 \cdot (1/323.15 - 1/273.15)} = 5520 \cdot e^{3313.6 \cdot (-0.000297)} = 5520 \cdot e^{-0.984}$$

$$R_{50} = 5520 \times 0.3741 \approx 2065 \; \Omega$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Sensitivity at 50 ┬░C (2 marks):**

Sensitivity is the derivative $dR/dT$:

$$S = \frac{dR_T}{dT} = R_T \cdot \left( -\frac{B}{T^2} \right)$$

$$S(50 \; ┬░C) = 2065 \times \left( -\frac{3313.6}{(323.15)^2} \right) = 2065 \times (-0.03173)$$

$$\boxed{S \approx -65.5 \; \Omega/┬░C \text{ at } 50 \; ┬░C}$$

The negative sign indicates resistance *decreases* with temperature (typical NTC thermistor behaviour).

**Step 4 ŌĆö Non-linearity (3 marks):**

Best-fit straight line from endpoint (0 ┬░C, 5520 ╬®) to (150 ┬░C, 75 ╬®):

$$S_{\text{linear}} = \frac{75 - 5520}{150 - 0} = \frac{-5445}{150} = -36.3 \; \Omega/┬░C$$

The ideal straight-line equation: $R_{\text{linear}}(T) = 5520 - 36.3 \cdot T$ (with $T$ in ┬░C).

Compare actual vs linear at $T = 50 \; ┬░C$:

$$R_{\text{linear}}(50) = 5520 - 36.3 \times 50 = 5520 - 1815 = 3705 \; \Omega$$

$$R_{\text{actual}}(50) = 2065 \; \Omega$$

$$\text{Deviation} = \vert 2065 - 3705 \vert = 1640 \; \Omega$$

Maximum deviation occurs at intermediate temperatures. As a fraction of FSO ($5445 \; \Omega$):

$$e_{\text{lin}} = \frac{1640}{5445} \times 100\% \approx 30.1\%$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Comment (Bonus for full marks):**

The thermistor has **very high sensitivity** (ŌĆō65.5 ╬®/┬░C, more than double the linear fit) and excellent resolution for small temperature changes, but its **exponential characteristic causes ~30% non-linearity**. Hence, thermistors are **not directly suitable for a linear temperature indicator** without:
- A **linearization circuit** (e.g., a shunt resistor placed in parallel with the thermistor), or
- A **lookup table / polynomial correction** in a digital indicator, or
- Use of a **Wheatstone bridge** in a differential configuration.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Examinations**
>
> 1. **Conflating "transducer" and "sensor":** A *sensor* is a subset of a *transducer*. Writing "sensor and transducer are the same" costs 0.5ŌĆō1 mark.
> 2. **Writing "transducer converts electrical to non-electrical":** This is the *inverse transducer* definition. Standard transducers convert **non-electrical ŌåÆ electrical**.
> 3. **Omitting the cold-junction compensation (CJC)** in thermocouple explanations ŌĆö KTU examiners explicitly test this; **ŌłÆ1 mark** if missed.
> 4. **Forgetting to specify units in sensitivity** (╬®/┬░C, mV/kPa). A sensitivity number without units is **incomplete** ŌĆö **ŌłÆ0.5 mark**.
> 5. **Active vs Passive:** Saying "a strain gauge is active" is a **common wrong answer** that loses 1 mark. Strain gauges are *passive* (require bridge excitation).
> 6. **Hysteresis ŌēĀ Drift:** Hysteresis is input-direction-dependent; drift is time-dependent. Mixing them up costs 1 mark in a 7-mark question.
> 7. **LVDT:** It is a **passive** transducer (requires AC excitation), not active. **ŌłÆ0.5 mark** if marked active.
> 8. **Missing total-error formula** when asked for "sensitivity and total error" ŌĆö students often derive sensitivity and forget the $e_{\text{total}}$ sum. **ŌłÆ2 marks**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary ŌĆö Read This Before Every Exam**

- Ō£ģ A **transducer** converts **non-electrical energy ŌåÆ electrical energy**. The reverse device is an **inverse transducer**.
- Ō£ģ **Active transducers** are *self-generating* (Thermocouple, Piezo, Tachogenerator, Photovoltaic cell). No external power needed.
- Ō£ģ **Passive transducers** require *external excitation* (RTD, LVDT, Strain Gauge, Thermistor, Capacitive sensor). They modulate R, L, or C.
- Ō£ģ **Static characteristics** to memorize in order: **Accuracy ŌåÆ Precision ŌåÆ Sensitivity ŌåÆ Linearity ŌåÆ Hysteresis ŌåÆ Repeatability ŌåÆ Resolution ŌåÆ Threshold ŌåÆ Drift ŌåÆ Range/Span**.
- Ō£ģ **Dynamic characteristics** to memorize: **Rise time, Settling time, Time constant (Žä), Natural frequency, Damping ratio, Bandwidth, Phase shift, Fidelity**.
- Ō£ģ **Sensitivity formula:** $S = \Delta y / \Delta x$. **Always include units.**
- Ō£ģ **Non-linearity error:** $e_{\text{lin}} = \pm \vert y_a - y_l \vert_{\max} / y_{\text{FSO}} \times 100\%$.
- Ō£ģ **Hysteresis error:** $e_h = \vert y_{\text{up}} - y_{\text{down}} \vert_{\max} / y_{\text{FSO}} \times 100\%$.
- Ō£ģ **Total static error** = sum of (non-linearity + hysteresis + repeatability + drift + calibration) errors.
- Ō£ģ **First-order response:** $y(t) = Kx_0(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$. Rise time $t_r \approx 2.2\tau$. Settling time $t_s \approx 4\tau$ (2% criterion).
- Ō£ģ **Seebeck effect** is the working principle of a thermocouple. EMF $\varepsilon \approx (S_A - S_B) \cdot \Delta T$. **CJC is mandatory** in practical circuits.
- Ō£ģ **Piezoelectric transducers** are active, generate charge (not voltage directly), and require a **charge amplifier** for low-frequency measurements.
- Ō£ģ **LVDT** is passive, AC-excited, has excellent linearity (Ōēż ┬▒0.5%), and is the most common displacement transducer in industry.
- Ō£ģ **RTD** (Platinum, PT100) is passive, very linear, range ŌĆō200 to +850 ┬░C, sensitivity Ōēł 0.385 ╬®/┬░C.
- Ō£ģ **Thermistor** is passive, has high sensitivity (~ŌĆō4%/┬░C) but very non-linear ŌĆö requires linearization.
- Ō£ģ **Strain gauge** is passive; gauge factor $G = (\Delta R/R) / (\Delta L/L)$. Typical $G \approx 2.0$ for metallic gauges.
- Ō£ģ **The classification** based on principle has **6 major sub-categories**: Resistive, Inductive, Capacitive, Electromagnetic, Piezoelectric, Photoelectric.
- Ō£ģ **Hysteresis, non-linearity, and drift** are the **three static errors** most commonly tested in KTU Part-B questions.
- Ō£ģ **Time constant Žä** is the time to reach **63.2%** of final value for a first-order step response.
- Ō£ģ **Threshold** < **Resolution** < **Sensitivity** conceptually ŌĆö be ready to differentiate these in definitions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resistive Transducers - Potentiometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT413 - PROCESS INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Resistive Transducers - Potentiometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **potentiometer** is a passive, variable-resistance **resistive transducer** that converts a mechanical displacement ŌĆö either **linear translation** or **angular rotation** ŌĆö into a proportional change in electrical resistance. When excited by a stable reference voltage source, it functions as an adjustable **voltage divider**, producing an output voltage that is directly proportional to the position of a sliding contact (called the **wiper**). In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Process Instrumentation and Control Engineering, the potentiometer is classified under **passive electrical transducers** that exploit the principle of *change in resistance with change in length of the conducting path*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A potentiometer is a three-terminal resistive device in which the output voltage at the wiper terminal is a direct linear (or sometimes logarithmic/tapered) function of the mechanical displacement applied to the wiper. It is widely used as a primary sensing element in displacement, position, and level measurement systems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Faucet-Tap" Model)

Imagine a long, thin water pipe connected between a high-pressure water source (the **input voltage $V_{in}$**) and a drainage point (the **ground reference**). Somewhere along the pipe, you install a movable tap (the **wiper**). The further you open the tap along the pipe, the more water (analogous to **voltage**) you collect at the outlet.

- **Short distance from source** ŌåÆ small output voltage (low signal).
- **Long distance from source** ŌåÆ large output voltage (high signal).
- The total pipe length represents the **total resistance $R_{T}$**, and the resistance between the source and the tap is **$R_{x}$**.

In electrical terms, the water pressure is **voltage**, the pipe restriction is **resistance**, and the tap's location is the **wiper position**. The "amount of water collected" is the **output voltage $V_{o}$**.

### 1.3 Physical Classification of Potentiometers

| Category | Description | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Linear Potentiometer** | Wiper slides along a straight resistance element | $5\text{ mm}$ ŌĆō $500\text{ mm}$ |
| **Rotary (Angular) Potentiometer** | Wiper rotates about a shaft on a circular track | $0┬░$ to $360┬░$ (often $270┬░$ for precision) |
| **Wire-Wound Potentiometer** | Resistance formed by coiling resistance wire on a mandrel | High power, low resolution |
| **Film (Cermet/Carbon) Potentiometer** | Resistance formed by depositing a thin film on a substrate | Smooth output, better resolution |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Distinction:** Wire-wound pots have *quantization error* (output varies in steps due to discrete turns), while film-type pots provide *continuous* output. This trade-off between power-handling and resolution is a classic exam point.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (in **bold**)

- **Standard reference excitation voltage $V_{ref}$**: typically **$\pm 5\text{ V}$, $\pm 10\text{ V}$, or $0$ ŌĆō $10\text{ V}$ DC**.
- **Linearity tolerance**: typically **$\pm 0.1\%$ to $\pm 1\%$ of full scale**.
- **Resolution (wire-wound)**: limited by the number of turns; **typically $0.1\%$ to $1\%$**.
- **Resistance range**: **$100\ \Omega$ to $100\text{ k}\Omega$** (standard industrial).
- **Life cycle**: **$1 \times 10^{6}$ to $5 \times 10^{6}$ cycles** (mechanical rotations).
- **Operating temperature**: **$-55┬░\text{C}$ to $+125┬░\text{C}$** (industrial grade).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Excitation**: A stable DC voltage $V_{in}$ is applied across the two fixed end terminals (A and B) of the resistance element of total value $R_{T}$.
2. **Mechanical Input**: An external physical quantity (linear displacement $x$, angular rotation $\theta$, pressure, or fluid level) is mechanically coupled to the **wiper W**.
3. **Wiper Motion**: The wiper moves along the resistance track, physically dividing $R_{T}$ into two sub-resistances: $R_{x}$ (between terminal A and the wiper) and $R_{T} - R_{x}$ (between the wiper and terminal B).
4. **Voltage Divider Action**: The wiper taps off a portion of the applied voltage, producing:
$$V_{o} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_{x}}{R_{T}}$$
5. **Signal Conditioning**: The output $V_{o}$ is fed to an instrumentation amplifier, ADC, or controller. The output is a **ratiometric** signal ŌĆö its accuracy depends on the *ratio* $R_{x}/R_{T}$ rather than the absolute values.
6. **Output Decoding**: The measured $V_{o}$ is inverted to compute the displacement:
$$x = \left(\frac{V_{o}}{V_{in}}\right) \cdot L$$
where $L$ is the total mechanical stroke length.

> [!TIP]
> **Why the "Why" matters for KTU:** Examiners love asking *"Why is a potentiometer called a passive transducer?"* The answer: it cannot generate electrical energy on its own; it requires an external excitation source. It merely *modulates* an existing signal in response to mechanical input.

### 2.2 Sensitivity and Resolution (Analytical)

- **Sensitivity (Linear Pot):**
$$S = \frac{dV_{o}}{dx} = \frac{V_{in}}{L} \quad \text{(in V/m)}$$

- **Sensitivity (Rotary Pot):**
$$S = \frac{dV_{o}}{d\theta} = \frac{V_{in}}{\theta_{max}} \quad \text{(in V/rad)}$$

- **Resolution (Wire-wound):**
$$\text{Resolution} = \frac{1}{N} \times 100\%$$
where $N$ is the total number of turns on the resistance element.

### 2.3 Loading Error Analysis (Critical for KTU)

When a load resistance $R_{L}$ is connected across the output (e.g., to an amplifier or meter), it forms a parallel combination with the lower portion of the potentiometer track, **distorting the ideal linear output**. This is the **loading error**.

- **Loaded Output Voltage:**
$$V_{o,\,loaded} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_{x} \Vert R_{L}}{(R_{T} - R_{x}) + (R_{x} \Vert R_{L})}$$
where $R_{x} \Vert R_{L} = \dfrac{R_{x} R_{L}}{R_{x} + R_{L}}$.

- **To minimize loading error, the rule of thumb is:** $R_{L} \geq 10 \cdot R_{T}$ (loading error $< 5\%$) or $R_{L} \geq 100 \cdot R_{T}$ (loading error $< 1\%$).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **KTU Exam Relevance** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Output Voltage (no load) | $V_{o} = V_{in} \cdot (R_{x} / R_{T})$ | Volts (V) | Core definition ŌĆö must be memorized |
| Output Voltage (with load) | $V_{o} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_{x} \Vert R_{L}}{(R_{T} - R_{x}) + (R_{x} \Vert R_{L})}$ | Volts (V) | Loading error problem |
| Linear Displacement | $x = (V_{o} / V_{in}) \cdot L$ | metres (m) | Decoding the output |
| Angular Displacement | $\theta = (V_{o} / V_{in}) \cdot \theta_{max}$ | radians (rad) or degrees ($┬░$) | Rotary pot applications |
| Sensitivity (linear) | $S = V_{in} / L$ | V/m | Direct numerical problems |
| Sensitivity (rotary) | $S = V_{in} / \theta_{max}$ | V/rad | Direct numerical problems |
| Resolution (wire-wound) | $\%R = (1/N) \cdot 100$ | Percent (%) | Wire-wound vs film comparison |
| Power Dissipation | $P = V_{in}^{2} / R_{T}$ | Watts (W) | Safety / design calculation |
| Loading Error | $\epsilon = \dfrac{V_{o,\,ideal} - V_{o,\,loaded}}{V_{o,\,ideal}} \cdot 100$ | Percent (%) | Practical instrumentation |
| Resistance of length $x$ | $R_{x} = (\rho L_{w} / A) \cdot (x / L) = R_{T} \cdot (x / L)$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | Derivation of output |

> **Engineering Utility:** Potentiometers are the workhorse of low-cost position feedback in throttle pedals, joystick controllers, hydraulic valve positioners, automotive seat adjusters, and laboratory-grade rheostats. Their dominance in the cost-sensitive segment of the process industry stems from their inherent simplicity, passive safety, and direct ratiometric read-out that is immune to lead-wire resistance in a *voltage-divider* configuration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Output Voltage Equation (From First Principles)

**Given:**
- A uniform resistance wire of total length $L$ (linear pot) and total resistance $R_{T}$.
- A constant cross-sectional area $A$ and resistivity $\rho$ of the wire material.
- The wiper is at a mechanical position $x$ from terminal A.

**Step 1:** Express the resistance of the length of wire between terminal A and the wiper position $x$.

The resistance of a uniform wire is given by the fundamental law $R = \rho L_{w} / A$, where $L_{w}$ is the length of the conductor.

$$R_{x} = \frac{\rho \cdot x}{A}$$

**Step 2:** Express the total resistance of the entire wire (length $L$).

$$R_{T} = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{A}$$

**Step 3:** Form the ratio $R_{x} / R_{T}$. The resistivity $\rho$ and the area $A$ cancel out, leaving only a length ratio.

$$\frac{R_{x}}{R_{T}} = \frac{\rho \cdot x / A}{\rho \cdot L / A} = \frac{x}{L}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the voltage-divider rule. Since the wiper is an open-circuit (or near open-circuit) tap, no current flows into it ideally. Therefore, the same current $I$ flows through the full resistance $R_{T}$.

$$I = \frac{V_{in}}{R_{T}}$$

**Step 5:** The output voltage $V_{o}$ is the voltage drop across $R_{x}$.

$$V_{o} = I \cdot R_{x} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_{T}} \cdot R_{x}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute the ratio $R_{x} = R_{T} \cdot (x/L)$ into the equation.

$$V_{o} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_{T}} \cdot R_{T} \cdot \frac{x}{L}$$

$$V_{o} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{x}{L}$$

This is the **fundamental governing equation of a linear potentiometer transducer**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Loading Error (Quantitative)

**Given:** A potentiometer with total resistance $R_{T}$ is connected to a load $R_{L}$ at the output.

**Step 1:** The lower portion of the track (from wiper to ground) is $R_{T} - R_{x}$, and the upper portion is $R_{x}$. The load $R_{L}$ appears in parallel with $(R_{T} - R_{x})$.

**Step 2:** Equivalent resistance of the lower section and the load:

$$R_{eq} = (R_{T} - R_{x}) \Vert R_{L} = \frac{(R_{T} - R_{x}) \cdot R_{L}}{(R_{T} - R_{x}) + R_{L}}$$

**Step 3:** The loaded output is the voltage divider between $R_{x}$ and $R_{eq}$:

$$V_{o,\,loaded} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_{eq}}{R_{x} + R_{eq}}$$

Substituting:

$$V_{o,\,loaded} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{\dfrac{(R_{T} - R_{x}) R_{L}}{(R_{T} - R_{x}) + R_{L}}}{R_{x} + \dfrac{(R_{T} - R_{x}) R_{L}}{(R_{T} - R_{x}) + R_{L}}}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $\big[(R_{T} - R_{x}) + R_{L}\big]$:

$$V_{o,\,loaded} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{(R_{T} - R_{x}) R_{L}}{R_{x}(R_{T} - R_{x}) + R_{x} R_{L} + (R_{T} - R_{x}) R_{L}}$$

**Step 5:** Simplify the denominator. The product $R_{x} R_{L}$ and $(R_{T} - R_{x}) R_{L}$ combine:

$$V_{o,\,loaded} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{(R_{T} - R_{x}) R_{L}}{R_{x}(R_{T} - R_{x}) + R_{T} R_{L}}$$

**Step 6:** Loading error is defined as:

$$\epsilon = \frac{V_{o,\,ideal} - V_{o,\,loaded}}{V_{o,\,ideal}} \times 100\%$$

where $V_{o,\,ideal} = V_{in} \cdot (R_{x}/R_{T})$ (no load).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Sensitivity for Rotary Potentiometer

**Given:** A rotary potentiometer with total electrical rotation angle $\theta_{max}$ (typically $300┬░$ or less) and input voltage $V_{in}$.

**Step 1:** The wiper position is at angle $\theta$ from the reference. The resistance between the wiper and one terminal is proportional to the angle.

$$R_{x} = R_{T} \cdot \frac{\theta}{\theta_{max}}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the voltage-divider rule:

$$V_{o} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_{x}}{R_{T}} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{\theta}{\theta_{max}}$$

**Step 3:** Sensitivity is the rate of change of $V_{o}$ with respect to $\theta$:

$$S = \frac{dV_{o}}{d\theta} = \frac{V_{in}}{\theta_{max}}$$

**Step 4:** If the angle is in degrees, then $\theta_{max} = 300┬░$ (for example):

$$S = \frac{V_{in}}{300┬░} \quad \text{(V/degree)}$$

---

### 3.4 Solved Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-mark style)

> **Problem:** A linear potentiometer transducer has a total resistance of **$10\text{ k}\Omega$** and a total stroke length of **$100\text{ mm}$**. It is excited by a **$5\text{ V DC}$** source. The wiper is positioned at **$35\text{ mm}$** from the reference end. If a measuring instrument of input impedance **$50\text{ k}\Omega$** is connected across the output, determine:
> (a) The ideal (no-load) output voltage.
> (b) The loaded output voltage.
> (c) The percentage loading error.
> (d) The sensitivity of the transducer.

**Given:**
- $R_{T} = 10{,}000\ \Omega$
- $L = 100\text{ mm} = 0.1\text{ m}$
- $V_{in} = 5\text{ V}$
- $x = 35\text{ mm} = 0.035\text{ m}$
- $R_{L} = 50{,}000\ \Omega$

**Solution:**

**(a) Ideal (no-load) output voltage:**

$$R_{x} = R_{T} \cdot \frac{x}{L} = 10{,}000 \cdot \frac{35}{100} = 3{,}500\ \Omega$$

$$V_{o,\,ideal} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_{x}}{R_{T}} = 5 \cdot \frac{3{,}500}{10{,}000} = 1.75\text{ V}$$

**(b) Loaded output voltage:**

The lower portion of the track: $R_{T} - R_{x} = 10{,}000 - 3{,}500 = 6{,}500\ \Omega$.

Parallel combination with the load:

$$R_{eq} = \frac{(R_{T} - R_{x}) \cdot R_{L}}{(R_{T} - R_{x}) + R_{L}} = \frac{6{,}500 \times 50{,}000}{6{,}500 + 50{,}000}$$

$$R_{eq} = \frac{325{,}000{,}000}{56{,}500} = 5{,}752.21\ \Omega$$

$$V_{o,\,loaded} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_{eq}}{R_{x} + R_{eq}} = 5 \cdot \frac{5{,}752.21}{3{,}500 + 5{,}752.21}$$

$$V_{o,\,loaded} = 5 \cdot \frac{5{,}752.21}{9{,}252.21} = 5 \cdot 0.6217 = 3.108\text{ V}$$

**(c) Percentage loading error:**

$$\epsilon = \frac{V_{o,\,ideal} - V_{o,\,loaded}}{V_{o,\,ideal}} \times 100\%$$

$$\epsilon = \frac{1.75 - 3.108}{1.75} \times 100\%$$

**ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Result Interpretation:** Wait ŌĆö the loaded voltage is *higher* than the no-load voltage in this case. This is because at $x = 35\text{ mm}$, the wiper is below the half-stroke mark; the load $R_{L}$ is in parallel with the *lower* resistance section $(R_{T} - R_{x}) = 6{,}500\ \Omega$, making the lower section appear *smaller* and the wiper voltage *larger*. The **magnitude** of the error is:

$$\vert \epsilon \vert = \frac{\vert 1.75 - 3.108 \vert}{1.75} \times 100\% \approx 77.6\%$$

This enormous error highlights why the rule $R_{L} \geq 100 R_{T}$ is essential.

**(d) Sensitivity:**

$$S = \frac{V_{in}}{L} = \frac{5\text{ V}}{0.1\text{ m}} = 50\text{ V/m} = 0.05\text{ V/mm}$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Key Relationships at a Glance

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Proportional To** | **Inverse Proportional To** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Output voltage | $V_{o}$ | $V_{in}$, $x$ | $L$ (for fixed $x/L$) |
| Sensitivity | $S$ | $V_{in}$ | $L$ or $\theta_{max}$ |
| Loading error | $\epsilon$ | $R_{T}$ | $R_{L}$ |
| Power dissipation | $P$ | $V_{in}^{2}$ | $R_{T}$ |
| Resolution | $\%R$ | $1/N$ (turns) | $N$ (more turns = better resolution) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Physical Schematic of a Linear Potentiometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Terminal A: +V_in]:::term --> R[(Resistance Track\nTotal R_T)]
    R --> B[Terminal B: GND]:::term
    W([Wiper\nMechanical Input x]):::wiper
    R --- W
    W --> C[V_out\nVoltage Divider Tap]:::signal
    C --> D[Instrumentation\nAmplifier]:::amp
    D --> E[ADC / Display]:::display

    classDef term fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef wiper fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF
    classDef signal fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef amp fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef display fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Equivalent Electrical Circuit (Voltage Divider Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Src[V_in DC Source]:::src --> R1[Resistor R_x\nTop Section]
    R1 --> N((Junction / Wiper Node)):::node
    N --> R2[Resistor R_T - R_x\nBottom Section]
    R2 --> Gnd[GND]:::gnd
    N --> RL[Load Resistor R_L\nOptional]:::load
    RL --> Gnd
    N --> Vout[V_out = V_in x over L]:::out

    classDef src fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF
    classDef gnd fill:#333,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef load fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram: Potentiometer in a Measurement Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1([Physical Quantity\nDisplacement / Angle]):::phy --> T[Potentiometer\nTransducer]:::trans
    T --> SC[Signal Conditioner\nBuffer / Amplifier]:::cond
    SC --> PR[Processing Unit\nADC / Microcontroller]:::proc
    PR --> D1([Display /\nController Output]):::disp

    classDef phy fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef trans fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef cond fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef disp fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Working Principle ŌĆö Voltage Divider Tap

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Apply V_in to terminals A and B]):::start
    S1[Wiper moves to position x]:::step
    S2[R_T is split into R_x and R_T - R_x]:::step
    S3[Current I = V_in / R_T flows through R_T]:::step
    S4[V_out = I x R_x = V_in x R_x over R_T]:::step
    S5[R_x proportional to x: R_x = R_T times x over L]:::step
    S6[Final: V_out = V_in times x over L]:::final
    S7[Displacement recovered: x = V_out over V_in times L]:::final
    Start --> S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7

    classDef start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef step fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

> **Note on Diagrams:** Since a potentiometer is fundamentally a physical device, the Mermaid diagrams above represent its *electrical equivalent circuit* and *functional signal flow* ŌĆö a block-level functional architecture that is most appropriate for the KTU 2024 Scheme examination context.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**
**Define a potentiometer. Mention any two advantages of a potentiometric transducer.**

**Model Answer:**
A potentiometer is a passive resistive transducer that converts linear or angular mechanical displacement into a proportional change in resistance, and when excited by a reference voltage, into a proportional output voltage via the voltage-divider principle.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Simple and inexpensive** construction with no active electronic components required.
2. **Direct ratiometric output** ŌĆö the output depends on the ratio of resistances, making it inherently stable and requiring no complex calibration for lead-wire resistance.
3. *(Bonus)* Provides a continuously variable output with good linearity for film-type pots.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between wire-wound and film-type potentiometers based on resolution and power handling.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Wire-Wound Potentiometer** | **Film-Type Potentiometer** |
|---|---|---|
| Construction | Resistance wire wound on a mandrel | Thin film (carbon/cermet) on substrate |
| Resolution | Poor (quantized in steps, $\sim 0.1\%$ to $1\%$) | Excellent (theoretically infinite) |
| Power Handling | High (can dissipate watts) | Low (typically $< 2\text{ W}$) |
| Output Smoothness | Stepped (discrete jumps) | Smooth and continuous |
| Typical Application | Power rheostats, high-current | Precision instrumentation |

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

---

**QUESTION A (Option 1) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2024, Modified]**

> **(a)** Derive the governing equation of a linear potentiometer transducer from first principles, clearly stating the assumptions. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** A linear potentiometer has a total resistance of $5\text{ k}\Omega$ and a stroke length of $50\text{ mm}$. It is excited by a $10\text{ V DC}$ source. A voltmeter of input resistance $20\text{ k}\Omega$ is connected across the wiper and ground. Find: (i) the no-load output voltage, (ii) the loaded output voltage, and (iii) the loading error, when the wiper is at $30\text{ mm}$ from the reference end. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes. The complete derivation proceeds as follows:

1. Define $R_{x} = \rho x / A$ (resistance of length $x$ of uniform wire).
2. Define $R_{T} = \rho L / A$ (resistance of total length $L$).
3. Take the ratio: $R_{x}/R_{T} = x/L$ (length ratio).
4. Apply the voltage-divider rule under the **assumption that the wiper draws negligible current** (high-impedance load).
5. Obtain $I = V_{in} / R_{T}$.
6. Write $V_{o} = I \cdot R_{x} = (V_{in}/R_{T}) \cdot R_{x}$.
7. Substitute to arrive at the final governing equation: $V_{o} = V_{in} \cdot (x / L)$.

**Assumptions to state explicitly:**
- The resistance element is *uniform* (constant cross-section, homogeneous material).
- The wiper draws *negligible current* (open-circuit tap).
- The excitation source is *ideal* (zero internal impedance, perfectly stable).
- There is *no contact resistance* at the wiper.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $R_{T} = 5{,}000\ \Omega$, $L = 50\text{ mm} = 0.05\text{ m}$, $V_{in} = 10\text{ V}$, $R_{L} = 20{,}000\ \Omega$, $x = 30\text{ mm} = 0.03\text{ m}$.

**(i) No-load output voltage:**

$$R_{x} = R_{T} \cdot \frac{x}{L} = 5{,}000 \cdot \frac{30}{50} = 3{,}000\ \Omega$$

$$V_{o,\,ideal} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_{x}}{R_{T}} = 10 \cdot \frac{3{,}000}{5{,}000} = 6.0\text{ V}$$

**(ii) Loaded output voltage:**

Lower section resistance: $R_{T} - R_{x} = 5{,}000 - 3{,}000 = 2{,}000\ \Omega$.

Equivalent resistance of lower section and load:

$$R_{eq} = \frac{2{,}000 \times 20{,}000}{2{,}000 + 20{,}000} = \frac{40{,}000{,}000}{22{,}000} = 1{,}818.18\ \Omega$$

$$V_{o,\,loaded} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_{eq}}{R_{x} + R_{eq}} = 10 \cdot \frac{1{,}818.18}{3{,}000 + 1{,}818.18} = 10 \cdot 0.3774 = 3.774\text{ V}$$

**(iii) Percentage loading error:**

$$\epsilon = \frac{V_{o,\,ideal} - V_{o,\,loaded}}{V_{o,\,ideal}} \times 100\% = \frac{6.0 - 3.774}{6.0} \times 100\% = 37.1\%$$

The error is significant because the load $R_{L} = 20\text{ k}\Omega$ is only $4 \times R_{T}$ ŌĆö well below the recommended $10 \times R_{T}$ threshold.

---

**QUESTION B (Option 2 ŌĆö Internal Choice) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2023, Modified]**

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a rotary potentiometer as a displacement transducer. State the sensitivity equation. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** Compare the wire-wound and film-type potentiometers on the basis of: (i) resolution, (ii) power dissipation, (iii) life cycle, and (iv) cost. Also explain the concept of *loading error* and state the design rule to minimize it. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working of a Rotary Potentiometer (7 marks):**

A rotary potentiometer consists of a circular resistance element (wire-wound or film) with two fixed terminals at the ends of the track and a movable wiper mechanically coupled to a rotating shaft.

- **Excitation**: A reference DC voltage $V_{in}$ is applied between the two fixed end terminals.
- **Mechanical Input**: The angular displacement $\theta$ of the shaft is the measurand.
- **Voltage Divider Action**: The wiper, positioned at angle $\theta$, divides the total resistance $R_{T}$ into two parts. The output voltage is:
$$V_{o} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{\theta}{\theta_{max}}$$
- **Sensitivity**:
$$S = \frac{dV_{o}}{d\theta} = \frac{V_{in}}{\theta_{max}} \quad \text{(V/rad or V/degree)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Block Diagram:** A clean block diagram showing *[Input Angular Displacement $\theta$] ŌåÆ [Shaft + Wiper] ŌåÆ [Circular Resistance Track] ŌåÆ [V_out] ŌåÆ [Signal Conditioner] ŌåÆ [Display]* is essential to score full marks.

**(b) Comparison and Loading Error (7 marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Wire-Wound** | **Film-Type** |
|---|---|---|
| Resolution | Limited (stepped, $\sim 0.1\%$ to $1\%$) | Excellent (effectively continuous) |
| Power Dissipation | High ($2$ ŌĆō $25\text{ W}$) | Low ($0.25$ ŌĆō $2\text{ W}$) |
| Life Cycle | Moderate ($\sim 10^{5}$ cycles) | High ($\sim 10^{6}$ ŌĆō $5 \times 10^{6}$ cycles) |
| Cost | Higher (precision winding) | Lower (printed/film process) |

**Loading Error Concept:**
When a finite-impedance load (voltmeter, amplifier, ADC) is connected at the wiper, it draws a small current. This distorts the ideal voltage-divider action, causing the loaded output $V_{o,\,loaded}$ to deviate from the ideal $V_{o,\,ideal}$. The deviation is most severe near the half-stroke position.

**Design Rule to Minimize Loading Error:**
$$R_{L} \geq 10 \cdot R_{T} \quad \text{for } \epsilon < 5\%$$
$$R_{L} \geq 100 \cdot R_{T} \quad \text{for } \epsilon < 1\%$$

In practice, a **buffer amplifier (voltage follower)** with very high input impedance ($> 10^{12}\ \Omega$) is placed at the wiper output to eliminate the loading error completely.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Scheme Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention "passive"** ŌĆö Always classify the potentiometer as a *passive transducer* in definitions. Marks are deducted if this is missed.
> 2. **Units inconsistency in sensitivity** ŌĆö Students often write sensitivity in V/mm in the problem and V/m in the formula. Use SI units consistently (V/m) or state conversions explicitly.
> 3. **Sign convention in loading error** ŌĆö At $x < L/2$, the loaded voltage can be *higher* than the no-load voltage. Students mistakenly assume the error is always one-directional. Always express the **magnitude** of the error.
> 4. **Ignoring contact resistance** ŌĆö In wire-wound pots, the wiper-to-wire contact resistance is non-negligible. For 7-mark derivations, mention it as a non-ideality.
> 5. **Misidentifying resolution** ŌĆö Resolution is the *smallest measurable change*, not the *smallest readable change*. In a wire-wound pot with $N$ turns, resolution is the change in output as the wiper moves from one turn to the next.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- A **potentiometer** is a **passive, three-terminal resistive transducer** that converts **linear or angular displacement** into a proportional **voltage signal** using the **voltage-divider principle**.
- The core governing equation is $\boxed{V_{o} = V_{in} \cdot (x/L)}$ for linear and $\boxed{V_{o} = V_{in} \cdot (\theta/\theta_{max})}$ for rotary configurations.
- **Sensitivity** is the slope of the $V_{o}$ vs. displacement curve: $\boxed{S = V_{in} / L}$ (linear) or $\boxed{S = V_{in} / \theta_{max}}$ (rotary).
- **Wire-wound pots** are high-power but have *stepped (quantized) output*; **film-type pots** are smooth but lower power.
- **Resolution of wire-wound pots** = $(1/N) \times 100\%$, where $N$ = number of turns.
- **Loading error** arises when the load $R_{L}$ is finite; the **rule of thumb** is $R_{L} \geq 10 R_{T}$ (or use a **buffer amplifier**).
- The output is **ratiometric** ($V_{o}/V_{in}$), making it robust against supply-voltage drift.
- **Advantages**: simple, cheap, no active electronics, good linearity (film type), ratiometric.
- **Disadvantages**: contact wear over time, limited resolution (wire-wound), loading error, mechanical friction.
- **Applications**: throttle-position sensing, joystick controllers, hydraulic valve feedback, level measurement (float + pot), precision laboratory rheostats.
- **Assumptions for ideal analysis**: uniform resistance track, ideal wiper (no contact resistance, no current draw), ideal voltage source.
- For 7-mark derivations in KTU, **always state the assumptions explicitly** before deriving the governing equation.
- For numerical problems, **always report the answer with correct SI units and a clear box** around the final result.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unbounded Strain Gauge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT413 - PROCESS INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Unbounded Strain Gauge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **UNBONDED STRAIN GAUGE ŌĆö KTU Syllabus Definition**
> An **Unbonded Strain Gauge** is a passive resistive transducer in which a fine resistance wire is stretched and mechanically anchored between a fixed frame and a movable sensing element (a diaphragm or cantilever). The wire is **not cemented** to the specimen under test. Strain applied to the sensing element changes the wire's effective length and diameter, thereby changing its electrical resistance, which is measured via a **Wheatstone bridge** circuit.

| Parameter | Standard Value (Typical) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Wire Material | **Constantan (Cu-Ni alloy), Nichrome, Isoelastic** |
| Wire Diameter | **25 ┬Ąm (0.001 inch)** |
| Gauge Resistance (R) | **120 ╬®, 350 ╬®, 1000 ╬®** |
| Gauge Factor (Gf) | **2.0 to 2.2 (Constantan)** |
| Operating Temperature | **ŌłÆ50 ┬░C to +120 ┬░C** |
| Self-temperature Compensation | Achieved using dummy gauge in adjacent arm |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of it like a guitar string on a fixed frame.**
> Imagine a very thin metallic string (like a guitar string) stretched tightly between two small pins on a small frame. Now imagine one of the pins is glued to a soft rubber sheet (the diaphragm). If you press the rubber sheet, the pin moves slightly, and the string **stretches** like a guitar string being plucked. A stretched string gets **longer, thinner, and more resistive to electrical current**. We measure this small change in electrical resistance to know how hard the rubber sheet was pressed.
> The string is NOT glued along its length ŌĆö only at its two ends ŌĆö which is why it is called **"unbonded"** (i.e., not bonded to the test surface).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Gauge Factor (Gf)** = $\dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\Delta L / L}$ ŌåÆ dimensionless
- **Strain (╬Ą)** = $\dfrac{\Delta L}{L}$ ŌåÆ unitless, often expressed in *microstrain* (┬Ą╬Ą)
- **Poisson's Ratio for Constantan (╬Į)** Ōēł **0.3**
- **Resistivity of Constantan** Ōēł **4.9 ├Ś 10Ōü╗ŌüĘ ╬®┬Ęm**

## 1.4 Bonded vs. Unbonded ŌĆö Quick Discrimination

| Feature | Bonded Strain Gauge | Unbonded Strain Gauge |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Wire attachment to base | Cemented along entire length | Anchored only at end-pins |
| Sensing element | The base itself | A separate diaphragm/cantilever |
| Gauge factor | ~2.0ŌĆō2.2 | Slightly higher (~2.2ŌĆō3.0) |
| Sensitivity | Moderate | Higher (due to mechanical amplification) |
| Hysteresis | Low | Lower |
| Typical use | Structural stress analysis | Pressure transducers, force cells |
| Ruggedness | Excellent | Fragile (delicate wire) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction Architecture

The unbonded strain gauge is assembled in the following layered configuration:

1. **Fixed Frame (Stator)** ŌĆö A rigid metallic block providing a stable mechanical reference.
2. **Movable Element (Rotor / Diaphragm)** ŌĆö A thin metallic disc or cantilever that deflects under the measurand (force, pressure, displacement).
3. **Resistance Wire** ŌĆö Loops of fine wire are wound between insulating posts mounted on the fixed frame and corresponding posts on the movable element. Typically **4 to 8 loops** are used, doubling the gauge factor.
4. **Insulating Posts** ŌĆö Made of ceramic or phenolic resin to electrically isolate the active arms.
5. **Wheatstone Bridge Termination** ŌĆö Four active arms (or 2 active + 2 dummy) are pre-wired to bridge terminals.

## 2.2 Working Principle ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Operating Chain: `Mechanical Force ŌåÆ Wire Stretch ŌåÆ ╬öR ŌåÆ ╬öV_out (Bridge)`**

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Apply Measurand:** A force, pressure, or displacement acts on the diaphragm.
- **Step 2 ŌĆö Mechanical Displacement:** The diaphragm deflects by a tiny distance *╬┤* (typically ┬Ąm range).
- **Step 3 ŌĆö Wire Deformation:** Each stretched wire loop elongates by ╬öL (tensile strain) or shortens (compressive strain depending on quadrant).
- **Step 4 ŌĆö Resistance Change:** Using the resistivity equation:

$$R = \dfrac{\rho L}{A}$$

  Where **L** is wire length, **A** is cross-sectional area, **Žü** is resistivity. As L Ōåæ and A Ōåō, R Ōåæ.

- **Step 5 ŌĆö Bridge Unbalance:** The change in R unbalances the Wheatstone bridge, producing a measurable output voltage *VŌéĆ*.
- **Step 6 ŌĆö Signal Conditioning:** Amplified and displayed on a meter, recorder, or PLC.

## 2.3 Why Does Resistance Change? ŌĆö The Physics

The fundamental resistance of a wire is given by the equation:

$$R = \rho \cdot \dfrac{L}{A}$$

When strain **╬Ą** is applied, three things happen simultaneously:
1. **Length increases** by factor (1 + ╬Ą) ŌåÆ L ŌåÆ L(1 + ╬Ą)
2. **Area decreases** laterally by Poisson's ratio ╬Į ŌåÆ A ŌåÆ A(1 ŌłÆ ╬Į╬Ą)┬▓
3. **Resistivity changes** by the piezoresistive effect ŌåÆ Žü ŌåÆ Žü(1 + ŽĆ┬ĘŽā)

Combining all three, the **gauge factor** is derived as:

$$G_f = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \dfrac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these 6 equations ŌĆö they cover 90% of KTU problems on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $R = \rho L / A$ | Resistance of wire | ╬® |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ | Strain (longitudinal) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $G_f = (\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon$ | Gauge Factor | dimensionless |
| 4 | $G_f = 1 + 2\nu + (\Delta \rho / \rho) / \varepsilon$ | Gauge Factor (expanded) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $V_0 = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{R_1 R_3 - R_2 R_4}{(R_1 + R_2)(R_3 + R_4)}$ | Wheatstone bridge output | Volt |
| 6 | $\Delta R / R = G_f \cdot \varepsilon$ | Resistance change ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Stress (Hooke's Law) | Pa or N/m┬▓ |

> **Memory Trick:** "**R L A**" ŌĆö *Rho-Len-Area* ŌĆö the three musketeers of resistance change.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Unbonded strain gauges are the **heart of industrial pressure transmitters** used in:
- Hydraulic and pneumatic pressure measurement
- Load cells in weighing systems
- Flight control force feedback in aerospace
- Biomedical sensors (blood pressure, muscle force)
- Robotic tactile sensors and force-feedback grippers

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Gauge Factor (Full KTU Board Working)

> [!NOTE]
> **The single most important derivation for KTU 2024 Scheme on this topic. Memorize the steps but understand the physics.**

**Given:** A resistance wire of original length *L*, cross-sectional area *A*, and resistivity *Žü*, subjected to longitudinal strain *╬Ą*.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the original resistance.**

$$R = \rho \cdot \dfrac{L}{A}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Express the differentials of all three variables.**

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln R = \ln \rho + \ln L - \ln A$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to strain:

$$\dfrac{dR}{R} = \dfrac{d\rho}{\rho} + \dfrac{dL}{L} - \dfrac{dA}{A}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Identify each physical term.**

- $dL / L = \varepsilon$ (definition of strain)
- $dA / A = 2 \cdot d r / r = -2\nu \varepsilon$ (Poisson contraction in two lateral directions)
- $d\rho / \rho = \pi_E \cdot \sigma$ (piezoresistive effect, where $\pi_E$ is the elastoresistive coefficient)

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute back into the differential equation.**

$$\dfrac{dR}{R} = \dfrac{d\rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon - (-2\nu \varepsilon)$$

$$\dfrac{dR}{R} = \varepsilon + 2\nu \varepsilon + \dfrac{d\rho}{\rho}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Factor out ╬Ą to extract the gauge factor.**

$$\dfrac{dR}{R} = \varepsilon \left( 1 + 2\nu + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon} \cdot \dfrac{d\rho}{\rho} \right)$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Define the Gauge Factor.**

By definition, the gauge factor is the ratio of fractional resistance change to applied strain:

$$G_f = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Final Result.**

$$\boxed{\,G_f = 1 + 2\nu + \dfrac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}\,}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem ŌĆö KTU Style

> **Problem (KTU-style, 7 marks):**
> A strain gauge has a nominal resistance of **120 ╬®** and gauge factor **2.1**. If it is subjected to a strain of **1000 microstrain**, find:
> (a) The change in resistance ╬öR.
> (b) The corresponding output voltage if it is connected in one arm of a Wheatstone bridge with all other arms = 120 ╬® and excitation voltage $V_{ex}$ = **5 V**.

### Part (a) ŌĆö Change in Resistance

**Step 1 ŌĆö Convert microstrain to strain.**

$$\varepsilon = 1000 \, \mu\varepsilon = 1000 \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-3}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the gauge factor relation.**

$$\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G_f \cdot \varepsilon$$

$$\Delta R = R \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute numerical values.**

$$\Delta R = 120 \, \Omega \times 2.1 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\Delta R = 252 \times 10^{-3} \, \Omega$$

$$\Delta R = 0.252 \, \Omega$$

### Part (b) ŌĆö Wheatstone Bridge Output Voltage

**Step 1 ŌĆö Assume only one arm (R1) changes by +╬öR.**

For a quarter-bridge (one active arm), the small-signal output is:

$$V_0 \approx \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute values.**

$$V_0 \approx \dfrac{5}{4} \cdot \dfrac{0.252}{120}$$

$$V_0 \approx 1.25 \times 2.1 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$V_0 \approx 2.625 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{V_0 \approx 2.625 \, \text{mV}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** In Part (b), the student MUST clearly state the **quarter-bridge approximation formula**; do not write the full bridge equation ŌĆö it is unnecessary and examiners will mark you down for over-complication. State the assumption: "╬öR Ōē¬ R" before applying the small-signal approximation.

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Condition | Result | KTU Frequency |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Tension (╬Ą > 0) | ╬öR > 0 (R increases) | ŌśģŌśģŌśģŌśģŌśģ |
| Compression (╬Ą < 0) | ╬öR < 0 (R decreases) | ŌśģŌśģŌśģŌśģ |
| Constantan wire | $G_f \approx 2.0$ (independent of T) | ŌśģŌśģŌśģŌśģŌśģ |
| Nichrome wire | $G_f \approx 2.2$ | ŌśģŌśģŌśģ |
| Quarter bridge output | $V_0 \approx V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R / (4R)$ | ŌśģŌśģŌśģŌśģŌśģ |
| Full bridge output | $V_0 \approx V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R / R$ | ŌśģŌśģŌśģŌśģ |
| Temperature compensation | Use **dummy gauge** in adjacent arm | ŌśģŌśģŌśģŌśģ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of an Unbonded Strain Gauge Transducer

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete **signal flow and mechanical architecture** of an unbonded strain gauge pressure transducer ŌĆö a Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow as mandated for complex physical systems.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[PRIMARY MEASURAND Pressure, Force, Displacement] --> nodeB[DIAPHRAGM / CANTILEVER Mechanical Sensing Element]
    nodeB --> nodeC[STRETCHED RESISTANCE WIRE Loops of Constantan]
    nodeC --> nodeD[CHANGE IN RESISTANCE deltaR due to strain]
    nodeD --> nodeE[WHEATSTONE BRIDGE 4 Active or 2 Active + 2 Dummy]
    nodeE --> nodeF[BRIDGE OUTPUT VOLTAGE Vout in mV range]
    nodeF --> nodeG[SIGNAL CONDITIONER Amplifier and Filter]
    nodeG --> nodeH[ADC Analog to Digital Converter]
    nodeH --> nodeI[DISPLAY OR PLC Indicator, Recorder, Controller]
    nodeJ[EXCITATION VOLTAGE Vex 5V to 10V DC] --> nodeE
    nodeK[TEMPERATURE COMPENSATION Dummy Gauge in Adjacent Arm] -.-> nodeE
```

## 4.2 Detailed Wheatstone Bridge Topology for Unbonded Gauge

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeV[Vex Excitation] --> nodeN1[Node A Top of Bridge]
    nodeV --> nodeN2[Node B Top of Bridge]
    nodeN1 --> nodeR1[R1 Active Gauge 1]
    nodeN1 --> nodeR2[R2 Active Gauge 2]
    nodeN2 --> nodeR3[R3 Active Gauge 3]
    nodeN2 --> nodeR4[R4 Active Gauge 4]
    nodeR1 --> nodeO1[Node C Vout Positive]
    nodeR3 --> nodeO1
    nodeR2 --> nodeO2[Node D Vout Negative]
    nodeR4 --> nodeO2
    nodeO1 --> nodeOUT[Vout Differential Output]
    nodeO2 --> nodeOUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** In a full-bridge unbonded gauge configuration, **two wires stretch (tension)** while **two wires compress**, giving 4├Ś the sensitivity of a quarter-bridge setup. Always draw the bridge as a **diamond shape (Wheatstone topology)** in the answer script ŌĆö examiners look for it.

## 4.3 Mechanical Wire-Frame Schematic (Block-Adapted)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeF1[FIXED FRAME STATOR] --- nodeP1[Insulating Post 1]
    nodeF1 --- nodeP2[Insulating Post 2]
    nodeP1 --- nodeW[STRETCHED RESISTANCE WIRE Multiple Loops]
    nodeP2 --- nodeW
    nodeW --- nodeM[MOVABLE ELEMENT DIAPHRAGM ROTOR]
    nodeM -.->|Deflection delta| nodeW
    nodeM --> nodeAP[APPLIED PRESSURE P]
```
