# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024 Model Question, 3 Marks]**
> **Define gauge factor. What is its typical value for a constantan-wire unbonded strain gauge?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Gauge Factor (Gf)** is defined as the ratio of the fractional change in electrical resistance ($\Delta R / R$) to the mechanical strain ($\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$) producing it:
>
> $$G_f = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$
>
> Mathematically, it is expanded as $G_f = 1 + 2\nu + (\Delta \rho / \rho)/\varepsilon$, where $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio and the last term is the piezoresistive contribution. For a **constantan-wire** unbonded strain gauge, the typical gauge factor is **2.0 to 2.1** and is largely **independent of temperature**, which is why constantan is preferred for industrial transducers. **(3 marks)**

### Question A2
> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 Model Question, 3 Marks]**
> **Differentiate between bonded and unbonded strain gauges. (Any 3 points)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Bonded Strain Gauge | Unbonded Strain Gauge |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Wire Attachment** | Wire is cemented along its length to the specimen | Wire is anchored only at the end-pins; not bonded |
| **Sensing Element** | The test specimen itself acts as the base | A separate diaphragm/cantilever acts as the sensing element |
| **Sensitivity** | Moderate | Higher (mechanical amplification possible) |
| **Application** | Stress analysis of structural members | Pressure transducers, load cells, force sensors |
| **Fragility** | Rugged, withstands harsh environments | Delicate, requires careful handling |

**(3 marks ŌĆö 1 mark per valid point, max 3 points)**

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A ŌĆö 14 Marks
> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Question aligned with 2024 Scheme, 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working principle of an unbonded strain gauge**. Discuss the role of the **Wheatstone bridge** in signal conditioning. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Derive the expression for the **gauge factor of a strain gauge**, starting from the fundamental resistance equation $R = \rho L / A$. State the typical range of gauge factor for constantan and nichrome. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution ŌĆö Question A(a)

The unbonded strain gauge consists of:

1. **A fixed frame (stator)** holding insulating posts on one side.
2. **A movable element (rotor/diaphragm)** on the opposite side, which deflects under the measurand.
3. **Fine resistance wires (Constantan, 25 ┬Ąm dia)** stretched between the stator posts and rotor posts in 4 to 8 loops, forming 4 active arms of a Wheatstone bridge.
4. **Insulating posts** of ceramic/phenolic to prevent short circuits.

**Working Principle:**
- When the measurand (force/pressure) acts on the diaphragm, the rotor moves by a small displacement ╬┤.
- Two opposite wires experience **tension** (R increases) and the other two experience **compression** (R decreases).
- This unbalances the Wheatstone bridge, producing a **differential output voltage** VŌéĆ proportional to the applied strain.

**Wheatstone Bridge Role:**
The bridge converts the tiny resistance change (╬öR ~ 0.1 to 0.5 ╬®) into a measurable voltage. With $V_{ex}$ = 5 V and quarter-bridge, $V_0 \approx V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon / 4$. It also enables **temperature compensation** by using a dummy gauge. **(7 marks ŌĆö 2 for construction, 3 for working, 2 for bridge role)**

### Model Solution ŌĆö Question A(b)

The full derivation is as shown in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The key result is:

$$G_f = 1 + 2\nu + \dfrac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$

**Typical Gauge Factor Values:**
| Wire Material | Gauge Factor (Gf) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Constantan | **2.0 ŌĆō 2.1** |
| Nichrome | **2.2 ŌĆō 2.5** |
| Isoelastic (Fe-Ni alloy) | **3.5 ŌĆō 3.7** |
| Semiconductor (Si) | **50 ŌĆō 200** (piezoresistive dominant) |

**(7 marks ŌĆö 4 for derivation steps, 2 for final formula, 1 for typical values)**

---

### Question Choice B ŌĆö 14 Marks (Alternative Internal Choice)
> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Question aligned with 2024 Scheme, 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** A strain gauge of nominal resistance **350 ╬®** and gauge factor **2.05** is bonded to a steel member subjected to a stress of **140 MPa**. The Young's modulus of steel is **200 GPa**. The gauge is connected in one arm of a Wheatstone bridge with all other arms = 350 ╬®. If the excitation voltage is **10 V**, find (i) the strain ╬Ą, (ii) ╬öR, and (iii) the bridge output voltage. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Explain the **temperature compensation techniques** used in unbonded strain gauges. Why is the dummy gauge method preferred? **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution ŌĆö Question Choice B(a)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Find strain from Hooke's Law.**

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{\sigma}{E} = \dfrac{140 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9} = 7 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\varepsilon = 700 \, \mu\varepsilon$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Find ╬öR.**

$$\Delta R = R \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon = 350 \times 2.05 \times 7 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\Delta R = 0.50225 \, \Omega \approx 0.502 \, \Omega$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Quarter-bridge output voltage.**

$$V_0 = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = \dfrac{10}{4} \cdot \dfrac{0.50225}{350}$$

$$V_0 = 2.5 \times 1.435 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{V_0 \approx 3.587 \, \text{mV}}$$

**(7 marks ŌĆö 2 for strain, 2 for ╬öR, 3 for VŌéĆ with correct formula used)**

### Model Solution ŌĆö Question Choice B(b)

**Temperature Effects on Strain Gauge:**
Temperature change causes:
- Apparent strain due to differential thermal expansion (gauge vs. base material)
- Change in gauge resistivity (intrinsic temperature coefficient of resistance, TCR)

**Compensation Techniques:**

1. **Self-compensating gauge:** Selected gauge alloy with near-zero TCR for the specific base material.
2. **Dummy gauge method (most preferred):**
   - A second identical strain gauge is mounted on the **same material** as the active gauge but **isolated from strain** (mounted perpendicular to the stress direction, or on a separate unstressed piece).
   - This dummy is placed in the **adjacent arm** of the Wheatstone bridge.
   - Both arms experience identical temperature-induced resistance changes, so the bridge remains balanced with respect to temperature, but only the active arm responds to mechanical strain.
3. **Half-bridge and full-bridge configurations** also provide inherent temperature compensation.

> **Why dummy gauge is preferred:** It is **simple, low-cost, and highly effective**; it cancels both the TCR effect and the differential thermal expansion effect simultaneously. **(7 marks ŌĆö 2 for effects, 3 for techniques, 2 for dummy-gauge justification)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Mark Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Writing `R = ŽüL/A` without defining variables** ŌåÆ Lose 1 mark. Always define Žü, L, A first.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert microstrain to strain** in numericals (1 ┬Ą╬Ą = 10Ōü╗ŌüČ) ŌåÆ Lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Using the full bridge equation instead of the quarter-bridge approximation** when only one arm is active ŌåÆ Lose 1 mark for over-complication; examiner deducts for "unnecessary complexity".
> 4. **Not labeling the bridge diagram with RŌéü, RŌéé, RŌéā, RŌéä and the VŌéĆ nodes** ŌåÆ Lose 1 mark in construction part.
> 5. **Mixing up tension and compression arms** in the working principle ŌåÆ Lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Omitting the units in the final answer** of numerical problems ŌåÆ Lose 0.5 mark (strict KTU valuation).
> 7. **Writing the gauge factor derivation in only 2 lines** without showing the differential step ŌåÆ Lose 3 marks; KTU 2024 Scheme mandates full step-by-step working.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö KTU 2024 Module 1**

- Ō£ģ Unbonded strain gauge = wire **anchored at end-pins only**, NOT cemented to specimen.
- Ō£ģ Sensing element is a **separate diaphragm or cantilever** ŌĆö the strain is transferred to the wire mechanically.
- Ō£ģ Resistance of wire: $R = \rho L / A$ ŌĆö change in any of the three produces ╬öR.
- Ō£ģ **Gauge Factor definition** = (fractional change in R) / (strain) = $G_f = 1 + 2\nu + (\Delta \rho / \rho)/\varepsilon$.
- Ō£ģ For Constantan, **Gf Ōēł 2.0**, Poisson's ratio ╬Į Ōēł 0.3.
- Ō£ģ Quarter-bridge output: $V_0 \approx (V_{ex}/4)(\Delta R/R)$.
- Ō£ģ Full-bridge is **4├Ś more sensitive** than quarter-bridge.
- Ō£ģ **Dummy gauge** in adjacent bridge arm = standard temperature compensation method.
- Ō£ģ Advantages: higher sensitivity, lower hysteresis, mechanically amplified displacement.
- Ō£ģ Disadvantages: delicate wire, limited range, requires careful mounting, more expensive than bonded.
- Ō£ģ Typical wire: **Constantan (Cu 60%, Ni 40%)**, diameter ~25 ┬Ąm, 4ŌĆō8 loops.
- Ō£ģ Applications: **Pressure transducers, load cells, force-feedback sensors, biomedical sensors**.
- Ō£ģ Active arms in tension ŌåæR; active arms in compression ŌåōR ŌĆö bridge goes unbalanced ŌåÆ VŌéĆ ŌēĀ 0.
- Ō£ģ Small-signal approximation is **valid only if ╬öR Ōē¬ R** (always true in practice for ┬Ą╬Ą range).
- Ō£ģ Units to remember: **╬Ą (dimensionless), R (╬®), VŌéĆ (V or mV), Gf (dimensionless), Žā (Pa)**.
- Ō£ģ Memory mantra: **"Rho L over A"** ŌåÆ R = ŽüL/A; change L ŌåÆ R, change A ŌåÆ R, change Žü ŌåÆ R.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bonded Strain Gauge and Load Cell
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT413 - PROCESS INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bonded Strain Gauge and Load Cell

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bonded Strain Gauge ŌĆö Formal Definition

A **Bonded Strain Gauge** (also called a **Bonded Resistance Strain Gauge** or **Bonded Foil/Wire Gauge**) is a passive resistive transduction element used to measure **mechanical strain (╬Ą)** on the surface of a structural member. It consists of a fine grid of resistive wire or metallic foil (the *sensing element*) sandwiched between a thin flexible carrier (the *base*) and a protective cover, and is permanently *bonded* to the test specimen using a specialized adhesive (typically cyanoacrylate or epoxy).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The "bonded" terminology distinguishes it from **unbonded strain gauges**, where the wire is stretched between fixed posts inside a housing. The bonded configuration directly transfers surface strain from the host structure to the grid via the adhesive shear layer.

The fundamental transduction principle relies on the **piezoresistive effect**: as the host surface deforms, the sensing grid deforms geometrically, altering its electrical resistance. This resistance change $\Delta R$ ŌĆö which is extremely small (typically a fraction of an ohm per 1000 ╬® base resistance) ŌĆö is detected using a **Wheatstone bridge** circuit excited by a stable DC or AC voltage source.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö "The Stretchy Guitar String"

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a guitar string stretched across a wooden block. If you pull the block apart, the string stretches ŌĆö it becomes longer and thinner. In electrical terms, *longer + thinner = more resistance*. A bonded strain gauge works on this exact idea. The "wooden block" is your machine part (a beam, shaft, or pressure vessel), and the "string" is a precisely etched metallic grid (Constantan, Karma alloy, or semiconductor) that *literally cannot move independently* of the block because it is glued to it. Strain in the block ŌåÆ strain in the string ŌåÆ change in electrical resistance ŌåÆ measurable electrical signal.

## 1.3 Load Cell ŌĆö Formal Definition

A **Load Cell** is a force-transducing instrument that converts an applied mechanical load (force, weight, or pressure) into a quantifiable electrical output, most commonly by employing **four bonded strain gauges** arranged in a full **Wheatstone bridge** configuration bonded to a precision-machined elastic element (a proving ring, bending beam, shear web, or column).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A load cell is essentially *an industrial packaging* of bonded strain gauges, with mechanical, thermal, and electrical optimization engineered around them. It is the **de-facto standard** for weighing systems, tank/silo inventory management, force testing rigs, and press-shop monitoring in process industries.

## 1.4 Standard Reference Quantities

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Base Gauge Resistance** | $R$ | **120 ╬®, 350 ╬®, 1000 ╬®** (most common: 350 ╬®) |
| **Gauge Factor** | $G_f$ | **Ōēł 2.0** (Constantan), **Ōēł 2.4** (Karma), **Ōēł 100ŌĆō200** (Semiconductor) |
| **Excitation Voltage** | $V_{ex}$ | **5 V, 10 V, 15 V DC** (typ. 10 V) |
| **Bridge Sensitivity** | mV/V | **1 mV/V, 2 mV/V, 3 mV/V** (full-scale rated output) |
| **Young's Modulus (steel)** | $E$ | **200 GPa** |
| **Poisson's Ratio (steel)** | $\nu$ | **0.30** |
| **Bridgman's Constant (Constantan)** | $C$ | Ōēł **1.0** |

> [!NOTE]
> The **gauge factor $G_f$** is the single most important non-dimensional parameter of a strain gauge ŌĆö it represents the **strain-to-resistance-change amplification** of the device. A higher $G_f$ means a larger output signal for the same strain.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Anatomy of a Bonded Strain Gauge

A bonded strain gauge is a layered composite, engineered for flawless strain transfer from the host surface:

1. **Sensing Grid (Active Element):** A serpentine pattern of metal foil (or fine wire) ŌĆö typically Constantan, Karma, or Nichrome. The serpentine shape maximizes active length within a small footprint. Total resistance usually 120 ╬®, 350 ╬®, or 1000 ╬®.
2. **Base (Carrier / Substrate):** A thin polymer film (polyimide, phenolic, or epoxy-glass) that provides mechanical handling strength and bonds to the specimen.
3. **Adhesive Layer:** Cyanoacrylate, modified phenolic, or epoxy cement. The *shear stiffness* of this layer limits the upper usable frequency (~100 kHz for foil, ~ MHz for semiconductor).
4. **Cover / Encapsulation:** Protective overlay that shields against humidity, abrasion, and chemical attack.
5. **Lead Wires / Solder Tabs:** Provide electrical connection to the bridge circuit.

> [!TIP]
> The grid's *transverse loops* are intentionally placed in regions that experience only **minimal transverse strain**, since only the longitudinal direction is intended to contribute to $\Delta R$. The end loops (loop-and-tuck terminations) minimize the transverse sensitivity $K_t$.

## 2.2 Operating Principle ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic

* **Step 1 ŌĆö Mechanical Strain Transfer:** When the host specimen is loaded, its surface undergoes strain $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$. The adhesive layer transmits this strain to the base and then to the sensing grid.
* **Step 2 ŌĆö Geometric Distortion of Grid:** The grid wires stretch (or compress), simultaneously becoming thinner (or thicker) due to the **Poisson effect** ($\nu = 0.3$ for typical alloys).
* **Step 3 ŌĆö Resistivity Change:** The material's bulk resistivity also changes (the *piezoresistive* contribution, characterized by Bridgman's constant $C$).
* **Step 4 ŌĆö Resistance Change $\Delta R$:** Combined geometric + piezoresistive effects produce a net, proportional resistance change.
* **Step 5 ŌĆö Electrical Measurement:** The $\Delta R$ is converted to a voltage by a **Wheatstone bridge** excited by $V_{ex}$.
* **Step 6 ŌĆö Output Linearization:** For $\varepsilon < 1000 \, \mu\varepsilon$ (microstrain), the response is highly linear; bridge non-linearity correction factors are applied for larger strains.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every formula required** for solving KTU 2024-Scheme problems on bonded strain gauges and load cells. Memorize these ŌĆö they are the *high-yield* items.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L}$ | Mechanical strain (longitudinal) | dimensionless (or $\mu\varepsilon$) |
| 2 | $G_f = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$ | **Gauge Factor** (definition) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G_f \cdot \varepsilon$ | Resistance change ratio | dimensionless |
| 4 | $G_f = 1 + 2\nu + C$ | **Gauge Factor** (analytical form) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Hooke's Law (stressŌĆōstrain) | Pa (or N/m┬▓) |
| 6 | $F = \sigma \cdot A$ | Axial force from stress | N |
| 7 | $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | **Bridge output ŌĆö 1 active gauge** | V |
| 8 | $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | **Bridge output ŌĆö 2 active gauges** (adjacent) | V |
| 9 | $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | **Bridge output ŌĆö 4 active gauges** (full bridge) | V |
| 10 | $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$ | Full-bridge output (4 active gauges) | V |
| 11 | $V_o\,[\text{mV/V}] = G_f \cdot \varepsilon \cdot 1000$ | Sensitivity in mV/V (for $\varepsilon$ in m/m) | mV/V |
| 12 | $W = F / g$ | Weight in mass units (where $g = 9.81$ m/s┬▓) | kg |
| 13 | $\varepsilon_b = \dfrac{6 \cdot F \cdot L}{E \cdot b \cdot h^2}$ | Bending strain on cantilever beam | dimensionless |
| 14 | $R_T = R_0 \left[1 + \alpha (T - T_0) + \beta (T - T_0)^2\right]$ | Temperature-induced resistance drift | ╬® |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Bridge Sensitivity Multiplier Rule:** The factor of 1/4, 1/2, or 1 in the output formula depends on **how many of the four bridge arms are active gauges and how they are positioned**. A common KTU mistake is assuming all 4-arm bridges give the *same* output as a 1-arm bridge.

## 2.4 Why Bonded Strain Gauges Dominate Process Instrumentation

- **High Accuracy:** $\pm 0.1\%$ of full scale is routine in industrial load cells.
- **Excellent Linearity:** Within 0.03% over a wide operating range.
- **Long-Term Stability:** Negligible zero drift; calibration stable over years.
- **Compactness & Ruggedness:** Hermetically sealed, immune to vibration noise.
- **Dynamic Response:** Capable of measuring both static and dynamic strains up to several hundred kHz.
- **Cost-Effectiveness:** Mass-manufactured, with a fully developed ecosystem of signal conditioners and instrumentation amplifiers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Gauge Factor $G_f$ from First Principles

We begin with the resistance of a uniform conductor:

$$
R = \rho \, \frac{L}{A}
$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Take the natural logarithm** of both sides to convert multiplication into addition, which makes differentiation straightforward:

$$
\ln R = \ln \rho + \ln L - \ln A
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Differentiate both sides** with respect to strain $\varepsilon$ (treating it as the independent variable):

$$
\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute the strain definition** for the longitudinal term. The strain $\varepsilon$ *is* $dL/L$ by definition:

$$
\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon - \frac{dA}{A}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Relate the area change to longitudinal strain using the Poisson effect.** For a circular cross-section, $A = \pi r^2$, so:

$$
\frac{dA}{A} = 2 \, \frac{dr}{r}
$$

By the **Poisson effect**, the lateral strain $dr/r$ is related to the longitudinal strain $\varepsilon$ through the Poisson's ratio $\nu$:

$$
\frac{dr}{r} = -\nu \, \varepsilon
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{dA}{A} = 2 \left( -\nu \varepsilon \right) = -2 \nu \varepsilon
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Express the piezoresistive term** using **Bridgman's constant $C$**, which captures the change in bulk resistivity per unit strain:

$$
\frac{d\rho}{\rho} = C \cdot \varepsilon
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Substitute (4) and (5) into (2):**

$$
\frac{dR}{R} = C \varepsilon + \varepsilon - \left( -2 \nu \varepsilon \right) = \varepsilon \left( 1 + 2 \nu + C \right)
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Define the Gauge Factor** as the ratio of fractional resistance change to strain:

$$
\boxed{G_f = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + C}
$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Numerical evaluation for a typical Constantan gauge** ($\nu = 0.30$, $C \approx 1.0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_f &= 1 + 2(0.30) + 1.0 \\
&= 1 + 0.6 + 1.0 \\
&= 2.6
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For Constantan, $G_f \approx 2.0\text{ŌĆō}2.6$ depending on alloy composition. The piezoresistive contribution ($C \approx 1$) is **comparable in magnitude** to the geometric contribution ($1 + 2\nu = 1.6$) ŌĆö a key point often asked in KTU viva voce.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Wheatstone Bridge Output Voltage

Consider a standard Wheatstone bridge with four arms $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$, excited by $V_{ex}$, with output taken between the two midpoints.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Output voltage expression** (by voltage divider analysis):

$$
V_o = V_{ex} \left[ \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right]
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Balanced bridge condition** (no strain, $V_o = 0$):

$$
\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{R_3}{R_4}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply a small resistance change $\Delta R$ to $R_1$** (single active gauge). Let $R_1 \to R_1 + \Delta R$. The output becomes:

$$
V_o = V_{ex} \left[ \frac{R_1 + \Delta R}{R_1 + \Delta R + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right]
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö For small $\Delta R$**, linearize using $\Delta R \ll R$. Assume initial balance with $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_o &= V_{ex} \left[ \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} - \frac{R}{2R} \right] \\
&= V_{ex} \left[ \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R(1 + \Delta R / 2R)} - \frac{1}{2} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply the binomial approximation** $(1 + x)^{-1} \approx 1 - x$ for small $x = \Delta R / 2R$:

$$
\frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} = \frac{1}{2} \left( 1 + \frac{\Delta R}{R} \right) \left( 1 - \frac{\Delta R}{2R} \right)
$$

Retaining only first-order terms in $\Delta R / R$:

$$
\frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} \approx \frac{1}{2} + \frac{\Delta R}{4R}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Final single-active-gauge output:**

$$
V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Substitute $\Delta R / R = G_f \cdot \varepsilon$:**

$$
\boxed{V_o = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon}{4} \quad \text{(1 active gauge)}}
$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Extension to all common bridge configurations:**

| Configuration | Active Gauges | Output Voltage | Multiplier |
|---|---|---|---|
| Quarter bridge | 1 | $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex} G_f \varepsilon}{4}$ | $\times 1/4$ |
| Half bridge (adjacent arms) | 2 | $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex} G_f \varepsilon}{2}$ | $\times 1/2$ |
| Half bridge (opposite arms) | 2 | $V_o = 0$ (temperature only) | $\times 0$ |
| Full bridge | 4 | $V_o = V_{ex} G_f \varepsilon$ | $\times 1$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Opposite-arm half-bridge gives zero output** because the two active gauges experience *opposite* strain signs (one in tension, one in compression) ŌĆö but the same temperature, so it is actually used as a *temperature-compensated* setup, not a strain-measuring one. This is a frequent KTU trick question.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Load Cell Cantilever Beam Analysis

A typical bending-beam load cell uses a **cantilever** of length $L$, rectangular cross-section $b \times h$, fixed at one end, with the load $F$ applied at the free end.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Bending moment** at distance $x$ from the free end:

$$
M(x) = F \cdot x
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Maximum bending stress** (at the fixed end, $x = L$) on the top or bottom surface:

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{6 \, F \, L}{b \, h^2}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Bending strain** at the surface (using Hooke's law $\varepsilon = \sigma / E$):

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon = \frac{6 \, F \, L}{E \, b \, h^2}}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Bridge output for a full-bridge load cell** (with $G_f$ known):

$$
V_o = V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon = \frac{6 \, V_{ex} \, G_f \, F \, L}{E \, b \, h^2}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Sensitivity** $S = V_o / F$:

$$
S = \frac{6 \, V_{ex} \, G_f \, L}{E \, b \, h^2}
$$

> [!TIP]
> The sensitivity scales **linearly** with $L$ and **inversely with the square of $h$**. Doubling the beam thickness quarters the sensitivity. This is why load-cell designers choose thin, slender beams for high resolution ŌĆö but at the cost of reduced load capacity and increased susceptibility to side loads.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö KTU Problem-Solving Workflow

| Step | Action | Equation / Check |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identify the type of bridge used | Quarter / Half / Full |
| 2 | Identify the number of active gauges | $n_{active} \in \{1, 2, 4\}$ |
| 3 | Compute the strain $\varepsilon$ | From force, stress, or directly given |
| 4 | Apply $\Delta R / R = G_f \cdot \varepsilon$ | Resistance change ratio |
| 5 | Multiply by bridge sensitivity factor | $1/4, 1/2, \text{or } 1$ |
| 6 | Multiply by excitation $V_{ex}$ | Final $V_o$ in volts |
| 7 | Convert to mV/V for spec compliance | Divide by $V_{ex}$, multiply by 1000 |
| 8 | Apply temperature compensation check | Verify opposite-arm pairing |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bonded Strain Gauge ŌĆö Layered Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Host Specimen Surface] --> B[Adhesive Shear Layer]
    B --> C[Base Substrate Polyimide Film]
    C --> D[Sensing Grid Serpentine Foil Constantan Karma]
    D --> E[Cover Protective Overlay]
    E --> F[Lead Wires Solder Tabs to Bridge]
    D --> G[Mechanical Strain epsilon]
    G --> H[Geometric Distortion]
    H --> I[Resistance Change delta R]
    I --> J[Wheatstone Bridge Excitation]
    J --> K[Output Voltage mV]
```

## 4.2 Wheatstone Bridge ŌĆö Full-Bridge Load Cell Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Vex[Excitation Voltage Vex] --> N1((Node A Top))
    Vex --> N4((Node C Bottom))
    N1 --> R1[R1 Active Gauge in Tension]
    N1 --> R2[R2 Active Gauge in Compression]
    N4 --> R3[R3 Active Gauge in Compression]
    N4 --> R4[R4 Active Gauge in Tension]
    R1 --> N2((Node B Output Plus))
    R2 --> N2
    R3 --> N3((Node D Output Minus))
    R4 --> N3
    R2 --> R3
    N2 --> Amp[Differential Instrumentation Amplifier]
    N3 --> Amp
    Amp --> Out[Output Vo in mV V]
    Out --> SigCond[Signal Conditioner and ADC]
    SigCond --> Display[Digital Display in kg or N]
```

## 4.3 Load Cell Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Input[Applied Mechanical Force F in N or kg] --> Mech[Elastic Element Beam Ring Column]
    Mech --> Strain[Surface Microstrain epsilon on Element]
    Strain --> G1[Gauge 1 Top of Beam Tension]
    Strain --> G2[Gauge 2 Bottom of Beam Compression]
    Strain --> G3[Gauge 3 Bottom of Beam Compression]
    Strain --> G4[Gauge 4 Top of Beam Tension]
    G1 --> Bridge[Full Wheatstone Bridge]
    G2 --> Bridge
    G3 --> Bridge
    G4 --> Bridge
    Bridge --> Amp[Low Noise Instrumentation Amplifier]
    Amp --> Filter[EMI and RFI Filter]
    Filter --> Comp[Linearization and Temperature Compensation]
    Comp --> Cal[Calibration Span and Zero Adjust]
    Cal --> Output[Standardized Output 4 to 20 mA or 0 to 10 V or Digital]
```

## 4.4 Strain Gauge Grid Layout (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Pad Left] --- L1[Longitudinal Loop 1]
    L1 --- T1[Transverse Turn 1]
    T1 --- L2[Longitudinal Loop 2]
    L2 --- T2[Transverse Turn 2]
    T2 --- L3[Longitudinal Loop 3]
    L3 --- T3[Transverse Turn 3]
    T3 --- L4[Longitudinal Loop 4]
    L4 --- P2[Pad Right]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the schematic above, the **longitudinal loops** (L1ŌĆōL4) are aligned with the *principal strain direction* and contribute to $G_f$. The **transverse turns** (T1ŌĆōT3) are aligned perpendicular to strain and contribute to the **transverse sensitivity** $K_t$, which is deliberately minimized in good gauge design (typically $K_t < 1\%$).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2023]

**Define Gauge Factor. Derive the expression for the gauge factor of a metallic strain gauge in terms of Poisson's ratio.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Gauge Factor ($G_f$)** is defined as the ratio of the fractional change in electrical resistance to the mechanical strain that produced it:

$$
G_f = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}
$$

The analytical form, derived in Section 3.1, is:

$$
G_f = 1 + 2\nu + C
$$

where $\nu$ is **Poisson's ratio** and $C$ is **Bridgman's constant** (piezoresistive coefficient). For typical metallic alloys (Constantan), $\nu \approx 0.3$ and $C \approx 1$, giving $G_f \approx 2.0$.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition (1), formula (1), numerical (1).

---

### **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022]

**List any three advantages of a full-bridge configuration over a quarter-bridge configuration in strain measurement.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Higher Sensitivity:** Full bridge gives 4├Ś the output voltage of a quarter bridge for the same strain (multiplier = 1 vs. 1/4). **(1 mark)**
2. **Built-in Temperature Compensation:** Opposite arms experience the same temperature but opposite strain signs, so thermal drift cancels out automatically. **(1 mark)**
3. **Non-linearity Reduction:** Cancellation of even-order non-linearities (e.g., the $\varepsilon^2$ term) improves linearity to better than 0.05%. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A.** [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]

#### **(a)** Explain the construction and working principle of a bonded strain gauge with a neat sketch. Discuss the role of the Wheatstone bridge in converting the resistance change to a measurable voltage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3 marks):** A bonded strain gauge consists of:
- A **sensing grid** of fine metallic wire or etched foil (Constantan / Karma), arranged in a serpentine pattern for high active length in a small area.
- A **base (carrier)** of thin polyimide or epoxy-glass, providing handling strength.
- A **cover** layer for protection against humidity, abrasion, and chemicals.
- **Solder tabs / lead wires** for electrical connections.
- An **adhesive layer** (cyanoacrylate or epoxy) bonding the entire assembly to the test specimen.

**Working Principle (2 marks):** When the specimen is subjected to mechanical load, the resulting surface strain is transferred via the adhesive to the grid. The grid's longitudinal wires stretch (or compress), changing both length and cross-sectional area (Poisson effect) and also slightly altering resistivity (piezoresistive effect). All three effects combined produce a fractional resistance change $\Delta R / R = G_f \cdot \varepsilon$, which is proportional to the applied strain.

**Role of the Wheatstone Bridge (2 marks):** The $\Delta R$ is far too small to be measured directly. The Wheatstone bridge converts $\Delta R$ into a voltage $V_o$ via an excitation $V_{ex}$. With one active arm:

$$
V_o = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon}{4}
$$

The bridge also provides automatic **temperature compensation** and **common-mode noise rejection** when multiple active gauges are used in opposite arms.

---

#### **(b)** A strain gauge with nominal resistance 350 ╬® and gauge factor 2.1 is bonded to a steel member subjected to a stress of 140 MPa. The Young's modulus of steel is 200 GPa. The gauge is connected in one active arm of a Wheatstone bridge with all other arms being 350 ╬® fixed resistors. The bridge excitation is 10 V. Calculate: **(i)** the strain on the gauge, **(ii)** the change in resistance, and **(iii)** the bridge output voltage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $R = 350 \, \Omega$
- $G_f = 2.1$
- $\sigma = 140 \, \text{MPa} = 140 \times 10^6 \, \text{Pa}$
- $E = 200 \, \text{GPa} = 200 \times 10^9 \, \text{Pa}$
- $V_{ex} = 10 \, \text{V}$
- Quarter-bridge (1 active gauge)

**(i) Strain (2 marks):**

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{140 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9}
$$

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon = 7 \times 10^{-4} = 700 \, \mu\varepsilon}
$$

**(ii) Change in resistance (2 marks):**

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_f \cdot \varepsilon = 2.1 \times 7 \times 10^{-4} = 1.47 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta R = 1.47 \times 10^{-3} \times 350 = 0.5145 \, \Omega}
$$

**(iii) Bridge output voltage (3 marks):** For a quarter-bridge (1 active arm):

$$
V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}
$$

$$
V_o = \frac{10}{4} \times 1.47 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_o = 3.675 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{V} = 3.675 \, \text{mV}}
$$

**In mV/V units (sensitivity rating):**

$$
\frac{V_o}{V_{ex}} = \frac{3.675 \, \text{mV}}{10 \, \text{V}} = 0.3675 \, \text{mV/V}
$$

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice).** [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]

#### **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **load cell** based on bonded strain gauges. Why are four gauges preferred over a single gauge? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** [Refer to Section 4.3 ŌĆö Load Cell Functional Block Architecture]

**Construction (2 marks):** A load cell consists of:
- An **elastic element** (proving ring, cantilever beam, shear web, or column) precision-machined from alloy steel or aluminum.
- **Four bonded strain gauges** glued to the elastic element ŌĆö two aligned along the principal strain direction (tension) and two perpendicular (compression), wired in a **full Wheatstone bridge**.
- A **housing** providing mechanical protection and environmental sealing.
- A **cable entry / connector** for excitation and signal output.

**Working (2 marks):** When a load $F$ is applied, the elastic element deforms predictably (linear elastic regime). The top surface of a bending element is in tension; the bottom in compression. The four gauges produce additive $\Delta R$ changes (two tension gauges increase in $R$, two compression gauges decrease in $R$), resulting in a maximum bridge output $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$.

**Why Four Gauges? (1 mark):**
- **4├Ś higher output voltage** than a single gauge.
- **Automatic temperature and common-mode noise compensation** since all four gauges are on the same element.
- **Improved linearity** due to cancellation of even-order harmonics.
- **Higher reliability** ŌĆö failure of one gauge does not cause catastrophic signal loss.

---

#### **(b)** A load cell uses a full bridge with four identical gauges of resistance 350 ╬® and gauge factor 2.0. The cell is mounted on a steel cantilever beam of length 200 mm, width 25 mm, and thickness 5 mm. A force of 500 N is applied at the free end. Young's modulus = 200 GPa. Bridge excitation = 10 V. Determine the bridge output voltage in mV. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $R = 350 \, \Omega$, $G_f = 2.0$
- $L = 200 \, \text{mm} = 0.2 \, \text{m}$
- $b = 25 \, \text{mm} = 0.025 \, \text{m}$
- $h = 5 \, \text{mm} = 0.005 \, \text{m}$
- $F = 500 \, \text{N}$
- $E = 200 \times 10^9 \, \text{Pa}$
- $V_{ex} = 10 \, \text{V}$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the bending strain** at the fixed end of the cantilever (2 marks):

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{6 \, F \, L}{E \, b \, h^2}
$$

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{6 \times 500 \times 0.2}{200 \times 10^9 \times 0.025 \times (0.005)^2}
$$

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{600}{200 \times 10^9 \times 0.025 \times 25 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{600}{200 \times 10^9 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{600}{125} = 4.8 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon = 4.8 \times 10^{-3} = 4800 \, \mu\varepsilon}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $\Delta R / R$** (1 mark):

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_f \cdot \varepsilon = 2.0 \times 4.8 \times 10^{-3} = 9.6 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute bridge output for a full bridge** (2 marks):

$$
V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = 10 \times 9.6 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
V_o = 9.6 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{V} = 96 \, \text{mV}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Express as sensitivity rating in mV/V** (2 marks):

$$
\boxed{\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{V_o}{V_{ex}} = \frac{96 \, \text{mV}}{10 \, \text{V}} = 9.6 \, \text{mV/V}}
$$

**Verification of elastic regime:** Maximum stress at the fixed end:

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{6 F L}{b h^2} = \frac{6 \times 500 \times 0.2}{0.025 \times 25 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{600}{6.25 \times 10^{-7}} = 9.6 \times 10^{8} \, \text{Pa} = 960 \, \text{MPa}
$$

This is **below the yield strength of structural steel (~250 MPa for mild steel, ~1500 MPa for high-tensile alloys)**, so the assumption of linear elasticity holds. (However, for **mild steel**, the stress exceeds yield ŌĆö students should be aware of this caveat in real-world load cell design.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Bonded Strain Gauge / Load Cell Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the bridge multiplier** (1/4, 1/2, or 1): Many students write $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$ for *all* bridge types, losing 2ŌĆō3 marks. **Always** identify whether it is a quarter, half, or full bridge.
> 2. **Mixing up strain units:** $\varepsilon$ is dimensionless but often expressed in $\mu\varepsilon$ (microstrain). Always **convert** to absolute strain ($1 \, \mu\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$) before substituting into the formulas.
> 3. **Unit errors in Young's modulus:** $E$ is usually given in **GPa** but formulas use **Pa**. Convert $200 \, \text{GPa} \to 200 \times 10^9 \, \text{Pa}$.
> 4. **Sign of $\Delta R$:** Temperature compensation arms in a half-bridge with *opposite* arms cancel the strain signal ŌĆö students often miscompute the output as nonzero.
> 5. **Beam dimension confusion:** In the bending-strain formula $\varepsilon = 6FL/(Ebh^2)$, the dimension $h$ is **thickness** (perpendicular to bending plane), and $b$ is **width** (parallel to neutral axis). Confusing them yields a $4\times$ error.
> 6. **Neglecting Poisson's contribution:** Writing $G_f = 1 + C$ instead of $1 + 2\nu + C$ is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 7. **Forgetting to convert N to kg** when the question specifies "weight" instead of "force" ŌĆö mass $m = F/g$ where $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] A **bonded strain gauge** measures strain by transducing geometric + piezoresistive resistance changes in a grid bonded to the test surface.
- [x] **Gauge Factor** is the **single most important parameter** ŌĆö it amplifies strain into a measurable $\Delta R / R$.
- [x] **Gauge Factor formula:** $G_f = 1 + 2\nu + C$ (geometric + piezoresistive contributions).
- [x] **Typical $G_f$ values:** Constantan Ōēł 2.0, Karma Ōēł 2.4, Silicon (semiconductor) Ōēł 100ŌĆō200.
- [x] **Wheatstone bridge** converts $\Delta R$ to voltage, with multipliers of **1/4, 1/2, or 1** for quarter, half, or full bridge.
- [x] **Full-bridge load cells** provide maximum sensitivity (4├Ś) and built-in temperature compensation.
- [x] **Bridge output for full bridge:** $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$.
- [x] **Bending strain on cantilever:** $\varepsilon = 6FL / (Ebh^2)$ ŌĆö sensitivity $\propto L$, $\propto 1/h^2$.
- [x] **Opposite-arm half-bridge** cancels strain signal but compensates for temperature.
- [x] **Standard load cell output rating:** 1, 2, or 3 mV/V ŌĆö sensitivity must be checked against excitation voltage.
- [x] **Base resistance values:** 120 ╬®, 350 ╬®, 1000 ╬® ŌĆö 350 ╬® is industry standard.
- [x] **Excitation voltage:** Typically **10 V DC** (5 V, 10 V, 15 V common; 10 V is most universal).
- [x] **Strain limit for linear operation:** $\varepsilon < 1000 \, \mu\varepsilon$ for quarter-bridge without correction.
- [x] **Hooke's law** is always assumed valid: $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ ŌĆö verify that applied stress is well below the material's yield strength.
- [x] **Transverse sensitivity** $K_t < 1\%$ for high-quality gauges ŌĆö but it is *non-zero* and contributes to measurement error in biaxial stress states.
- [x] **Adhesive selection** is critical: cyanoacrylate (short-term), epoxy (long-term), phenolic (high-temperature).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** **"G = 1 + 2╬Į + C"** ŌåÆ "**1** (length term) **+ 2╬Į** (area / Poisson term) **+ C** (Constantan-like piezoresistive term)." Recite this *exactly* in derivations and you will not lose the formula mark.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inductive Transducers - Differential Output Transducers - LVDT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT413 - PROCESS INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Inductive Transducers - Differential Output Transducers - LVDT # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Inductive Transducers ŌĆö Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Inductive Transducer**: A passive transducer that converts a physical measurand (displacement, force, pressure, etc.) into a measurable change in inductance (L), mutual inductance (M), or reluctance (Ōä£) of an electrical circuit. The output is typically a change in voltage, current, impedance, or frequency. These transducers operate on the fundamental electromagnetic principle that **inductance depends on the number of turns (N), magnetic permeability (╬╝), and the geometry of the magnetic circuit (length *l*, cross-sectional area *A*)**: $$L = \frac{N^2}{\Re} = \frac{N^2 \mu A}{l}$$ The measurand alters one or more of these parameters, producing a proportional change in *L* which can be measured using AC bridge circuits or oscillators. ## 1.2 Differential Output Transducers ŌĆö Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Differential Output Transducer**: A transducer whose output is derived from the **algebraic difference** of two similar but opposing signals generated by two active sensing elements. This architecture inherently cancels non-common-mode disturbances (temperature drift, supply variations, common-mode noise), yielding superior linearity, sensitivity, and temperature stability. Common differential configurations: 1. **Differential Inductive** ŌĆö Two coils whose inductances change in opposite directions. 2. **Differential Capacitive** ŌĆö Two capacitors acting oppositely. 3. **Differential Reluctive** ŌĆö Two arms of a magnetic bridge. ## 1.3 LVDT ŌĆö Linear Variable Differential Transformer > [!NOTE] > **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)**: A passive, electromechanical, **inductive differential-output displacement transducer** that converts linear displacement of a movable soft-iron core into a corresponding differential AC voltage. It contains one primary winding and two identical secondary windings connected in **series opposition** (anti-series), wound on a cylindrical former. The word *differential* in LVDT explicitly denotes the two-opposing-secondary construction, and *transformer* reflects the mutual-inductance coupling between primary and secondary windings. ## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö "The Seesaw of Coils" > [!TIP] > **Real-world intuition (the Seesaw Analogy):** > Imagine a **seesaw (balance plank)**. A neutral position exists at the center where the plank is perfectly horizontal. Push the seesaw to the **left** ŌĆö the left side goes down; to the **right** ŌĆö right side goes down. > > In an LVDT, the **soft-iron core is the seesaw's pivot point**, and the **two secondary windings are the two arms of the plank**. The AC excitation on the primary is like someone pushing the seesaw rhythmically. The output voltage is the *difference* between the two arms. When the core is centered ŌåÆ both arms are equal ŌåÆ output is **zero**. Move the core left or right ŌåÆ one arm gets more magnetic flux ŌåÆ a net **differential voltage** appears whose **magnitude** is proportional to displacement and whose **phase** indicates direction. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why "Differential"?** The two secondaries are wound in opposite phase and connected in series opposition. So the output is: > $$V_{out} = V_{s1} - V_{s2}$$ > This subtraction ŌĆö performed physically by the winding connection ŌĆö is what makes the LVDT immune to common-mode disturbances. ## 1.5 Standard Metrics (Highlighted in Bold) - **Operating frequency:** Typically **1 kHz to 10 kHz** (carrier frequency for primary excitation). - **Operating range:** **┬▒0.1 mm to ┬▒250 mm** (commercial models). - **Linearity:** Better than **0.1% of full scale** in high-grade LVDTs. - **Resolution:** Practically **infinite** (limited by detector noise; commonly **< 0.1 ╬╝m**). - **Sensitivity:** Typically **0.5 V/mm to 2.4 V/mm** depending on excitation. - **Repeatability:** Better than **0.01% of full scale**. - **Life expectancy:** **> 10ŌüĖ cycles** (no contact between core and windings). --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Construction of an LVDT ŌĆö Structural Breakdown An LVDT comprises three mechanical parts wound on a hollow cylindrical former: | Component | Description | Function | |---|---|---| | **Primary Winding (P)** | Wound centrally on the former with $N_p$ turns | Energized by an **AC excitation voltage** $V_p$ (typically sinusoidal, 1ŌĆō10 kHz) | | **Secondary Winding SŌéü** | Wound on the **left** side of the primary with $N_{s1}$ turns | Generates induced EMF $E_{s1}$ | | **Secondary Winding SŌéé** | Wound on the **right** side of the primary with $N_{s2}$ turns | Generates induced EMF $E_{s2}$ | | **Movable Core** | Soft-iron/ferromagnetic cylindrical slug attached to the measurand shaft | Varies the mutual coupling between P and each S | | **Former (Bobbin)** | Hollow non-magnetic cylinder (e.g., ceramic, phenolic) | Mechanical support for windings | | **Shield** | Outer metal casing (mu-metal) | Electromagnetic shielding against external fields | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Crucial construction rule:** $N_{s1} = N_{s2}$ (identical secondaries) and they are **series-opposition connected** (i.e., the dotted ends are tied together; the un-dotted ends form the output terminals). This makes $E_{s1}$ and $E_{s2}$ of opposite phase in the output loop. ## 2.2 Operating Principle ŌĆö Structured Logic **Step 1 ŌĆö Excitation** - A stable **AC reference voltage** $V_p$ of frequency $f$ is applied to the primary winding. **Step 2 ŌĆö Flux Generation** - $V_p$ drives a primary current $I_p$, which produces an alternating magnetic flux $\Phi_p$ in the core region. **Step 3 ŌĆö Mutual Induction** - The flux links with the two secondaries. By **Faraday's Law**, EMFs are induced: $$e = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Core Position Effect (The "How")** - The ferromagnetic core **channels flux** preferentially toward the secondary closer to it. - If the core is **at null (center)** ŌåÆ flux coupling to SŌéü and SŌéé is **equal** ŌåÆ induced EMFs are **equal in magnitude and opposite in phase** (due to series opposition) ŌåÆ **net output Ōēł 0**. - If the core **shifts left by displacement x** ŌåÆ more flux links SŌéü ŌåÆ $E_{s1} > E_{s2}$ ŌåÆ **net output $E_{out} = E_{s1} - E_{s2}$ is non-zero**, with a phase **in phase with $V_p$**. - If the core **shifts right by displacement x** ŌåÆ more flux links SŌéé ŌåÆ $E_{s2} > E_{s1}$ ŌåÆ **net output is non-zero**, with a phase **180┬░ out of phase with $V_p$**. **Step 5 ŌĆö Demodulation** - The AC differential output is rectified and filtered to obtain a DC output proportional to displacement. The **phase difference** with respect to the primary indicates **direction** of displacement. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Inductance of coil | $L = \dfrac{N^2 \mu A}{l}$ | H (Henry) | | 2 | Mutual Inductance | $M = k \sqrt{L_1 L_2}$ | H; $k$ = coupling coefficient | | 3 | Induced EMF in secondary | $E_s = -M \dfrac{dI_p}{dt} = j \omega M I_p$ | Volts (phasor form) | | 4 | Output of LVDT (general) | $V_{out} = V_{s1} - V_{s2}$ | Volts | | 5 | LVDT output at displacement x | $V_{out} = K_s \cdot x$ | V; $K_s$ = sensitivity (V/mm) | | 6 | LVDT output (phasor) | $\dot{V}_{out} = j\omega (M_1 - M_2) \dot{I}_p$ | V | | 7 | Null Voltage (residual) | $V_{null} \approx$ (1 to 3) % of full-scale | Due to imperfect symmetry | | 8 | Sensitivity | $K_s = \dfrac{V_{out}}{x}$ | V/mm or V/cm | | 9 | % Linearity | $\%\text{Linearity} = \dfrac{\text{Max deviation}}{\text{Full scale}} \times 100$ | < 0.5% typical | | 10 | Output phase (sign of x) | $x>0 \Rightarrow \phi=0┬░;\ x<0 \Rightarrow \phi=180┬░$ | Phase-sensitive detection | > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip (KTU):** The "sensitivity" $K_s$ and the **phase of $V_{out}$** are the two pillars of any LVDT problem. Always quote both: *magnitude* tells you *how much*, *phase* tells you *which way*. ## 2.4 Why "Differential"? ŌĆö Engineering Utility The differential topology offers three engineering wins: 1. **Linearity** ŌĆö The difference $M_1 - M_2$ is a **linear function of core displacement** over the central ┬▒5 mm range, even though each individual $M_1(x)$ and $M_2(x)$ is non-linear. 2. **Temperature Stability** ŌĆö Both secondaries experience identical thermal expansion, drift of winding resistance, and aging ŌåÆ these effects **cancel** in the difference. 3. **Common-Mode Rejection** ŌĆö Stray 50/60 Hz pickup and supply variations affect both secondaries equally ŌåÆ they cancel. 4. **Null Indication** ŌĆö Provides a precise, reproducible **zero reference** ŌĆö ideal for closed-loop servos. 5. **No-Contact Operation** ŌĆö The core is not in contact with the windings ŌåÆ no friction, no wear, theoretically infinite resolution. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Domain | |---|---| | Force-balancing pressure transducers | Aerospace, process control | | Position feedback in hydraulic/pneumatic cylinders | Industrial automation | | LVDT-based load cells | Civil/mechanical testing | | Aircraft control-surface position sensing | Aviation | | Fuel-level measurement (with float) | Automotive | | Machine tool positioning | CNC machines | | Stretch / elongation measurement in tensile testing | Materials engineering | --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of LVDT Output Voltage (Phasor Form) ### Setup Let: - $V_p$ = primary excitation voltage (sinusoidal, frequency $\omega = 2\pi f$) - $I_p$ = primary current (phasor $\dot{I}_p$) - $M_1$ = mutual inductance between primary and secondary SŌéü - $M_2$ = mutual inductance between primary and secondary SŌéé - $E_{s1}$, $E_{s2}$ = induced EMFs in the two secondaries ### Step 1 ŌĆö Apply AC Excitation to Primary The primary current varies sinusoidally: $$I_p(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$$ The corresponding phasor: $$\dot{I}_p = I_m \angle 0┬░$$ ### Step 2 ŌĆö Induced EMF in Each Secondary By **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, the EMF induced in any secondary is governed by the mutual inductance with the primary: $$\dot{E}_{s1} = j \omega M_1 \dot{I}_p$$ $$\dot{E}_{s2} = j \omega M_2 \dot{I}_p$$ **Why the $j\omega$?** Because differentiating $\sin(\omega t)$ gives $\omega \cos(\omega t) = \omega \sin(\omega t + 90┬░)$. In phasor form, the time-derivative operator becomes multiplication by $j\omega$. ### Step 3 ŌĆö Net Differential Output The two secondaries are connected in **series opposition**, meaning their polarities are arranged so that the induced EMFs **subtract** in the output loop. The net open-circuit output voltage is therefore: $$\dot{V}_{out} = \dot{E}_{s1} - \dot{E}_{s2}$$ Substituting the phasor expressions: $$\dot{V}_{out} = j \omega M_1 \dot{I}_p - j \omega M_2 \dot{I}_p$$ Factoring out the common terms: $$\dot{V}_{out} = j \omega (M_1 - M_2) \dot{I}_p$$ This is the **fundamental phasor equation of the LVDT**. ### Step 4 ŌĆö Displacement Dependence of Mutual Inductances Define $x$ as the displacement of the core from the **null (center) position**, with $x > 0$ meaning the core moves **toward SŌéü** and $x < 0$ meaning it moves **toward SŌéé**. For small displacements within the linear range: $$M_1(x) = M_0 + K_m \, x$$ $$M_2(x) = M_0 - K_m \, x$$ where: - $M_0$ = mutual inductance at null (geometric mean of both secondaries) - $K_m$ = a constant (H/mm) that depends on core permeability, winding geometry, and overlap length > **Logic:** As the core slides toward SŌéü, the flux coupling with SŌéü increases and with SŌéé decreases. The change in mutual inductance is **linear** with $x$ within the designed range. ### Step 5 ŌĆö Substitute into Output Equation $$M_1 - M_2 = (M_0 + K_m x) - (M_0 - K_m x) = 2 K_m x$$ Therefore: $$\dot{V}_{out} = j \omega (2 K_m x) \dot{I}_p = 2 j \omega K_m \dot{I}_p \, x$$ ### Step 6 ŌĆö Final LVDT Transfer Equation $$\boxed{\;\dot{V}_{out} = K_s \, x\;}$$ where the **sensitivity constant** is: $$K_s = 2 \omega K_m I_m \quad \text{(V/mm)}$$ > **Key takeaway:** The output is **directly proportional to displacement $x$**, with a $90┬░$ phase lead over the primary current (the $j$ factor). The **magnitude** $|V_{out}| = K_s \vert x \vert$ gives displacement size, while the **sign of $x$** is encoded in the **phase** (0┬░ if $x>0$, 180┬░ if $x<0$). ### Step 7 ŌĆö Magnitude Form $$|V_{out}| = 2 \omega K_m I_m \, |x| = K_s \, |x|$$ > [!NOTE] > **Phase-Sensitive Detection (PSD)** is required in the signal conditioner to recover the sign of $x$ and demodulate the AC output into a bipolar DC signal. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem (KTU Module 1 ŌĆö Sample):** An LVDT has a sensitivity of **0.5 V/mm** with an excitation of **5 V, 5 kHz**. The signal conditioner has a gain of 100. The core is displaced by **3 mm** to the left of null. **Find:** (a) The magnitude of the differential output voltage. (b) The DC output after the signal conditioner. (c) The phase of the output with respect to the primary. **Solution:** **(a)** $$|V_{out}| = K_s \cdot |x| = 0.5 \;\text{V/mm} \times 3 \;\text{mm} = 1.5 \;\text{V}$$ **(b)** $$V_{DC} = 1.5 \;\text{V} \times 100 = 150 \;\text{V}$$ **(c)** Core moves left ŌåÆ $x < 0$ in the standard convention ŌåÆ output is **180┬░ out of phase** with the primary excitation. > [!TIP] > **For KTU full-mark scoring:** Always state the **magnitude**, the **DC equivalent**, AND the **phase relationship** ŌĆö these three together are what examiners look for. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Advantages & Limitations | **Advantages** | **Limitations** | |---|---| | Infinite resolution (theoretically) | Requires AC excitation (not natively DC-compatible) | | Very high linearity (< 0.1%) | Sensitive to stray external magnetic fields (needs shielding) | | Frictionless, no mechanical wear | Output is temperature-sensitive if not properly compensated | | Bidirectional (sign of x via phase) | Limited stroke length for a given size | | Excellent dynamic response (high-frequency measurands possible) | Null voltage / residual offset at center reduces accuracy near zero | | Robust construction; high over-range tolerance | Demodulation electronics add cost and complexity | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 LVDT ŌĆö Physical Construction (Block-Level Functional Architecture) The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **physical layout and magnetic flux pathways** inside an LVDT, mapped as an architectural flow for clarity (since a literal cross-section is challenging in Mermaid): ```mermaid flowchart LR AC[AC Excitation Source

Explain the significance of the term "differential" in its construction.** **Model Answer:** > The **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** is a passive electromechanical transducer that converts linear displacement into a proportional differential AC voltage. It consists of one primary winding and **two identical secondary windings** wound on a cylindrical former, with a movable soft-iron core. > > The term **"differential"** signifies that the two secondaries are connected in **series opposition** so that their induced EMFs **subtract**. This differential arrangement: > 1. Provides a **null reference** at the center position (zero output), > 2. Cancels **common-mode disturbances** (temperature, supply variations), > 3. Encodes the **direction of displacement** in the **phase** of the output, > 4. Yields superior **linearity** even when individual coil characteristics are nonlinear. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023] (3 Marks) **What is a differential output transducer? Give two examples.** **Model Answer:** > A **differential output transducer** produces an output proportional to the **algebraic difference of two signals** generated by two active sensing elements subjected to the same measurand. This design inherently rejects common-mode errors, improves linearity, and provides a stable null reference. > > **Examples:** > 1. **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** ŌĆö two secondaries, series-opposed. > 2. **Differential Capacitive Pressure Sensor** ŌĆö two capacitors in a half-bridge. > 3. **Strain-gauge Wheatstone bridge** (one active, one dummy arm) is a *quasi-differential* form. --- ## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Module Internal Choice (Answer ANY ONE full question: 1 ├Ś 14 = 14 Marks) --- ### ­¤öĘ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024] **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working principle** of an LVDT. Show that the output voltage is **directly proportional to core displacement** in the linear region. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** An LVDT is excited by a **6 V, 4 kHz** sinusoidal source. The sensitivity is **0.4 V/mm**. The core is displaced by **+4 mm** from null. Determine: 1. The RMS value of the differential output voltage. 2. The phase of the output with respect to the primary. 3. The DC output after a demodulator with a gain of 50. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(a) **Construction:** An LVDT consists of a hollow cylindrical former on which **one primary winding (P)** is wound centrally and **two identical secondary windings (SŌéü and SŌéé)** are wound on either side of the primary. A soft-iron core, attached to the measurand shaft, slides inside the former. The whole assembly is enclosed in a mu-metal shield. **Working Principle:** - An **AC excitation** $V_p$ is applied to the primary. - This produces an alternating primary current $I_p$ and an alternating magnetic flux $\Phi_p$. - The flux links with both secondaries and induces EMFs $E_{s1}$ and $E_{s2}$. - The two secondaries are connected in **series opposition** so the net output is: $$V_{out} = E_{s1} - E_{s2}$$ - When the core is at the **null (center)** position, $E_{s1} = E_{s2}$ and $V_{out} = 0$. - When the core moves toward SŌéü, $E_{s1} > E_{s2}$ ŌåÆ $V_{out} \neq 0$ (in phase with $V_p$). - When the core moves toward SŌéé, $E_{s2} > E_{s1}$ ŌåÆ $V_{out} \neq 0$ (180┬░ out of phase with $V_p$). **Proof of Linear Output:** Let $M_1 = M_0 + K_m x$ and $M_2 = M_0 - K_m x$. Then: $$V_{out} = j\omega(M_1 - M_2) I_p = j\omega (2K_m x) I_p = 2 \omega K_m I_p \cdot x$$ This is of the form $V_{out} = K_s \cdot x$, confirming direct proportionality between output and core displacement. ŌłÄ --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(b) **Given:** - $V_p = 6\;\text{V (RMS)},\ f = 4\;\text{kHz}$ - $K_s = 0.4\;\text{V/mm}$ - $x = +4\;\text{mm}$ (core displaced toward SŌéü) - Demodulator gain $G = 50$ **(1) RMS differential output voltage:** $$|V_{out, RMS}| = K_s \cdot |x| = 0.4 \times 4 = 1.6\;\text{V (RMS)}$$ **(2) Phase of output:** - $x > 0$ ŌåÆ core moves toward SŌéü ŌåÆ $V_{out}$ is **in phase with $V_p$** (phase difference = 0┬░). **(3) DC output after demodulator:** $$V_{DC} = G \times |V_{out, RMS}| = 50 \times 1.6 = 80\;\text{V (DC)}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake (KTU Examiner's Pitfall):** Many students report the DC output as a negative value when $x < 0$. **DC output is always reported as a magnitude (positive value); the *sign* of $x$ is conveyed by the *phase relationship* of the AC signal before demodulation.** Marking loses ~1 mark for sign confusion. --- ### ­¤öĘ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023] **(a)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of LVDTs. Explain why they are classified as **differential output transducers**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** An LVDT is used to measure the displacement of a pressure-sensitive diaphragm. The full-scale displacement range is **┬▒5 mm** and the LVDT sensitivity is **0.6 V/mm** with a **5 V, 5 kHz** excitation. A pressure of **1.5 MPa** causes the diaphragm to deflect **+3.5 mm**. Calculate: 1. The peak differential output voltage. 2. The output voltage if the displacement is in the **opposite direction**. 3. The relationship between **LVDT output and applied pressure** if diaphragm deflection is **linear with pressure**. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(a) **Advantages:** - **Theoretically infinite resolution** (no friction, no contact). - **Excellent linearity** (< 0.1% in the linear range). - **High sensitivity** and **low hysteresis**. - **Bidirectional measurement** with phase-sensitive detection. - **Rugged construction** with long life (> 10ŌüĖ cycles). - **Insensitive to temperature variations** in the differential mode. **Limitations:** - Requires **AC excitation**; cannot operate directly with DC. - Sensitive to **stray magnetic fields** ŌåÆ needs shielding. - **Limited stroke length** for a given physical size. - The signal needs **demodulation and amplification** ŌåÆ added cost. - Has a small **residual (null) voltage** at the center, which must be nulled out. **Why "Differential Output"?** - Two secondaries connected in **series opposition** ŌåÆ output is the **difference** of two EMFs. - This makes the device a **differential output transducer** because: 1. The output is **zero at null**, providing a stable reference. 2. **Common-mode noise** (e.g., temperature drift, supply variation) affects both secondaries equally and is **rejected**. 3. The **phase of the output** identifies the **direction** of displacement. 4. The **linear range is extended** because the non-linearities of the two halves cancel in the difference. --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(b) **Given:** - Full-scale displacement: $\pm 5\;\text{mm}$ - Sensitivity: $K_s = 0.6\;\text{V/mm}$ - Excitation: $V_p = 5\;\text{V (RMS)},\ f = 5\;\text{kHz}$ - At $P = 1.5\;\text{MPa}$, displacement $x = +3.5\;\text{mm}$ **(1) Peak differential output voltage (note: $V_{out,peak} = \sqrt{2} \times V_{out,RMS}$):** $$V_{out, RMS} = K_s \times |x| = 0.6 \times 3.5 = 2.1\;\text{V (RMS)}$$ $$V_{out, peak} = 2.1 \times \sqrt{2} \approx 2.97\;\text{V}$$ **(2) Output for opposite-direction displacement:** - If $x = -3.5\;\text{mm}$, the **magnitude** is the same: $|V_{out, RMS}| = 2.1\;\text{V}$. - The **phase** flips by **180┬░** with respect to the primary. **(3) Relationship between output and pressure:** - Given linear diaphragm: $x \propto P \Rightarrow x = k_p P$ where $k_p$ is a constant (mm/MPa). - Substituting into the LVDT equation: $$V_{out} = K_s \cdot x = K_s \cdot k_p \cdot P$$ - Define a **composite sensitivity** $K_{sp} = K_s \cdot k_p$ (V/MPa): $$\boxed{V_{out} = K_{sp} \cdot P}$$ - The LVDT output is therefore **directly proportional to applied pressure**. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall (KTU):** Students often forget the $\sqrt{2}$ conversion when the question asks for **peak** voltage and the sensitivity is given in **RMS** terms. Always verify whether the quoted sensitivity is RMS or peak. **~2 marks lost** for unit confusion. --- ## 5.3 General KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Points in LVDT Problems (Module 1):** > 1. **Forgetting to state the phase relationship** ŌĆö the LVDT output is an AC signal; examiners expect both magnitude AND phase (or direction). > 2. **Confusing RMS and peak** values ŌĆö read the question carefully. > 3. **Drawing an LVDT without labeling the series-opposition connection** ŌĆö at least 1 mark reserved for the sketch. > 4. **Failing to explain "differential"** ŌĆö it is a direct syllabus keyword; a 1-line answer loses 1ŌĆō2 marks. > 5. **Skipping the null-voltage concept** ŌĆö examiners often ask "what is the output at null?" (Answer: ideally zero; practically a small residual). > 6. **Mixing up displacement direction sign** ŌĆö core moves left vs right is the most common confusion; remember: *phase flips at null*. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read this twice before the exam):** > > ŌśÉ LVDT = **L**inear **V**ariable **D**ifferential **T**ransformer. > > ŌśÉ **Construction:** 1 primary + 2 identical secondaries in **series opposition** + movable soft-iron core. > > ŌśÉ **Working:** Displacement ŌåÆ change in mutual coupling ŌåÆ differential AC output. > > ŌśÉ **Core at null (center):** $V_{out} = 0$ (ideal); in practice, a small **null voltage (1ŌĆō3%)** exists. > > ŌśÉ **Core displaced by x:** $V_{out} = K_s \cdot x$, linear in the central region. > > ŌśÉ **Direction of displacement** is conveyed by the **phase** of the output (0┬░ for $x>0$, 180┬░ for $x<0$). > > ŌśÉ **Sensitivity** $K_s$ is in **V/mm** or **V/cm**; typical range **0.5 ŌĆō 2.4 V/mm**. > > ŌśÉ **Excitation frequency** is typically **1 ŌĆō 10 kHz** (AC, not DC). > > ŌśÉ **Phasor equation:** $\dot{V}_{out} = j\omega(M_1 - M_2)\dot{I}_p$. > > ŌśÉ **Advantages:** infinite resolution, high linearity, low hysteresis, no contact, bidirectional. > > ŌśÉ **Limitations:** AC excitation required, sensitive to stray fields, limited stroke, needs phase-sensitive demodulation. > > ŌśÉ **Applications:** force-balance pressure sensors, position feedback, load cells, machine tool positioning, aircraft control surfaces. > > ŌśÉ **Why "Differential"?** Cancels common-mode errors, gives null reference, encodes direction via phase, extends linear range. > > ŌśÉ **Always quote BOTH magnitude AND phase** of LVDT output in KTU answers. --- **End of Notes ŌĆö KTU Module 1: Inductive Transducers ŌĆö Differential Output Transducers ŌĆö LVDT** *ŌĆö Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 (NEP 2020 / 2024 Scheme)*

topics.title: Measurement of Temperature - Bimetallic and Pressure Thermometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT413 - PROCESS INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Temperature - Bimetallic and Pressure Thermometers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bimetallic Thermometer ŌĆö Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)**
> A **Bimetallic Thermometer** is a mechanical temperature-sensing instrument that operates on the principle of **differential linear thermal expansion** between two dissimilar metals having widely different coefficients of thermal expansion, mechanically bonded (welded or riveted) along their entire length. When subjected to a temperature change, the composite strip bends toward the metal with the lower expansion coefficient, and this mechanical deflection is converted into a pointer movement over a calibrated scale.

The two metals are commonly designated as:
- **High-expansion (active) element** ŌĆö e.g., **Invar (╬▒ Ōēł 1ŌĆō2 ├Ś 10Ōü╗ŌüČ /┬░C)** is actually low; typically the **active** layer is **brass, copper, or Monel**.
- **Low-expansion (passive) element** ŌĆö e.g., **Invar (Fe-Ni alloy, 64% Fe + 36% Ni)** which has ╬▒ Ōēł **1.2 ├Ś 10Ōü╗ŌüČ /┬░C**.

## 1.2 Pressure Thermometer ŌĆö Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)**
> A **Pressure Thermometer** is a temperature-measuring device in which the sensing element (bulb) is filled with a fluid (gas, liquid, or saturated vapour). The temperature at the bulb causes a change in the **internal pressure** of the confined fluid, which is transmitted through a capillary tube to an **elastic pressure-sensing element** (Bourdon tube, diaphragm, or bellows). The elastic element deflects proportionally to the pressure change, and the deflection is indicated on a calibrated temperature scale.

The three principal classes are:
1. **Liquid-filled thermometers** (e.g., mercury, alcohol)
2. **Gas-filled thermometers** (e.g., nitrogen, helium) ŌĆö ideal-gas behaviour
3. **Vapour-pressure thermometers** (saturated liquid-vapour system)

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

> [!TIP]
> **Bimetallic Analogy (The Bimetallic Strip Toy)**
> Imagine two plastic strips of different brands glued together. If you soak them in hot water, one expands more than the other. Since they cannot slide (they are bonded), the whole strip **curls toward the side that expands less** ŌĆö this is exactly how a bimetallic strip works. The thermostat in your old **iron box** or **refrigerator** uses the same principle to switch off a heater when a set temperature is reached.

> [!TIP]
> **Pressure Thermometer Analogy (The Syringe Plunger)**
> Think of a sealed syringe (the **bulb**) connected by a thin tube (the **capillary**) to a small spring-loaded gauge (the **Bourdon tube**). If you heat the syringe, the trapped gas pushes the gauge harder ŌĆö the gauge needle moves. The hotter it gets, the higher the pressure, and the more the needle moves. This is the essence of a pressure thermometer.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Reference Data

| Parameter | Value / Range |
| :--- | :--- |
| Invar coefficient (╬▒_Invar) | **1.2 ├Ś 10Ōü╗ŌüČ /┬░C** |
| Brass coefficient (╬▒_Brass) | **18.7 ├Ś 10Ōü╗ŌüČ /┬░C** |
| ╬ö╬▒ (typical pair) | **~16ŌĆō18 ├Ś 10Ōü╗ŌüČ /┬░C** |
| Universal gas constant **R** | **8.314 J/(mol┬ĘK)** |
| Bimetallic range (industrial) | **ŌłÆ50 ┬░C to +500 ┬░C** |
| Pressure thermometer range | **ŌłÆ200 ┬░C to +600 ┬░C** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bimetallic Thermometer ŌĆö Working Principle (Step Logic)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the strip bend?**
> When two metals A and B with **╬▒_A > ╬▒_B** are riveted/welded along their length and heated, metal A *wants* to be longer than metal B. Because they are bonded, the strip cannot simply expand uniformly; it accommodates the length difference by **bending into an arc**, with the lower-expansion metal (B) on the **inside** of the curve.

**Operational Logic Chain:**
1. **Temperature input** ╬öT is applied uniformly along the strip.
2. The active metal tries to expand by **╬öL_A = ╬▒_A ┬Ę L ┬Ę ╬öT**.
3. The passive metal expands by **╬öL_B = ╬▒_B ┬Ę L ┬Ę ╬öT**.
4. Length mismatch = (╬▒_A ŌłÆ ╬▒_B) ┬Ę L ┬Ę ╬öT.
5. This mismatch produces a **bending moment** proportional to ╬öT.
6. Tip deflection **╬┤** is read out mechanically via a linkage ŌåÆ pointer on the dial.
7. The pointer position is mapped to a **pre-calibrated temperature scale**.

## 2.2 Forms of Bimetallic Strips

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Favourite ŌĆö Forms of Bimetallic Elements**
> 1. **Cantilever / Flat Strip** ŌĆö simplest; one end fixed, the other free. Used in ON/OFF thermostats.
> 2. **Spiral Coil** ŌĆö strip wound in a flat spiral. Compact, large deflection, used in dial thermometers.
> 3. **Helical Coil** ŌĆö strip wound helically. **Most common in industrial dial thermometers** because the pointer can rotate > 300┬░ for full scale.

## 2.3 Deflection Equation ŌĆö Quick Derivation Outline

The tip deflection of a cantilever bimetallic strip of length **L**, total thickness **t**, with modulus ratio **m = EŌéü/EŌéé** and thickness ratio **n = tŌéü/tŌéé**, when subjected to a temperature change **╬öT**, is:

$$\delta = K \cdot \frac{(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot \Delta T \cdot L^{2}}{t}$$

where **K** is a constant depending on m and n (typically 0.5ŌĆō1.0). The **angle of rotation** of the free end is:

$$\theta = K' \cdot \frac{(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot \Delta T \cdot L}{t}$$

## 2.4 Pressure Thermometer ŌĆö Detailed Working

> [!NOTE]
> **System Architecture (always remember the 3 parts):**
> **BULB** ŌåÆ **CAPILLARY** ŌåÆ **BOURDON TUBE (or bellows/diaphragm)**
> - **Bulb**: inserted in the medium whose temperature is to be measured. Large surface area, small volume, high thermal conductivity (usually stainless steel or Inconel).
> - **Capillary**: small-bore tube (typically **0.5ŌĆō1.5 mm** ID) carrying the pressure signal. Long capillaries (up to 30 m) are permissible, but introduce **ambient-temperature errors** that must be compensated.
> - **Pressure Element**: Bourdon tube (C, spiral, or helical) is standard. Its tip deflection is geared to a pointer.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö Master Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All board-exam-relevant equations for this topic:**

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Variables / Units | Applicable Class |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Bimetallic tip deflection | $\delta = K \cdot \dfrac{(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\, \Delta T\, L^{2}}{t}$ | L [m], t [m], ╬öT [K] | All bimetallic |
| 2 | Bimetallic rotation angle | $\theta = K' \cdot \dfrac{(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\, \Delta T\, L}{t}$ | ╬Ė [rad] | All bimetallic |
| 3 | Gas-filled ŌĆö Constant Volume | $P = \dfrac{m R T}{V}$ | P [Pa], V [m┬│], T [K] | Gas thermometer |
| 4 | Gas-filled ŌĆö Linearised | $P - P_{0} = \dfrac{m R}{V}\,(T - T_{0})$ | PŌéĆ, TŌéĆ : reference | Gas thermometer |
| 5 | Vapour pressure (Clausius-Clapeyron approx.) | $\ln P = -\dfrac{L_{v}}{R T} + C$ | L_v : latent heat | Vapour thermometer |
| 6 | Liquid-filled ŌĆö Pressure rise | $\Delta P = \beta \, \Delta T$ | ╬▓ : bulk modulus [Pa/K] | Liquid thermometer |
| 7 | Bourdon tube deflection | $\delta_{B} = \dfrac{P\, r^{3}}{E\, h^{3}} \cdot f(\phi)$ | r, h : tube dims | Pressure element |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bimetallic Thermometers**: Used as **ON/OFF thermostats** in domestic appliances (iron, geyser, AC, refrigerator), **industrial dial thermometers** (cheap, robust, no power needed), **thermal overload relays** in motors, and **fire-sprinkler** glass bulbs.
- **Pressure Thermometers**: Used in **chemical reactors, refineries, boilers**, and any plant requiring **remote indication** of temperature. They are **intrinsically safe** (no electrical power at the bulb) ŌĆö perfect for **explosive atmospheres (Ex zones)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I ŌĆö Bimetallic Strip Deflection (Cantilever)

> [!NOTE]
> We treat the strip as a **composite beam** with two layers of equal width b. Layer 1 (active, high ╬▒) has Young's modulus EŌéü, thickness tŌéü; Layer 2 (passive) has EŌéé, tŌéé. Total thickness t = tŌéü + tŌéé.

### Step 1: Free thermal strains

If both layers were free, the active layer would elongate more than the passive:

$$\varepsilon_{1}^{th} = \alpha_1 \Delta T \quad , \quad \varepsilon_{2}^{th} = \alpha_2 \Delta T$$

### Step 2: Compatibility ŌĆö equal strains at the bonded interface

When bonded, both layers must share the same neutral axis curvature. The mechanical strain in each layer is the difference between the total strain and the thermal strain.

$$\varepsilon_{1}^{mech} = \varepsilon - \alpha_1 \Delta T \quad , \quad \varepsilon_{2}^{mech} = \varepsilon - \alpha_2 \Delta T$$

where ╬Ą is the strain at the neutral axis.

### Step 3: Force equilibrium (axial force = 0)

The axial force in the strip is zero (no external load). The sum of stresses integrated over area must vanish.

$$\int_{A_1} \sigma_1 \, dA + \int_{A_2} \sigma_2 \, dA = 0$$

Substituting Žā = E ┬Ę ╬Ą_mech and integrating over the cross-section:

$$E_1 b t_1 (\varepsilon - \alpha_1 \Delta T) + E_2 b t_2 (\varepsilon - \alpha_2 \Delta T) = 0$$

### Step 4: Solve for ╬Ą

$$\varepsilon = \frac{E_1 t_1 \alpha_1 + E_2 t_2 \alpha_2}{E_1 t_1 + E_2 t_2}\, \Delta T$$

This is the **axial strain at the neutral surface**.

### Step 5: Bending moment about neutral axis

The moment about the neutral axis produced by the two layers is:

$$M = \int_{A_1} E_1 (\varepsilon - \alpha_1 \Delta T) \, y \, dA + \int_{A_2} E_2 (\varepsilon - \alpha_2 \Delta T) \, y \, dA$$

Evaluating with y measured from the neutral axis:

$$M = \frac{E_1 E_2 b t_1 t_2 (t_1 + t_2)\, (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\, \Delta T}{4(E_1 t_1 + E_2 t_2)}$$

### Step 6: Curvature ╬║ of the bimetallic strip

Using M = EI_eq ┬Ę ╬║, and for equal widths the equivalent flexural rigidity simplifies to:

$$\kappa = \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{M}{E I_{eq}} = \frac{6\,(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\, \Delta T\, (1+m)^{2}}{t\,[3(1+m)^{2} - (1+mn)(m^{2} + \frac{1}{mn})]}$$

where m = tŌéü/tŌéé and n = EŌéü/EŌéé.

### Step 7: Tip deflection of a cantilever

For a cantilever of length L, tip deflection is:

$$\delta = \frac{\kappa L^{2}}{2} = \frac{3\,(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\, \Delta T\, L^{2}}{t} \cdot f(m,n)$$

**Final compact form (the KTU answer-ready version):**

$$\boxed{\;\delta = K \cdot \frac{(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot \Delta T \cdot L^{2}}{t}\;}$$

For a **helical coil** of N turns with mean coil radius R, the **pointer rotation** in radians is:

$$\theta = \frac{2 \pi N L (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T}{t}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation II ŌĆö Gas-Filled Thermometer (Constant Volume)

> [!NOTE]
> A **constant-volume gas thermometer** assumes the bulb, capillary, and Bourdon chamber together hold a fixed mass m of ideal gas at constant volume V.

### Step 1: Apply the ideal gas law

$$P V = m R T$$

For a sealed system (m, V constant):

$$\frac{P}{T} = \frac{m R}{V} = \text{constant}$$

### Step 2: Reference condition

At calibration reference TŌéĆ, the pressure is PŌéĆ:

$$P_{0} V = m R T_{0}$$

### Step 3: Divide the two equations

$$\frac{P}{P_{0}} = \frac{T}{T_{0}}$$

### Step 4: Express in measured temperature

$$T = T_{0} \cdot \frac{P}{P_{0}}$$

### Step 5: Linear pressure-temperature relationship

This shows that **pressure is linearly proportional to absolute temperature** for an ideal gas:

$$P = \frac{m R}{V} T$$

### Step 6: Differential (sensitivity form)

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{m R}{V} = \frac{P_{0}}{T_{0}}$$

**Higher fill pressure and lower reference temperature ŌåÆ higher sensitivity.**

### Step 7: When ambient temperature around the capillary changes

The capillary is NOT at the measured temperature T_meas. If capillary temperature changes, the **effective gas volume V** changes. The corrected relation is:

$$T_{meas} = \frac{V_{total}}{m R} \cdot P_{act} - \frac{V_{cap}}{m R} \cdot P_{cap}$$

where V_cap is the capillary volume and P_cap is the gas pressure at capillary ambient temperature. **Inert-gas compensation lines** (a second capillary of compensating pressure) are used in industrial designs.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III ŌĆö Vapour-Pressure Thermometer

> [!NOTE]
> The bulb is partially filled with a volatile liquid + its saturated vapour. Above the liquid, the pressure depends only on the **saturation temperature** of the liquid (not on the volume of vapour).

### Step 1: Clausius-Clapeyron equation

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{L_{v}}{T \Delta v}$$

Assuming the vapour behaves as an ideal gas (╬öv Ōēł v_vap = RT/P) and L_v is constant:

$$\frac{dP}{P} = \frac{L_v}{R} \cdot \frac{dT}{T^{2}}$$

### Step 2: Integrate

$$\ln P = -\frac{L_{v}}{R T} + C$$

### Step 3: Exponentiate

$$P = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{L_{v}}{R T}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **strongly non-linear** relation. The vapour-pressure thermometer has a **non-uniform scale** ŌĆö widely spaced at low T, closely spaced near the critical point.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Property | Bimetallic | Pressure (Gas) | Pressure (Vapour) | Pressure (Liquid) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
