| Sensing principle | Differential expansion | PV = mRT (constant V) | Saturation pressure | Thermal expansion of liquid |
| Output | Mechanical (pointer) | Pressure ŌåÆ pointer | Pressure ŌåÆ pointer | Pressure ŌåÆ pointer |
| Range | ŌłÆ50 to +500 ┬░C | ŌłÆ200 to +600 ┬░C | ŌłÆ50 to +300 ┬░C | ŌłÆ100 to +500 ┬░C |
| Accuracy | ┬▒1ŌĆō2 % FS | ┬▒0.5 % FS | ┬▒1 % FS | ┬▒0.5 % FS |
| Scale linearity | Linear | **Linear (in T)** | **Non-linear** | Approximately linear |
| Remote reading | No (local only) | Yes (long capillary) | Yes | Yes |
| Power needed | None | None | None | None |
| Intrinsic safety | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Cost | Very low | High | Moderate | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bimetallic Thermometer ŌĆö Helical Coil Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Measured Medium] --> B[Helical Bimetallic Coil]
    B --> C[Rotary Shaft]
    C --> D[Pinion Gear]
    D --> E[Pointer]
    E --> F[Calibrated Temperature Dial]
    B -.->|thermal expansion| G[Tip Deflection theta]
    G --> C
    H[Fixed End Anchor] --- B
```

## 4.2 Pressure Thermometer ŌĆö System Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Sensing_Part
        A1[Process Fluid / Medium] --> B1[Bulb]
    end
    subgraph Transmission_Part
        B1 --> C1[Capillary Tube]
        C1 --> D1[Compensation Capillary optional]
    end
    subgraph Indication_Part
        C1 --> E1[Bourdon Tube]
        E1 --> F1[Linkage and Gear Train]
        F1 --> G1[Pointer]
        G1 --> H1[Calibrated Scale in degree C]
    end
    B1 -.->|Fluid pressure change| C1
    E1 -.->|Elastic deflection| F1
    D1 -.->|Ambient temp compensation| E1
```

## 4.3 Bimetallic Working Detail (Cantilever Form)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Two bonded metal strips] --> A1[Metal 1: High alpha e.g. Brass]
    A0 --> A2[Metal 2: Low alpha e.g. Invar]
    A1 --> B1[Heated: T rises by dT]
    A2 --> B2[Same temperature rise]
    B1 --> C1[Brass tries to expand more]
    B2 --> C2[Invar expands less]
    C1 --> D1[Composite bends toward Invar side]
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Tip deflection delta proportional to dT]
    E1 --> F1[Pointer rotates by angle theta]
    F1 --> G1[Reading on calibrated scale]
```

## 4.4 Comparison Flow ŌĆö Selection Logic for an Engineer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Need to measure Temperature] --> Q1{Need remote indication?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Budget tight?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q3{Process is hazardous zone?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| R1[Choose Bimetallic Dial Thermometer]
    Q2 -->|No| R2[Consider RTD or Thermocouple]
    Q3 -->|Yes| R3[Choose Vapour or Gas filled Pressure Thermometer intrinsically safe]
    Q3 -->|No| R4[Choose Liquid filled Pressure Thermometer]
    R1 --> END[End Selection]
    R2 --> END
    R3 --> END
    R4 --> END
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the ideal-gas law basis** in gas thermometers ŌĆö many students write P = kT but forget the constant V,m conditions. Always state the assumption clearly.
> 2. **Unit confusion** between ┬░C and K in gas-law equations ŌĆö gas thermometers are fundamentally **absolute** (Kelvin); report T in Kelvin.
> 3. **Conflating bimetallic vs pressure thermometers** ŌĆö bimetallics are **local** and **self-contained**; pressure thermometers have a **bulb + capillary + Bourdon** and can be **remote**.
> 4. **Vapour-pressure scale is non-linear** ŌĆö students often wrongly claim all pressure thermometers have linear scales.
> 5. **Omitting the factor (╬▒Ōéü ŌłÆ ╬▒Ōéé)** in deflection ŌĆö examiners specifically check whether you identified the *differential* expansion as the driver.

---

## Part A ŌĆö 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer / Conceptual)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]
**With a neat sketch, explain the working of a bimetallic thermometer. List any two advantages.**

**Model Answer:**
A bimetallic thermometer consists of two strips of metals having widely different coefficients of thermal expansion (e.g., brass and Invar), welded or riveted together. When the strip is heated, the metal with higher ╬▒ (brass) tries to expand more than the metal with lower ╬▒ (Invar). Since they are bonded, the composite strip **bends towards the Invar side**. This bending is converted into rotation of a pointer through a mechanical linkage, and the pointer indicates the temperature on a calibrated dial. Common industrial forms are **helical** and **spiral** coils.

**Two advantages:**
1. **No external power supply** required ŌĆö fully self-contained and intrinsically safe.
2. **Robust, cheap, and maintenance-free** ŌĆö ideal for harsh industrial environments.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for explanation + 1 for advantages)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]
**State the principle of a constant-volume gas-filled pressure thermometer. Why must the temperature be expressed in Kelvin?**

**Model Answer:**
A constant-volume gas-filled pressure thermometer works on the **principle that the pressure of a fixed mass of gas at constant volume is directly proportional to its absolute (Kelvin) temperature**, as given by the ideal gas law: **P V = m R T**, hence **P ŌłØ T** (Kelvin).

> [!NOTE]
> Temperature must be expressed in **Kelvin** because the ideal-gas law is valid only for **absolute temperature**. The zero of the Celsius scale is arbitrary (273.15 K below absolute zero), so any direct proportionality only holds on the Kelvin scale. The gas thermometer is the primary standard for temperature measurement in the range above 0 ┬░C.

*(3 marks)*

---

## Part B ŌĆö 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A ŌĆö Bimetallic Thermometer (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper, PESFT413]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the tip deflection of a cantilever-type bimetallic strip. State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bimetallic strip is made of **brass (╬▒Ōéü = 19 ├Ś 10Ōü╗ŌüČ /┬░C)** and **Invar (╬▒Ōéé = 1.2 ├Ś 10Ōü╗ŌüČ /┬░C)**, each of thickness **0.5 mm**, total length **50 mm**, and width **5 mm**. It is used as a cantilever. If the temperature rises by **100 ┬░C**, calculate the **tip deflection**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a) ŌĆö Derivation (7 marks):**

The detailed derivation is presented in **Section 3.1** above. The key steps a student must reproduce for full marks:

1. State assumptions: (i) both metals perfectly bonded, (ii) plane sections remain plane, (iii) temperature uniform along length, (iv) small deflections. **(1 mark)**
2. Write free thermal strains ╬ĄŌéü_th = ╬▒Ōéü╬öT, ╬ĄŌéé_th = ╬▒Ōéé╬öT. **(1 mark)**
3. Apply axial force equilibrium ŌåÆ derive ╬Ą at the neutral surface:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{E_1 t_1 \alpha_1 + E_2 t_2 \alpha_2}{E_1 t_1 + E_2 t_2}\, \Delta T$$

**(1.5 marks)**

4. Compute the bending moment M about the neutral axis:

$$M = \frac{E_1 E_2 b\, t_1 t_2 (t_1 + t_2)\, (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\, \Delta T}{4(E_1 t_1 + E_2 t_2)}$$

**(1.5 marks)**

5. From M = EI_eq ┬Ę ╬║ and ╬║ = 2╬┤ / L┬▓, obtain the final deflection:

$$\delta = \frac{3\,(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\, \Delta T\, L^{2}}{t} \cdot f(m,n) \approx K \cdot \frac{(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\, \Delta T\, L^{2}}{t}$$

**(2 marks)** ŌĆö full credit for final boxed equation.

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b) ŌĆö Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- ╬▒Ōéü (brass) = 19 ├Ś 10Ōü╗ŌüČ /┬░C
- ╬▒Ōéé (Invar) = 1.2 ├Ś 10Ōü╗ŌüČ /┬░C
- tŌéü = tŌéé = 0.5 mm ŌåÆ total thickness **t = 1 mm = 1 ├Ś 10Ōü╗┬│ m**
- Length L = 50 mm = 50 ├Ś 10Ōü╗┬│ m = 0.05 m
- ╬öT = 100 ┬░C = 100 K
- Assume standard K Ōēł 0.75 for equal-thickness, equal-modulus pair **(use K = 0.75 unless given otherwise).**

**Step 1:** Compute (╬▒Ōéü ŌłÆ ╬▒Ōéé):

$$\alpha_1 - \alpha_2 = (19 - 1.2) \times 10^{-6} = 17.8 \times 10^{-6}\; /^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**Step 2:** Compute L┬▓:

$$L^{2} = (0.05)^{2} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}\; \text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the deflection formula:

$$\delta = K \cdot \frac{(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\, \Delta T\, L^{2}}{t}$$

**Step 4:** Plug in numbers:

$$\delta = 0.75 \times \frac{(17.8 \times 10^{-6}) \times (100) \times (2.5 \times 10^{-3})}{1 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\delta = 0.75 \times \frac{17.8 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\delta = 0.75 \times \frac{4.45 \times 10^{-6}}{1 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\delta = 0.75 \times 4.45 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{\;\delta \approx 3.34 \times 10^{-3}\; \text{m} = 3.34\; \text{mm}\;}$$

**Result:** The free end of the bimetallic cantilever deflects by approximately **3.34 mm** for a 100 ┬░C temperature rise.

---

### QUESTION B ŌĆö Pressure Thermometer (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper, PESFT413]**

**(a)** Explain with a block diagram the construction and working of a **gas-filled pressure thermometer**. Derive the relationship between pressure and temperature. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **nitrogen-filled** constant-volume pressure thermometer has a bulb volume of **20 cm┬│** and contains **0.0005 mol** of nitrogen. The capillary and Bourdon volume is **5 cm┬│**. If the measured temperature is **300 ┬░C** and the ambient temperature around the capillary is **40 ┬░C**, calculate the **pressure indicated by the Bourdon tube**. Take R = 8.314 J/(mol┬ĘK). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a) ŌĆö Theory (7 marks):**

A gas-filled pressure thermometer (refer to **Section 4.2** for the block diagram) consists of three parts:

1. **Bulb** ŌĆö placed in the measured medium. **(0.5 mark)**
2. **Capillary tube** ŌĆö transmits pressure to the indicator. **(0.5 mark)**
3. **Bourdon tube (or bellows)** ŌĆö at the indicator end; deflects proportionally to pressure. **(0.5 mark)**

The bulb and connecting lines are filled with nitrogen (or helium). When the bulb temperature rises, gas pressure increases according to the **ideal gas law** (assuming constant V and m): **(1 mark)**

$$P V = m R T$$

**Derivation:** **(4 marks, distributed as below)**

- Reference state: $P_{0} V = m R T_{0}$ ŌĆ” (1)
- Measured state: $P V = m R T$ ŌĆ” (2)
- Divide: $P/P_{0} = T/T_{0}$
- Solve: $P = P_{0} \cdot (T/T_{0})$
- Therefore **P is linearly proportional to absolute (Kelvin) temperature** when V and m are held constant.

**Key assumption:** m and V are constant ŌĆö i.e., the system is sealed and rigid. Any change in capillary ambient temperature changes the **effective V**; compensation capillaries are used in industrial designs. **(0.5 mark for stating the assumption)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b) ŌĆö Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- V_bulb = 20 cm┬│ = 20 ├Ś 10Ōü╗ŌüČ m┬│
- n = 0.0005 mol (moles of NŌéé)
- V_cap_Bourdon = 5 cm┬│ = 5 ├Ś 10Ōü╗ŌüČ m┬│
- T_bulb = 300 ┬░C = **573.15 K**
- T_capillary = 40 ┬░C = **313.15 K**
- R = 8.314 J/(mol┬ĘK)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Conceptual Trap:** The gas in the *bulb* is at 300 ┬░C, but the gas in the *capillary + Bourdon* is at 40 ┬░C (ambient). The pressure transmitted to the Bourdon must be calculated using the gas in the **capillary/Bourdon side** (since the Bourdon is what physically experiences the pressure). Standard exam practice: assume the **bulb dominates** the volume and is at measured temperature; capillary correction is often a follow-up.

### Step 1: Use total volume (combined bulb + capillary + Bourdon)

$$V_{total} = V_{bulb} + V_{cap+Bourdon} = 20 + 5 = 25\;\text{cm}^{3} = 25 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{m}^{3}$$

### Step 2: Apply ideal gas law at the bulb temperature (assuming all gas at T_bulb)

$$P \cdot V_{total} = n R T_{bulb}$$

### Step 3: Solve for P

$$P = \frac{n R T_{bulb}}{V_{total}} = \frac{0.0005 \times 8.314 \times 573.15}{25 \times 10^{-6}}$$

### Step 4: Numerator calculation

$$\text{Numerator} = 0.0005 \times 8.314 \times 573.15$$

$$= 0.0005 \times 4764.78 \approx 2.382\;\text{J}$$

### Step 5: Final division

$$P = \frac{2.382}{25 \times 10^{-6}} = 95280\;\text{Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\;P \approx 9.53 \times 10^{4}\;\text{Pa} = 0.953\;\text{bar}\;}$$

### Step 6: (Bonus ŌĆö Capillary ambient correction)

If the examiner asks for capillary correction, treat the capillary/Bourdon gas as at T_cap:

$$P_{corrected} = \frac{n R T_{bulb}}{V_{bulb} + V_{cap} \cdot (T_{bulb}/T_{cap})}$$

$$P_{corrected} = \frac{0.0005 \times 8.314 \times 573.15}{20 \times 10^{-6} + 5 \times 10^{-6} \times (573.15/313.15)}$$

$$P_{corrected} = \frac{2.382}{20 \times 10^{-6} + 9.15 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{2.382}{29.15 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$P_{corrected} \approx 8.17 \times 10^{4}\;\text{Pa} = 0.817\;\text{bar}$$

**Final answer (no correction):** P Ōēł 0.953 bar. **With capillary correction:** P Ōēł 0.817 bar. *(3.5 marks for core calculation, 1.5 for units, 1 mark for stating the ideal-gas assumption, 1 mark for any ambient correction commentary.)*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bimetallic Thermometer ŌĆö Key Points:**
> - Two metals with **different ╬▒** (typically **brass + Invar**). **(High yield for Part A)**
> - Strip bends toward the **low-╬▒** (Invar) side on heating.
> - Tip deflection: **╬┤ = K ┬Ę (╬▒Ōéü ŌłÆ ╬▒Ōéé) ┬Ę ╬öT ┬Ę L┬▓ / t**.
> - Forms: cantilever, spiral, helical (helical = industrial standard).
> - Range: **ŌłÆ50 ┬░C to +500 ┬░C**; accuracy ┬▒1ŌĆō2 % FS.
> - Used in **thermostats, dial thermometers, overload relays**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pressure Thermometer ŌĆö Key Points:**
> - Three parts: **Bulb ŌåÆ Capillary ŌåÆ Bourdon Tube** (or bellows/diaphragm).
> - Three classes: **liquid-filled, gas-filled, vapour-pressure**.
> - Gas-filled obeys **P V = m R T** ŌåÆ **P ŌłØ T (in Kelvin)**. **Linear scale.**
> - Vapour-pressure follows **P = A ┬Ę exp(ŌłÆL_v / RT)**. **Non-linear scale.**
> - Liquid-filled uses **thermal expansion of liquid** to raise pressure; **╬öP = ╬▓ ╬öT**.
> - **Intrinsically safe** ŌĆö preferred in **hazardous/explosive** zones.
> - Range: **ŌłÆ200 ┬░C to +600 ┬░C**; accuracy ┬▒0.5ŌĆō1 % FS.
> - **Capillary ambient-temperature errors** must be compensated.

> [!TIP]
> **One-liners for the KTU Board Exam:**
> 1. "Bimetallic uses **differential expansion**; pressure uses **fluid pressure change**."
> 2. "Gas thermometer is **linear in T (Kelvin)**; vapour thermometer is **non-linear**."
> 3. "Bimetallic = local, cheap, robust; Pressure = remote, intrinsically safe, expensive."
> 4. "Always quote **(╬▒Ōéü ŌłÆ ╬▒Ōéé)** in bimetallic, not individual ╬▒'s."
> 5. "For gas thermometers, **T must be in Kelvin**; Celsius is wrong."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermocouples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT413 - PROCESS INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Thermocouples # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Thermocouple (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** > A *thermocouple* is a **passive, thermoelectric temperature transducer** consisting of two dissimilar conductors (thermoelements) joined at one end to form a **measuring junction (hot junction)** while the other ends remain at a **reference junction (cold junction)**. The temperature difference between these two junctions generates a continuous, measurable electromotive force (EMF) governed by three fundamental thermoelectric phenomena ŌĆö the **Seebeck Effect**, the **Peltier Effect**, and the **Thomson Effect**. The magnitude of the generated EMF is a deterministic, monotonic function of the temperature difference between the hot and cold junctions, allowing indirect inference of the unknown process temperature from the measured millivolt signal. > [!NOTE] > **Key Term ŌĆö Thermoelements:** The two individual metallic wires forming the thermocouple are called *thermoelements* (positive leg and negative leg). The combination of two specific thermoelements defines the *thermocouple calibration type* (e.g., Type K = Chromel / Alumel). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö The Two-Stick Fire Trick:** > Imagine holding two different metal rods (say, one iron, one copper) and twisting their free ends together. When you heat the twisted tip in a candle flame, a tiny voltage appears across the open ends ŌĆö enough to deflect a sensitive analog meter. The thermocouple works on exactly this principle. The **heat** at the measuring tip **kicks electrons** from the hotter metal into the cooler one at different rates (because every metal has a different electron "appetite"), and this imbalance manifests as a **measurable micro-voltage**. The colder the free ends stay (or the more accurately we compensate for their temperature), the more reliable the reading of the hot end. Think of the thermoelectric EMF as a *thermal pressure gauge*: heat applied at the junction acts like a pump that pushes electrical charge from one metal into the other. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Seebeck Coefficient | $S$ | $\mu\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$ (typically **5ŌĆō60** $\mu$V/┬░C) | | Reference Temperature | $T_\text{ref}$ | **0 ┬░C** (273.15 K) ŌĆö standard ice-bath reference | | Output Range | $E(T)$ | **0 ŌĆō 70 mV** for industrial Type K | | Standard Calibration | ŌĆö | **IEC 60584 / ASTM E230 / ISA MC96.1** | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Seebeck Coefficient** is the *signature fingerprint* of a thermocouple type. For Type K (Chromel-Alumel), $S \approx 41 \ \mu\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$ at room temperature ŌĆö this is why a 1000 ┬░C span produces roughly **40 mV** of signal, not a volt-level signal. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Thermoelectric Phenomena A thermocouple is governed by **three interconnected thermoelectric effects**, of which only the Seebeck Effect is directly used for temperature measurement. ### A. Seebeck Effect (Used in Measurement) - **Discovery:** Thomas Johann Seebeck (1821). - **Principle:** When two dissimilar metals form a closed loop and their junctions are held at different temperatures, a continuous EMF circulates in the loop. - **Why it happens:** Free electrons in the hotter metal gain kinetic energy and diffuse toward the colder metal at a rate that depends on the metal's electron density of states. Because the two metals have **different** electron mobility profiles, an electrical potential gradient builds up. ### B. Peltier Effect (Reverse Phenomenon) - **Discovery:** Jean Charles Athanase Peltier (1834). - **Principle:** When an *external* current is forced through the junction of two dissimilar metals, heat is either **absorbed** (cooling) or **released** (heating) at the junction ŌĆö depending on the current direction. - **Significance:** This effect is exploited in **thermoelectric coolers (TECs)** and **Peltier refrigerators** ŌĆö *not* used in measurement, but often asked in viva voce. ### C. Thomson Effect - **Discovery:** William Thomson (Lord Kelvin, 1854). - **Principle:** In a single homogeneous conductor carrying current while subjected to a temperature gradient along its length, heat is either absorbed or evolved along the wire. - **Significance:** Mathematically links Seebeck and Peltier effects via the Kelvin-Onsager relations. --- ## 2.2 Operational Logic ŌĆö Step-by-Step Working 1. **Junction Formation:** The two thermoelements are welded, brazed, or twisted at the measuring end to form the *hot (measuring) junction*. The free ends form the *cold (reference) junction*. 2. **Thermal Excitation:** The hot junction is exposed to the process whose temperature $T_\text{hot}$ is to be measured. 3. **Reference Maintenance:** The cold junction is held at a *known* reference temperature $T_\text{ref}$ (typically 0 ┬░C using an ice bath, or electronically compensated). 4. **EMF Generation:** The temperature difference $\Delta T = T_\text{hot} - T_\text{ref}$ drives free electrons across the junction, producing an EMF: $$E = \int_{T_\text{ref}}^{T_\text{hot}} \big[S_B(T) - S_A(T)\big] \, dT$$ where $S_A(T)$ and $S_B(T)$ are the temperature-dependent Seebeck coefficients of the two thermoelements. 5. **Signal Conditioning:** The millivolt-level signal is amplified, **cold-junction-compensated (CJC)**, linearized (using lookup tables or polynomial equations), and displayed in ┬░C. --- ## 2.3 The Three Fundamental Thermocouple Laws (KTU Repeatedly Tested) > [!IMPORTANT] > These three laws are a **permanent fixture** in KTU Part A and Part B question papers ŌĆö memorize them verbatim. ### Law 1 ŌĆö Law of Homogeneous Materials A thermoelectric EMF generated by a thermocouple depends **only** on the materials of the thermoelements and the temperatures of the two junctions. The EMF is **independent** of: - The length of the wires - The cross-sectional area - The temperature distribution *along the length* of each wire (provided the material is homogeneous) $$\boxed{E_{AB}(T_1, T_2) \ \text{is independent of wire geometry}}$$ ### Law 2 ŌĆö Law of Intermediate Metals If a third metal $C$ is introduced anywhere in the circuit (e.g., a copper lead wire, solder joint, or terminal block), the **net EMF remains unchanged**, *provided both junctions formed with metal C are held at the same temperature*. $$E_{ABC}(T_1, T_2) = E_{AB}(T_1, T_2) \quad \text{if } T_C = T_\text{ambient}$$ **Engineering Implication:** This is why we can use copper extension/lead wires and screw terminals without recalibration. ### Law 3 ŌĆö Law of Intermediate (Successive) Temperatures The EMF generated with junctions at $T_1$ and $T_3$ equals the **algebraic sum** of EMFs generated between $T_1 \leftrightarrow T_2$ and $T_2 \leftrightarrow T_3$. $$\boxed{E_{AB}(T_1, T_3) = E_{AB}(T_1, T_2) + E_{AB}(T_2, T_3)}$$ **Engineering Implication:** This is the *theoretical foundation* of **electronic cold-junction compensation** ŌĆö we mathematically "subtract" the cold-junction temperature from the measured EMF to obtain a 0 ┬░C-referenced reading. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below are **directly solvable** items for KTU Part B numerical questions. | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E = S \cdot \Delta T$ | Approximate EMF (linear region) | mV, $\mu$V/┬░C, ┬░C | | 2 | $E = \int_{T_\text{ref}}^{T_\text{hot}} [S_B(T) - S_A(T)]\, dT$ | Exact EMF (non-linear Seebeck) | mV | | 3 | $E_{AB}(T_1, T_3) = E_{AB}(T_1, T_2) + E_{AB}(T_2, T_3)$ | Law of Intermediate Temperatures | mV | | 4 | $T_\text{hot} = T_\text{ref} + \dfrac{E_\text{meas}}{S_{AB}}$ | Inverse temperature from EMF (linear approx.) | ┬░C | | 5 | $E_\text{corr} = E_\text{meas} + E(T_\text{ref}, 0\,^\circ\text{C})$ | Cold-junction-corrected EMF | mV | | 6 | $\eta_\text{thermal} = \dfrac{T_\text{hot} - T_\text{ref}}{E}$ | Sensitivity indicator | ┬░C/mV | | 7 | $E_\text{Peltier} = \Pi_{AB} \cdot I$ | Peltier heat rate | W, V, A | | 8 | $Q = \tau \cdot I \cdot \Delta T$ | Thomson heat | W | --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Petrochemical plants:** Type K sheathed probes monitor reactor skin temperatures up to **1260 ┬░C**. - **Gas turbines:** Bare-bead Type R / S thermocouples measure exhaust gas temperatures exceeding **1500 ┬░C**. - **Cryogenics:** Type T (Copper-Constantan) measures down to **ŌłÆ250 ┬░C** in LNG and liquid nitrogen systems. - **HVAC & Building Automation:** Low-cost Type K probes for duct and AHU temperature monitoring. - **Automotive:** Type K exhaust sensors for emissions ECU feedback. - **Industrial Furnaces & Kilns:** Platinel II and Type B (Pt-Rh) thermocouples in high-temperature ceramic firing. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Seebeck EMF Integral ### Starting Premise Consider two dissimilar metals A and B joined at the measuring junction held at $T_\text{hot}$, with the reference junction at $T_\text{ref}$. ### Step 1 ŌĆö Define the Differential EMF For an *infinitesimal* temperature differential $dT$ across a differential element of wire, the differential EMF generated is: $$dE = S(T) \, dT$$ where $S(T)$ is the absolute Seebeck coefficient of the material at temperature $T$. ### Step 2 ŌĆö Express the *Relative* Seebeck Coefficient Because the circuit contains **two** different metals A and B, the *effective* (relative) Seebeck coefficient of the thermocouple pair is: $$S_{AB}(T) = S_B(T) - S_A(T)$$ The relative coefficient is the difference between the individual absolute coefficients of the two legs. ### Step 3 ŌĆö Form the Integral Integrating from the reference temperature to the hot junction temperature: $$\boxed{E_{AB}(T_\text{hot}, T_\text{ref}) = \int_{T_\text{ref}}^{T_\text{hot}} \big[ S_B(T) - S_A(T) \big] \, dT}$$ ### Step 4 ŌĆö Practical Polynomial Form In real calibration tables (NIST ITS-90), the inverse function is provided as a *polynomial*: $$T = a_0 + a_1 E + a_2 E^2 + a_3 E^3 + \cdots + a_n E^n$$ where the coefficients $a_i$ are different for different thermocouple types and temperature sub-ranges. This is why thermocouple instrumentation uses a **lookup table** or polynomial engine, **not** a simple linear formula. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example ŌĆö Cold Junction Compensation > [!NOTE] > **Exam Pattern (Frequently Repeated):** > *"The hot junction of a Type K thermocouple is at 800 ┬░C. The reference (cold) junction is at 30 ┬░C. The measured EMF is 33.27 mV. Compute the cold-junction-compensated EMF referenced to 0 ┬░C."* **Given:** - Measured EMF: $E_\text{meas} = 33.27$ mV (Type K, $T_\text{hot} = 800$ ┬░C, $T_\text{ref} = 30$ ┬░C) - From Type K tables: - $E(800\,^\circ\text{C},\ 0\,^\circ\text{C}) = 33.277$ mV - $E(30\,^\circ\text{C},\ 0\,^\circ\text{C}) = 1.203$ mV **Step 1 ŌĆö Apply Law of Intermediate Temperatures:** $$E_{AB}(800, 0) = E_{AB}(800, 30) + E_{AB}(30, 0)$$ Rearranging for the unknown EMF corrected to 0 ┬░C: $$E_{AB}(800, 30) = E_{AB}(800, 0) - E_{AB}(30, 0)$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute the Table Values:** $$\boxed{E_{AB}(800, 30) = 33.277 \text{ mV} - 1.203 \text{ mV} = 32.074 \text{ mV}}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Verification:** The corrected EMF (32.074 mV) corresponds to a true temperature of approximately **770 ┬░C** when referenced to a 0 ┬░C cold junction ŌĆö confirming that without CJC, a 30 ┬░C ambient would have caused an **error of 30 ┬░C**. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Standard Thermocouple Types (KTU Must-Know) | Type | Positive Leg | Negative Leg | Range (┬░C) | Seebeck Coeff. (╬╝V/┬░C @ 25 ┬░C) | Atmosphere Suitability | Typical Application | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **K** | Chromel (Ni-Cr) | Alumel (Ni-Al) | ŌłÆ200 to **1260** | 40.6 | Oxidizing, inert | **General purpose, most widely used** | | **J** | Iron (Fe) | Constantan (Cu-Ni) | ŌłÆ40 to **760** | 51.5 | Reducing, vacuum | Older industrial furnaces | | **T** | Copper (Cu) | Constantan (Cu-Ni) | ŌłÆ250 to **370** | 40.6 | Mild oxidizing, moist | **Cryogenics, food industry** | | **E** | Chromel (Ni-Cr) | Constantan (Cu-Ni) | ŌłÆ200 to **870** | 60.9 | Oxidizing | High sensitivity measurement | | **R** | Pt-13%Rh | Platinum (Pt) | ŌłÆ50 to **1480** | 6.0 | Oxidizing, inert | Laboratory, high-temp standards | | **S** | Pt-10%Rh | Platinum (Pt) | ŌłÆ50 to **1480** | 6.0 | Oxidizing, inert | **Industry standard for high temp** | | **B** | Pt-30%Rh | Pt-6%Rh | 0 to **1700** | ~5.0 | Oxidizing, inert | **Furnace, glass, steel industries** | | **N** | Nicrosil (Ni-Cr-Si) | Nisil (Ni-Si-Mg) | ŌłÆ200 to **1260** | 38.0 | Oxidizing | Improved Type K (green-rot resistant) | > [!WARNING] > **Do not memorize all EMF values.** In the exam, EMF values for Type K/J/T are usually provided in the question stem or a table excerpt. Focus your preparation on the **application**, **atmosphere**, and **range** columns. --- ## 3.4 Numerical ŌĆö Sensitivity Calculation **Given:** A Type E thermocouple produces $E = 60.9$ mV across a temperature span of **1000 ┬░C**. **Find:** Sensitivity in ╬╝V/┬░C. **Step 1 ŌĆö Convert Units:** $$S = \dfrac{E}{\Delta T} = \dfrac{60.9 \text{ mV}}{1000\,^\circ\text{C}}$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Convert mV to ╬╝V:** $$S = \dfrac{60{,}900\ \mu\text{V}}{1000\,^\circ\text{C}}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Final Sensitivity:** $$\boxed{S = 60.9\ \mu\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}}$$ Type E has the **highest** Seebeck coefficient among standard thermocouples ŌĆö making it the choice for measuring *small temperature differences* in laboratory applications. --- ## 3.5 Numerical ŌĆö Using Intermediate Temperature Law **Given:** - $E_{AB}(150\,^\circ\text{C}, 30\,^\circ\text{C}) = 4.91$ mV - $E_{AB}(30\,^\circ\text{C}, 0\,^\circ\text{C}) = 1.20$ mV **Find:** $E_{AB}(150\,^\circ\text{C}, 0\,^\circ\text{C})$ **Step 1 ŌĆö Apply Law:** $$E_{AB}(150, 0) = E_{AB}(150, 30) + E_{AB}(30, 0)$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute:** $$\boxed{E_{AB}(150, 0) = 4.91 + 1.20 = 6.11 \text{ mV}}$$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Physical Schematic ŌĆö Thermocouple Construction ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef hot fill:#ff6347,stroke:#8b0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef cold fill:#4682b4,stroke:#00008b,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef meter fill:#ffd700,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef wireA fill:#d3d3d3,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000000 classDef wireB fill:#a9a9a9,stroke:#2f4f4f,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#ffffff nodeH((HOT JUNCTION
Measuring Point
T_hot)):::hot nodeC((COLD JUNCTION
Reference End
T_ref)):::cold nodeM[/" mV METER "
"or Cold Junction
Compensator"/]:::meter wireA1[ " " ]:::wireA wireB1[ " " ]:::wireB wireA2[ " " ]:::wireA wireB2[ " " ]:::wireB nodeH --- wireA1 --- nodeM nodeH --- wireB1 --- nodeM nodeM --- wireA2 --- nodeC nodeM --- wireB2 --- nodeC ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** > - The **red HOT JUNCTION** is exposed to the process whose temperature we measure. > - The **blue COLD JUNCTION** is held at a known reference temperature (ice bath or electronic reference). > - The **gold METER** block represents the signal-conditioning electronics that measure the millivolt EMF. > - The **grey wires** are the two dissimilar thermoelements (positive and negative leg). --- ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Thermocouple Signal Chain ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef process fill:#ff6347,stroke:#8b0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef sensor fill:#ffa500,stroke:#ff8c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef amp fill:#ffd700,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef cjc fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef lin fill:#87cefa,stroke:#00008b,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef disp fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#800080,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff proc((PROCESS
T_hot unknown)):::process probe[ "TC PROBE
Hot + Cold Junction
Seebeck EMF" ]:::sensor lead[ "Extension / Lead Wires
Law of Intermediate Metals" ]:::sensor amp[ "Instrumentation Amplifier
Gain = 100 to 1000
mV ŌåÆ V" ]:::amp cjc[ "Cold Junction Compensator
Ice-Bath / Electronic
Adds E T_ref 0C" ]:::cjc lin[ "Linearizer
Polynomial or LUT
E ŌåÆ T Conversion" ]:::lin disp[ "Display / Transmitter
4 to 20 mA HART
or Digital Readout" ]:::disp proc --> probe probe --> lead lead --> amp amp --> cjc cjc --> lin lin --> disp ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the Functional Block Diagram:** > 1. **TC PROBE:** Generates the raw Seebeck EMF (mV-level). > 2. **Extension Wires:** Carry the EMF from the field to the control room. Because both junctions with the lead-wire metal are at the same temperature (Law 2), no error is introduced. > 3. **Instrumentation Amplifier:** Boosts the tiny mV signal to a usable volt-level. > 4. **CJC Block:** Uses a separate temperature sensor (typically a precision silicon diode or platinum RTD) to measure the terminal-block temperature and mathematically add the corresponding EMF (Law 3). > 5. **Linearizer:** Converts the non-linear EMF back to a temperature reading using NIST polynomial coefficients. > 6. **Display / Transmitter:** Outputs the result as an analog 4ŌĆō20 mA signal, HART, or Foundation Fieldbus. --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart ŌĆö Three Thermoelectric Effects Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef root fill:#ff4500,stroke:#8b0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff classDef branch fill:#4682b4,stroke:#00008b,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef leaf fill:#98fb98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000000 rootA((THERMOELECTRIC
EFFECTS)):::root branchB[ "1. SEEBECK EFFECT
Heat to Electricity
Used in Measurement" ]:::branch branchC[ "2. PELTIER EFFECT
Electricity to Heat
Used in Cooling TEC" ]:::branch branchD[ "3. THOMSON EFFECT
Heat and Current
in Single Conductor" ]:::branch leafE[ "Thomas Johann Seebeck
1821" ]:::leaf leafF[ "Jean Charles Peltier
1834" ]:::leaf leafG[ "Lord Kelvin
1854" ]:::leaf rootA --> branchB rootA --> branchC rootA --> branchD branchB --- leafE branchC --- leafF branchD --- leafG ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]** *State and explain the **Law of Intermediate Metals** as applied to thermocouples. Why is it significant in industrial temperature measurement?* **Model Answer:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Statement:** When a third metal is introduced into a thermocouple circuit, the net thermoelectric EMF remains **unchanged**, provided both junctions formed with the third metal are maintained at the **same temperature**. > > **Mathematical Form:** $E_{ABC}(T_1, T_2) = E_{AB}(T_1, T_2)$, subject to $T_{C1} = T_{C2}$. > > **Engineering Significance:** > - Enables use of **copper extension/lead wires** to bring the millivolt signal from the field probe to the control-room instrument. > - Permits **soldering**, **crimping**, and use of **terminal blocks** in the circuit without recalibration. > - Justifies the use of **bimetallic terminal strips** inside thermocouple heads. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]** *Define the **Seebeck Effect**. What is the role of the **Seebeck Coefficient** in characterizing a thermocouple?* **Model Answer:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Seebeck Effect:** When two dissimilar metals form a closed circuit and their two junctions are maintained at different temperatures, a continuous electromotive force is generated that drives a current around the loop. The magnitude of this EMF depends only on the materials and the temperature difference. > > **Seebeck Coefficient ($S$):** It is the EMF generated per unit temperature difference (╬╝V/┬░C) for a given thermocouple pair. It is a **fingerprint** of the thermoelectric properties ŌĆö Type E has the highest sensitivity ($\sim 60.9\ \mu\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$), while noble-metal types (R/S/B) have lower sensitivity ($\sim 6\ \mu\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$). The coefficient is mildly **non-linear** with temperature, which is why calibration tables and polynomial linearization are required. --- ## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > As per the **KTU 2024 End Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern, Module 1 carries 14 marks. Students must answer *one* of two internal choices. Below are two **complete** 14-mark questions with detailed solutions. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **A (a)** Explain the construction and working of a thermocouple with a neat block diagram. Discuss the **Seebeck Effect** and the **Peltier Effect**. (7 Marks) **A (b)** A Type K thermocouple is used to measure the temperature of a furnace. The hot junction is at **900 ┬░C** and the cold junction is at **50 ┬░C**. The measured EMF is **36.13 mV**. Using the standard Type K calibration table values: - $E(900\,^\circ\text{C},\ 0\,^\circ\text{C}) = 37.325$ mV - $E(50\,^\circ\text{C},\ 0\,^\circ\text{C}) = 2.023$ mV Calculate the **true furnace temperature** corrected for cold-junction error. Also compute the **percentage error** introduced if cold-junction compensation is *not* applied. (7 Marks) --- ### Solution to Question A **A (a) Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Construction:** A thermocouple consists of two dissimilar metal wires (thermoelements) welded together at one end (hot junction) and connected to the measuring instrument at the other end (cold junction). The hot junction is housed inside a protective **sheath** (stainless steel or Inconel) filled with **mineral oxide insulation** (MgO) for vibration and thermal-shock resistance. > [!NOTE] > **Working:** The temperature difference between the hot and cold junctions generates a small DC millivolt EMF via the Seebeck Effect. This EMF is amplified and conditioned by the transmitter to provide a temperature reading. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Seebeck Effect (1821):** A temperature gradient across a junction of two dissimilar conductors produces a measurable EMF proportional to the temperature difference. > - *Equation:* $E = S \cdot \Delta T$ > - *Application:* Primary sensing principle in all thermocouples. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Peltier Effect (1834):** When an external current flows through a junction of two dissimilar metals, heat is either absorbed or released at the junction depending on current direction. > - *Equation:* $Q = \Pi_{AB} \cdot I$ > - *Application:* Thermoelectric coolers (PELTIER modules) used in portable refrigerators, CPU coolers, and dew-point hygrometers ŌĆö *not* used in temperature measurement. **A (b) Model Solution:** **Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the cold-junction-compensated EMF using Law of Intermediate Temperatures:** $$E(900, 50) = E(900, 0) - E(50, 0)$$ Substitute the table values: $$E(900, 50) = 37.325 - 2.023 = 35.302 \text{ mV}$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Verify with the Measured EMF:** The measured value of **36.13 mV** is slightly higher than the calculated 35.302 mV, indicating a possible measurement instrument drift or interpolation inaccuracy from the calibration table. **Step 3 ŌĆö Use Linear Approximation to Estimate the True Temperature:** For Type K, $S \approx 41\ \mu\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$ in the linear region. $$T_\text{hot} \approx 50 + \dfrac{E_\text{meas}}{S}$$ $$T_\text{hot} \approx 50 + \dfrac{36.13 \times 1000}{41} \approx 50 + 881 = 931\,^\circ\text{C}$$ For a more accurate result, we use the corrected EMF: $$T_\text{hot} \approx 50 + \dfrac{35.302 \times 1000}{41} \approx 50 + 861 = 911\,^\circ\text{C}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the Percentage Error Without CJC:** $$\text{Error} = \dfrac{T_\text{meas(no CJC)} - T_\text{true}}{T_\text{true}} \times 100\%$$ Without CJC, the displayed value would simply be the **cold-junction-shifted temperature**: $$T_\text{no CJC} = T_\text{hot} - T_\text{cold} \approx 900 - 50 = 850\,^\circ\text{C} \quad \text{(simplified)}$$ $$\text{Error} = \dfrac{850 - 900}{900} \times 100\% = -5.56\%$$ In a real table-based comparison: $$\text{Error} = \dfrac{T(E_\text{meas at 0┬░C reference}) - T_\text{true}}{T_\text{true}} \times 100\%$$ $$\boxed{\text{Error} \approx -5.56\% \text{ (indicating an under-read)}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall ŌĆö Examiner's Trap:** > Students often *forget* to apply the Law of Intermediate Temperatures and simply use the measured EMF to look up the temperature directly from the table. This implicitly **assumes** the cold junction is at 0 ┬░C, causing an error of exactly $T_\text{ref}$ degrees. Always state the assumption: *"Assuming the cold junction is referenced to 0 ┬░C"* or *"Applying CJC for a 50 ┬░C reference."* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Model Paper]** **B (a)** State and prove the **Law of Intermediate Temperatures**. Show, with an example, how it enables **electronic cold-junction compensation** in modern thermocouple transmitters. (7 Marks) **B (b)** Compare the following thermocouple types in tabular form: **Type K, Type J, Type T, Type R, and Type B**. Mention the **composition of thermoelements**, **temperature range**, **atmosphere suitability**, and **one typical industrial application** for each. (7 Marks) --- ### Solution to Question B **B (a) Model Answer:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Statement of the Law:** The thermoelectric EMF generated by a thermocouple with its junctions at temperatures $T_1$ and $T_3$ is equal to the **algebraic sum** of the EMFs that would be generated between $T_1 \leftrightarrow T_2$ and $T_2 \leftrightarrow T_3$, where $T_2$ is any intermediate temperature. > > $$\boxed{E_{AB}(T_1, T_3) = E_{AB}(T_1, T_2) + E_{AB}(T_2, T_3)}$$ **Proof:** Consider the EMF integral: $$E_{AB}(T_1, T_3) = \int_{T_1}^{T_3} S_{AB}(T)\, dT$$ Split the integral at $T_2$: $$\int_{T_1}^{T_3} S_{AB}(T)\, dT = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} S_{AB}(T)\, dT + \int_{T_2}^{T_3} S_{AB}(T)\, dT$$ By definition of the integral form: $$E_{AB}(T_1, T_3) = E_{AB}(T_1, T_2) + E_{AB}(T_2, T_3)$$ Hence proved. ŌłÄ > [!NOTE] > **Application to Cold-Junction Compensation:** > In a modern smart transmitter, the terminal block where the thermocouple wires connect to the instrument is at ambient temperature $T_\text{amb}$ (e.g., 30 ┬░C). The transmitter contains a precision platinum RTD that measures $T_\text{amb}$. The microcontroller then: > 1. Reads the measured EMF: $E_\text{meas} = E_{AB}(T_\text{hot}, T_\text{amb})$. > 2. Reads the calibration-table value for the ambient temperature: $E_\text{cold} = E_{AB}(T_\text{amb}, 0\,^\circ\text{C})$. > 3. Adds them: $E_\text{corr} = E_\text{meas} + E_\text{cold} = E_{AB}(T_\text{hot}, 0\,^\circ\text{C})$. > 4. Looks up $T_\text{hot}$ from the calibration table corresponding to $E_\text{corr}$. > > **Example:** If measured EMF = 33.27 mV (Type K) at $T_\text{amb} = 30$ ┬░C, then $E_\text{cold} = 1.203$ mV. So $E_\text{corr} = 33.27 + 1.203 = 34.473$ mV, which corresponds to $T_\text{hot} \approx 830\,^\circ\text{C}$ (looked up from the Type K table). **B (b) Model Answer ŌĆö Comparison Table:** | Type | Positive Leg | Negative Leg | Range (┬░C) | Atmosphere | Typical Application | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **K** | Chromel (Ni-Cr) | Alumel (Ni-Al) | ŌłÆ200 to 1260 | Oxidizing, inert | **General-purpose** industrial process | | **J** | Iron (Fe) | Constantan (Cu-Ni) | ŌłÆ40 to 760 | Reducing, vacuum | Older furnace monitoring | | **T** | Copper (Cu) | Constantan (Cu-Ni) | ŌłÆ250 to 370 | Mild oxidizing, moist | **Cryogenics**, food, dairy | | **R** | Pt-13% Rh | Pure Platinum | ŌłÆ50 to 1480 | Oxidizing, inert | Laboratory standards, steel | | **B** | Pt-30% Rh | Pt-6% Rh | 0 to 1700 | Oxidizing, inert | **Glass & steel furnaces** | > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall ŌĆö Examiner's Trap:** > 1. Students often confuse **Type R and Type S** ŌĆö both are Pt-Rh based with similar ranges. The difference is in the Rhodium percentage (**10% for S, 13% for R**). Type S has slightly lower EMF at the same temperature. > 2. **Type J** cannot be used above **760 ┬░C** because the iron leg oxidizes rapidly above this temperature. > 3. **Type B** has the unique property of generating **negligible EMF below 50 ┬░C**, eliminating the need for cold-junction compensation in moderate-ambient applications. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks on Thermocouple Questions:** > 1. **Confusing the Hot and Cold Junctions:** Remember ŌĆö the **hot junction** is at the *measuring* end (process side), the **cold junction** is at the *reference/terminal* end. > 2. **Forgetting Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC):** A measured EMF is *not* directly the 0 ┬░C-referenced EMF unless explicitly stated. Always apply the Law of Intermediate Temperatures when the cold junction is non-zero. > 3. **Mixing Up Thermocouple Types:** Type K is the most general; Type T is for cryogenics; Type R/S/B are for high-temperature precious-metal applications. Mismatching the type with the application loses the "atmosphere suitability" marks. > 4. **Linear Assumption Error:** Using $T = E/S$ across wide temperature spans introduces **non-linearity error** because $S$ varies with $T$. For exams, use table values when provided; otherwise state the linear approximation explicitly. > 5. **Omitting Units:** Always include **mV** for EMF and **┬░C** (or K) for temperature. A numerically correct answer without units often receives **partial credit only**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö Module 1: Thermocouples** - [x] **Definition:** A passive, thermoelectric sensor using the Seebeck Effect between two dissimilar metals to convert a temperature difference into a millivolt EMF. - [x] **Three Thermoelectric Effects:** **Seebeck** (T ŌåÆ E, used in measurement), **Peltier** (I ŌåÆ Q, used in coolers), **Thomson** (Q in single conductor with I and ╬öT). - [x] **Reference Temperature:** Standard is **0 ┬░C** (ice-bath); in modern instruments, CJC is performed electronically using a platinum RTD. - [x] **Three Laws (MEMORIZE):** - **Homogeneous Materials:** EMF independent of wire geometry. - **Intermediate Metals:** Third metal at same temperature does not change EMF. - **Intermediate Temperatures:** EMF is additive across successive temperatures. - [x] **Key Formula:** $E = \int_{T_\text{ref}}^{T_\text{hot}} [S_B(T) - S_A(T)]\, dT$ - [x] **Most Common Thermocouple:** **Type K (Chromel-Alumel)**, range ŌłÆ200 to 1260 ┬░C. - [x] **Highest Sensitivity:** **Type E**, $S \approx 60.9\ \mu\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$. - [x] **Cryogenic Champion:** **Type T**, usable down to **ŌłÆ250 ┬░C**. - [x] **High-Temperature Champion:** **Type B (Pt-Rh)**, up to **1700 ┬░C**. - [x] **Construction:** Hot junction (welded) + sheath + MgO insulation + extension wires + cold junction at terminals. - [x] **Advantages:** Wide range, rugged, fast response, inexpensive, self-powered (no excitation needed). - [x] **Disadvantages:** Low sensitivity (mV-level signal), non-linearity requires linearization, cold-junction compensation mandatory, susceptible to **thermocouple aging** and **drift** at high temperatures. - [x] **Error Sources:** Lead-wire resistance, cold-junction drift, calibration drift, **green-rot** in Type K, contamination at high temperature, reference junction instability.

topics.title: Resistance Thermometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT413 - PROCESS INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Resistance Thermometers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Resistance Thermometer** (also called a **Resistance Temperature Detector** or **RTD**) is a precision temperature sensor that exploits the predictable, monotonic variation of the **electrical resistance** of a pure metal with temperature. It is a **passive transducer** that converts thermal energy (temperature) into an electrical quantity (resistance) using a metallic sensing element whose resistance varies as a deterministic function of temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PESFT413):** "A resistance thermometer is a temperature-measuring device that operates on the principle that the electrical resistance of a metal wire changes with temperature in a known, repeatable manner, and this resistance is measured using a Wheatstone bridge or similar circuit to infer the temperature."

The most widely used industrial RTD is the **PT100**, a platinum (Pt) element with a nominal resistance of **RŌéĆ = 100 ╬® at 0 ┬░C**, standardized under **IEC 60751** (also adopted by ASTM E1137). Other standardized versions include **PT500** and **PT1000** for higher sensitivity and lower lead-resistance error in 2-wire configurations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine water flowing through a pipe. When the pipe is smooth and warm, water rushes through easily (low resistance). When the pipe gets very cold, the metal contracts, atoms vibrate less, and the "pipe for electrons" becomes slightly narrower and bumpier ŌĆö electrons collide more often with the lattice, so resistance **increases**. As temperature rises, atoms vibrate more violently (lattice scattering increases), again increasing resistance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö The Crowded Hallway:**
> Think of a narrow corridor filled with people (atoms). When everyone is calm (cold), they walk in an orderly line and exit quickly (low resistance). When a fire alarm goes off (hot), everyone panics, jostles, and pushes ŌĆö exit becomes slow and chaotic (high resistance). An RTD measures this "crowd chaos" electrically to determine temperature.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **RŌéĆ (PT100)** | **100 ╬® at 0 ┬░C** | Reference resistance (ice point) |
| **╬▒ (Temperature Coefficient)** | **0.00385 ╬®/╬®/┬░C** (European/IEC) or **0.00392** (American) | Average slope of R-vs-T curve |
| **Range (Industrial)** | **ŌłÆ200 ┬░C to +850 ┬░C** (Pt) | Operational span of platinum RTDs |
| **Nominal Current** | **1 mA to 5 mA** | To limit self-heating |
| **Standard** | **IEC 60751 / ASTM E1137** | Manufacturing tolerance classes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** In every KTU question on RTDs, always specify whether the platinum follows the **European curve (╬▒ = 0.00385)** or the **American curve (╬▒ = 0.00392)**. Marks are frequently lost when students assume a default value without stating it.

### 1.4 Advantages & Limitations (Syllabus Snapshot)

**Advantages over Thermocouples:**
- Higher accuracy and repeatability (┬▒0.1 ┬░C or better)
- Excellent long-term stability
- Linear (or near-linear) output over wide range
- No cold-junction compensation required

**Limitations:**
- Require external excitation (current source)
- Self-heating error
- Lead-wire resistance error (mitigated by 3-wire / 4-wire methods)
- Higher cost than thermocouples for equivalent durability
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle ŌĆö Step-by-Step

1. **Excitation:** A precision constant current (typically 1 mA) is passed through the RTD element.
2. **Resistance Change:** As temperature changes, the metal's resistivity changes due to **lattice vibration (phonon scattering)** and **impurity/defect scattering** (Matthiessen's rule).
3. **Voltage Measurement:** The voltage drop across the RTD is measured: $V = I \cdot R(T)$.
4. **Signal Conditioning:** The voltage is amplified, linearized (RTD output is mildly non-linear), and digitized.
5. **Conversion:** A lookup table or the **CallendarŌĆōVan Dusen equation** converts $R(T)$ back to temperature $T$.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why a constant current?** Because RTDs are resistive, an unknown current would produce an ambiguous $V = IR$ reading. A known, stable $I$ lets us solve for $R$ directly.
- **Why platinum?** Platinum is chemically inert, has a high melting point (1768 ┬░C), exhibits a near-linear $R$ŌĆō$T$ relationship, and is reproducible ŌĆö meaning two PT100s made anywhere in the world give nearly identical readings at the same temperature.
- **Why not copper or nickel?** Copper oxidizes; nickel has a non-linear curve and lower repeatability. They are used in cost-sensitive, narrow-range applications.

### 2.3 Matthiessen's Rule (Underlying Physics)

The total resistivity of a metal at temperature $T$ is the sum of a temperature-independent term (impurity/defect scattering) and a temperature-dependent term (phonon scattering):

$$\rho(T) = \rho_{residual} + \rho_{phonon}(T)$$

For pure metals in the temperature range of interest, the phonon contribution is approximately linear, giving:

$$\rho(T) \approx \rho_0 \left[1 + \alpha (T - T_0)\right]$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention:** All pipes `\vert` are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve the markdown table. Use these formulas in your answer sheet directly.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Linear Approximation** | $R(T) = R_0 \left[1 + \alpha(T - T_0)\right]$ | $R$ in ╬®, $T$ in ┬░C | Narrow range (~0ŌĆō100 ┬░C) |
| 2 | **CallendarŌĆōVan Dusen (T Ōēź 0 ┬░C)** | $R(T) = R_0 \left[1 + A T + B T^2\right]$ | $A, B$ in ┬░CŌü╗┬╣, ┬░CŌü╗┬▓ | 0 ┬░C to 850 ┬░C |
| 3 | **CallendarŌĆōVan Dusen (T < 0 ┬░C)** | $R(T) = R_0 \left[1 + A T + B T^2 + C (T - 100) T^3\right]$ | $A, B, C$ constants | ŌłÆ200 ┬░C to 0 ┬░C |
| 4 | **Sensitivity** | $S = \dfrac{dR}{dT} = R_0 \alpha$ | ╬®/┬░C | Linear region |
| 5 | **Self-Heating Error** | $\Delta T_{sh} = \dfrac{I^2 R}{S_h}$ | $S_h$ = self-heat coeff. (mW/┬░C) | All RTDs |
| 6 | **Lead-Wire (2-wire)** | $R_{measured} = R_{RTD} + 2 R_{lead}$ | $R_{lead}$ per wire | Error-causing |
| 7 | **Wheatstone Bridge Balance** | $\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_{RTD}}$ | Dimensionless | Bridge output = 0 |
| 8 | **PT100 Constant (IEC)** | $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}$ ┬░CŌü╗┬╣ | Constant | 0ŌĆō850 ┬░C |
| 9 | **PT100 Constant (IEC)** | $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}$ ┬░CŌü╗┬▓ | Constant | 0ŌĆō850 ┬░C |
| 10 | **PT100 Constant (IEC)** | $C = -4.183 \times 10^{-12}$ ┬░CŌü╗Ōü┤ | Constant (T<0 only) | ŌłÆ200 ┬░C to 0 ┬░C |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Industries:** Reactor vessel temperature monitoring in chemical plants (HŌéé, NHŌéā, hydrocarbon reformers).
- **HVAC & Cold Chain:** Pharmaceutical cold-storage warehouses and food transport (Class A PT100 with ┬▒0.15 ┬░C tolerance at 0 ┬░C).
- **Power Generation:** Stator winding temperature in large alternators (embedded PT100 sensors).
- **Aerospace & Cryogenics:** LNG (Liquefied Natural Gas) storage tanks using Class AA PT100s operating at ŌłÆ196 ┬░C.
- **Calibration Labs:** National metrology institutes use **SPRT (Standard Platinum Resistance Thermometer)** as a primary standard from ŌłÆ189 ┬░C to 660 ┬░C.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Linear Approximation

We start from the experimental observation that, over a moderate temperature range, the resistance of a metal is approximately linear with temperature. Define $R_0$ as the resistance at reference temperature $T_0$ (usually 0 ┬░C).

**Step 1:** Assume a linear relationship of the form:

$$R(T) = R_0 \left[1 + \alpha(T - T_0)\right]$$

**Step 2:** Verify by checking two known calibration points. For a PT100 at $T = 0$ ┬░C, $R = 100$ ╬®. Substituting $T = T_0 = 0$:

$$R(0) = 100 \left[1 + \alpha(0 - 0)\right] = 100 \text{ ╬®} \checkmark$$

**Step 3:** At $T = 100$ ┬░C, $R \approx 138.5$ ╬® for European curve. Substituting:

$$138.5 = 100 \left[1 + 100\alpha\right] \implies \alpha = \dfrac{138.5 - 100}{100 \times 100} = 3.85 \times 10^{-3} \text{ ┬░C}^{-1}$$

This matches the IEC standard value. The **sensitivity** is:

$$S = \dfrac{dR}{dT} = R_0 \alpha = 100 \times 0.00385 = 0.385 \text{ ╬®/┬░C}$$

So **every 1 ┬░C change produces a 0.385 ╬® change** in a PT100 ŌĆö a critical KTU calculation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the CallendarŌĆōVan Dusen Equation

The linear approximation fails at extreme temperatures because the $R$ŌĆō$T$ curve has a slight downward concavity (curvature). The CallendarŌĆōVan Dusen equation is a polynomial fit to the empirical platinum data.

**Step 1:** Assume a quadratic polynomial:

$$R(T) = R_0 \left[1 + A T + B T^2\right]$$

**Step 2:** Apply calibration at three points: $T = 0$, $T = 100$ ┬░C, and $T = 444.6$ ┬░C (the zinc freezing point). At $T = 0$, the equation trivially gives $R(0) = R_0$.

**Step 3:** At $T = 100$ ┬░C, the measured resistance is $R_{100} = 138.5$ ╬® (IEC). Substituting:

$$138.5 = 100 \left[1 + 100A + (100)^2 B\right]$$

$$0.385 = 100A + 10{,}000B \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 4:** At $T = 444.6$ ┬░C (zinc point), $R_{Zn} \approx 248.0$ ╬®. Substituting:

$$248.0 = 100 \left[1 + 444.6A + (444.6)^2 B\right]$$

$$1.480 = 444.6A + 197{,}669.16 B \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 5:** Solve (i) and (ii) simultaneously. From (i): $100A = 0.385 - 10{,}000B$, so $A = (0.385 - 10{,}000B)/100$.

Substituting into (ii):

$$1.480 = 444.6 \cdot \dfrac{0.385 - 10{,}000B}{100} + 197{,}669.16 B$$

$$1.480 = 1.7117 - 44.46 \cdot 1000 B \cdot 10^{-3} \cdot \text{(rearranging)} + 197{,}669.16 B$$

Solving carefully:

$$1.480 - 1.7117 = -4446 B + 197{,}669.16 B$$

$$-0.2317 = 193{,}223.16 B$$

$$B = -1.199 \times 10^{-6} \text{ ┬░C}^{-2}$$

This yields, after refining with high-precision platinum data, the **IEC 60751 standard constants**:

$$A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} \text{ ┬░C}^{-1}$$

$$B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7} \text{ ┬░C}^{-2}$$

**Step 6:** For $T < 0$ ┬░C, a fourth-order term is needed to capture the curvature inversion at sub-zero temperatures:

$$R(T) = R_0 \left[1 + A T + B T^2 + C (T - 100) T^3\right]$$

with $C = -4.183 \times 10^{-12} \text{ ┬░C}^{-4}$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example (KTU Problem-Style)

> **Problem:** A PT100 RTD is used to measure the temperature of a chemical reactor. The measured resistance is $R = 137.85$ ╬®. Using the linear approximation, determine the temperature.

**Solution:**

Using $R(T) = R_0 [1 + \alpha(T - T_0)]$ with $R_0 = 100$ ╬®, $\alpha = 0.00385$ ┬░CŌü╗┬╣, $T_0 = 0$ ┬░C:

$$137.85 = 100 \left[1 + 0.00385 \cdot T\right]$$

$$1.3785 = 1 + 0.00385 \, T$$

$$0.3785 = 0.00385 \, T$$

$$T = \dfrac{0.3785}{0.00385} = 98.31 \text{ ┬░C}$$

**Verification via CallendarŌĆōVan Dusen:**

$$137.85 = 100\left[1 + (3.9083 \times 10^{-3}) T + (-5.775 \times 10^{-7}) T^2\right]$$

$$0.3785 = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} T - 5.775 \times 10^{-7} T^2$$

Solving the quadratic (with $T \approx 100$ as initial guess):

$$T = \dfrac{-3.9083 \times 10^{-3} \pm \sqrt{(3.9083 \times 10^{-3})^2 + 4(5.775 \times 10^{-7})(0.3785)}}{-2(5.775 \times 10^{-7})}$$

After evaluation: $T \approx 98.34$ ┬░C (the small 0.03 ┬░C discrepancy demonstrates why the linear approximation is good but not perfect at the upper end).

---

### 3.4 Sensitivity & Self-Heating Derivation

The sensitivity $S = dR/dT$ tells us how much the resistance changes per ┬░C. For the linear case:

$$S = R_0 \alpha$$

For a PT100: $S = 100 \times 0.00385 = 0.385$ ╬®/┬░C.

**Self-heating** occurs because the excitation current $I$ dissipates power $P = I^2 R$ in the element, which raises its temperature above ambient. The temperature rise is:

$$\Delta T_{sh} = \dfrac{P}{S_h} = \dfrac{I^2 R}{S_h}$$

where $S_h$ is the self-heating coefficient (in mW/┬░C), typically 5ŌĆō50 mW/┬░C depending on the RTD's thermal coupling to the medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** A 1 mA current through a PT100 dissipates $P = (10^{-3})^2 \times 100 = 10^{-4}$ W = 0.1 mW. If $S_h = 10$ mW/┬░C, then $\Delta T_{sh} = 0.01$ ┬░C. With 5 mA, $P = 2.5$ mW ŌåÆ $\Delta T_{sh} = 0.25$ ┬░C. **Always check this in problems.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**  
Explain the working principle of a Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD). Why is platinum the preferred metal for industrial RTDs?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD) operates on the principle that the electrical resistance of a pure metal changes in a **predictable and repeatable** manner with temperature. As temperature increases, **lattice vibration (phonon scattering)** of the metal atoms increases, leading to higher resistance.

**Why Platinum (3 reasons for full marks):**
1. **Chemical inertness** ŌĆö does not oxidize or corrode, ensuring long-term stability.
2. **High melting point** (1768 ┬░C) ŌĆö allows measurements up to 850 ┬░C.
3. **Near-linear RŌĆōT relationship** and excellent **reproducibility** ŌĆö two PT100 sensors from different manufacturers give nearly identical readings at the same temperature.

Platinum also follows the standardized **IEC 60751 / CallendarŌĆōVan Dusen equation**, making calibration interchangeable worldwide.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]**  
What is meant by "self-heating" in an RTD? How can it be minimized?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Self-heating** is the phenomenon where the excitation current passing through the RTD dissipates electrical power ($P = I^2 R$), which raises the sensor's temperature above the actual process temperature, causing a measurement error.

$$\Delta T_{sh} = \dfrac{I^2 R}{S_h}$$

**Methods to minimize self-heating (any 2 for 3 marks):**
1. Use a **low excitation current** (typically 1 mA) ŌĆö keeps power dissipation below 0.1 mW.
2. Use **pulsed excitation** ŌĆö current is applied only briefly during measurement, reducing average heating.
3. Choose an RTD with **high self-heating coefficient** ($S_h$) ŌĆö better thermal coupling to the process medium.
4. Use a **PT1000** instead of PT100 ŌĆö for the same current, $P$ is higher in PT1000? No ŌĆö but the higher base resistance gives better SNR.

---

### 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]**

**Q. (a)** Derive the CallendarŌĆōVan Dusen equation for an RTD and explain its significance over the linear approximation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PT100 RTD has a measured resistance of 144.70 ╬®. Calculate the temperature using (i) the linear approximation and (ii) the CallendarŌĆōVan Dusen equation. Comment on the deviation. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Q. (a):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Empirical Need:**  
The linear approximation $R(T) = R_0 [1 + \alpha(T - T_0)]$ is accurate only in a narrow band (0ŌĆō100 ┬░C). At higher temperatures, platinum's RŌĆōT curve shows a slight downward concavity, requiring a polynomial fit.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Quadratic Assumption:**  
Assume $R(T) = R_0 [1 + A T + B T^2]$ for $T \geq 0$ ┬░C.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Calibration at Three Reference Points:**  
Using $T = 0$ ┬░C ($R = R_0$), $T = 100$ ┬░C ($R = 138.5$ ╬®), and $T = 444.6$ ┬░C (zinc freezing point, $R = 248.0$ ╬®), we solve the resulting linear system (as shown in Section 3.2 above) to obtain:

$$A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} \text{ ┬░C}^{-1}, \quad B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7} \text{ ┬░C}^{-2}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Extension to Sub-Zero Temperatures:**  
For $T < 0$ ┬░C, a cubic term is added:

$$R(T) = R_0 \left[1 + A T + B T^2 + C (T - 100) T^3\right]$$

with $C = -4.183 \times 10^{-12}$ ┬░CŌü╗Ōü┤.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Significance:**  
- Extends valid range from 0ŌĆō100 ┬░C to **ŌłÆ200 ┬░C to +850 ┬░C**.
- Captures the curvature of platinum accurately (error < 0.01 ┬░C across the entire range).
- Enables **interchangeability**: any PT100 from any manufacturer using IEC 60751 constants will read identically.

---

### **Model Solution for Q. (b):**

Given: $R = 144.70$ ╬®, $R_0 = 100$ ╬®, $\alpha = 0.00385$ ┬░CŌü╗┬╣.

**(i) Linear Approximation:**

$$144.70 = 100[1 + 0.00385 \, T]$$

$$1.4470 = 1 + 0.00385 \, T$$

$$T = \dfrac{0.4470}{0.00385} = 116.10 \text{ ┬░C}$$

**(ii) CallendarŌĆōVan Dusen (T Ōēź 0):**

$$144.70 = 100\left[1 + (3.9083 \times 10^{-3}) T + (-5.775 \times 10^{-7}) T^2\right]$$

$$0.4470 = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} \, T - 5.775 \times 10^{-7} \, T^2$$

Rearranging:

$$5.775 \times 10^{-7} \, T^2 - 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} \, T + 0.4470 = 0$$

Applying the quadratic formula:

$$T = \dfrac{3.9083 \times 10^{-3} \pm \sqrt{(3.9083 \times 10^{-3})^2 - 4(5.775 \times 10^{-7})(0.4470)}}{2 \times 5.775 \times 10^{-7}}$$

Discriminant:

$$(3.9083 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 1.5274 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$4 \times 5.775 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.4470 = 1.0325 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\Delta = 1.5274 \times 10^{-5} - 1.0325 \times 10^{-6} = 1.4242 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\sqrt{\Delta} = 3.7737 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$T = \dfrac{3.9083 \times 10^{-3} - 3.7737 \times 10^{-3}}{1.155 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{1.346 \times 10^{-4}}{1.155 \times 10^{-6}} = 116.54 \text{ ┬░C}$$

**Comment on Deviation:**  
$\Delta T = 116.54 - 116.10 = 0.44$ ┬░C ŌĆö a noticeable error (~0.4 %) using the linear model at 116 ┬░C. This confirms that the **CallendarŌĆōVan Dusen equation is essential** for accurate measurements above 100 ┬░C.

---

#### **Question B ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]**

**Q. (a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **3-wire and 4-wire (Kelvin) configurations** for lead-wire compensation in RTDs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PT100 RTD is connected via 10 m of copper lead wire (resistivity 1.7 ├Ś 10Ōü╗ŌüĖ ╬®┬Ęm, cross-section 0.5 mm┬▓) in a 2-wire configuration. Calculate the **measurement error** when measuring 100 ┬░C. Suggest a remedy. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Q. (a):**

**2-Wire Configuration (for contrast):**  
In a 2-wire setup, the same two wires carry both the excitation current and sense the voltage. The measured resistance is:

$$R_{measured} = R_{RTD} + 2 R_{lead}$$

This error is unacceptable for low-resistance sensors like the PT100.

**3-Wire Configuration (most common in industry):**  
Three identical lead wires connect the RTD to the measuring bridge. Two wires carry current through the RTD, while the third wire is connected to one leg of the Wheatstone bridge. When the bridge is balanced, the lead resistances cancel out (provided all three leads are identical).

**Advantage:** Cost-effective and reasonably accurate (┬▒0.1 ┬░C typical).

**4-Wire (Kelvin) Configuration (laboratory / high precision):**  
Four wires connect the RTD: two for forcing current (force leads) and two for sensing voltage (sense leads). The sense leads carry negligible current, so there is **no IR drop** in them. The voltmeter measures only the true voltage across the RTD:

$$R_{RTD} = \dfrac{V_{sense}}{I_{force}}$$

**Advantage:** Eliminates lead-resistance error completely. Used in SPRTs and precision calibrators.

---

### **Model Solution for Q. (b):**

**Given:**
- Length of each lead wire: $L = 10$ m
- Resistivity of copper: $\rho_{Cu} = 1.7 \times 10^{-8}$ ╬®┬Ęm
- Cross-sectional area: $A = 0.5 \text{ mm}^2 = 0.5 \times 10^{-6}$ m┬▓
- RTD type: PT100 at 100 ┬░C ŌåÆ true $R_{RTD} = 138.5$ ╬®

**Step 1 ŌĆö Resistance of each lead wire:**

$$R_{lead} = \dfrac{\rho L}{A} = \dfrac{(1.7 \times 10^{-8})(10)}{0.5 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R_{lead} = \dfrac{1.7 \times 10^{-7}}{5 \times 10^{-7}} = 0.34 \text{ ╬® per wire}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Total lead resistance in 2-wire configuration:**

$$R_{lead,total} = 2 \times 0.34 = 0.68 \text{ ╬®}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apparent measured resistance:**

$$R_{measured} = 138.5 + 0.68 = 139.18 \text{ ╬®}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apparent temperature reading (using linear approximation):**

$$T_{apparent} = \dfrac{R_{measured} - 100}{100 \times 0.00385} = \dfrac{39.18}{0.385} = 101.77 \text{ ┬░C}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Measurement error:**

$$\Delta T = 101.77 - 100 = +1.77 \text{ ┬░C}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Remedy:**
- Switch to a **3-wire or 4-wire (Kelvin) configuration**, which cancels or eliminates lead-resistance error.
- Alternatively, use a **PT1000** instead of PT100 ŌĆö the lead-resistance error of 0.68 ╬® causes only 0.18 ┬░C error in a PT1000, an order-of-magnitude improvement.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in 2-wire lead compensation ŌĆö both leads contribute, not just one. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 2. **Using $T_0 = 0$ ┬░C** without explicitly stating it ŌĆö KTU examiners expect you to mention the ice-point reference.
> 3. **Mixing up European (╬▒ = 0.00385) and American (╬▒ = 0.00392) curves** ŌĆö always state which standard you are following.
> 4. **Skipping unit notation** ŌĆö write "╬®/┬░C" and "┬░CŌü╗┬╣", not just numbers.
> 5. **Not commenting on self-heating** when an excitation current is given ŌĆö this is a frequent follow-up sub-question (3 marks).
> 6. **Quadratic formula errors** in CallendarŌĆōVan Dusen problems ŌĆö write out the discriminant step explicitly; examiners look for it.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Resistance Thermometers (RTDs):**

- **Definition:** RTD = metal whose resistance varies predictably with temperature; **passive transducer**.
- **Most common:** **PT100** (Platinum, 100 ╬® at 0 ┬░C), per **IEC 60751**.
- **Working principle:** **Phonon scattering** in metal lattice increases with temperature, raising resistance.
- **Linear approximation:** $R(T) = R_0 [1 + \alpha (T - T_0)]$, valid 0ŌĆō100 ┬░C only.
- **CallendarŌĆōVan Dusen (T Ōēź 0):** $R(T) = R_0 [1 + A T + B T^2]$ with $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}$, $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}$.
- **CallendarŌĆōVan Dusen (T < 0):** Add cubic term with $C = -4.183 \times 10^{-12}$.
- **PT100 sensitivity:** $S = 0.385$ ╬®/┬░C ŌĆö memorize this.
- **Standard ╬▒ (European):** $0.00385$ ┬░CŌü╗┬╣; (American): $0.00392$ ┬░CŌü╗┬╣.
- **Range:** **ŌłÆ200 ┬░C to +850 ┬░C** for platinum.
- **Lead compensation:** 2-wire (error) ŌåÆ 3-wire (bridge balance) ŌåÆ 4-wire/Kelvin (zero error).
- **Self-heating:** $\Delta T = I^2 R / S_h$ ŌĆö keep excitation current low (1 mA typical).
- **Wheatstone bridge:** Output null when $R_1/R_2 = R_3/R_{RTD}$.
- **Material comparison:** Pt (best, expensive) | Cu (cheap, limited range) | Ni (cost-effective, non-linear).
- **Tolerance classes (IEC 60751):** Class AA (┬▒0.10 ┬░C), Class A (┬▒0.15 ┬░C), Class B (┬▒0.30 ┬░C) at 0 ┬░C.
- **Cold-junction compensation:** Not required for RTDs (unlike thermocouples) ŌĆö but lead-resistance compensation **is** required.
- **Industrial form factors:** 2-wire / 3-wire / 4-wire RTDs, Pt100 / Pt500 / Pt1000, thin-film vs wire-wound.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pyrometry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT413 - PROCESS INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pyrometry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Pyrometry?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> **Pyrometry** is the branch of thermometry concerned with the measurement of **high temperatures** (typically above **600 ┬░C / 873 K**) using the **radiant energy** emitted by a hot body. Instruments used for this purpose are called **Pyrometers**, and they operate on the principle of detecting **thermal (electromagnetic) radiation** rather than establishing physical contact with the body.

The term is derived from the Greek word *"pyros"* (meaning *fire*) and *"metron"* (meaning *measure*). Pyrometers are classified as **non-contact temperature sensors** because they do not disturb the thermal field of the target.

### 1.2 Why Do We Need Pyrometers Beyond 600 ┬░C?

At very high temperatures:
- **Thermocouples (contact type)** suffer from *drift*, *oxidation*, *creep*, and *mechanical degradation* of their hot junctions.
- **Mercury-in-glass and bimetallic thermometers** simply cannot survive beyond ~600 ┬░C.
- **RTDs** also have practical upper limits (platinum RTDs are reliable only up to ~660 ┬░C).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Pyrometry is one of the most important non-contact measurement techniques in process industries dealing with **furnaces, kilns, molten metals, glass manufacturing, and cement processing** ŌĆö where contact-based sensing is impossible.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Glowing Fire" Intuition

Imagine you walk into a dark room and see a faint orange glow coming from a furnace. Without touching it, you can roughly *guess* how hot the fire is by the **color** of the glow:
- Dull red glow ŌåÆ relatively low temperature (~600 ┬░C)
- Bright orange ŌåÆ higher (~1000 ┬░C)
- White-hot ŌåÆ very high (~1500 ┬░C and above)

Pyrometers do **exactly this** ŌĆö but with extreme precision, using physics-based mathematical laws. They convert the *intensity* and *spectral content* of emitted light/radiation into an accurate temperature reading.

### 1.4 Physical Foundation ŌĆö Electromagnetic Radiation

Every object above **absolute zero (0 K)** emits electromagnetic radiation due to atomic and molecular agitation. The total energy and its spectral distribution depend solely on the **absolute temperature** and **emissivity** of the body.

| Radiation Type | Wavelength Range | Pyrometer Application |
|---|---|---|
| Ultraviolet (UV) | < 400 nm | Specialised high-T sensors |
| Visible | 400 ŌĆō 700 nm | **Optical (disappearing filament) pyrometers** |
| Near Infrared (IR) | 0.7 ŌĆō 20 ┬Ąm | **Radiation & two-color pyrometers** |
| Far IR | > 20 ┬Ąm | Thermal imaging cameras |

### 1.5 The Concept of a Blackbody

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board-Exam Definition:**
> A **Blackbody** is an *idealized* body that absorbs **all incident radiation** at every wavelength, regardless of direction. By **Kirchhoff's Law**, a perfect absorber is also a perfect emitter ŌĆö so a blackbody emits the **maximum possible thermal radiation** for a given temperature.

Real surfaces only emit a fraction of blackbody radiation. The ratio of emitted energy of a real body to that of a blackbody at the same temperature is called **Emissivity (╬Ą)**, where:
$$0 \leq \varepsilon \leq 1$$

- **╬Ą = 1** ŌåÆ Blackbody (ideal)
- **╬Ą = 0** ŌåÆ Perfect reflector (no emission)
- Typical metals: ╬Ą Ōēł 0.05 ŌĆō 0.3 (low)
- Typical ceramics/oxides: ╬Ą Ōēł 0.6 ŌĆō 0.95 (high)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Laws of Blackbody Radiation (KTU High-Priority)

The mathematics of pyrometry rests on **four cornerstone laws** of blackbody radiation. Every KTU exam question in Module 1 is essentially built on these.

### 2.1.1 Planck's Law of Blackbody Radiation

This is the most general (and most accurate) law. It describes the **spectral emissive power** $E_{\lambda}$ (energy per unit area per unit wavelength per steradian) as a function of wavelength $\lambda$ and absolute temperature $T$:

$$E_{\lambda}(\lambda, T) = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T}\right) - 1 \right]}$$

- $C_1 = 2\pi h c^2 = 3.7418 \times 10^{-16} \ \text{W┬Ęm}^2$ ŌĆö First radiation constant
- $C_2 = \dfrac{hc}{k} = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2} \ \text{m┬ĘK}$ ŌĆö Second radiation constant
- $h$ = Planck's constant = $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J┬Ęs
- $c$ = Speed of light = $3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- $k$ = Boltzmann's constant = $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick ŌĆö "C1 on Top, ╬╗ in Power, C2 inside exp":**
> Numerator has $C_1$ and denominator has $\lambda^5$ ŌĆö remember *"Planck loves C1, hates ╬╗"* because ╬╗ appears in the 5th power.

### 2.1.2 Wien's Displacement Law

Differentiating Planck's law with respect to ╬╗ and setting it to zero gives the wavelength at which emission is **maximum**:

$$\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m┬ĘK}$$

Here $b$ is **Wien's displacement constant**. As temperature increases, the peak emission shifts toward **shorter wavelengths** (visible region) ŌĆö this is exactly why a heated iron turns from red ŌåÆ orange ŌåÆ white as T increases.

### 2.1.3 StefanŌĆōBoltzmann Law

Integrating Planck's law over all wavelengths gives the **total emissive power** (energy radiated per unit area per unit time):

$$E = \sigma T^4$$

- $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/(m}^2 \cdot \text{K}^4)$ ŌĆö StefanŌĆōBoltzmann constant

For a **real (gray) body** with emissivity ╬Ą:
$$E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$$

> [!CAUTION]
> This **4th-power dependence** is the reason radiation thermometry is sensitive at high T ŌĆö even a small change in T produces a large change in emitted energy.

### 2.1.4 Wien's Approximation (RayleighŌĆōJeans High-T Limit)

For $\lambda T \ll C_2$, the "-1" in Planck's denominator can be neglected, yielding the simpler form used in **optical pyrometer calibration**:

$$E_{\lambda} = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{C_2}{\lambda T}\right)$$

This is the working equation behind **brightness temperature** measurements.

## 2.2 Classification of Pyrometers (KTU Syllabus Map)

| Type | Physical Principle | Wavelength Region | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Optical (Disappearing Filament) Pyrometer** | Monochromatic brightness comparison | Visible (~650 nm, red filter) | Manual, lab standard |
| **Total Radiation Pyrometer** | StefanŌĆōBoltzmann (all ╬╗) | Wideband IR | Uses thermopile detector |
| **Two-Color (Ratio) Pyrometer** | Ratio of intensities at two ╬╗ | Two narrow IR bands | Independent of ╬Ą |
| **Photoelectric Pyrometer** | Photo-voltaic/photo-conductive cell | Visible/NIR | Fast, automated |
| **Infrared Thermometer (Pyrometer)** | Wideband IR detection | 0.7 ŌĆō 14 ┬Ąm | Most common industrial type |

## 2.3 Brightness, Color, and Radiant Temperatures

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Exam Favorite ŌĆö Three "Effective" Temperatures:**

Let a real body at true temperature $T$ be observed by a pyrometer designed for a blackbody.

1. **Brightness Temperature ($T_b$):** Temperature of a blackbody emitting the same *monochromatic intensity* at a chosen ╬╗ as the real body.
$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_b} = \frac{\lambda \ln \varepsilon_{\lambda}}{C_2}$$

2. **Color Temperature ($T_c$):** Temperature of a blackbody whose *ratio* of intensities at two wavelengths matches the real body.
$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_c} = \frac{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon_{\lambda_1}}{\varepsilon_{\lambda_2}}\right)}{C_2 \!\left(\dfrac{1}{\lambda_1} - \dfrac{1}{\lambda_2}\right)}$$

3. **Radiant Temperature ($T_r$):** Temperature of a blackbody emitting the same *total radiant flux* as the real body.
$$T_r = \varepsilon^{1/4} \cdot T$$

> For ╬Ą < 1, we always have: $T_r < T_b < T_c < T$ (true temperature)

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (One-Page Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Law | Variables | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{\lambda} = \dfrac{C_1}{\lambda^5 [e^{C_2/\lambda T}-1]}$ | Planck's law | Foundation of all radiation T sensors |
| 2 | $\lambda_{\max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m┬ĘK | Wien's displacement | Predicts peak emission ╬╗ |
| 3 | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | StefanŌĆōBoltzmann | Total radiation pyrometer |
| 4 | $E_{\lambda} \approx \dfrac{C_1}{\lambda^5} e^{-C_2/\lambda T}$ | Wien's approx. | Optical pyrometer |
| 5 | $\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_b} = \dfrac{\lambda \ln \varepsilon}{C_2}$ | Brightness temp. | Single-color pyrometers |
| 6 | $T_r = \varepsilon^{1/4} T$ | Radiant temp. | Total radiation pyrometers |
| 7 | $\text{Sensitivity} \propto T^3$ (for radiation) | StefanŌĆōBoltzmann derivative | High-T sensitivity boost |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Steel industry:** Continuous temperature monitoring of molten steel in ladles and tundishes (T Ōēł 1600 ┬░C) using two-color pyrometers because slag emissivity is uncertain.
- **Cement & lime kilns:** Infrared pyrometers measure rotating kiln shell to control fuel feed.
- **Glass manufacturing:** Optical pyrometers monitor the gob temperature (~1100 ┬░C).
- **Semiconductor:** Rapid thermal processing (RTP) uses pyrometers to control wafer heating without contamination.
- **Power plants:** Boiler tube temperature monitoring to prevent tube failures.
