# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of StefanŌĆōBoltzmann Law from Planck's Law

**Goal:** Show that integrating Planck's spectral emissive power over all wavelengths (0 ŌåÆ Ōł×) yields the fourth-power law.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the total emissive power as the integral of the spectral emissive power:**
$$E = \int_0^{\infty} E_{\lambda}(\lambda, T) \, d\lambda$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute Planck's law expression:**
$$E = \int_0^{\infty} \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T}\right) - 1 \right]} \, d\lambda$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Introduce the substitution** $x = \dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T}$, so $\lambda = \dfrac{C_2}{xT}$ and $d\lambda = -\dfrac{C_2}{x^2 T} dx$:

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \int_{\infty}^{0} \frac{C_1}{\left(\dfrac{C_2}{xT}\right)^{5} [e^{x} - 1]} \cdot \left(-\frac{C_2}{x^2 T}\right) dx \\
&= \int_0^{\infty} \frac{C_1 \cdot x^5 T^5}{C_2^{5} [e^{x} - 1]} \cdot \frac{C_2}{x^2 T} \, dx \\
&= \frac{C_1 T^4}{C_2^{4}} \int_0^{\infty} \frac{x^{3}}{e^{x} - 1} \, dx
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Evaluate the integral** using the standard result for the Riemann zeta function:
$$\int_0^{\infty} \frac{x^{3}}{e^{x} - 1} dx = \frac{\pi^{4}}{15}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Substitute back:**
$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{C_1 T^4}{C_2^{4}} \cdot \frac{\pi^{4}}{15} \\
  &= \left(\frac{2 \pi^{5} k^{4}}{15 h^{3} c^{2}}\right) T^{4} \\
  &= \sigma T^{4}
\end{aligned}$$

where $\sigma = \dfrac{2\pi^{5}k^{4}}{15 h^{3} c^{2}} = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W┬Ęm}^{-2}\text{┬ĘK}^{-4}$.

**Conclusion:** The StefanŌĆōBoltzmann constant Žā emerges naturally from fundamental constants, validating the $T^4$ dependence.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Wien's Displacement Law

**Goal:** Find the wavelength $\lambda_{\max}$ at which $E_{\lambda}$ is maximum for a given T.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Set derivative of Planck's law to zero:**
$$\frac{\partial E_{\lambda}}{\partial \lambda} = 0$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply logarithm differentiation to** $E_{\lambda} = C_1 \lambda^{-5} \left(e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1\right)^{-1}$:

$$\ln E_{\lambda} = \ln C_1 - 5 \ln \lambda - \ln\!\left(e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1\right)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Differentiate with respect to ╬╗:**
$$\frac{1}{E_{\lambda}} \frac{dE_{\lambda}}{d\lambda} = -\frac{5}{\lambda} - \frac{-C_2/\lambda^2 T \cdot e^{C_2/\lambda T}}{e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1} = 0$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Simplify using the substitution** $y = \dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
5 \left(1 - e^{-y}\right) &= y \\
5 - 5 e^{-y} &= y
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö The transcendental equation** $y = 5(1 - e^{-y})$ is solved numerically to give:
$$y = 4.9651...$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Therefore:**
$$\lambda_{\max} T = \frac{C_2}{y} = \frac{1.4388 \times 10^{-2}}{4.9651} = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m┬ĘK}$$

This is the constant $b$ in Wien's Displacement Law. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example ŌĆö Brightness Temperature Correction

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Model Problem (Frequently Asked):**
> A pyrometer calibrated for a blackbody reads a brightness temperature of **1323 K** when sighted on a metal surface. If the surface has emissivity **╬Ą = 0.6** at the pyrometer's operating wavelength **╬╗ = 0.65 ┬Ąm**, find the **true temperature** of the surface. Use $C_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2}$ m┬ĘK.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the brightness temperature relation:**
$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_b} = \frac{\lambda \ln \varepsilon}{C_2}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute** $\lambda \ln \varepsilon$:
$$\lambda \ln \varepsilon = (0.65 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot \ln(0.6)$$
$$\ln(0.6) = -0.5108$$
$$\lambda \ln \varepsilon = (0.65 \times 10^{-6}) \times (-0.5108) = -3.320 \times 10^{-7} \ \text{m}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute** $\dfrac{\lambda \ln \varepsilon}{C_2}$:
$$\frac{-3.320 \times 10^{-7}}{1.4388 \times 10^{-2}} = -2.308 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute** $\dfrac{1}{T_b}$:
$$\frac{1}{1323} = 7.5586 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Solve for 1/T:**
$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_b} + \frac{\lambda \ln \varepsilon}{C_2} = 7.5586 \times 10^{-4} - 2.308 \times 10^{-5} = 7.3278 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Invert to get T:**
$$T = \frac{1}{7.3278 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1364.7 \ \text{K}$$

**Result:** True temperature Ōēł **1365 K** (or 1092 ┬░C). The pyrometer under-reads by 42 K because the surface is not a blackbody. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example ŌĆö Radiant Temperature

> A furnace wall has emissivity 0.8. A total radiation pyrometer indicates a radiant temperature of **1500 K**. Find the true temperature.

**Solution:**

Using $T_r = \varepsilon^{1/4} T$:
$$T = \frac{T_r}{\varepsilon^{1/4}} = \frac{1500}{(0.8)^{0.25}}$$

$$\varepsilon^{1/4} = (0.8)^{0.25} = 0.9457$$

$$T = \frac{1500}{0.9457} = 1586.1 \ \text{K}$$

**Result:** True temperature Ōēł **1586 K** (Ōēł 1313 ┬░C). $\blacksquare$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram ŌĆö Generic Radiation Pyrometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hot Target Body] -->|Emitted Radiation| B[Optical Lens / Mirror]
    B --> C[Radiation Chopper Modulator]
    C --> D[Narrowband Optical Filter]
    D --> E[Infrared Detector Thermopile]
    E -->|Electrical Signal| F[Amplifier with AC Coupling]
    F --> G[Linearisation and Emissivity Compensation]
    G --> H[Display and 4-20 mA Output]
    H --> I[Process Controller PLC DCS]
```

**Working of Each Block:**
- **A ŌåÆ Hot Target:** Source of broadband thermal radiation.
- **B ŌåÆ Lens/Mirror:** Collects and focuses radiation onto detector.
- **C ŌåÆ Chopper:** Mechanical or electronic modulator that AC-cuts the signal to eliminate DC drift and ambient thermal effects.
- **D ŌåÆ Filter:** Selects specific wavelength (e.g., 0.65 ┬Ąm for optical, 0.9 ┬Ąm for IR).
- **E ŌåÆ Detector:** Converts radiation into voltage ŌĆö commonly a **thermopile**, photodiode, or photomultiplier.
- **F ŌåÆ Amplifier:** Boosts weak signal; AC coupling rejects ambient temperature drift.
- **G ŌåÆ Linearisation:** The output is non-linear in T (e.g., $T^4$); compensator linearises.
- **H ŌåÆ Output:** 4ŌĆō20 mA current loop or digital signal for the control room.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Disappearing Filament Optical Pyrometer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OpticalPath [Optical Path]
        P1[Pyrometer Lamp Filament] -->|Eyepiece View| O1[Objective Lens]
        O1 --> R1[Red Filter 650 nm]
        R1 --> EYE[Eye of Observer]
    end
    subgraph Circuit [Calibration Circuit]
        V1[Variable DC Source] -->|Rheostat Knob| RHEO[Rheostat]
        RHEO --> P1
        V1 --> AMM[Ammeter Calibrated in Temperature]
    end
    TG[Target Object] --> O1
    AMM -->|Reading| OBS[Observer Reads T]
```

**Principle:** The observer sees the target's image superimposed on a calibrated tungsten filament. The current through the filament is varied until the filament **disappears** (matches brightness of target) ŌĆö at that moment, the ammeter reading directly gives the **brightness temperature**.

---

## 4.3 Two-Color (Ratio) Pyrometer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Target Hot Body] --> B[Beam Splitter Optics]
    B --> C1[Filter 1: 0.9 micrometer]
    B --> C2[Filter 2: 1.05 micrometer]
    C1 --> D1[Detector 1 Photodiode]
    C2 --> D2[Detector 2 Photodiode]
    D1 --> R[Ratio Computation Circuit]
    D2 --> R
    R --> L[Logarithmic Amplifier]
    L --> OUT[Display Temperature]
```

**Key Advantage:** Because the ratio $E_{\lambda_1}/E_{\lambda_2}$ does not depend on ╬Ą (assuming gray body), the result is **emissivity-independent** ŌĆö ideal for dirty or partially obscured targets like molten metal in a foundry.

---

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart ŌĆö Selecting the Right Pyrometer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Target Temperature Range?}
    Q1 -->|600 to 3000 C| Q2{Target Emissivity Known Stable?}
    Q1 -->|>3000 C| OPT[Use Optical Pyrometer 650 nm]
    Q2 -->|Yes Single color OK| SC[Single-Color Brightness Pyrometer]
    Q2 -->|No Noisy Dusty| TC[Two-Color Ratio Pyrometer]
    Q2 -->|Just need average T| TR[Total Radiation Pyrometer]
    SC --> CAL[Apply Emissivity Correction]
    TC --> NC[No Correction Needed]
    TR --> CAL2[Apply T_r equals e to 1 by 4 times T correction]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
**Define Pyrometry. State the working principle of an optical pyrometer.**

**Model Answer:**
Pyrometry is the technique of measuring high temperatures (above 600 ┬░C) by detecting the **thermal radiation** emitted by a hot body, *without* making physical contact with it. The working principle of an **optical (disappearing filament) pyrometer** is based on **monochromatic brightness comparison**: the brightness of a calibrated tungsten filament inside the instrument is visually matched (made to "disappear") against the brightness of the target body at a fixed wavelength (usually 0.65 ┬Ąm using a red filter). The filament current required for the match is a direct measure of the target's **brightness temperature**. The true temperature is then obtained by applying the emissivity correction equation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (Typical):** Definition ŌĆö 1.5 marks, Working Principle ŌĆö 1.5 marks.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]**
**Distinguish between Brightness Temperature and Radiant Temperature.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Brightness Temperature ($T_b$) | Radiant Temperature ($T_r$) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Temperature of a blackbody emitting the same **monochromatic intensity** at a given ╬╗ | Temperature of a blackbody emitting the same **total radiant flux** |
| Relation to T | $\dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{T_b} + \dfrac{\lambda \ln \varepsilon}{C_2}$ | $T_r = \varepsilon^{1/4} T$ |
| Used by | Optical (single-color) pyrometers | Total radiation pyrometers |
| Dependence on ╬╗ | Depends on wavelength | Independent of wavelength |
| Typical magnitude | Closer to T than $T_r$ (less error) | Lower than T (more error) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

### **Question A ŌĆö Set 1 (Optical Pyrometer Deep Dive)**

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Model Paper, Q1(a) + Q1(b)]**

**(a)** With a neat functional diagram, explain the **construction and working of an optical (disappearing filament) pyrometer**. List any **two advantages** and **two limitations**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A disappearing filament pyrometer is sighted on a hot metal surface and the filament disappears at a current corresponding to a brightness temperature of **1423 K**. The surface has emissivity **0.55** at the operating wavelength of **0.65 ┬Ąm**. Calculate the **true temperature** of the metal. Given $C_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2}$ m┬ĘK. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a)**

**Construction (4 marks):**
1. **Objective Lens:** Collects radiation from the target and forms its image in the plane of the **pyrometer lamp filament**.
2. **Pyrometer Lamp:** A calibrated tungsten filament whose brightness is controlled by varying current through a **rheostat**.
3. **Red Filter (╬╗ Ōēł 0.65 ┬Ąm):** Placed in the optical path to make the comparison monochromatic.
4. **Eyepiece:** Magnifies the combined image of target and filament for the observer.
5. **Rheostat + Ammeter (calibrated in ┬░C or K):** Adjusts filament current and reads the corresponding temperature.

**Working (2 marks):**
The observer adjusts the rheostat until the filament image exactly disappears into the target's image ŌĆö meaning their **monochromatic brightness at 0.65 ┬Ąm is equal**. The ammeter reading at this "disappearance" point gives the **brightness temperature** $T_b$.

**Advantages (0.5 mark each):**
- Very high accuracy (┬▒0.5% in lab conditions).
- No contact with target ŌåÆ no disturbance of temperature field.
- Self-contained and portable.

**Limitations (0.5 mark each):**
- Manual operation; cannot be automated easily.
- Requires emissivity correction (affected by smoke, dust, view obstructions).
- Operator skill dependent (subjective match).

---

#### **Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b)**

**Given:** $T_b = 1423$ K, $\varepsilon = 0.55$, $\lambda = 0.65 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $C_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2}$ m┬ĘK

**Formula:**
$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_b} = \frac{\lambda \ln \varepsilon}{C_2}$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $\lambda \ln \varepsilon$:**
$$\ln(0.55) = -0.5978$$
$$\lambda \ln \varepsilon = (0.65 \times 10^{-6}) \times (-0.5978) = -3.886 \times 10^{-7} \ \text{m}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the RHS:**
$$\frac{\lambda \ln \varepsilon}{C_2} = \frac{-3.886 \times 10^{-7}}{1.4388 \times 10^{-2}} = -2.701 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $1/T_b$:**
$$\frac{1}{1423} = 7.0274 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Find $1/T$:**
$$\frac{1}{T} = 7.0274 \times 10^{-4} - 2.701 \times 10^{-5} = 6.7573 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Invert:**
$$T = 1480.0 \ \text{K} \approx \mathbf{1207 \, ┬░C}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ŌĆö Most Common Mistake:**
> Students frequently forget that the formula uses **absolute temperature (K)**, not ┬░C, and **forget the negative sign** in $\ln \varepsilon$ when ╬Ą < 1. Also, **do not** confuse brightness temperature with true temperature ŌĆö they are equal *only* when ╬Ą = 1 (blackbody). Forgetting the emissivity correction loses **3 full marks**.

---

### **Question B ŌĆö Set 2 (Total Radiation Pyrometer & Laws)**

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022 Past Paper Style, Q2(a) + Q2(b)]**

**(a)** State and explain **Stefan's law** and **Wien's displacement law**. Show how **Planck's law** reduces to **Wien's approximation** in the short-wavelength limit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **working of a total radiation pyrometer** with a neat sketch. A total radiation pyrometer sighted on a furnace wall (╬Ą = 0.7) reads a radiant temperature of **1700 K**. Find the **true temperature** of the furnace. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a)**

**Stefan's Law (2 marks):**
The total radiant energy emitted per unit area per unit time by a blackbody is proportional to the **fourth power of its absolute temperature**:
$$E_b = \sigma T^4, \quad \sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W┬Ęm}^{-2}\text{┬ĘK}^{-4}$$
For a real body: $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$.

**Wien's Displacement Law (2 marks):**
The wavelength at which the spectral emissive power of a blackbody is maximum is **inversely proportional** to its absolute temperature:
$$\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m┬ĘK}$$
Higher T ŌåÆ shorter $\lambda_{\max}$ (color shifts from red to white).

**Planck ŌåÆ Wien Reduction (3 marks):**
Planck's law: $E_{\lambda} = \dfrac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1\right]}$

For $\lambda T \ll C_2$, we have $\dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T} \gg 1$, so $e^{C_2/\lambda T} \gg 1$, and the "-1" in the denominator can be dropped:

$$E_{\lambda} \approx \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5} \cdot e^{-C_2/\lambda T}$$

This is **Wien's approximation** ŌĆö valid for short wavelengths or high temperatures (the regime used by optical pyrometers).

---

#### **Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b)**

**Working of Total Radiation Pyrometer (4 marks):**
A concave mirror focuses the **total radiation** from the target onto a **thermopile** (a series of thermocouples). The thermopile generates an EMF proportional to the incident radiative power, which is then displayed/recorded as temperature. A chopper modulates the radiation to eliminate ambient thermal drift. Output is calibrated assuming a blackbody target (╬Ą = 1).

**Calculation (3 marks):**

**Given:** $T_r = 1700$ K, $\varepsilon = 0.7$

**Formula:** $T_r = \varepsilon^{1/4} \cdot T \Rightarrow T = \dfrac{T_r}{\varepsilon^{1/4}}$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $\varepsilon^{1/4}$:**
$$(0.7)^{0.25} = e^{0.25 \ln(0.7)} = e^{0.25 \times (-0.3567)} = e^{-0.0892} = 0.9147$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute T:**
$$T = \frac{1700}{0.9147} = 1858.5 \ \text{K}$$

**Result:** True furnace temperature Ōēł **1859 K** (Ōēł 1586 ┬░C). $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Some students incorrectly use $T = T_r + \text{constant}$ or $T = T_r / \varepsilon$. The **correct** relation is $T = T_r / \varepsilon^{1/4}$. Misapplying this loses 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Pyrometry = non-contact T measurement above ~600 ┬░C** using emitted radiation.
- [x] **Blackbody** is a perfect absorber & emitter; real bodies have **╬Ą < 1**.
- [x] **Planck's law** is the master equation; integrates to **StefanŌĆōBoltzmann** ($T^4$) and differentiates to **Wien's displacement** ($\lambda_{\max} T = b$).
- [x] **Wien's approximation** ($E_{\lambda} \propto \frac{1}{\lambda^5} e^{-C_2/\lambda T}$) is the working equation for **optical pyrometers**.
- [x] **Three effective temperatures** ŌĆö Brightness $T_b$ (monochromatic), Color $T_c$ (two-╬╗ ratio), Radiant $T_r$ (total flux).
- [x] Order: $T_r < T_b < T_c < T$ (true) when $\varepsilon < 1$.
- [x] **Disappearing filament pyrometer** uses visual brightness match at 0.65 ┬Ąm (red filter).
- [x] **Total radiation pyrometer** uses thermopile + StefanŌĆōBoltzmann law.
- [x] **Two-color pyrometer** measures ratio $E_{\lambda_1}/E_{\lambda_2}$ ŌåÆ **emissivity-independent** (key advantage).
- [x] **Constants to memorize:** $C_1 = 3.7418 \times 10^{-16}$ W┬Ęm┬▓, $C_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2}$ m┬ĘK, $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m┬ĘK, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W┬ĘmŌü╗┬▓┬ĘKŌü╗Ōü┤.
- [x] **Key correction equation (must memorize):**
  $$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_b} = \frac{\lambda \ln \varepsilon}{C_2}$$
- [x] **Sensitivity of radiation pyrometer** $\propto T^3$ (derivative of $T^4$).
- [x] **Always use absolute temperature (K)** in all radiation-law equations.
- [x] For ╬Ą ŌåÆ 1 (blackbody), $T_b = T_c = T_r = T$ (true temperature).

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Takeaway for KTU Viva:** *"Pyrometers turn the color and intensity of light from a hot body into a precise temperature ŌĆö using Planck, Wien, and StefanŌĆōBoltzmann as their mathematical backbone."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calibration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT413 - PROCESS INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Calibration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Calibration** is the systematic process of comparing a measuring instrument's output (or indicated value) against a *traceable reference standard* of *known and higher accuracy*, and adjusting the instrument (if necessary) so that its output conforms to the accepted tolerance band over the specified range of measurement. It is fundamentally a **verification + adjustment + documentation** activity that establishes the **metrological traceability** of the instrument to national/international standards such as those maintained by the **National Physical Laboratory (NPL, India)**, **NIST (USA)**, or **BIPM (Bureau International des Poids et Mesures)**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (per ISA-51.1 / ISO 9001:2015 vocabulary):
> *"Calibration is the set of operations that establish, under specified conditions, the relationship between the values of quantities indicated by a measuring instrument and the corresponding values realized by standards."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Calibration of industrial instruments is treated as the *cornerstone* of all process measurements. Every subsequent module (temperature, pressure, flow, level) implicitly relies on the calibration principles defined here. A question on calibration terminology is statistically the **highest-weightage definition** in this module.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Weighing Scale at a Tea Stall" Intuition

Imagine a tea vendor in Kerala using a spring balance. He places a known **1 kg brass weight** (his "standard") on the pan. If the needle points to **1.05 kg**, his scale has a **+0.05 kg error** at that point. To **calibrate**, he adjusts the internal screw so that the needle *exactly* points to 1.00 kg. He then repeats the test at 2 kg, 3 kg, and 5 kg, and *records the deviation* at each step. This act of:
1. **Comparing** against a known standard,
2. **Adjusting** to remove error, and
3. **Recording** the inputŌĆōoutput relationship,

ŌĆ”is exactly what industrial calibration does, except the "tea scale" is replaced by a **transmitter, RTD, orifice meter, or control valve**, and the "brass weight" is replaced by a **dead-weight tester, dry-block calibrator, or primary flow rig** that is itself traceable to NPL.

## 1.3 Why Calibration is Non-Negotiable in Process Industries

| Reason | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|
| **Safety** | Uncalibrated pressure transmitters can over/under-report boiler pressure ŌåÆ boiler explosion risk. |
| **Product Quality** | Off-set thermocouples cause incorrect curing temperatures in rubber/vulcanization. |
| **Regulatory Compliance** | ISO 9001, ISO 17025, OHSAS, API, FDA mandates calibration records. |
| **Economy** | Avoids raw-material giveaway in BPCL, BPC, refinery blending operations. |
| **Traceability** | Establishes a documented chain back to SI units (kg, m, s, A, K, mol, cd). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Remember:**
> - **1 mA** = 0.001 **A** (SI base unit for current loop signals)
> - **Standard current loop:** **4ŌĆō20 mA** (Live-zero signaling; 4 mA = 0%, 20 mA = 100%)
> - **Standard pneumatic signal:** **3ŌĆō15 psig** (0.2ŌĆō1.0 kg/cm┬▓)
> - **Standard voltage signal:** **1ŌĆō5 V DC** or **0ŌĆō10 V DC**

## 1.4 Vocabulary Spine ŌĆö The Seven "Must-Know" Terms

These terms appear in nearly every KTU question paper on this module. Memorize them verbatim.

1. **Range** ŌĆö The region between the lower and upper limits of measurement (e.g., 0ŌĆō100 ┬░C).
2. **Span** ŌĆö The *algebraic difference* between the upper and lower range values: $\text{Span} = URV - LRV$ (e.g., 100 ┬░C).
3. **Zero** ŌĆö The lower range value (LRV), 0% of input.
4. **Accuracy** ŌĆö The closeness of agreement between an indicated value and the *true* value (expressed as ┬▒% of span or ┬▒% of reading).
5. **Precision / Repeatability** ŌĆö The ability to reproduce the same reading under identical conditions.
6. **Sensitivity** ŌĆö The ratio of output change to input change (slope of calibration curve).
7. **Drift** ŌĆö The slow, time-dependent change in output at a constant input (zero drift, span drift).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Calibration Chain (Hierarchy of Standards)

Industrial calibration does **not** rely on a single standard. It follows a strict pyramid:

```
                ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
                Ōöé   International Standards Ōöé  (BIPM ŌĆö SI units)
                ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌö¼ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś
                               Ōöé
                ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢╝ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
                Ōöé   National Standards      Ōöé  (NPL India / NIST USA)
                ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌö¼ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś
                               Ōöé
                ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢╝ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
                Ōöé   Primary Standards       Ōöé  (Lab-grade references)
                ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌö¼ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś
                               Ōöé
                ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢╝ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
                Ōöé   Secondary (Working)     Ōöé  (Portable calibrators)
                Ōöé   Standards               Ōöé
                ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌö¼ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś
                               Ōöé
                ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢╝ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
                Ōöé   Field Instrument Under  Ōöé  (PT, TT, FT, LT)
                Ōöé   Test (UUT)              Ōöé
                ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At every step down the pyramid, the **uncertainty multiplies** (typically by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$ to 4). The UUT must therefore be calibrated by an instrument whose accuracy is at least **4├Ś better (1:4 TUR ŌĆö Test Uncertainty Ratio)** than the UUT's own accuracy.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Calibration Procedure (The "10-Step Method")

1. **Identify** the instrument, its tag number, range, and previous calibration record.
2. **Select** the appropriate reference standard (accuracy Ōēź 4├Ś UUT).
3. **Isolate** the instrument from the live process (block valves, depressurize, vent).
4. **Connect** the standard to the UUT (dead-weight tester, dry-block, signal generator).
5. **Apply inputs** in *ascending* order at 5 points: **0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100%** of range.
6. **Record** the UUT output at each input step (as-**found** readings).
7. **Reverse** the inputs in *descending* order and re-record (for hysteresis check).
8. **Compute** errors; if any error exceeds the tolerance, **adjust** the **Zero** (at 0% input) and **Span** (at 100% input) potentiometers.
9. **Iterate** (Zero ŌåÆ Span ŌåÆ Zero ŌåÆ Span) until both endpoints lie within tolerance.
10. **Re-record** the as-**left** readings and **certify** with a calibration sticker + traceability certificate.

## 2.3 Static vs Dynamic Calibration

| Aspect | Static Calibration | Dynamic Calibration |
|---|---|---|
| Input signal | Constant (DC) | Time-varying (step, ramp, sine) |
| Purpose | Verifies **accuracy, linearity, hysteresis** | Verifies **speed of response, damping, frequency response** |
| Standards | Dead-weight tester, hand pump, decade box | Function generator, pressure pulse generator |
| Typical for | Industrial panel meters, gauges, transmitters | Control valves, flow meters, dynamic analyzers |
| KTU Weightage | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ (Most common) | ŌŁÉŌŁÉŌŁÉ (Advanced topic) |

## 2.4 Five-Point Calibration Data Format (As-Found and As-Left)

A standard KTU 5-point calibration table looks like this:

| Input (% of Range) | Input (Engineering Units) | As-Found Output (UUT) | As-Left Output (UUT) | Error | % Error |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0% | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |
| 25% | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |
| 50% | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |
| 75% | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |
| 100% | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Absolute Error** | $E = I - T$ | Same as measurement | $I$ = indicated, $T$ = true |
| 2 | **% of Reading Error** | $\%E_R = \dfrac{E}{T} \times 100$ | % | More accurate at low end |
| 3 | **% of Span Error** | $\%E_S = \dfrac{E}{\text{Span}} \times 100$ | % | Industry-preferred; conservative at low end |
| 4 | **% of Full-Scale (FS) Error** | $\%E_{FS} = \dfrac{E}{FS} \times 100$ | % | Equivalent to % of span |
| 5 | **Span (algebraic)** | $S = URV - LRV$ | Engineering units | Not the same as range! |
| 6 | **Linearity (Terminal)** | $L_T = \dfrac{\max \vert \Delta I - \Delta I_{\text{ideal}} \vert}{S} \times 100$ | % | Uses best-fit straight line through endpoints |
| 7 | **Linearity (Independent)** | $L_I = \dfrac{\max(+D, -D)}{S} \times 100$ | % | Best-fit line *not* forced through endpoints |
| 8 | **Hysteresis** | $H = \dfrac{\vert I_{up} - I_{down} \vert_{\max}}{S} \times 100$ | % | Maximum gap between up- and down-curve readings |
| 9 | **Repeatability (n cycles)** | $R = \dfrac{I_{\max} - I_{\min}}{S} \times 100$ | % | At same input, same conditions, same direction |
| 10 | **Reproducibility** | Same as repeatability but *different* operator / lab / conditions | % | |
| 11 | **Dead Band / Dead Zone** | Smallest change in input that produces a perceptible output change | % of span | |
| 12 | **Sensitivity (static)** | $K = \dfrac{\Delta O}{\Delta I}$ | Output unit / Input unit | Slope of calibration curve |
| 13 | **Zero Drift** | $\Delta I \big\vert_{I=0}$ over time | Engineering units | With input held at zero |
| 14 | **Span Drift** | Change in gain over time | % of span/month | |
| 15 | **Test Uncertainty Ratio (TUR)** | $\text{TUR} = \dfrac{\text{Accuracy of UUT}}{\text{Accuracy of Standard}}$ | Dimensionless | Should be Ōēź 4 |
| 16 | **Combined Standard Uncertainty ($u_c$)** | $u_c = \sqrt{u_1^2 + u_2^2 + \ldots + u_n^2}$ | Same as measurand | Root-sum-square (RSS) method |
| 17 | **Expanded Uncertainty ($U$)** | $U = k \cdot u_c$ | Same as measurand | $k = 2$ for ~95% confidence |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value and determinant symbols in the above table are written using `\vert` and `\Delta` for safe LaTeX rendering. KTU examiners specifically look for *correct sign convention* (always $I - T$, never $T - I$).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petroleum Refineries (BPCL Kochi, CPCL Chennai):** Calibration of orifice flow meters ensures custody-transfer accuracy worth crores of rupees.
- **Pharmaceutical Plants (USFDA-regulated):** Calibration of temperature/humidity chambers is mandatory per 21 CFR Part 11.
- **Power Plants (NTPC):** Calibration of boiler drum level transmitters prevents dry-out accidents.
- **Aerospace (ISRO):** Calibration of pressure transducers in cryogenic engines requires traceability to NPL.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Conversion Between % of Span, % of Reading, and % of Full Scale

Given an instrument with:
- Range: 0 to 100 ┬░C ŌåÆ **Span = 100 ┬░C**
- Indicated value $I = 95 ┬░C$
- True value $T = 94 ┬░C$

**Step 1.** Compute the absolute error.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= I - T \\
  &= 95\,┬░C - 94\,┬░C \\
  &= 1\,┬░C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.** Compute **% of Span** error (used by most transmitters):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%E_S &= \frac{E}{\text{Span}} \times 100 \\
      &= \frac{1\,┬░C}{100\,┬░C} \times 100 \\
      &= 1.0\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3.** Compute **% of Reading** error (used by some lab instruments):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%E_R &= \frac{E}{T} \times 100 \\
      &= \frac{1\,┬░C}{94\,┬░C} \times 100 \\
      &= 1.064\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4.** Compute **% of Full Scale** error (equivalent to % of span when range starts at 0):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%E_{FS} &= \frac{E}{FS} \times 100 \\
         &= \frac{1\,┬░C}{100\,┬░C} \times 100 \\
         &= 1.0\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5.** Insight: At *low* end of range, % of span is *more conservative* (larger number ŌåÆ stricter tolerance) than % of reading. This is why **process transmitters are universally calibrated in % of span**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Linearity (Terminal) from Five-Point Data

Suppose a 0ŌĆō100 ┬░C temperature transmitter was tested at 5 points (ascending) and the following data were obtained:

| Input $T$ (┬░C) | Indicated $I$ (┬░C) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0.5 |
| 25 | 25.8 |
| 50 | 51.2 |
| 75 | 76.0 |
| 100 | 100.5 |

**Step 1.** Compute ideal (endpoint) straight line. Endpoints are (0, 0.5) and (100, 100.5).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Slope} &= \frac{100.5 - 0.5}{100 - 0} = \frac{100.0}{100} = 1.000 \\
\text{Intercept} &= 0.5 \\
\text{Ideal equation: } I_{\text{ideal}} &= 1.000 \cdot T + 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.** Compute deviation $\Delta = I - I_{\text{ideal}}$ at each test point.

| $T$ (┬░C) | $I$ (┬░C) | $I_{\text{ideal}}$ | $\Delta = I - I_{\text{ideal}}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.5 | 0.5 | 0.0 |
| 25 | 25.8 | 25.5 | +0.3 |
| 50 | 51.2 | 50.5 | +0.7 |
| 75 | 76.0 | 75.5 | +0.5 |
| 100 | 100.5 | 100.5 | 0.0 |

**Step 3.** Terminal linearity (worst-case positive deviation):

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_T &= \frac{\max \vert \Delta \vert}{\text{Span}} \times 100 \\
    &= \frac{0.7\,┬░C}{100\,┬░C} \times 100 \\
    &= 0.70\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Hysteresis from Up- and Down-Cycle Data

A pressure transmitter (0ŌĆō10 bar) was tested with the following readings:

| Input (bar) | Output Going UP (mA) | Output Going DOWN (mA) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 4.00 | 4.05 |
| 2.5 | 8.00 | 8.10 |
| 5.0 | 12.00 | 12.10 |
| 7.5 | 16.00 | 16.05 |
| 10.0 | 20.00 | 20.00 |

**Step 1.** Convert mA to % of span (4ŌĆō20 mA range ŌåÆ Span = 16 mA, or 100%).

For each input, compute % of span for both up and down readings.

**Step 2.** Compute the absolute difference $\vert I_{up} - I_{down} \vert$ at each input:

| Input (bar) | $\vert I_{up} - I_{down} \vert$ (mA) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0.05 |
| 2.5 | 0.10 |
| 5.0 | 0.10 |
| 7.5 | 0.05 |
| 10.0 | 0.00 |

**Step 3.** Maximum difference = **0.10 mA**.

**Step 4.** Compute hysteresis as % of span:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \frac{0.10\,mA}{16\,mA} \times 100 \\
  &= 0.625\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 ŌĆö Test Uncertainty Ratio (TUR) with RSS Uncertainty Combination

A thermocouple (UUT) is specified at $\pm 0.5\,┬░C$ accuracy. The standard used to calibrate it has an accuracy of $\pm 0.1\,┬░C$. The ambient temperature contribution is $\pm 0.05\,┬░C$, and the reference-junction compensation contributes $\pm 0.08\,┬░C$.

**Step 1.** Compute the Test Uncertainty Ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TUR} &= \frac{0.5\,┬░C}{0.1\,┬░C} = 5.0
\end{aligned}
$$

This satisfies the **1:4** rule (TUR Ōēź 4). Ō£ō

**Step 2.** Compute combined standard uncertainty $u_c$ (RSS):

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_c &= \sqrt{(0.1)^2 + (0.05)^2 + (0.08)^2} \\
    &= \sqrt{0.0100 + 0.0025 + 0.0064} \\
    &= \sqrt{0.0189} \\
    &= 0.1375\,┬░C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3.** Compute expanded uncertainty at $k = 2$ (Ōēł 95% confidence):

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= k \cdot u_c = 2 \times 0.1375 = 0.275\,┬░C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4.** Final reported uncertainty: **$T_{\text{calibrated}} \pm 0.275\,┬░C$ (k = 2)**.

> [!NOTE]
> This RSS method is the **GUM (Guide to the Expression of Uncertainty in Measurement)** standard, published by JCGM 100:2008. It is the international benchmark for uncertainty expression.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix ŌĆö Which Calibration Parameter to Use?

| Scenario | Use % of Span | Use % of Reading | Use % of FS | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Differential Pressure (DP) transmitter | Ō£ģ | ŌØī | Ō£ģ | Industrial standard |
| Lab-grade digital multimeter | ŌØī | Ō£ģ | ŌØī | Specified at reading |
| Bourdon pressure gauge | Ō£ģ | ŌØī | Ō£ģ | Industry standard |
| Thermocouple with ice-bath | Ō£ģ | ŌØī | Ō£ģ | Process instrumentation convention |
| Orifice flow meter (custody) | Ō£ģ | ŌØī | Ō£ģ | AGA / API standard |
| Glass thermometer (lab) | ŌØī | Ō£ģ | ŌØī | Reading-based accuracy |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Calibration Loop Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RS[Reference Standard NPL Traceable] -->|Known Input| PAD[Input Conditioning Dead Weight Tester Hand Pump Decade Box]
    PAD -->|Stimulus Signal| UUT[Instrument Under Test UUT PT TT FT LT]
    UUT -->|Output Signal 4 to 20 mA| READ[Precision Readout Multimeter HMI]
    READ -->|Recorded Value| COMP[Error Calculation Module]
    PAD -->|True Value| COMP
    COMP -->|Error Exceeds Tolerance| ADJ[Adjustment Unit Zero Pot Span Pot]
    ADJ -->|Corrected Calibration| UUT
    COMP -->|Within Tolerance| CERT[Calibration Certificate and Sticker]
    ADJ -->|After Adjustment| CERT
    CERT -->|Archive| DB[(Calibration Database ISO 9001)]
```

## 4.2 Calibration Decision Tree (Accept / Re-Adjust / Reject)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Connect Standard to UUT] --> B[Apply 5 Inputs Ascending 0 25 50 75 100 percent]
    B --> C[Record As Found Readings]
    C --> D[Apply 5 Inputs Descending 100 75 50 25 0 percent]
    D --> E[Record As Left Readings]
    E --> F{Compute Error at Each Point}
    F -->|All within Tolerance| G[Accept Issue Sticker PASS]
    F -->|Zero out of Tolerance| H[Adjust Zero Screw at 0 percent Input]
    F -->|Span out of Tolerance| I[Adjust Span Screw at 100 percent Input]
    H --> J[Re iterate Zero Span]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Re test Both Endpoints]
    K -->|Now within Tolerance| L[Accept Issue Sticker PASS]
    K -->|Still out of Tolerance| M[Reject Mark REPAIR and Re calibrate]
    G --> N[Archive in Calibration Database]
    L --> N
    M --> N
```

## 4.3 Calibration Hierarchy Pyramid (Traceability Chain)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Level 1 International Standards BIPM SI Units] --> L2[Level 2 National Standards NPL India NIST USA]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3 Primary Standards Lab Grade]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4 Secondary Working Standards Portable Calibrators]
    L4 --> L5[Level 5 Field Instrument Under Test UUT]
    L5 --> L6[Level 6 Process Measurement]
    L1 -.Uncertainty grows.-> L2
    L2 -.Uncertainty grows.-> L3
    L3 -.Uncertainty grows.-> L4
    L4 -.Uncertainty grows.-> L5
    L5 -.Measurement Output.-> L6
```

## 4.4 Static Calibration Test Setup (Pressure Transmitter)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SUP[Pressure Source Subsystem]
        DWT[Dead Weight Tester Piston and Weights] -->|Hydraulic Oil| ISO[Isolating Valve Manifold]
    end
    subgraph UUT_S[Instrument Under Test]
        ISO -->|Process Connection| PT[Pressure Transmitter PT 101]
    end
    subgraph READ[Readout Subsystem]
        PT -->|Current Loop 4 to 20 mA| PSU[24 V DC Power Supply]
        PT -->|Signal Lines| DMM[Digital Multimeter Read mA]
    end
    DWT -->|Certified Pressure Reference| CERT[Calibration Logbook]
    DMM -->|Measured Indication| CERT
    CERT --> OUT[Traceable Certificate and Sticker]
```

## 4.5 Zero-Span Adjustment Iteration Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start at 0 percent Input] --> A1[Adjust Zero until Output is 4 mA]
    A1 --> S1[Move to 100 percent Input]
    S1 --> A2[Adjust Span until Output is 20 mA]
    A2 --> S2[Return to 0 percent Input]
    S2 --> A3[Re check Zero is still 4 mA]
    A3 -->|Drift Detected| A1
    A3 -->|Stable| END[Calibration Complete]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compliance Check:** All node IDs are purely alphanumeric and prefixed with descriptive text (e.g., `PAD`, `UUT`, `READ`, `SUP`, `DWT`). No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone node names. All labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric ŌĆö no markdown bold or italics inside quoted labels.
