1. The orifice plate must be installed with the **flow direction arrow matching the fluid stream direction**; reversed installation doubles the indicated DP and produces large errors.
2. The plate face must be **perpendicular to the pipe axis** within 1┬░; any tilt causes a 1ŌĆō3 % bias in $C_d$.
3. The **plate bore edge must remain sharp** (edge radius < 0.0004 d); any damage alters $C_d$.
4. **Gaskets must not intrude** into the flow area; they should sit flat against the flange face and not extend into the bore.
5. For steam service, **condensate pots** must be installed in the impulse lines to protect the DP transmitter from water hammer.

**Part (b) ŌĆō Numerical Solution** (7 marks)

**Step 1 ŌĆō Compute $\beta$ and bore area (1 mark):**

$$
\beta = 0.625,\qquad d = \beta D = 0.625\times 0.250 = 0.15625\;\text{m}
$$

$$
A_{2} = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi(0.15625)^{2}}{4} = 1.918 \times 10^{-2}\;\text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆō Compute $C_d$ using the Stolz equation (2 marks):**

For flange taps and $Re_D$ unknown, we first **assume a typical $Re_D = 1 \times 10^{5}$** (a common KTU-board shortcut acceptable in a 7-mark sub-question) and verify afterwards.

$$
C_{d} = 0.5961 + 0.0261\beta^{2} - 0.216\beta^{8} + 0.000521\left(\dfrac{10^{6}\beta}{Re_{D}}\right)^{0.7}
$$

$$
\beta^{2} = 0.3906,\qquad \beta^{8} = 0.01483
$$

$$
\dfrac{10^{6}\beta}{Re_{D}} = \dfrac{10^{6}\times 0.625}{10^{5}} = 6.25
$$

$$
(6.25)^{0.7} = 3.71
$$

$$
C_{d} = 0.5961 + 0.0261(0.3906) - 0.216(0.01483) + 0.000521(3.71)
$$

$$
C_{d} = 0.5961 + 0.01019 - 0.00320 + 0.00193 = 0.6050
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆō Compute $Q$ (2 marks):**

$$
Q = C_{d}\,A_{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{2\,\Delta P}{\rho\,(1-\beta^{4})}}
$$

$$
1-\beta^{4} = 1-(0.625)^{4} = 1 - 0.1526 = 0.8474
$$

$$
\sqrt{\dfrac{2\times 12000}{996\times 0.8474}} = \sqrt{28.43} = 5.332\;\text{m/s}
$$

$$
Q = 0.6050 \times 1.918\times 10^{-2} \times 5.332 = 6.187 \times 10^{-2}\;\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q \approx 6.19 \times 10^{-2}\;\text{m}^{3}/\text{s} \;\;\approx\; 222.7\;\text{m}^{3}/\text{h}}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆō Compute $Re_D$ and verify the assumption (2 marks):**

Bulk velocity $v_{1} = Q/A_{1}$, with $A_{1} = \pi D^{2}/4 = 4.909 \times 10^{-2}$ m┬▓.

$$
v_{1} = \dfrac{6.187\times 10^{-2}}{4.909\times 10^{-2}} = 1.260\;\text{m/s}
$$

$$
Re_{D} = \dfrac{\rho v_{1} D}{\mu} = \dfrac{996 \times 1.260 \times 0.250}{8.0\times 10^{-4}} = 3.92 \times 10^{5}
$$

Since $Re_{D} = 3.92\times 10^{5}$ is much greater than the assumed $10^{5}$, the **Stolz correction term** becomes smaller (the last term falls from 0.00193 to Ōēł 0.00074), giving a refined $C_{d}\approx 0.6044$ ŌĆō a change of < 0.1 %. For exam purposes, the answer **Q Ōēł 0.0619 m┬│/s and Re_D Ōēł 3.92 ├Ś 10ŌüĄ** is accepted full-marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆō Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain with diagrams the **three standard orifice-plate geometries** (concentric, eccentric, segmental). For each, state **one specific service** in which it is the preferred choice. [7 marks]

**(b)** A natural-gas custody-transfer station uses a **D-and-D/2 tapped concentric orifice** in a pipe of internal diameter $D = 0.500\,\text{m}$. The gas at upstream conditions has density $\rho = 22.4\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$ and the indicated differential pressure is $\Delta P = 20\,\text{kPa}$. The orifice bore is $d = 0.300\,\text{m}$. Assuming the expansion factor $Y = 0.95$ and $C_d = 0.610$, compute (i) the **mass flow rate $\dot m$** and (ii) the **permanent pressure loss $\delta P$** in kPa. [7 marks]

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) ŌĆō Plate Geometries (7 marks)**

*(1 mark for sketch of each, 1 mark for service ŌĆō total 6 marks; 1 mark for overall comparison.)*

| Plate | Sketch Description | Preferred Service |
|---|---|---|
| **Concentric** | Circular bore centred on the pipe axis; sharp upstream edge. | **Clean steam, dry air, treated water** in refineries and power plants. |
| **Eccentric** | Circular bore offset by 0.98 D from pipe wall. Bore at the **bottom for liquid** service and at the **top for gas** service. | **Slurries, pulp, waste water, wet steam** ŌĆō prevents solids accumulation on the upstream face. |
| **Segmental** | Half-circular opening, chord = pipe diameter. | **Two-phase gasŌĆōliquid mixtures** and very dirty effluents. |

**Part (b) ŌĆō Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1 ŌĆō Compute $\beta$ and $A_2$ (1 mark):**

$$
\beta = \dfrac{d}{D} = \dfrac{0.300}{0.500} = 0.600
$$

$$
A_{2} = \dfrac{\pi(0.300)^{2}}{4} = 7.069\times 10^{-2}\;\text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆō Compute $Q$ with the expansion factor (2 marks):**

$$
Q = C_{d}\,A_{2}\,Y\sqrt{\dfrac{2\,\Delta P}{\rho\,(1-\beta^{4})}}
$$

$$
1-\beta^{4} = 1-(0.6)^{4} = 0.8704
$$

$$
\sqrt{\dfrac{2\times 20000}{22.4\times 0.8704}} = \sqrt{2052.6} = 45.31\;\text{m/s}
$$

$$
Q = 0.610 \times 7.069\times 10^{-2} \times 0.95 \times 45.31 = 1.854\;\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆō Compute mass flow rate $\dot m$ (2 marks):**

$$
\dot m = \rho\,Q = 22.4 \times 1.854 = 41.5\;\text{kg/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot m \approx 41.5\;\text{kg/s}}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆō Compute permanent pressure loss $\delta P$ (2 marks):**

$$
\delta P = \dfrac{(1-\beta^{4})\bigl(1-\beta^{4}(1-C_{d}^{2})\bigr)}{1+\beta^{4}}\;\Delta P
$$

$$
C_{d}^{2} = 0.3721
$$

$$
1-\beta^{4}(1-C_{d}^{2}) = 1-0.8704(1-0.3721) = 1-0.5470 = 0.4530
$$

$$
\text{Numerator factor} = (0.8704)(0.4530) = 0.3943
$$

$$
\text{Denominator factor} = 1+0.1296 = 1.1296
$$

$$
\delta P = \dfrac{0.3943}{1.1296}\times 20000 = 0.3491 \times 20000 = 6981\;\text{Pa} \approx 6.98\;\text{kPa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta P \approx 6.98\;\text{kPa}}
$$

**Engineering interpretation (carry 0 marks, but expected in viva):** About **35 %** of the indicated DP is permanently lost; the operator should size the upstream booster compressor accordingly.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas ŌĆō KTU 2024 Board Pattern**
> 1. **Forgetting the downstream straight-pipe length** (Ōēź 5 D). Many students write only the upstream length and lose 1ŌĆō2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing DŌĆōD/2 with D and D/4 taps.** DŌĆōD/2 means **one D upstream and half-D downstream** ŌĆō not a quarter-D.
> 3. **Ignoring the expansion factor $Y$** for gases. For air, steam, or natural gas, omitting $Y$ can introduce **3 ŌĆō 6 % error**, and examiners deduct 1 mark in a 7-marker.
> 4. **Using $C_d = 0.62$ universally.** The Stolz / ISO equation is mandatory when $Re_D$ is given, even if an approximate value is acceptable.
> 5. **Wrong units in the final answer.** Always state $Q$ in **m┬│/h or L/s** for water and **kg/s or kg/h** for gases ŌĆō not in m┬│/s without explicit conversion.
> 6. **Plate orientation for eccentric plates.** Examiners expect "bore at bottom for liquid, bore at top for gas" ŌĆō stating the wrong orientation loses 1 mark.
> 7. **Not stating the installation tolerances** (e.g., plate perpendicularity within 1┬░, edge radius < 0.0004 d). Vague answers such as "install correctly" are penalised.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Orifice installation = correct plate + correct taps + correct straight lengths + correct orientation + clean piping.**
- The **canonical flow equation** is $Q = C_{d}\,A_{2}\sqrt{2\Delta P/[\rho(1-\beta^{4})]}$ with $Y = 1$ for liquids and $Y < 1$ for compressible gases.
- **Flange taps** = 1ŌĆ│ (25.4 mm) up- and downstream ŌĆō used for small D clean services.
- **Corner taps** = exactly at plate faces ŌĆō used in research-grade accuracy work.
- **DŌĆōD/2 taps** = 1 D up, 0.5 D down ŌĆō mandatory for **AGA-3 / ISO 5167 custody-transfer gas metering**.
- **Vena-contracta taps** = 1 D up, variable down ŌĆō older practice, high-╬▓ preferred.
- **Minimum straight lengths:** 10 D up (no fittings), 18 D up (one elbow), 5 D down ŌĆō **always state the downstream length too**.
- **Concentric plate** ŌåÆ clean, single-phase fluids only.
- **Eccentric plate** ŌåÆ slurries and wet steam; **bore at bottom for liquids, bore at top for gases**.
- **Segmental plate** ŌåÆ two-phase mixtures and very dirty effluents.
- **Permanent pressure loss** Ōēł 35 ŌĆō 65 % of ╬öP for typical ╬▓; this is why orifice plates are avoided in low-pumping-head services.
- **Discharge coefficient $C_d$** is computed from the **Stolz equation** when ISO compliance is required; otherwise $C_d \approx 0.62$ is acceptable for short 7-mark problems.
- **Plate orientation** ŌĆō handle on top for horizontal pipe; flow-direction arrow on plate must match stream direction.
- **Tap impulse lines** ŌĆō slope at least 1:12, slope downward to transmitter for liquids, upward for gases; install **condensate pots for steam** and **dirt pots for dirty gases**.
- **KTU favourite one-liners for 2-mark questions:**
  1. "D and D/2 taps are recommended for large diameter pipes in custody transfer of natural gas."
  2. "The vena contracta is the point of minimum cross-sectional area of the fluid jet downstream of the orifice."
  3. "An eccentric orifice plate is preferred for measuring flow of liquids containing suspended solids."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pitot Tube
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT413 - PROCESS INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pitot Tube

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitot Tube:** A *Pitot tube* is a **flow measurement device** used to measure the **local fluid velocity** at a specific point in a flow stream by converting the **kinetic energy** of the flowing fluid into a **pressure head** (potential energy) that can be measured using a suitable manometer or pressure indicator.

The device operates on the principle of **converting velocity head (dynamic pressure) into pressure head (stagnation pressure)**. Mathematically, a Pitot tube measures the difference between:
- **Total (Stagnation) Pressure** ($P_0$) ŌĆö measured at the impact opening where fluid is brought to rest.
- **Static Pressure** ($P$) ŌĆö measured at a location where the fluid is flowing undisturbed.

The **dynamic pressure** is then $P_0 - P$, which is directly proportional to the square of the fluid velocity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** The Pitot tube was invented by French engineer **Henri Pitot in 1732**, originally to measure the flow velocity of the Seine River. It was later refined into the modern Pitot-static tube by French scientist **Henry Darcy** in the 19th century.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a strong wind and holding a cup facing into the wind. The wind pressure inside the cup is much higher than the side wind pressure. The Pitot tube works exactly the same way:
- The **impact (stagnation) opening** faces directly into the flow ŌåÆ fluid is forced to stop ŌåÆ its kinetic energy is fully converted to pressure energy.
- The **static opening** is on the side ŌåÆ it measures only the existing static pressure in the pipe (no kinetic energy contribution).
- The **difference** between these two pressures tells us how fast the fluid is moving.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Examples:**
> - Aircraft use Pitot tubes (combined with static ports) to measure **airspeed**.
> - In **chemical process plants**, they are used to measure local velocities in chimneys, ducts, and large pipes.
> - In **hydraulic engineering**, they estimate river and open-channel flow rates.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit (SI) | Other Common Units |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Fluid Velocity | $v$ | **m/s** | ft/s |
| Density of flowing fluid | $\rho$ | **kg/m┬│** | slug/ft┬│ |
| Density of manometric fluid | $\rho_m$ | **kg/m┬│** | slug/ft┬│ |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s┬▓** | 32.2 ft/s┬▓ |
| Stagnation (Total) Pressure | $P_0$ | **N/m┬▓ (Pa)** | psi, bar |
| Static Pressure | $P$ | **N/m┬▓ (Pa)** | psi, bar |
| Manometer Deflection | $h$ or $R$ | **m** | mm, cm, in |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle ŌĆö The "Why" and "How"

A Pitot tube measures velocity by exploiting the **principle of conservation of energy** in an ideal, incompressible, steady flow.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. **Stagnation Point Creation:** When the impact opening of the Pitot tube faces the flow, the fluid particle is brought to **rest** (velocity = 0) just at the mouth of the tube. This is the *stagnation point*.
2. **Energy Conversion:** By Bernoulli's principle, the kinetic energy of this fluid particle is **fully converted** into pressure energy at the stagnation point.
3. **Dual Pressure Measurement:** The Pitot (impact) opening registers **Total Pressure** ($P_0 = P + \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2$). A nearby **static pressure tap** registers only the **Static Pressure** ($P$).
4. **Differential Sensing:** A manometer connected across the two openings records a deflection $h$ proportional to the **dynamic pressure** $\frac{1}{2}\rho v^2$.
5. **Velocity Computation:** Rearranging gives $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_0 - P)}{\rho}}$.

### 2.2 Components of a Standard Pitot Tube

- **Impact (Pitot) Opening:** Faces the flow directly; measures stagnation pressure.
- **Static Openings:** Located on the side walls of the tube, parallel to flow; measure static pressure.
- **Stem:** Connects openings to the manometer.
- **Manometer (U-tube / Inclined / Differential Pressure Gauge):** Indicates the pressure difference.
- **Support / Mounting Flange:** Holds the tube at the desired location in the flow cross-section.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Types of Pitot Tubes (Frequently asked in KTU Exams):**
> 1. **Simple Pitot Tube** ŌĆö only stagnation opening; requires separate static pressure tap.
> 2. **Pitot-Static Tube (Combined)** ŌĆö both stagnation and static openings in one unit (e.g., Prandtl tube).
> 3. **S-Type Pitot Tube** ŌĆö used in dirty fluids; two separate tubes (impact + static).
> 4. **Annular Pitot Tube** ŌĆö averages velocity across the pipe; used in ducts.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL:** When writing manometer equations in KTU answer sheets, **never** write `|x|` directly inside markdown tables ŌĆö always use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` for absolute-value expressions.

| # | Formula Description | Mathematical Expression | Units | Boundary / Limiting Condition |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Velocity from pressure difference | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_0 - P)}{\rho}}$ | m/s | Incompressible, frictionless, along streamline |
| 2 | Velocity from stagnation head | $v = \sqrt{2g \, h_s}$ | m/s | $h_s$ = stagnation head in metres of flowing fluid |
| 3 | Velocity from U-tube manometer (heavier manometric fluid) | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2g \, h \, (\rho_m - \rho_f)}{\rho_f}}$ | m/s | $\rho_m > \rho_f$, $h$ = manometer reading |
| 4 | Velocity from inverted U-tube manometer (lighter manometric fluid) | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2g \, h \, (\rho_f - \rho_a)}{\rho_f}}$ | m/s | $\rho_a < \rho_f$ (e.g., air), $h$ = deflection |
| 5 | Discharge through pipe (using mean velocity $v_m$) | $Q = A \cdot v_m = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot v_m$ | m┬│/s | $D$ = pipe diameter, $A$ = cross-sectional area |
| 6 | Average velocity from centerline velocity | $v_{avg} = C \cdot v_{max} = 0.85 \, v_{max}$ (approx., turbulent) | m/s | $C$ Ōēł 0.85 for turbulent; Ōēł 0.5 for laminar |
| 7 | Reynolds number (to check flow regime) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | $Re > 4000$ ŌåÆ turbulent; $Re < 2000$ ŌåÆ laminar |
| 8 | Coefficient-based velocity | $v = C_v \sqrt{\dfrac{2g \, h \, (\rho_m - \rho_f)}{\rho_f}}$ | m/s | $C_v$ Ōēł 0.98 to 1.0 for well-designed tubes |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Airspeed indicators on aircraft rely on Pitot-static systems.
- **Power Plants:** Used to measure steam/gas flow in large ducts.
- **HVAC Industry:** Measures airflow in ventilation ducts.
- **Process Industries:** Local velocity profiling in reactors and large pipelines.
- **Meteorology:** Weather stations use Pitot-type anemometers.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is the Pitot tube preferred for large pipes?**
> Unlike orifice plates or venturimeters, the Pitot tube introduces **negligible pressure loss** in the flow stream ŌĆö making it ideal for applications where the flow disturbance must be minimal.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Velocity from Pressure Difference (Bernoulli's Equation)

**Assumptions:**
- Steady, incompressible, frictionless flow.
- Flow along a single streamline from point 1 (free stream) to point 2 (stagnation point).
- Elevation difference is negligible ($z_1 = z_2$).

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply Bernoulli's equation between point 1 (in the free stream) and point 2 (stagnation point at the impact opening):**

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the stagnation condition at point 2.** At the stagnation point, the fluid velocity becomes zero because the impact opening forces the fluid to come to rest:

$$
v_2 = 0
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the elevation equivalence.** Since the two points lie on the same horizontal streamline in the flow:

$$
z_1 = z_2
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute the conditions $v_2 = 0$ and $z_1 = z_2$ into Bernoulli's equation:**

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + 0
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Recognise that at point 1, the pressure is the static pressure $P$ and at point 2 it is the stagnation pressure $P_0$:**

$$
\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_0}{\rho g}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Rearrange to isolate the velocity term:**

$$
\frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_0 - P}{\rho g}
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Multiply both sides by $2g$ to obtain $v_1^2$:**

$$
v_1^2 = \frac{2(P_0 - P)}{\rho}
$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Take the positive square root (velocity is non-negative):**

$$
\boxed{\,v = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_0 - P)}{\rho}}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Where $P_0 - P$ is the dynamic pressure** of the flowing fluid. This equation is the **fundamental result** of the Pitot tube.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Velocity from U-tube Manometer Deflection

In a real experiment, the stagnation pressure $P_0$ and static pressure $P$ are sensed by a **U-tube manometer** filled with a heavier manometric fluid (e.g., mercury for water flow, or CClŌéä for air flow).

**Step 1 ŌĆö Express the pressure difference in terms of the manometer deflection $h$.** For a U-tube manometer with heavier fluid of density $\rho_m$ measuring the pressure difference across a fluid of density $\rho_f$:

$$
P_0 - P = g \cdot h \cdot (\rho_m - \rho_f)
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute this into the velocity equation from Derivation 1:**

$$
v^2 = \frac{2 \, [g \cdot h \cdot (\rho_m - \rho_f)]}{\rho_f}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Simplify by cancelling one factor of $g$:**

$$
v^2 = \frac{2 g h (\rho_m - \rho_f)}{\rho_f}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Take the square root to obtain the final Pitot tube equation with manometer:**

$$
\boxed{\,v = \sqrt{\frac{2 g h (\rho_m - \rho_f)}{\rho_f}}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most-used equation** in KTU numerical problems. Always verify that $\rho_m > \rho_f$ for a U-tube with heavier manometric fluid; otherwise, use the **inverted U-tube formula** for lighter manometric fluids (like oil measuring water flow).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Inverted U-tube Manometer Case (Lighter Manometric Fluid)

For an inverted U-tube manometer where the manometric fluid (density $\rho_a$) is **lighter** than the flowing fluid (density $\rho_f$):

**Step 1 ŌĆö Pressure balance for inverted U-tube:**

$$
P_0 - P = g \cdot h \cdot (\rho_f - \rho_a)
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute into the velocity expression:**

$$
v^2 = \frac{2(P_0 - P)}{\rho_f} = \frac{2 g h (\rho_f - \rho_a)}{\rho_f}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Final result:**

$$
\boxed{\,v = \sqrt{\frac{2 g h (\rho_f - \rho_a)}{\rho_f}}\,}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 ŌĆö Discharge Through a Pipe Using a Pitot Tube

**Step 1 ŌĆö Average velocity is related to centerline (maximum) velocity by a velocity coefficient $C$:**

$$
v_{avg} = C \cdot v_{max}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Discharge is the product of average velocity and pipe cross-sectional area:**

$$
Q = A \cdot v_{avg} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot C \cdot v_{max}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute $v_{max}$ from the Pitot tube formula:**

$$
\boxed{\,Q = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot C \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 g h (\rho_m - \rho_f)}{\rho_f}}\,}
$$

> [!TIP]
> For **turbulent flow** in smooth pipes, $C \approx 0.85$. For **laminar flow**, $C \approx 0.5$. Always check $Re$ before selecting $C$.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö KTU Problem-Solving Decision Tree

| Given in the Problem | Use This Formula | Key Substitute |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $P_0$, $P$, and $\rho_f$ | $v = \sqrt{2(P_0 - P)/\rho_f}$ | Direct substitution |
| $h$ and $\rho_m$ (heavier manometer) | $v = \sqrt{2gh(\rho_m - \rho_f)/\rho_f}$ | Confirm $\rho_m > \rho_f$ |
| $h$ and $\rho_a$ (lighter manometer) | $v = \sqrt{2gh(\rho_f - \rho_a)/\rho_f}$ | Confirm $\rho_a < \rho_f$ |
| Discharge $Q$ required with diameter $D$ | $Q = \frac{\pi}{4}D^2 \cdot C \cdot v_{max}$ | First find $v_{max}$ from above |
| Coefficient of velocity $C_v$ given | $v = C_v \sqrt{2gh(\rho_m - \rho_f)/\rho_f}$ | $C_v \approx 0.98$ŌĆō$1.0$ |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Pitot Tube Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Flowing Fluid Stream] --> B[Impact Opening of Pitot Tube]
    A --> C[Static Pressure Tap]
    B --> D[Stagnation Pressure P0]
    C --> E[Static Pressure P]
    D --> F[Differential Manometer]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Manometer Deflection h]
    G --> H[Velocity Computation v = sqrt 2g h rho m minus rho f divided by rho f]
    H --> I[Display or Recorder]
    I --> J[Process Control / Logging]
```

### 4.2 Detailed Constructional Block Diagram of a Pitot-Static Tube

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PipeWall[Pipe Wall with Inserted Pitot-Static Tube]
        direction TB
        A1[Impact Nose Facing Flow Direction] --> A2[Central Tube for Stagnation Pressure P0]
        A3[Static Holes on Side Walls] --> A4[Annular Space for Static Pressure P]
        A2 --> A5[Inner Stem Lead]
        A4 --> A6[Outer Stem Lead]
    end
    A5 --> B1[Manometer Limb 1]
    A6 --> B2[Manometer Limb 2]
    B1 --> C1[Heavier Manometric Fluid rho m]
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Deflection h in metres]
    D1 --> E1[Computation of Dynamic Pressure]
    E1 --> F1[Velocity v in m per sec]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Block Mapping ŌĆö Pressure Conversion in a Pitot Tube

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Kinetic Energy of Fluid] --> B[Conversion at Stagnation Point]
    B --> C[Pressure Energy Increase by rho v squared over 2]
    D[Static Pressure of Stream] --> E[Subtraction Block]
    C --> E
    E --> F[Dynamic Pressure P0 minus P]
    F --> G[Square Root and Scale Factor]
    G --> H[Velocity Output v]
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree for Selecting Pitot Tube Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Pitot Tube Type] --> B{Fluid Cleanliness}
    B -- Clean --> C[Prandtl or Combined Pitot-Static Tube]
    B -- Dirty or Wet --> D[S-Type Pitot Tube]
    C --> E{Pipe Size}
    E -- Small --> F[Prandtl Tube]
    E -- Large Duct --> G[Annular or Averaging Pitot]
    D --> H[Use Frequent Calibration]
    G --> I[Connect to Differential Manometer]
    F --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Compute v and Q]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The **impact opening** (always pointing upstream) gives the *total* pressure, while the **side openings** (parallel to flow) give the *static* pressure. The differential is the dynamic pressure that drives the manometer.
