## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define a Pitot tube and state the principle on which it works.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Pitot tube** is a device used to measure the **local velocity** of a flowing fluid. It works on the principle of **conversion of kinetic energy (velocity head) into pressure head (stagnation pressure)**. The device has two openings: an **impact opening** (facing the flow) that senses the **total (stagnation) pressure** $P_0$, and a **static opening** (on the side) that senses the **static pressure** $P$. The difference $P_0 - P$ is the **dynamic pressure**, which is proportional to $\frac{1}{2}\rho v^2$, allowing the velocity to be calculated as $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_0 - P)}{\rho}}$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**

**Q2. Why is a Pitot tube preferred for measuring flow in large pipes and ducts?**

**Model Answer:**

A Pitot tube is preferred in large pipes and ducts because:
1. It introduces **negligible pressure loss** in the flow stream (unlike orifice plates or venturimeters).
2. It is **simple in construction** and inexpensive.
3. It can measure **local velocity at any point** in the cross-section, enabling velocity profiling.
4. It is suitable for **dirty and corrosive fluids** when made of appropriate materials.
5. It has a **high turndown ratio** and reasonable accuracy (typically ┬▒1ŌĆō2% with calibration).

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]**

### ŌØō Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **Pitot-static tube**. Derive the expression for the velocity of flow in terms of manometer deflection. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Pitot tube is inserted in a pipe carrying oil of specific gravity **0.8** to measure the flow velocity. The stagnation pressure head recorded is **2.5 m of oil** and the static pressure head is **1.8 m of oil**. Calculate: **(i)** the velocity of oil flow, and **(ii)** the discharge in litres/second if the pipe diameter is **200 mm**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) ŌĆö Construction, Working, and Derivation:**

**Construction:** A Pitot-static tube consists of two concentric tubes:
- The **inner tube** has an **impact opening** at its tip facing the flow direction ŌĆö this senses the **stagnation (total) pressure** $P_0$.
- The **outer tube** has a ring of **small static holes** drilled perpendicular to the flow direction near the tip ŌĆö these sense the **static pressure** $P$.
- The two tubes are connected independently to a **differential manometer** that indicates the deflection $h$.

**Working Principle:** When placed in a flow stream, the impact opening brings the fluid to rest at the stagnation point, converting all its kinetic energy into pressure energy. The static holes, being parallel to the flow, register the ambient static pressure. The manometer measures the differential pressure.

**Derivation of Velocity Expression:**

Apply Bernoulli's equation between point 1 (in free stream) and point 2 (stagnation point at the impact opening):

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2
$$

At the stagnation point, $v_2 = 0$, and assuming $z_1 = z_2$:

$$
\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{v^2}{2g} = \frac{P_0}{\rho g}
$$

Solving for $v$:

$$
v = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_0 - P)}{\rho}}
$$

For a U-tube manometer with heavier manometric fluid (density $\rho_m$):

$$
P_0 - P = g h (\rho_m - \rho_f)
$$

Substituting:

$$
\boxed{\,v = \sqrt{\frac{2 g h (\rho_m - \rho_f)}{\rho_f}}\,}
$$

**Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Specific gravity of oil = $0.8 \Rightarrow \rho_f = 800 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Stagnation pressure head $h_0 = 2.5 \text{ m of oil}$
- Static pressure head $h_s = 1.8 \text{ m of oil}$
- Pipe diameter $D = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the dynamic head in metres of oil:**

$$
\Delta h = h_0 - h_s = 2.5 - 1.8 = 0.7 \text{ m of oil}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the dynamic pressure (Pa):**

$$
P_0 - P = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot \Delta h = 800 \times 9.81 \times 0.7
$$

$$
P_0 - P = 5493.6 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the velocity:**

$$
v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5493.6}{800}} = \sqrt{13.734} = 3.706 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the discharge:**

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.2)^2 = 0.0314 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
Q = A \times v = 0.0314 \times 3.706 = 0.1164 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Convert to litres/second:**

$$
Q = 0.1164 \times 1000 = \boxed{116.4 \text{ L/s}}
$$

---

### ŌØō Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö *Internal Choice Alternative*

**(a)** Explain the differences between a **simple Pitot tube** and a **Pitot-static tube**. Derive the expression for discharge through a pipe using a Pitot tube. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Pitot tube is used to measure the velocity of water flowing in a pipe. A differential U-tube manometer using **mercury** shows a deflection of **120 mm**. Calculate the velocity of water at the location of the Pitot tube. Take $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. If the pipe diameter is **150 mm**, calculate the discharge in **m┬│/s**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) ŌĆö Differences and Discharge Derivation:**

| Feature | Simple Pitot Tube | Pitot-Static Tube |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Openings | Only impact (stagnation) opening | Both impact and static openings |
| Static pressure | Requires **separate** static tap | Measured by the **same** device |
| Accuracy | Lower (static tap far from impact point) | Higher (both pressures at same location) |
| Use | Rarely used in modern practice | Standard in industry and aerospace |

**Derivation of Discharge:**

The average velocity $v_{avg}$ is related to the centerline (maximum) velocity $v_{max}$ by:

$$
v_{avg} = C \cdot v_{max}
$$

where $C \approx 0.85$ for turbulent flow.

The discharge is:

$$
Q = A \cdot v_{avg} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot C \cdot v_{max}
$$

Substituting $v_{max}$ from the Pitot equation:

$$
\boxed{\,Q = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot C \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 g h (\rho_m - \rho_f)}{\rho_f}}\,}
$$

**Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $h = 120 \text{ mm} = 0.12 \text{ m}$
- $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $D = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the velocity using the Pitot tube manometric formula:**

$$
v = \sqrt{\frac{2 g h (\rho_m - \rho_f)}{\rho_f}}
$$

$$
v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.12 \times (13600 - 1000)}{1000}}
$$

$$
v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.12 \times 12600}{1000}}
$$

$$
v = \sqrt{\frac{29669.04}{1000}} = \sqrt{29.669}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,v = 5.447 \text{ m/s}\,}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the pipe cross-sectional area:**

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.15)^2 = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the discharge (using centerline velocity as $v_{max}$, assume $C = 1$ for simplicity, or use $C = 0.85$ for average):**

Without coefficient:

$$
Q = A \cdot v = 0.01767 \times 5.447 = 0.0962 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

With coefficient $C = 0.85$:

$$
Q = 0.85 \times 0.0962 = 0.0818 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q \approx 0.0962 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \text{ (without coefficient)}\,}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mm to m:** The manometer reading $h$ must always be in **metres** before substitution. Writing $h = 120$ directly without converting leads to a 1ŌĆō2 mark deduction.
> 2. **Confusing which density to subtract:** Always subtract the *flowing fluid* density from the *manometric fluid* density. Mixing up $\rho_m - \rho_f$ with $\rho_f - \rho_m$ gives a negative under the square root and is a common blunder.
> 3. **Forgetting the coefficient of velocity $C$:** When discharge is asked, examiners expect you to apply $v_{avg} = C \cdot v_{max}$. Omitting $C$ (or using $C = 1$) loses 2ŌĆō3 marks unless the problem explicitly says "local velocity at the centerline".
> 4. **Wrong area formula:** Using $A = \pi D$ instead of $A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$ is a recurring error.
> 5. **Unit mismatch:** Always express $g = 9.81$ m/s┬▓ (not cm/s┬▓) when working in SI units.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist ŌĆö Must Memorise Before Exam:**

- Ō£ģ **Pitot tube** measures **local fluid velocity** by converting kinetic energy to pressure energy at a **stagnation point**.
- Ō£ģ **Impact opening** ŌåÆ measures **stagnation (total) pressure** $P_0$.
- Ō£ģ **Static opening** ŌåÆ measures **static pressure** $P$.
- Ō£ģ **Dynamic pressure** = $P_0 - P$ = $\frac{1}{2}\rho v^2$.
- Ō£ģ **Fundamental formula:** $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_0 - P)}{\rho}}$.
- Ō£ģ **With U-tube manometer (heavier fluid):** $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2gh(\rho_m - \rho_f)}{\rho_f}}$.
- Ō£ģ **With inverted U-tube (lighter fluid):** $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2gh(\rho_f - \rho_a)}{\rho_f}}$.
- Ō£ģ **Discharge formula:** $Q = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot C \cdot v_{max}$.
- Ō£ģ **Velocity coefficient** $C \approx 0.85$ for **turbulent**, $C \approx 0.5$ for **laminar** flow.
- Ō£ģ **Coefficient of Pitot tube** $C_v \approx 0.98$ to $1.0$ ŌĆö nearly ideal.
- Ō£ģ **Advantages:** minimal pressure loss, simple, suitable for large pipes and dirty fluids.
- Ō£ģ **Disadvantages:** measures only **point velocity**, clogging in dirty fluids, sensitive to alignment.
- Ō£ģ **Inventor:** Henri Pitot (1732); Modern Pitot-static by Henry Darcy.
- Ō£ģ **Always convert** $h$ to **metres** and densities to **kg/m┬│** before calculation.
- Ō£ģ **Check $\rho_m > \rho_f$** for U-tube and $\rho_a < \rho_f$ for inverted U-tube to avoid sign errors.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic ŌĆö "IPS PR":**
> **I**mpact = **P**ressure total (stagnation); **P**ipe wall tap = **Static**; **R**esult = Dynamic ŌåÆ **V**elocity!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Area Flow Meters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT413 - PROCESS INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Area Flow Meters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Area Flow Meter** (commonly known as a **Rotameter** or **Variable Area Flow Meter, VA Meter**) is a volumetric flow measurement device in which the **cross-sectional area of the flow stream varies** in response to the flow rate, while the **pressure differential across the meter remains essentially constant**. The instantaneous flow rate is indicated by the **vertical position of a free-floating element (the float)** inside a vertically mounted, precision-machined tapered tube.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Area flow meters are categorized under *Variable Head vs. Variable Area* meters. Unlike orifice plates, venturi tubes, or pitot-static probes (which maintain a constant area and vary the differential pressure), the rotameter **maintains a constant ╬öP and varies the area** ŌĆö this is the *defining conceptual inversion* examiners look for in two-mark definitional questions.

The standard construction consists of three principal elements:
1. A **graduated metering tube** (borosilicate glass, metal, or polymer-lined) with internal bore that widens linearly toward the top.
2. A **precision float** (density-calibrated, geometrically shaped) whose center-of-gravity lies below its geometric center for self-centering stability.
3. An **end-fitting assembly** that couples the meter to the process piping, often with process connections, packing glands, and magnetic coupling ports for electronic transmitters.

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant of the Device:** The float density (Žü_f), float geometry (V_f, A_f), and tube taper angle (typically **0.5┬░ to 6┬░**) are fixed by design. This makes the rotameter a **calibrated, direct-reading** device ŌĆö the operator simply reads the float height against the etched scale.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are standing under a **rain shower with a fixed water-pressure knob (constant ╬öP)**. If you partially cover the showerhead hole with your thumb, the water column shoots higher. As you gradually remove your thumb, the water column height drops. The **annular gap (the uncovered ring around your thumb)** behaves exactly like the rotameter's flow passage:

- **Small annular gap ŌåÆ small area ŌåÆ low flow**
- **Large annular gap ŌåÆ large area ŌåÆ high flow**

Now, replace your thumb with a **floating ball inside a glass cone**. The water pushes the ball up, the ball uncovers a wider passage beneath it, and it settles where the upward hydraulic force exactly balances its weight (minus buoyancy). The **height of the ball on the scale IS the flow rate**. This is the rotameter in one sentence.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Units

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit | Notes |
|----------|--------|---------------|-------|
| Volumetric flow rate | Q | **m┬│/h** (industrial), L/min (lab) | Direct reading |
| Float density | Žü_f | **kg/m┬│** | Typically 7800 to 19000 (metallic), 2000 to 2800 (plastic) |
| Fluid density | Žü | **kg/m┬│** | Reference: **1000 kg/m┬│** (water at 4┬░C) |
| Gravitational acceleration | g | **9.80665 m/s┬▓** | Standard |
| Float volume | V_f | **m┬│** | Geometric quantity |
| Float cross-sectional area | A_f | **m┬▓** | Maximum projected area |
| Annular flow area | A_h | **m┬▓** | Variable; depends on float height |
| Discharge coefficient | C_d | dimensionless (typically **0.6 to 0.98**) | Function of float geometry & Reynolds number |
| Tube taper half-angle | ╬▒ | degrees or radians | Typically **2┬░ to 6┬░** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **discharge coefficient C_d** is *not* a constant in the strict sense ŌĆö it varies with float geometry and Reynolds number. For exam purposes, however, it is often treated as a constant for a given float design.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic ŌĆö Step by Step

The working principle is grounded in a **dynamic force balance** on the float at equilibrium.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Initial State (Q = 0):**
The float rests at the bottom seat. Buoyancy and weight are unbalanced; gravity wins. Annular area A_h is essentially zero (the float seals against the seat).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Flow Onset:**
Fluid enters the bottom, accelerates through the annular gap between float and tube wall. The pressure drop across the float creates a **net upward hydraulic force**.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Hydraulic Forces Acting on the Float:**
- **Drag force (F_D):** Resulting from momentum transfer as fluid deflects around the float.
- **Buoyancy effect (F_B'):** The pressure difference (╬öP) acts over the float's maximum cross-section A_f, generating an additional upward force = ╬öP ├Ś A_f.
- **Form drag from float profile:** Critical for sharp-edged (skirted) floats.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Gravitational Force (downward):**
$$F_g = V_f \times \rho_f \times g$$

This is partially offset by classical buoyancy (Archimedes):
$$F_b = V_f \times \rho \times g$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Equilibrium Condition (Floating Position):**
The float rises until all upward forces equal the effective downward weight:
$$F_D + F_B' = F_g - F_b$$
$$\Delta P \times A_f + F_D = V_f \times g \times (\rho_f - \rho)$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Geometric Constraint (Tapered Tube):**
As the float rises by height *h*, the annular area increases linearly:
$$A_h = A_f + h \times \pi \times D_t \times \tan(\alpha)$$

where D_t is the tube diameter at the float's maximum cross-section and ╬▒ is the half-angle of taper.

**Step 7 ŌĆö Flow Continues Until Equilibrium:**
For any given Q, the float finds a unique equilibrium height *h* such that the energy dissipation (Bernoulli + drag) equals the effective weight differential. This **h is the visual output of the meter**.

### 2.2 The Core Derivation ŌĆö Governing Flow Equation

Starting from Bernoulli's equation between inlet (point 1, below float) and the annular throat (point 2, at float equator), with the float treated as a submerged bluff body:

$$P_1 + \frac{1}{2} \rho v_1^2 + \rho g z_1 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2} \rho v_2^2 + \rho g z_2 + \Delta P_{loss}$$

For an ideal incompressible flow and assuming the velocity in the tube above the float is the same as the approach velocity:

$$\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho v_2^2 \left(1 - \left(\frac{A_f}{A_h}\right)^2\right)$$

The **continuity equation** at the throat:
$$Q = A_h \times v_2$$

Substituting:
$$\Delta P = \frac{1}{2} \rho \left(\frac{Q}{A_h}\right)^2 \left(1 - \left(\frac{A_f}{A_h}\right)^2\right)$$

Equating this to the force-balance expression:
$$V_f \times g \times (\rho_f - \rho) = \frac{1}{2} \rho \left(\frac{Q}{A_h}\right)^2 \left(1 - \left(\frac{A_f}{A_h}\right)^2\right) A_f$$

Solving for Q with the discharge coefficient C_d to account for viscous and form losses:

$$\boxed{Q = C_d \times A_h \sqrt{\frac{2 \times g \times V_f \times (\rho_f - \rho)}{\rho \times A_f \times \left(1 - (A_f/A_h)^2\right)}}}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula No. | Equation | Engineering Utility |
|-------------|----------|---------------------|
| F-1 | $Q = C_d A_h \sqrt{2 g V_f (\rho_f - \rho) / [\rho A_f (1-(A_f/A_h)^2)]}$ | Master flow equation ŌĆö **most important** for 7/14-mark derivations |
| F-2 | $A_h = A_f + h \pi D_t \tan(\alpha)$ | Geometry of taper ŌĆö relates float height *h* to annular area |
| F-3 | $\Delta P_{float} = V_f (\rho_f - \rho) g / A_f$ | Constant pressure drop across float (valid for high h) |
| F-4 | $Q_{actual} = Q_{water} \sqrt{(\rho_{water}/\rho_{fluid}) \times ((\rho_f - \rho_{fluid})/(\rho_f - \rho_{water}))}$ | **Density correction** for fluids other than calibration fluid |
| F-5 | $Re = \rho v D_e / \mu = 4 \rho Q / (\pi D_e \mu)$ | Reynolds number for viscosity-effect correction |
| F-6 | $v_{throat} = Q / A_h$ | Annular throat velocity (continuity) |
| F-7 | $h_{float} = (A_h - A_f) / (\pi D_t \tan \alpha)$ | Float height position (inverse of F-2) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition Simplification:** For tall rotameters where A_h >> A_f, the term $(1 - (A_f/A_h)^2) \to 1$, and the equation simplifies to:
> $$Q \approx C_d A_h \sqrt{2 g V_f (\rho_f - \rho) / (\rho A_f)}$$
> This **simplified form** is the version most often expected in KTU examinations.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Area flow meters are the **workhorses of low-to-medium flow applications** in industries where simplicity, cost, and direct visual reading outweigh the need for high accuracy. Typical deployment domains:

- **Chemical & Petrochemical Plants:** Bypass indicators on main process lines, dosing lines, additive injection.
- **Pharmaceutical & Biotech:** Sterile, sanitary-grade (3-A, EHEDG) rotameters with CIP/SIP compatibility for clean-in-place operations.
- **Water & Wastewater Treatment:** Chlorine gas dosing, polymer injection, aeration tank air measurement.
- **HVAC & Building Services:** Chilled water flow, hot water recirculation loops.
- **Food & Beverage:** Milk, beer, syrup lines ŌĆö hygienic designs with polished internals.
- **Laboratory & Pilot Plants:** Quick set-up, no power requirement, direct read.

The integrated **electronic transmitters** (with HART, 4-20 mA, or Foundation Fieldbus outputs) have extended the rotameter's relevance into modern DCS architectures, where the float's magnetic field is sensed by a Hall-effect or magnetoresistive sensor coupled externally through the non-magnetic tube wall.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Master Flow Equation

This derivation is the **most frequently asked 7-mark analytical problem** in KTU examinations. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly.

**Given:**
A rotameter with float of mass m_f, volume V_f, maximum cross-sectional area A_f, and density Žü_f is immersed in a flowing fluid of density Žü. The float reaches equilibrium at height h, exposing an annular area A_h. The discharge coefficient is C_d.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply Force Balance at Equilibrium:**

The three forces acting on the float are:
- Downward: True weight of float = $W = m_f g = V_f \rho_f g$
- Upward: Buoyancy due to displaced fluid = $F_b = V_f \rho g$
- Upward: Net hydraulic force from pressure drop = $F_P = \Delta P \times A_f$

At equilibrium, the upward forces equal the downward force:
$$\Delta P \times A_f = V_f g \rho_f - V_f g \rho$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Isolate ╬öP:**

$$\Delta P = \frac{V_f g (\rho_f - \rho)}{A_f}$$

This shows that **╬öP is a constant** for a given float design and fluid ŌĆö a defining property of variable-area meters.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply Bernoulli's Equation:**

Between a point just below the float (point 1) and the annular throat at the float's equator (point 2):

$$P_1 + \frac{1}{2} \rho v_1^2 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2} \rho v_2^2$$

(The elevation terms cancel as both points are essentially at the same height, and we ignore losses for now.)

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply Continuity:**

$$Q = A_h v_2 = A_1 v_1$$

$$v_1 = \frac{Q}{A_1}, \quad v_2 = \frac{Q}{A_h}$$

But $A_1 = A_h + A_f$ (tube cross-section is annular area plus float area), so:
$$v_1 = \frac{Q}{A_h + A_f}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Subtract Pressures:**

$$P_1 - P_2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho (v_2^2 - v_1^2)$$

$$\Delta P = \frac{1}{2} \rho \left[ \left(\frac{Q}{A_h}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{Q}{A_h + A_f}\right)^2 \right]$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Factor the Right-Hand Side:**

$$\Delta P = \frac{1}{2} \rho Q^2 \left[ \frac{1}{A_h^2} - \frac{1}{(A_h + A_f)^2} \right]$$

Common-denominator manipulation:
$$\Delta P = \frac{1}{2} \rho Q^2 \left[ \frac{(A_h + A_f)^2 - A_h^2}{A_h^2 (A_h + A_f)^2} \right]$$

Expanding the numerator:
$$(A_h + A_f)^2 - A_h^2 = A_h^2 + 2 A_h A_f + A_f^2 - A_h^2 = 2 A_h A_f + A_f^2$$

Factor out A_f:
$$= A_f (2 A_h + A_f)$$

Therefore:
$$\Delta P = \frac{1}{2} \rho Q^2 \times \frac{A_f (2 A_h + A_f)}{A_h^2 (A_h + A_f)^2}$$

Note that $(2 A_h + A_f) = (A_h + A_f) + A_h$. For the dominant term when $A_h \gg A_f$:

**Step 7 ŌĆö Alternative Simplified Form:**

The classic form (used in textbooks) arises by directly equating the kinetic energy of the jet emerging from the annular area to the work done by the float:

$$\Delta P = \frac{1}{2} \rho v_2^2 \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{A_f}{A_h}\right)^2 \right]$$

This is the form most commonly tested. Derivation: applying Bernoulli between a point in the tube at the float equator (with effective area A_h + A_f) and just past the float.

**Step 8 ŌĆö Equate the Two ╬öP Expressions:**

$$\frac{V_f g (\rho_f - \rho)}{A_f} = \frac{1}{2} \rho v_2^2 \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{A_f}{A_h}\right)^2 \right]$$

**Step 9 ŌĆö Solve for Velocity v_2:**

$$v_2^2 = \frac{2 V_f g (\rho_f - \rho)}{\rho A_f \left[ 1 - (A_f/A_h)^2 \right]}$$

**Step 10 ŌĆö Introduce Discharge Coefficient and Convert to Q:**

The real-world flow experiences viscous and form losses, so we insert C_d:
$$v_2 = C_d \sqrt{ \frac{2 V_f g (\rho_f - \rho)}{\rho A_f \left[ 1 - (A_f/A_h)^2 \right]} }$$

Finally, multiply by A_h:
$$\boxed{Q = C_d \cdot A_h \sqrt{ \frac{2 V_f g (\rho_f - \rho)}{\rho A_f \left[ 1 - (A_f/A_h)^2 \right]} }}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A rotameter has a float of volume 8.0 cm┬│ and maximum cross-sectional area 1.2 cm┬▓. The float density is 7840 kg/m┬│ (carbon steel), and the fluid is air at standard conditions (Žü = 1.225 kg/m┬│). At a certain flow rate, the float stabilizes at a position where the annular area is 4.5 cm┬▓. If C_d = 0.98, determine the volumetric flow rate. Neglect the term (A_f/A_h)┬▓.

**Solution:**

**Given:**
- V_f = 8.0 cm┬│ = 8.0 ├Ś 10Ōü╗ŌüČ m┬│
- A_f = 1.2 cm┬▓ = 1.2 ├Ś 10Ōü╗Ōü┤ m┬▓
- A_h = 4.5 cm┬▓ = 4.5 ├Ś 10Ōü╗Ōü┤ m┬▓
- Žü_f = 7840 kg/m┬│
- Žü = 1.225 kg/m┬│
- C_d = 0.98
- g = 9.81 m/s┬▓

**Check the assumption** (A_f/A_h)┬▓ = (1.2/4.5)┬▓ = (0.2667)┬▓ = 0.0711 ŌĆö not negligible, but we follow the problem's instruction.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the denominator term:**
$$1 - (A_f/A_h)^2 = 1 - 0.0711 = 0.9289$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the numerator inside the square root:**
$$2 \times V_f \times g \times (\rho_f - \rho) = 2 \times 8.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 9.81 \times (7840 - 1.225)$$

$$= 2 \times 8.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 9.81 \times 7838.775$$

$$= 2 \times 8.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 76918.2$$

$$= 1.2307 \text{ N (numerator force term)}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the full bracket under the square root:**
$$\frac{2 V_f g (\rho_f - \rho)}{\rho A_f [1 - (A_f/A_h)^2]} = \frac{1.2307}{1.225 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.9289}$$

$$= \frac{1.2307}{1.3654 \times 10^{-4}} = 9014.0$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Take the square root:**
$$\sqrt{9014.0} = 94.94 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute Q:**
$$Q = C_d \times A_h \times 94.94 = 0.98 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 94.94$$

$$Q = 0.98 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 94.94 = 0.04186 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q \approx 150.7 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Sanity check:** This is a reasonable value for air flow in an industrial rotameter of this size, confirming the calculation.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Design Trade-offs

| Parameter | Effect of Increasing Parameter | Design Implication |
|-----------|-------------------------------|---------------------|
| Float density (Žü_f) | Increases maximum measurable flow | Limits use to higher-density fluids |
| Float volume (V_f) | Increases differential pressure ŌåÆ stability | Larger floats = stiffer meter |
| Tube taper angle (╬▒) | Greater area per unit height ŌåÆ higher turn-down | Too steep = poor low-flow sensitivity |
| Tube length | Wider flow range (turn-down ratio 10:1) | Vertical space requirement |
| Fluid viscosity | Reduces C_d, shifts calibration | Requires viscosity correction curves |
| Float shape | Edge profile affects C_d stability | Sharp-edged floats have better C_d |

### 3.4 Density Correction Formula ŌĆö Detailed Derivation

When a rotameter is calibrated with water but used for another fluid, the indicated flow Q_w must be corrected:

From F-1, holding A_h and C_d fixed:
$$Q \propto \sqrt{\frac{(\rho_f - \rho)}{\rho}}$$

Therefore:
$$\frac{Q_{fluid}}{Q_{water}} = \sqrt{\frac{(\rho_f - \rho_{fluid})/\rho_{fluid}}{(\rho_f - \rho_{water})/\rho_{water}}}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{fluid} = Q_{water} \sqrt{\frac{\rho_{water}(\rho_f - \rho_{fluid})}{\rho_{fluid}(\rho_f - \rho_{water})}}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Rotameter with Electronic Transmitter

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **functional flow architecture** of a modern industrial rotameter system, mapping the signal path from process fluid to DCS/SCADA display. This satisfies the KTU 2024 schematic-rendering requirement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PIPEIN["PROCESS INLET PIPE"] --> STRAIGHT1["INLET STRAENER 5D MIN"]
    STRAIGHT1 --> TUBE["TAPERED METERING TUBE"]
    TUBE --> FLOAT["FLOAT RISES TO EQUILIBRIUM H"]
    FLOAT --> ANNULAR["ANNULAR FLOW AROUND FLOAT"]
    ANNULAR --> TUBE2["TUBE CONTINUES UPWARD"]
    TUBE2 --> STRAIGHT2["OUTLET STRAENER 3D MIN"]
    STRAIGHT2 --> PIPEOUT["PROCESS OUTLET PIPE"]

    FLOAT --> MAGFLD["FLOAT CARRIES PERMANENT MAGNET"]
    MAGFLD --> HALL["HALL EFFECT SENSOR OUTSIDE TUBE"]
    HALL --> SIGCON["SIGNAL CONDITIONER 4 TO 20 MA"]
    SIGCON --> HART["HART MODEM DIGITAL OVERLAY"]
    SIGCON --> DCS["DCS OR PLC INPUT CARD"]
    HART --> HANDHELD["HART HANDHELD CONFIGURATOR"]

    SIGCON --> LCD["LOCAL LCD DISPLAY LPH OR PERCENT"]
    LCD --> OPENGUY["OPERATOR READS DIRECTLY"]

    classDef process fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef meter fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#663300,color:#000
    classDef elec fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#003300,color:#000
    classDef ctl fill:#fff0f0,stroke:#660000,color:#000

    class PIPEIN,STRAIGHT1,TUBE2,STRAIGHT2,PIPEOUT,ANNULAR process
    class TUBE,FLOAT,MAGFLD meter
    class HALL,SIGCON,HART,LCD elec
    class DCS,HANDHELD,OPENGUY ctl
```

### 4.2 Force-Balance Free-Body Diagram (Conceptual Mermaid)

This second diagram maps the **force interactions** on the float at equilibrium ŌĆö a core element of derivation-based questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FW["WEIGHT W DOWNWARD\nW EQUALS Vf RHOF G"] --> FBAL["FLOAT IN EQUILIBRIUM"]
    FB["BUOYANCY FB UPWARD\nFB EQUALS Vf RHO G"] --> FBAL
    FPH["HYDRAULIC FORCE FPH UPWARD\nFPH EQUALS DELTA P TIMES AF"] --> FBAL
    FD["DRAG FORCE FD UPWARD\nFD EQUALS CD FORM DRAG"] --> FBAL

    FBAL --> EQ["FORCE BALANCE\nDELTA P AF PLUS FD\nEQUALS Vf G RHOF MINUS RHO"]

    EQ --> HREL["FLOAT HEIGHT H\nDETERMINES ANNULAR AREA AH"]
    HREL --> QREL["VOLUMETRIC FLOW Q\nEQUALS CD AH VTHROAT"]

    classDef force fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#663300,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#003300,color:#000
    classDef q fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000

    class FW,FB,FPH,FD force
    class FBAL,HREL,EQ out
    class QREL q
```

### 4.3 Float Geometry Catalog (Schematic Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1["BALL FLOAT\nSIMPLE LOW COST\nMODERATE CD"]
    F2["CYLINDRICAL FLOAT\nHIGH STABILITY\nGOOD CD"]
    F3["ELIPSOIDAL FLOAT\nSELF CENTERING\nWIDELY USED"]
    F4["SKIRTED FLOAT\nSHARP EDGES\nBEST CD 0.95 PLUS"]
    F5["PLUNGER PISTON\nHIGH PRESSURE\nMETAL TUBES"]

    F1 --> APP1["GAS AND CLEAN LIQUID LOW FLOW"]
    F2 --> APP2["INDUSTRIAL LIQUID GENERAL PURPOSE"]
    F3 --> APP3["PHARMA AND FOOD SANITARY"]
    F4 --> APP4["HIGH ACCURACY METAL TUBE METERS"]
    F5 --> APP5["STEAM AND HIGH TEMP SERVICES"]

    classDef float fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600,color:#000
    classDef app fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    class F1,F2,F3,F4,F5 float
    class APP1,APP2,APP3,APP4,APP5 app
```

### 4.4 Installation Requirements ŌĆö Physical Layout Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1["MOUNT VERTICALLY\nTOLERANCE PLUS MINUS 5 DEG"]
    R2["INLET STRAIGHT PIPE 5D MIN\nNO VALVE OR ELBOW NEAR INLET"]
    R3["OUTLET STRAIGHT PIPE 3D MIN\nFREE DISCHARGE OK"]
    R4["FLOW DIRECTION UPWARD\nALWAYS BOTTOM TO TOP"]
    R5["ISOLATION VALVES UPSTREAM\nAND DOWNSTREAM"]
    R6["BYPASS LINE WITH VALVES\nFOR MAINTENANCE"]
    R7["RELIEF TO ATMOSPHERE\nFOR STEAM OR GAS IF GLASS TUBE"]
    R8["SUPPORT BRACKET\nFOR VIBRATION PROTECTION"]

    R1 --> SUCC["SUCCESSFUL INSTALLATION"]
    R2 --> SUCC
    R3 --> SUCC
    R4 --> SUCC
    R5 --> SUCC
    R6 --> SUCC
    R7 --> SUCC
    R8 --> SUCC

    classDef req fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#7f2704,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#d6fce4,stroke:#046327,color:#000
    class R1,R2,R3,R4,R5,R6,R7,R8 req
    class SUCC out
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
Define a *Variable Area Flow Meter*. How does it differ fundamentally from a *Variable Head* flow meter?

**Model Answer:**

A Variable Area Flow Meter (Area Flow Meter or Rotameter) is a volumetric flow measurement device in which the **flow area varies** with flow rate while the **pressure drop across the meter remains essentially constant**. A float inside a tapered tube rises to an equilibrium position where the upward hydraulic force balances the float's effective weight; this height is the flow rate indicator.

**Fundamental Difference:**

| Aspect | Variable Area Meter | Variable Head Meter (e.g., orifice) |
|--------|---------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Flow area | **Varies** with flow | Constant (fixed orifice) |
| Pressure drop | **Constant** (Ōēł constant) | Varies with square of flow |
| Output | Float **height** | Differential **pressure** |
| Calibration | Direct reading | Square-root extraction required |
| Typical accuracy | ┬▒2% to ┬▒6% FS | ┬▒1% to ┬▒3% FS |

In essence, the rotameter is the **conceptual dual** of the orifice plate ŌĆö what one holds constant, the other varies. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]**
State the **role of the discharge coefficient (C_d)** in a rotameter. What is its typical range, and what physical factors does it depend upon?

**Model Answer:**

The discharge coefficient **C_d** is a dimensionless correction factor that accounts for the **deviation of real flow from the idealized Bernoulli prediction** when fluid passes around the float. It lumps together viscous losses, form (pressure) drag, turbulence, and vena-contracta effects into a single empirical multiplier applied to the theoretical velocity.

**Typical Range:** C_d = **0.60 to 0.98** for commercial rotameters. Sharp-edged *skirted floats* achieve the highest C_d (>0.95); smooth ball floats have lower values.

**Physical Dependencies:**
1. **Float geometry** ŌĆö sharp edges give higher C_d; rounded floats give lower.
2. **Reynolds number** of the flow at the throat ŌĆö C_d varies with Re, particularly below Re Ōēł 10Ōü┤.
3. **Fluid viscosity** ŌĆö higher viscosity reduces C_d; very viscous fluids require viscosity-corrected calibration curves.
4. **Surface roughness** of float and tube.
5. **Float eccentricity** ŌĆö float must be self-centering for stable C_d.

**(3 marks)**

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working principle** of a variable area flow meter. Discuss the role of each major component and indicate the typical materials of construction.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the **governing flow equation** for a rotameter:
$$Q = C_d A_h \sqrt{ \frac{2 g V_f (\rho_f - \rho)}{\rho A_f [1 - (A_f/A_h)^2]} }$$

State clearly the assumptions made during the derivation.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**Constructional Details:**

A variable area flow meter consists of:

1. **Tapered Metering Tube:**
   - Precision-bored, internal bore increases linearly upward.
   - Materials: **borosilicate glass** (low-pressure, transparent applications), **stainless steel** (SS 316, high pressure/temperature), **PTFE-lined metal** (corrosive chemicals), **polycarbonate** (low-cost, lab use).
   - Graduated scale etched or printed on the outside.

2. **Float (Plummet):**
   - Precision-machined, density-calibrated element.
   - Common shapes: **ball, ellipsoidal, cylindrical, skirted (sharp-edged)**.
   - Materials: **SS 316, titanium, Hastelloy, PTFE, aluminium, ceramic (zirconia)**.
   - Contains a permanent magnet (rare-earth Nd-Fe-B) for electronic coupling.
   - Self-centering geometry ensures stable position along tube axis.

3. **End Fittings:**
   - Inlet and outlet connections: flanged (ANSI/DIN), threaded (NPT/BSP), or tri-clamp (sanitary).
   - Packing glands for high-pressure metal tubes.
   - Bleed/vent ports for gas service.

4. **Optional Electronic Transmitter:**
   - Magnetic coupling to external Hall-effect or reed-switch sensor.
   - 4-20 mA + HART output.
   - Local LCD display.

**Working Principle:**

When fluid flows upward through the tube, it encounters the float. The pressure drop across the float creates a **net upward hydraulic force** = ╬öP ├Ś A_f, while gravity (weight minus buoyancy) pulls it down. The float rises until equilibrium:

$$V_f g (\rho_f - \rho) = \Delta P \times A_f$$

The tapered geometry ensures that for higher flow rates, the float rises to a position with a **larger annular area**, which increases flow capacity while maintaining nearly constant ╬öP. Each float position corresponds uniquely to one flow rate, allowing **direct visual reading** off the scale or electronic transmission via the magnetic sensor.

The meter is always installed **vertically with flow upward**. Inlet straight pipe of at least **5 pipe diameters** and outlet of **3 diameters** are required to avoid swirl-induced measurement error.

**(7 marks ŌĆö full credit requires labelled diagram, materials, and explicit force discussion)**

---

**(b) Derivation ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady, incompressible, single-phase flow.
2. Float is in static equilibrium (negligible oscillation).
3. Pressure acts uniformly over the maximum cross-section A_f of the float.
4. Vena contracta and form losses are lumped into C_d.
5. Elevation difference between inlet and throat is negligible.
6. Fluid is Newtonian with constant Žü and ╬╝.

**Derivation (full step-by-step as in Section 3.1):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Force balance on float at equilibrium:**

The downward weight is $W = V_f \rho_f g$. The upward buoyancy is $F_b = V_f \rho g$. The net upward hydraulic force is $F_P = \Delta P \cdot A_f$. The drag force on the float is $F_D$. Equilibrium:

$$\Delta P \cdot A_f + F_D = V_f g (\rho_f - \rho)$$

For ideal conditions with the drag lumped into C_d:

$$\Delta P \cdot A_f = V_f g (\rho_f - \rho) \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Bernoulli between inlet (point 1) and annular throat (point 2):**

$$P_1 + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho v_1^2 = P_2 + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho v_2^2$$

$$\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho (v_2^2 - v_1^2) \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply Continuity:**

$$Q = A_1 v_1 = A_h v_2 \implies v_1 = \frac{Q}{A_h + A_f}, \quad v_2 = \frac{Q}{A_h}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute into (2):**

$$\Delta P = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho Q^2 \left[ \frac{1}{A_h^2} - \frac{1}{(A_h + A_f)^2} \right] = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho \left(\frac{Q}{A_h}\right)^2 \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{A_f}{A_h}\right)^2 \right]$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Equate (1) and (2) per unit area, solve for Q:**

$$V_f g (\rho_f - \rho) = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho \left(\frac{Q}{A_h}\right)^2 A_f \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{A_f}{A_h}\right)^2 \right]$$

$$Q^2 = \frac{2 g V_f (\rho_f - \rho) A_h^2}{\rho A_f [1 - (A_f/A_h)^2]}$$

$$Q = A_h \sqrt{ \frac{2 g V_f (\rho_f - \rho)}{\rho A_f [1 - (A_f/A_h)^2]} }$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Insert discharge coefficient C_d to account for real losses:**

$$\boxed{Q = C_d A_h \sqrt{ \frac{2 g V_f (\rho_f - \rho)}{\rho A_f [1 - (A_f/A_h)^2]} }}$$

**(7 marks ŌĆö full credit requires all 6 steps, every assumption explicitly stated, and the boxed final equation)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks ŌĆö Alternative Choice)

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare the construction and performance characteristics of **glass-tube** versus **metal-tube** rotameters. List at least **three advantages and three limitations** of variable area meters in industrial use.

**(b) [7 marks]** A rotameter is calibrated for water at 25┬░C (Žü_w = 997 kg/m┬│). The float has volume 12 cm┬│ and density 7900 kg/m┬│. At a float height corresponding to an annular area of 5.0 cm┬▓, the meter reads 600 L/h of water. Determine the **actual flow rate** when the same meter is used to measure kerosene at 25┬░C (Žü_k = 780 kg/m┬│) at the same float height. Assume the discharge coefficient is unchanged.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Glass vs Metal Tube Rotameters ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

| Feature | Glass-Tube Rotameter | Metal-Tube Rotameter |
|---------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Tube material | Borosilicate glass | SS 316, Hastelloy, Inconel |
| Pressure rating | Low (typically Ōēż 6 bar) | High (up to 100+ bar) |
| Temperature rating | Ōēż 200┬░C | Ōēż 400┬░C (with thermal isolation) |
| Visual reading | Direct (transparent) | Indirect (magnetic coupling, electronic) |
| Corrosion resistance | Moderate (glass to most chemicals) | High (selectable alloy) |
| Cost | Low | Medium to high |
| Safety on rupture | Brittle failure ŌåÆ high hazard | Robust, fails gracefully |
| Output | Visual only (standard) | 4-20 mA + HART (standard) |
| Typical fluids | Water, air, low-pressure gases | Steam, hydrocarbons, high-T chemicals |
| Application | Lab, pilot, low-risk services | Refineries, power plants, chemical plants |

**Advantages of Variable Area Meters (any 3):**
1. **Direct visual reading** ŌĆö no power, no signal conditioning required.
2. **No straight pipe run** required upstream (5D only) ŌĆö compact installation.
3. **Self-cleaning** to some extent ŌĆö float wipes the tube wall.
4. **Low pressure drop** ŌĆö typically 0.1 to 1.0 kPa.
5. **Wide turn-down ratio** ŌĆö typically 10:1.
6. **Simple calibration** ŌĆö direct scale reading.

**Limitations (any 3):**
1. **Must be installed vertically** with flow upward ŌĆö restricts layout.
2. **Accuracy is limited** ŌĆö typically ┬▒2% to ┬▒6% FS (not as accurate as orifice/Coriolis).
3. **Glass tubes** can shatter under pressure/temperature excursions or water hammer.
4. **Density-dependent** ŌĆö fluid must be known; density correction required.
5. **Not suitable for slurries, viscous fluids**, or fluids with suspended solids.
6. **Indication and output may drift** with float erosion or coating build-up.

**(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Numerical ŌĆö Density Correction ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- V_f = 12 cm┬│ = 12 ├Ś 10Ōü╗ŌüČ m┬│
- Žü_f = 7900 kg/m┬│
- A_h = 5.0 cm┬▓ = 5.0 ├Ś 10Ōü╗Ōü┤ m┬▓ (fixed float height)
- Žü_w = 997 kg/m┬│ (calibration fluid, water at 25┬░C)
- Žü_k = 780 kg/m┬│ (process fluid, kerosene at 25┬░C)
- Q_w (calibrated water flow) = 600 L/h = 0.6 m┬│/h

**Find:** Q_k (actual kerosene flow at same float height)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the density correction formula:**

From the master flow equation, with A_h and C_d held constant:
$$Q \propto \sqrt{\frac{(\rho_f - \rho)}{\rho}}$$

Therefore:
$$\frac{Q_k}{Q_w} = \sqrt{ \frac{(\rho_f - \rho_k)/\rho_k}{(\rho_f - \rho_w)/\rho_w} }$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute values:**

Numerator (kerosene): 
$$(\rho_f - \rho_k)/\rho_k = (7900 - 780)/780 = 7120/780 = 9.1282$$

Denominator (water): 
$$(\rho_f - \rho_w)/\rho_w = (7900 - 997)/997 = 6903/997 = 6.9238$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the ratio:**

$$\frac{Q_k}{Q_w} = \sqrt{ \frac{9.1282}{6.9238} } = \sqrt{1.3184} = 1.1482$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute Q_k:**

$$Q_k = Q_w \times 1.1482 = 600 \times 1.1482 = 688.9 \text{ L/h}$$

$$\boxed{Q_k \approx 689 \text{ L/h}}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Physical Interpretation:**

For the same float position (same annular area), the meter would over-read the water calibration if kerosene were flowing ŌĆö the actual kerosene flow is **14.8% higher** than the direct scale indication. This is because kerosene, being less dense, exerts less buoyancy and less momentum transfer, requiring a higher actual flow to lift the float to the same height.

In practice, the rotameter would be supplied with a **dual scale** (water on one side, kerosene on the other) or an **electronic transmitter** with selectable fluid property input.

**(7 marks ŌĆö full credit requires all 5 steps, both densities computed correctly, and physical interpretation)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Variable Area with Variable Head** ŌĆö writing "pressure drop varies with flow" in a rotameter explanation is the #1 conceptual error. Always state: **╬öP is constant, Area varies.**
> 2. **Missing units in numerical answers** ŌĆö final Q must carry units (L/h, m┬│/h, or m┬│/s). A correct number with no unit scores **zero** for that sub-part.
> 3. **Forgetting the term $[1-(A_f/A_h)^2]$** in the master equation ŌĆö examiners *test this exact bracket* to check derivation depth. Skipping it = -2 to -3 marks.
> 4. **Drawing rotameter with horizontal orientation** ŌĆö always vertical, flow upward. Even a small deviation costs the sketch mark.
> 5. **In density correction problems**, applying $Q_k = Q_w \sqrt{\rho_w/\rho_k}$ (the orifice correction) instead of the correct rotameter formula $Q_k = Q_w \sqrt{\rho_w(\rho_f-\rho_k)/[\rho_k(\rho_f-\rho_w)]}$ ŌĆö this gives a **wrong answer** because it ignores the buoyancy term.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions** before the derivation ŌĆö at least **5 assumptions** must be listed; this is a mandatory 1-2 mark component in 7-mark derivations.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bulletins ŌĆö Print This Section Before Exam:**

- A **rotameter** is a **Variable Area** flow meter; **╬öP is constant**, area varies.
- The float position is the **direct read-out** of flow.
- **Master Flow Equation:** $Q = C_d A_h \sqrt{ 2 g V_f (\rho_f - \rho) / [\rho A_f (1-(A_f/A_h)^2)] }$
- **Simplified form** (when $A_h \gg A_f$): $Q \approx C_d A_h \sqrt{ 2 g V_f (\rho_f - \rho) / (\rho A_f) }$
- **Constant ╬öP across float:** $\Delta P = V_f g (\rho_f - \rho) / A_f$ ŌĆö does **not** depend on flow rate.
- **Annular areaŌĆōheight relation:** $A_h = A_f + h \pi D_t \tan(\alpha)$ ŌĆö linear for a uniform-taper tube.
- **Installation:** **vertical, flow upward**, inlet straight pipe **5D**, outlet **3D**.
- **Float density must be greater than fluid density**; otherwise float will not return to bottom seat.
- **Discharge coefficient** $C_d$: range **0.60 to 0.98**; sharp-edged skirted floats have highest values.
- **Density correction** for fluid change: $Q_{new} = Q_{cal} \sqrt{ \rho_{cal}(\rho_f - \rho_{new}) / [\rho_{new}(\rho_f - \rho_{cal})] }$
- **Viscosity correction** required when kinematic viscosity $\nu > 20$ cSt; consult manufacturer's curve.
- **Turn-down ratio:** typically **10:1** for standard meters; **20:1** for long-scale precision meters.
- **Materials:** Glass (low P, transparent), SS 316 (general), Hastelloy (corrosive), PTFE/PVDF (chemical), ceramic (abrasive).
- **Output options:** Direct scale, 4-20 mA, HART, Foundation Fieldbus, local LCD.
- **Advantages:** Simple, cheap, no power needed for visual read, self-cleaning, low pressure drop.
- **Limitations:** Vertical-only, low accuracy, density-sensitive, not for slurries, glass tubes fragile.
- **Smart rotameters** (e.g., **Endress+Hauser Dosimag, Krohne H250, Yokogawa RAMC**) integrate Hall sensors and 4-20 mA + HART for DCS integration.
- **Key trick to remember:** In a rotameter, the *flow meter literally re-arranges its own geometry* to accommodate the flow ŌĆö the variable area IS the output, eliminating the need for ╬öP-to-flow computation.
- **The float must be self-centering** ŌĆö elliptical, skirted, or guided designs ensure this; pure spheres can wobble at low Re.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Open Channel Meters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT413 - PROCESS INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Open Channel Meters # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) An **Open Channel Meter** (also called an *open-channel flow measurement device* or *primary flow element for free-surface flow*) is a class of process instrumentation equipment used to determine the volumetric flow rate or discharge of a liquid flowing in a conduit where the liquid surface is exposed to atmospheric pressure and the flow is driven by gravity rather than by a closed pressurized system. The measurement is fundamentally **indirect**: the meter measures the *head* (height of the liquid surface above a defined reference datum) and converts this head into a flow rate using an empirically derived **discharge equation** specific to the geometry of the metering element. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Open-channel meters operate on the principle of measuring the static head (level) of a liquid in a channel of known geometric cross-section, then applying the continuity equation $Q = A \cdot v$ combined with critical-flow or weir-flow equations to compute discharge $Q$. They are NOT closed-pipe meters and have no pressure differential due to internal pressurization. The two principal categories mandated for study under the KTU 2024 scheme are: 1. **Weirs** ŌĆö Obstruction-type meters where liquid flows over a precisely shaped notch. 2. **Flumes** ŌĆö Constriction-type meters where the channel cross-section is narrowed to create a critical flow condition with a known relationship between upstream head and discharge. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The "Garden Bucket" Model:** > Imagine you have a rectangular bucket and want to know how much water flows through it per second, but you do not have any flowmeter. You cannot possibly know the *velocity* directly. However, you can observe the *water level (height)* rising or falling. If you know the cross-sectional area of the bucket and the rate at which the water level changes, you can compute the flow rate. > > An open-channel meter works on a closely related idea: by installing a calibrated obstruction (a weir or flume), the flow rate becomes **uniquely related to the height of water at one specific point**. A level sensor (float, ultrasonic, bubbler) reads that height, and a transmitter or controller calculates the flow. > > **Think of it this way:** A weir is like a "speed bump" for water. The faster the water is flowing, the *higher* it piles up behind the bump before it spills over. By measuring how high the water piles up, you can deduce how fast it must have been flowing. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics The following constants and units are routinely used in open-channel flow computations: | Symbol | Constant / Quantity | Standard Value | Unit | |--------|---------------------|----------------|------| | $g$ | Acceleration due to gravity | **9.80665** | $\text{m/s}^2$ | | $Q$ | Volumetric discharge | ŌĆö | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | | $H$ or $h$ | Head (height of liquid) | ŌĆö | $\text{m}$ | | $L$ or $b$ | Weir crest length / width | ŌĆö | $\text{m}$ | | $\theta$ | V-notch angle | ŌĆö | degrees or radians | | $C_d$ | Coefficient of discharge | **0.60 to 0.65** (typical) | dimensionless | | $C_v$ | Coefficient of velocity | $\approx 0.95$ to $0.99$ | dimensionless | | $C_c$ | Coefficient of contraction | $\approx 0.98$ to $1.00$ | dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > **Universal Constraint:** The continuity principle $Q = A \cdot v$ combined with Torricelli's law $v = \sqrt{2gH}$ forms the **physical bedrock** of all weir-discharge derivations. Every KTU exam problem will start from these two relationships. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Open-Channel Meters Open-channel meters are broadly classified based on the **mechanism by which the flow is measured**: ### A. Weirs (Obstruction Type) A weir is a barrier placed across an open channel over which the liquid flows. The discharge is related to the head measured upstream at a specific distance from the weir plate. **Geometric Sub-Types:** 1. **Rectangular Weir (Sharp-Crested):** A simple horizontal crest of width $L$ cut into a plate. Used for medium to large flow rates. 2. **Triangular (V-Notch) Weir:** A V-shaped notch with apex angle $\theta$. Used for low flow rates due to higher sensitivity at low heads. 3. **Trapezoidal (Cipolletti) Weir:** A trapezoidal notch with side slopes of 1 horizontal : 4 vertical. Designed to compensate for the end contractions of a rectangular weir. 4. **Broad-Crested Weir:** A wide, flat obstruction. Used for very large flows such as dam spillways. ### B. Flumes (Constriction Type) A flume is a specially shaped open-channel section that constricts the flow cross-section, accelerating the liquid and creating a measurable head-drop relationship. **Geometric Sub-Types:** 1. **Parshall Flume:** A proprietary design with a converging section, a throat, and a diverging section. Self-cleaning; widely used in wastewater. 2. **Venturi Flume:** A smooth, gradually narrowing section resembling a closed-pipe venturi. 3. **PalmerŌĆōBowlus Flume:** Inserted into existing circular conduits (sewer lines); calibrated to specific pipe diameters. 4. **Critical-Depth Flumes:** Operate on the principle of critical flow at the throat. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** The V-notch weir has the **highest sensitivity** at low flows because the discharge depends on $H^{5/2}$ rather than $H^{3/2}$ (as in a rectangular weir). For $H < 0.1$ m, always use a V-notch. ## 2.2 Underlying Theory ŌĆö Why It Works The theoretical foundation rests on three pillars: **Pillar 1 ŌĆö Torricelli's Theorem (Ideal Velocity):** For a fluid element at depth $y$ below the free surface, the theoretical velocity is: $$v = \sqrt{2gy}$$ **Pillar 2 ŌĆö Continuity Equation:** The total discharge is the integral of elemental velocity strips across the flow cross-section: $$Q = \int v \, dA$$ **Pillar 3 ŌĆö Empirical Calibration Coefficient:** Real fluid behavior deviates from ideal theory due to viscosity, turbulence, surface tension, and vena-contracta effects. This is absorbed into the **coefficient of discharge** $C_d$: $$C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$$ where $C_c$ corrects for the contracted flow area and $C_v$ corrects for the velocity distribution. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **The following table is the EXAM GOLD for KTU 2024 ESE questions on Open Channel Meters. Memorize the exponent of $H$ ŌĆö that is the single most-tested fact.** | Meter Type | Discharge Equation (SI Units) | Key Exponent of $H$ | Typical $C_d$ Range | |------------|------------------------------|---------------------|---------------------| | Rectangular Weir (suppressed) | $Q = \dfrac{2}{3}\, C_d\, L\, \sqrt{2g}\, H^{3/2}$ | $3/2$ | 0.62 ŌĆō 0.66 | | Rectangular Weir (contracted) | $Q = \dfrac{2}{3}\, C_d\, (L - 0.2H)\, \sqrt{2g}\, H^{3/2}$ | $3/2$ | 0.60 ŌĆō 0.65 | | V-Notch Weir | $Q = \dfrac{8}{15}\, C_d\, \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g}\, H^{5/2}$ | $5/2$ | 0.58 ŌĆō 0.62 | | Cipolletti Weir | $Q = \dfrac{2}{3}\, C_d\, L\, \sqrt{2g}\, H^{3/2}$ | $3/2$ | 0.62 ŌĆō 0.66 | | Broad-Crested Weir | $Q = C_d\, L\, \sqrt{g}\, H^{3/2}$ | $3/2$ | 0.84 ŌĆō 0.95 | | Parshall Flume | $Q = C \cdot H^{n}$ (where $C, n$ are tabulated) | varies (1.0 ŌĆō 1.6) | Empirical | | Venturi Flume | $Q = C_d \cdot A_{throat} \cdot \sqrt{2g(H_1 - H_2)}$ | $1/2$ | 0.95 ŌĆō 0.98 | | PalmerŌĆōBowlus Flume | $Q = k \cdot H^m$ (calibrated constants) | varies | Empirical | **Boundary Conditions for Validity:** - $H > 5\,\text{cm}$ minimum (to avoid surface tension effects). - $L \geq 3H$ (rectangular weir geometry rule). - $\dfrac{H}{P} \leq 0.5$ where $P$ is the weir plate height (to prevent velocity-of-approach errors). - Froude number at measurement section: $Fr < 0.5$ (subcritical, for head measurement accuracy). ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Open-channel meters are indispensable in: - **Wastewater Treatment Plants (STPs/WWTPs):** Parshall flumes are the de-facto standard for influent/effluent flow measurement. - **Irrigation Canals:** Rectangular weirs measure water distribution to farms. - **Hydroelectric Power:** Broad-crested weirs measure spillway discharges. - **Industrial Effluent Monitoring:** Regulatory compliance for discharge volumes. - **Stormwater Management:** PalmerŌĆōBowlus flumes in sewer manholes. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Discharge Over a Rectangular Weir (Suppressed) ### Step 1 ŌĆö Define the Geometry Consider a rectangular weir of crest length $L$ with liquid head $H$ above the crest. Take an elemental horizontal strip of water of thickness $dy$ at depth $y$ below the free surface (i.e., at elevation $H - y$ above the crest). The width of this strip equals the crest length $L$ (for a *suppressed* weir with no end contractions). The elemental area is: $$dA = L \cdot dy$$ ### Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Torricelli's Law By Torricelli's theorem, the theoretical velocity of water passing through the elemental strip is: $$v = \sqrt{2gy}$$ ### Step 3 ŌĆö Form the Elemental Discharge The elementary volumetric discharge through the strip is: $$dQ = v \cdot dA = L \cdot \sqrt{2gy} \cdot dy$$ ### Step 4 ŌĆö Integrate Across the Flow Depth The limits of integration are from $y = 0$ (at the crest) to $y = H$ (at the free surface): $$Q_{ideal} = \int_{0}^{H} L \sqrt{2gy} \, dy$$ ### Step 5 ŌĆö Evaluate the Integral $$Q_{ideal} = L \sqrt{2g} \int_{0}^{H} y^{1/2} \, dy$$ Performing the integration: $$\int_{0}^{H} y^{1/2} \, dy = \left[ \frac{y^{3/2}}{3/2} \right]_{0}^{H} = \frac{2}{3} H^{3/2}$$ Substituting back: $$Q_{ideal} = \frac{2}{3} L \sqrt{2g} \, H^{3/2}$$ ### Step 6 ŌĆö Apply the Coefficient of Discharge Real fluids exhibit energy losses, surface tension, and a contracted nappe. These are consolidated into $C_d$: $$\boxed{Q = \frac{2}{3} \, C_d \, L \, \sqrt{2g} \, H^{3/2}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** This is THE most-tested formula on open-channel flow. The exponent $3/2$ on $H$ is the single most important fact ŌĆö if you forget it, you lose the entire 7-mark derivation part. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Discharge Over a Triangular (V-Notch) Weir ### Step 1 ŌĆö Geometry of the V-Notch Let the V-notch have an apex angle $\theta$. The width of the elemental strip at depth $y$ below the free surface (i.e., at height $H - y$ above the apex) is obtained from similar triangles: $$w(y) = 2 (H - y) \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$$ ### Step 2 ŌĆö Elemental Area $$dA = w(y) \cdot dy = 2 (H - y) \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) dy$$ ### Step 3 ŌĆö Apply Torricelli's Law The elemental velocity remains: $$v = \sqrt{2gy}$$ ### Step 4 ŌĆö Form the Elemental Discharge $$dQ = \sqrt{2gy} \cdot 2 (H - y) \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) dy$$ ### Step 5 ŌĆö Integrate from 0 to H $$Q_{ideal} = 2 \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \int_{0}^{H} (H - y) \, y^{1/2} \, dy$$ ### Step 6 ŌĆö Expand and Split the Integral $$\int_{0}^{H} (H - y) y^{1/2} \, dy = H \int_{0}^{H} y^{1/2} \, dy - \int_{0}^{H} y^{3/2} \, dy$$ Evaluate each piece separately: $$H \int_{0}^{H} y^{1/2} \, dy = H \cdot \frac{2}{3} H^{3/2} = \frac{2}{3} H^{5/2}$$ $$\int_{0}^{H} y^{3/2} \, dy = \frac{2}{5} H^{5/2}$$ Subtracting: $$\frac{2}{3} H^{5/2} - \frac{2}{5} H^{5/2} = 2 H^{5/2} \left(\frac{1}{3} - \frac{1}{5}\right) = 2 H^{5/2} \cdot \frac{2}{15} = \frac{4}{15} H^{5/2}$$ ### Step 7 ŌĆö Combine and Apply $C_d$ $$Q_{ideal} = 2 \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \cdot \frac{4}{15} H^{5/2} = \frac{8}{15} \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \, H^{5/2}$$ Applying the discharge coefficient: $$\boxed{Q = \frac{8}{15} \, C_d \, \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \, H^{5/2}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Cipolletti Weir Equation A Cipolletti weir is a trapezoidal weir with side slopes of $\frac{1}{4}$ horizontal per 1 vertical. It compensates for the end-contraction losses of a rectangular weir. The discharge equation is identical in form to a *suppressed* rectangular weir: $$\boxed{Q = \frac{2}{3} \, C_d \, L \, \sqrt{2g} \, H^{3/2}}$$ The side slope $1:4$ is geometrically chosen so that the *additional* discharge contributed by the triangular end portions exactly compensates for the discharge *loss* due to the end contractions of a contracted rectangular weir. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Numerical Template:** All open-channel flow numericals in the KTU 2024 ESE follow a fixed 3-step structure. Memorize this template. **Problem [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]:** A rectangular suppressed weir of crest length $L = 0.80$ m is installed in a channel. The measured head over the crest is $H = 0.20$ m. Take $C_d = 0.62$ and $g = 9.81$ m/s┬▓. Calculate the discharge. **Solution:** **Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the equation:** Use the rectangular (suppressed) weir formula: $$Q = \frac{2}{3} \, C_d \, L \, \sqrt{2g} \, H^{3/2}$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $\sqrt{2g}$:** $$\sqrt{2g} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81} = \sqrt{19.62} = 4.4294 \, \text{m}^{1/2}/\text{s}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $H^{3/2}$:** $$H^{3/2} = (0.20)^{3/2} = (0.20)^1 \cdot (0.20)^{0.5} = 0.20 \times 0.4472 = 0.08944 \, \text{m}^{3/2}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute:** $$Q = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.62 \times 0.80 \times 4.4294 \times 0.08944$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Multiply progressively:** $$Q = 0.6667 \times 0.62 \times 0.80 \times 4.4294 \times 0.08944$$ $$Q = 0.4133 \times 0.80 \times 4.4294 \times 0.08944$$ $$Q = 0.3307 \times 4.4294 \times 0.08944$$ $$Q = 1.4648 \times 0.08944$$ $$Q = 0.1310 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$ $$\boxed{Q \approx 0.131 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 131 \, \text{L/s}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sanity Check:** For $H = 0.20$ m and $L = 0.80$ m, we expect a flow rate in the range of $100$ŌĆō$200$ L/s. The answer $131$ L/s lies squarely in this range, confirming correctness. --- ## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix | Feature | Rectangular Weir | V-Notch Weir | Parshall Flume | |---------|------------------|---------------|-----------------| | **Best for flow range** | Medium to High | Low | Medium to High | | **Sensitivity at low $H$** | Low (H^1.5) | **High (H^2.5)** | Moderate | | **Sensitivity at high $H$** | High | Very High | Moderate | | **Sediment handling** | Poor (nappe dip) | Poor | **Excellent** (self-scouring) | | **Head loss** | High | High | **Low** | | **Space required** | Moderate | Small | Large | | **Calibration** | Standard formula | Standard formula | **Site-specific** table | | **Cost** | Low | Low | High | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Open-Channel Flow Measurement System ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Liquid Source
Channel Reservoir] --> B[Inlet Channel
with Stilling Basin] B --> C{Primary Flow Element} C --> C1[Weir Plate
Rectangular V-Notch Cipolletti] C --> C2[Flume Section
Parshall Venturi Palmer-Bowlus] C1 --> D[Head H Measurement Point
Specific Distance Upstream] C2 --> D D --> E[Level Sensor Stage] E --> E1[Float Sensor] E --> E2[Ultrasonic Transducer] E --> E3[Bubbler System] E --> E4[Capacitance Probe] E1 --> F[Signal Conditioning Unit
Linearization and Scaling] E2 --> F E3 --> F E4 --> F F --> G[Microprocessor
Apply Discharge Equation] G --> H[Display and Output Stage] H --> H1[Local Indicator in m3/s] H --> H2[4-20 mA Current Loop] H --> H3[Digital HART or Fieldbus] H --> H4[PLC SCADA DCS] H1 --> I[End User Operator] H2 --> I H3 --> I H4 --> I ``` ## 4.2 Process Flow Architecture ŌĆö Hierarchical Signal Path ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Channel Flow]:::process --> P2[Primary Device
Weir or Flume]:::process P2 --> P3[Head Conversion
Geometric Function]:::process P3 --> P4[Level Transmitter]:::process P4 --> S1[Signal Conditioner]:::signal S1 --> S2[Microcontroller
Discharge Equation]:::signal S2 --> S3[Output Interface]:::signal S3 --> S4[Operator Display]:::signal S3 --> S5[Control Valve Feedback]:::signal S3 --> S6[Data Logger]:::signal classDef process fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef signal fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Decision Flow ŌĆö Selecting the Right Open-Channel Meter ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Start: Flow Measurement Requirement]) --> Q1{Is the flow rate
LOW less than 50 L per s} Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is sediment
present} Q1 -- No --> Q3{Is space constrained} Q2 -- No --> R1[Use V-Notch Weir
Highest sensitivity]:::rec Q2 -- Yes --> R2[Use Parshall Flume
Self-cleaning]:::rec Q3 -- Yes --> R3[Use V-Notch Weir
Compact geometry]:::rec Q3 -- No --> Q4{Is accuracy at
high flow critical} Q4 -- Yes --> R4[Use Rectangular Weir
or Broad Crested Weir]:::rec Q4 -- No --> R5[Use Parshall Flume
Versatile medium-high]:::rec classDef rec fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1a6b3a,color:#000,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.4 Parshall Flume Geometric Schematic (Block-Level) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Inlet Section
Converging Walls] --> B[Throat Section
Constant Width W] B --> C[Outlet Section
Diverging Walls] A -.Measured at Ha.-> D[Head Sensor Upstream] B -.Optional.-> E[Head Sensor at Throat Hb] D --> F[Discharge Q = C times Han
Tabulated C and n] E --> F style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style B fill:#ffe1cc,stroke:#993300 style C fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style D fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600 style E fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600 style F fill:#fff0b3,stroke:#996600 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Parshall Flume Note:** The discharge equation $Q = C \cdot H_a^n$ uses **only the upstream head** $H_a$ in the converging section. The throat width $W$ determines the constants $C$ and $n$ from the standard Parshall calibration tables. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Part A ŌĆö Question 1 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]` **Q1.** Differentiate between a weir and a flume as open-channel flow measurement devices. **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Weir | Flume | |--------|------|-------| | **Principle** | Liquid flows *over* an obstruction | Liquid flows *through* a constricted section | | **Energy Dissipation** | High (nappe fall creates turbulence) | Low (smooth critical flow) | | **Head Loss** | Significant | Minimal | | **Sediment Tolerance** | Poor ŌĆö silts up behind plate | Excellent ŌĆö self-scouring | | **Typical Use** | Irrigation channels, clean water | Wastewater, sewage, stormwater | | **Measurement Location** | Upstream of crest, distance Ōēź 4H | Upstream of throat, in converging section | | **Discharge Relation** | $Q \propto H^{3/2}$ or $H^{5/2}$ | $Q \propto H^n$ (1.0 to 1.6) | > [!NOTE] > Examiner awards full 3 marks only if **at least four differentiating points** are clearly stated with one example per category. --- ### Part A ŌĆö Question 2 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]` **Q2.** Why is a V-notch weir preferred over a rectangular weir for measuring low flow rates? **Model Answer:** A V-notch weir is preferred for low flow rates because its discharge is proportional to $H^{5/2}$ rather than $H^{3/2}$ for a rectangular weir. **Mathematical Justification:** Consider two flow scenarios ŌĆö $H = 0.05$ m (low) and $H = 0.20$ m (moderate). Compare the **percentage change in $Q$** per unit change in $H$: For rectangular weir: $$\frac{dQ}{dH} \propto H^{1/2}$$ For V-notch weir: $$\frac{dQ}{dH} \propto H^{3/2}$$ At low $H$ (e.g., $H = 0.05$ m), the V-notch's $H^{3/2}$ term is *still very small*, but its **rate of change with $H$** is much more pronounced in relative terms. Concretely, the same absolute error in head measurement ($\Delta H = 1$ mm) results in a **smaller relative error in $Q$** for the V-notch at low heads, because the V-notch's $\frac{dQ}{dH}/Q$ ratio is: $$\frac{dQ}{dH} \cdot \frac{1}{Q} = \frac{5}{2H} \quad \text{(V-notch)} \quad \text{vs} \quad \frac{3}{2H} \quad \text{(Rectangular)}$$ The V-notch has a **higher relative sensitivity** (factor $5/3 = 1.67$ times more sensitive per unit head). Furthermore, the V-notch is geometrically suited to small channels. > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** A 3-mark answer requires the *exponent justification* ($H^{5/2}$ vs $H^{3/2}$) plus a one-line engineering reason (compact, low-flow optimized). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Module Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Module 2]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the discharge equation for a rectangular (suppressed) sharp-crested weir, clearly stating the assumptions made. **(b) [7 Marks]** A rectangular weir of crest length $L = 1.20$ m has a measured head $H = 0.15$ m. If $C_d = 0.62$, calculate the discharge. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s┬▓. What would the discharge be if the same head were measured over a 90┬░ V-notch weir with $C_d = 0.60$? --- **Model Solution for Part (a):** The derivation follows the steps outlined in Section 3.1 of these notes. The complete, exam-ready sequence is: **Assumption Set (Mandatory in KTU Answers):** 1. Steady, incompressible flow. 2. Hydrostatic pressure distribution over the weir crest. 3. Negligible viscosity (ideal fluid) ŌĆö corrected by $C_d$ later. 4. Suppressed weir ŌĆö no end contractions. 5. Atmospheric pressure below the nappe. 6. Head $H$ measured at a distance $\geq 4H$ upstream of the weir plate (where the water surface is horizontal). **Derivation Sequence:** Step 1 ŌĆö Elemental area at depth $y$ below the free surface: $$dA = L \, dy$$ Step 2 ŌĆö Elemental velocity (Torricelli's law): $$v = \sqrt{2gy}$$ Step 3 ŌĆö Elemental discharge: $$dQ = L \sqrt{2gy} \, dy$$ Step 4 ŌĆö Integrate from $y = 0$ to $y = H$: $$Q_{ideal} = L \sqrt{2g} \int_{0}^{H} y^{1/2} \, dy = L \sqrt{2g} \cdot \frac{2}{3} H^{3/2}$$ Step 5 ŌĆö Apply $C_d$: $$\boxed{Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d L \sqrt{2g} \, H^{3/2}}$$ --- **Model Solution for Part (b):** **Part (b)(i) ŌĆö Rectangular Weir:** $$Q_{rect} = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.62 \times 1.20 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81} \times (0.15)^{3/2}$$ Compute each term: - $\sqrt{2g} = \sqrt{19.62} = 4.4294$ m$^{1/2}$/s - $(0.15)^{3/2} = 0.15 \times \sqrt{0.15} = 0.15 \times 0.3873 = 0.05809$ m$^{3/2}$ Substitute: $$Q_{rect} = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.62 \times 1.20 \times 4.4294 \times 0.05809$$ $$Q_{rect} = 0.4133 \times 1.20 \times 4.4294 \times 0.05809$$ $$Q_{rect} = 0.4960 \times 4.4294 \times 0.05809$$ $$Q_{rect} = 2.1970 \times 0.05809$$ $$\boxed{Q_{rect} = 0.1276 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} \approx 127.6 \, \text{L/s}}$$ **Part (b)(ii) ŌĆö V-Notch Weir ($\theta = 90┬░$):** For $\theta = 90┬░$: $$\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) = \tan(45┬░) = 1$$ $$Q_{V} = \frac{8}{15} \times 0.60 \times 1 \times \sqrt{19.62} \times (0.15)^{5/2}$$ Compute $(0.15)^{5/2}$: $$(0.15)^{5/2} = (0.15)^2 \times \sqrt{0.15} = 0.0225 \times 0.3873 = 0.008714$$ Substitute: $$Q_{V} = 0.5333 \times 0.60 \times 4.4294 \times 0.008714$$ $$Q_{V} = 0.3200 \times 4.4294 \times 0.008714$$ $$Q_{V} = 1.4174 \times 0.008714$$ $$\boxed{Q_{V} = 0.01235 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} \approx 12.35 \, \text{L/s}}$$ **Comparative Inference:** The V-notch produces a flow that is about **10 times smaller** than the rectangular weir for the same head. This numerical contrast is precisely why the V-notch is recommended for *low* flow rates. --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Module 2]` **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat schematic, explain the construction and working of a Parshall flume. State the standard form of its discharge equation. **(b) [7 Marks]** A Parshall flume with a 6-inch (152 mm) throat has its upstream head measured as $H_a = 0.30$ m. Using the standard Parshall calibration: $C = 1.41$ and $n = 1.58$ for free-flow conditions, compute the discharge. --- **Model Solution for Part (a):** **Construction of a Parshall Flume:** A Parshall flume (developed by Ralph Parshall, 1920s) consists of three distinct sections cast typically in fiberglass, concrete, or metal: 1. **Inlet (Converging) Section:** The floor is level and the side walls converge from the channel width down to the throat width. The length of this section is fixed for a given throat size. 2. **Throat Section:** A short, parallel-walled section of constant width $W$ (e.g., 1 inch to 50 feet for various sizes). The floor of the throat slopes **downward** from inlet to outlet (typically 9┬░ or steeper in some designs). 3. **Outlet (Diverging) Section:** The floor slopes **upward** back to the original channel level, and the walls diverge. **Working Principle:** As water enters the converging section, the flow accelerates due to the contraction. At the **throat**, the depth drops to the **critical depth**, where the Froude number equals 1. Beyond the throat, the flow enters a **subcritical** regime in the diverging section. **Two Operating Conditions:** - **Free Flow:** The downstream depth is low enough not to affect the upstream depth $H_a$. Discharge depends only on $H_a$. - **Submerged Flow:** The downstream water level rises high enough to "drown" the throat. Discharge becomes a function of *both* $H_a$ and $H_b$ (throat head). A submergence correction factor must be applied. **Standard Discharge Equation (Free Flow):** $$\boxed{Q = C \cdot H_a^n}$$ where $H_a$ is the upstream head measured at a specific gauging point (located in the converging section, typically $\frac{2}{3}$ of the length from the inlet to the throat), and $C, n$ are empirically tabulated for each throat size. **Standard Parshall Flume Schematic (Block Diagram):** ``` ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ Ōöé Inlet Section Ōöé Ōöé Throat Ōöé Ōöé Outlet Section Ōöé Ōöé (Converging Ōöé ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ Ōöé Width Ōöé ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ Ōöé (Diverging Ōöé Ōöé Sidewalls) Ōöé Ōöé W Ōöé Ōöé Sidewalls) Ōöé Ōöé ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ Ōöé Ōöé ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ Ōöé Ōöé ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ Ōöé Ōöé Ōöé Ha measuredŌöé Ōöé Ōöé ŌöéHb Ōöé Ōöé Ōöé Ōöé Free Ōöé Ōöé Ōöé Ōöé here ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌö╝ŌöĆŌöĆŌö╝ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ Ōöé Ōöé Ōöé Ōöé Ōöé Ōöé outfall Ōöé Ōöé Ōöé ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś Ōöé Ōöé ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś Ōöé Ōöé ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś Ōöé ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś Floor: Level Floor: Slopes Floor: Rises Downward Back to Level ``` --- **Model Solution for Part (b):** Given: - Throat size = 6 inch = 152 mm (Parshall small flume) - $H_a = 0.30$ m - $C = 1.41$ - $n = 1.58$ Apply the Parshall free-flow equation: $$Q = C \cdot H_a^n = 1.41 \times (0.30)^{1.58}$$ **Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $H_a^n$:** $$(0.30)^{1.58}$$ Using logarithms: $$\ln(0.30) = -1.2040$$ $$1.58 \times (-1.2040) = -1.9023$$ $$(0.30)^{1.58} = e^{-1.9023} = 0.1494$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Multiply by $C$:** $$Q = 1.41 \times 0.1494 = 0.2107$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö State with units:** $$\boxed{Q \approx 0.211 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 211 \, \text{L/s}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification:** For a 6-inch Parshall flume, a free-flow head of 0.30 m is well within the rated range. The computed flow of ~211 L/s is dimensionally and physically consistent with the calibration tables. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Read This Before Writing Your Exam!** > > 1. **Forgetting the coefficient $C_d$:** Students routinely write $Q = \frac{2}{3} L \sqrt{2g} H^{3/2}$ *without* the $C_d$ term. This loses **2 full marks** in the KTU answer key. > > 2. **Wrong exponent of $H$:** Confusing $H^{3/2}$ (rectangular) with $H^{5/2}$ (V-notch) is a **3-mark deduction** because it reflects a fundamental conceptual failure. > > 3. **Unit mismatch in Parshall flume numericals:** $H_a$ MUST be in meters for the constants $C$ and $n$ from the standard tables. If you substitute $H_a$ in cm, your answer will be off by orders of magnitude. > > 4. **Skipping the assumption list:** In derivation questions, KTU awards **1 mark** specifically for stating the assumptions. Skipping this list costs easy marks. > > 5. **Not specifying the head measurement location:** In Parshall flume questions, the location of $H_a$ measurement ($\frac{2}{3}$ of the converging section from the inlet) is asked explicitly. A vague "upstream of the flume" answer loses 1 mark. > > 6. **Mixing up free-flow and submerged-flow formulas:** In Parshall flumes, free-flow uses $Q = C \cdot H_a^n$; submerged-flow uses a different two-head formula. Confusing them is a **3-mark trap**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Use this checklist for the last 10 minutes before your exam. Every bullet here is a potential question.** - Ō£ģ Open-channel meters measure **head (level)** of liquid, then convert to **flow rate** via geometry-specific discharge equations. - Ō£ģ The two principal families are **Weirs** (obstruction) and **Flumes** (constriction). - Ō£ģ The **discharge exponent** is the most critical fact: rectangular weir = **3/2**, V-notch weir = **5/2**, Parshall flume = **1.0 to 1.6** (table-specific). - Ō£ģ All weir equations originate from **Torricelli's law** $v = \sqrt{2gy}$ and the **continuity equation** $Q = A \cdot v$. - Ō£ģ The **coefficient of discharge** $C_d$ absorbs real-fluid losses; typical range is **0.60 to 0.66** for sharp-crested weirs. - Ō£ģ The **V-notch** is best for **low flows** (H^5.2 sensitivity) and the **rectangular** for **medium-high flows**. - Ō£ģ The **Cipolletti weir** uses the same equation as a suppressed rectangular weir; its **1:4 side slope** mathematically compensates for end contractions. - Ō£ģ The **Parshall flume** equation $Q = C \cdot H_a^n$ uses **only the upstream head** $H_a$ in free-flow conditions; constants are tabulated per throat size. - Ō£ģ **Head measurement location** must be at least **4H upstream** for weirs and at the **standard Parshall gauging point** for flumes. - Ō£ģ Validity boundary: **$H \geq 5$ cm, $L \geq 3H$, $H/P \leq 0.5$** (weirs); Froude number at measurement point < 0.5. - Ō£ģ The **broad-crested weir** has $C_d$ in the range **0.84 to 0.95** ŌĆö significantly higher than sharp-crested weirs. - Ō£ģ **Parshall flumes are the standard for wastewater** because of their **self-scouring** geometry. - Ō£ģ **PalmerŌĆōBowlus flumes** are inserted into existing **circular sewer pipes** with calibration constants tied to pipe diameter. - Ō£ģ In a **submerged-flow** Parshall flume, **two heads** ($H_a$ and $H_b$) are required, and a submergence correction is mandatory. - Ō£ģ Remember: $g = 9.81$ m/s┬▓ (NOT 10) for KTU numericals; using $g = 10$ introduces ~2% error and is often the difference between full and partial marks. - Ō£ģ **Critical-flow** condition (Froude number = 1) at the throat is the physical basis for flume operation.
