topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT413 - PROCESS INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Level Measurement - Direct Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Level Measurement** is defined as the determination of the position of the interface between two fluids (most commonly a liquid and a gas/vapour) with respect to a fixed reference datum, expressed as a linear distance (e.g., meters) or a volumetric quantity (e.g., m┬│) within a process vessel.

The **Direct Method** of level measurement is a category of level sensing techniques in which the level of the process fluid is read or determined **without the need for inferential computation** (such as radioactive, ultrasonic, or radar echo time-of-flight analysis). In direct methods, the sensing element **physically contacts the fluid** or directly intercepts the fluid surface, producing a measurement signal that is **linearly and directly proportional** to the actual liquid level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The 2024 Scheme classifies level measurement broadly into **Direct** and **Indirect** methods. Direct methods include **Sight Glass, Float (Float-Operated, Tape Float, Chain Float), Dip Stick, and Hydrostatic Head types**. The examiner will expect a clear block diagram, working principle, working fluid medium, advantages, and limitations for each.

The KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education framework maps this topic to the following Course Outcomes:
- **CO2:** Analyse the static and dynamic characteristics of industrial process measurement systems.
- **CO3:** Select and justify appropriate level measurement techniques for specific industrial process applications.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö The Home Water Tank**
> Imagine an overhead water storage tank in a typical Kerala household. To "directly" measure the water level, you can:
> 1. **Climb up and look inside** (this is the **Sight Glass** principle ŌĆö visual reading).
> 2. **Tie a stone to a rope and lower it to touch the water, then mark the rope** (this is the **Dip Stick** / **Chain Float** principle ŌĆö mechanical contact).
> 3. **Place a float (empty plastic bottle) on the water and read the float's position on a graduated rod** (this is the **Float-Type** principle).
> 4. **Connect a transparent pipe to the side of the tank ŌĆö the water rises inside the pipe to the same level as in the tank** (this is the **Hydrostatic Sight Tube** principle).
>
> All four methods **directly interact with the fluid surface** and give a reading that is *literally* the level. This is the essence of the **Direct Method**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics and physical constants govern all direct level measurement systems:

- **Density of water (Žü_water):** **1000 kg/m┬│** at **4 ┬░C** (standard reference temperature).
- **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** **9.80665 m/s┬▓** (standard gravitational acceleration).
- **Standard atmospheric pressure (P_atm):** **101.325 kPa** or **1.01325 ├Ś 10ŌüĄ N/m┬▓**.
- **Specific Gravity (SG):** Dimensionless ratio of fluid density to density of water at 4 ┬░C.
- **Unit of level:** **metre (m)**, **millimetre (mm)**, or **percentage (% of full scale)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Anchor for KTU Exams:** Whenever you see a hydrostatic level problem, the **Holy Trinity of Formulas** is **P = Žügh**, **F_buoyancy = Žü ├Ś g ├Ś V_displaced**, and **SG = Žü_fluid / Žü_water**. Memorise these ŌĆö they appear in nearly every numerical.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Direct Level Measurement Methods

The KTU 2024 syllabus recognises **five principal direct methods**. They are categorised by the *physical mechanism* used to sense the liquid surface.

### 2.1.1 Sight Glass (Gauge Glass) ŌĆö Visual Direct Reading

- **Principle:** A transparent glass tube or window is fitted externally to the side of the vessel, connected to the vessel at top and bottom. The liquid level inside the sight glass **automatically equalises** with the level inside the vessel (by the principle of communicating vessels).
- **Types:**
  - **Transparent Sight Glass:** Two flat parallel glass plates with a gasket-sealed chamber between them. Used for clear, non-corrosive liquids.
  - **Reflex Sight Glass:** A single glass plate with one face moulded into horizontal prismatic grooves. Light striking the glass above the liquid is reflected (so the glass appears silvery), but light is refracted at the liquid-glass interface below the surface, making the liquid section appear dark. This creates a sharp **black/silver contrast** for easy reading.
- **Working:** Hydrostatic equilibrium forces the liquid to seek the same horizontal level in both the vessel and the glass.
- **Real-world utility:** Boiler steam drums, refinery distillation columns, lube oil tanks.

### 2.1.2 Float-Type Level Measurement (Float-Operated)

- **Principle:** A **buoyant float** rests on the liquid surface. As the level rises or falls, the float moves vertically. The float is mechanically coupled (via a lever arm or a torque tube) to a **pointer or transducer**, producing a reading.
- **Force balance on the float:**

  The float is in equilibrium when **buoyancy force = weight of float**. As long as the float is fully submerged and the liquid has a stable density, the float simply tracks the surface.

- **Real-world utility:** Car fuel tanks, small chemical storage tanks, water reservoirs.

### 2.1.3 Tape Float Gauge (Float-and-Pulley Gauge)

- **Principle:** A float inside a chamber is connected via a **tape or wire** that passes over a **measuring pulley** and is balanced by a **counterweight** on the other side. As the float rises, the tape unwinds, rotating the pulley, and the **counterweight falls**. The rotation of the pulley is calibrated to read the level.
- **Equation governing pulley rotation:** If the float rises by a height $h$, the tape unwinds by length $h$, and the pulley rotates by an angle $\theta$:

  $$\theta = \frac{h}{r}$$

  where $r$ is the effective radius of the measuring pulley.
- **Real-world utility:** Large storage tanks, marine ballast tanks.

### 2.1.4 Chain Float Gauge (Float-and-Chain)

- **Principle:** A float is attached to a **graduated chain** that passes over a sprocket/wheel. A counterweight maintains tension. As the float moves, the chain moves, and the position is read off a graduated dial.
- **Difference from Tape Float:** The chain provides a **rigid, graduated scale** that is read directly ŌĆö no need for a tape counter.
- **Real-world utility:** Open-top tanks, sumps, and reservoirs in water treatment plants.

### 2.1.5 Dip Stick (Gauge Rod)

- **Principle:** A graduated metal rod is **dipped** into the liquid, withdrawn, and the **wetted length** is read against graduations on the rod.
- **Working:** The depth of immersion is equal to the depth of the liquid surface from the top reference.
- **Real-world utility:** Diesel tanks in generator rooms, lubricating oil sumps, fuel storage in depots.

### 2.1.6 Hydrostatic Head Type Level Measurement

- **Principle:** The **hydrostatic pressure** exerted by a column of liquid at a fixed depth below the surface is measured. This pressure is **linearly proportional to the liquid height**.
- **Governing equation (THE most important equation in this module):**

  $$P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

  where:
  - $P$ = hydrostatic pressure at the measurement point (Pa or N/m┬▓)
  - $\rho$ = density of the process liquid (kg/m┬│)
  - $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s┬▓**
  - $h$ = height of liquid column above the measurement point (m)

- **Why it works:** A column of liquid exerts pressure at its base due to its weight. The weight per unit area = (mass per unit volume) ├Ś g ├Ś height = $\rho g h$.
- **Bubbler Type (a special hydrostatic variant):** A purge gas (usually air or nitrogen) is bubbled slowly through a dip tube inserted into the vessel. The pressure required to maintain a steady bubble rate equals the hydrostatic head of the liquid above the dip tube outlet. The pressure is measured by a remote gauge.
- **Real-world utility:** Corrosive chemicals, slurries, sealed/pressurised vessels, refineries.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Equation / Concept** | **Mathematical Form** | **Variables & Units** | **Boundary / Validity Condition** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydrostatic Pressure | $P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | $P$ (Pa), $\rho$ (kg/m┬│), $g$ (9.81 m/s┬▓), $h$ (m) | Valid for static, incompressible liquid |
| 2 | Level from Pressure | $h = \dfrac{P}{\rho \cdot g}$ | Same as above | Must account for pressurised vessel head pressure |
| 3 | Buoyancy Force on Float | $F_b = \rho \cdot g \cdot V_{disp}$ | $F_b$ (N), $V_{disp}$ (m┬│) | Float must be fully submerged for linear output |
| 4 | Float Equilibrium | $F_{buoy} = W_{float}$ | $W_{float}$ (N) | Net upward force = 0 at rest |
| 5 | Specific Gravity | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{fluid}}{\rho_{water}}$ | Dimensionless | Reference: water at 4 ┬░C, $\rho_{water}$ = **1000 kg/m┬│** |
| 6 | Pulley Rotation (Tape Float) | $\theta = \dfrac{h}{r}$ | $\theta$ (rad), $r$ (m) | Tape assumed inextensible and slip-free |
| 7 | Pressure with Vessel Overpressure | $P_{gauge} = P_{atm} + \rho g h - P_{atm} = \rho g h$ | For open tank | Use differential pressure for pressurised tanks |
| 8 | Bubbler Pressure Equation | $P_{bubble} = \rho g h + P_{vessel-head}$ | $P_{vessel-head}$ (Pa) | Bubbler purge rate must be low & steady |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The hydrostatic equation is used universally in process plants ŌĆö from simple **differential pressure (DP) transmitters** on chemical reactors to **bubbler systems** in slurry tanks and **manometer readings** in legacy boiler installations. Almost every refinery on the KochiŌĆōMangalore industrial belt uses sight glasses on distillation columns because of their **zero calibration drift** and **intrinsic safety** (no electrical power required).

## 2.3 Why "Direct" and Not "Indirect"?

> [!TIP]
> **Key Differentiator (Frequently Asked in KTU 2-mark Questions):**
> - **Direct Method:** Sensing element is **in physical contact** with the fluid surface; output is **directly proportional** to level without any complex inferential computation.
> - **Indirect Method:** Level is **inferred** from another physical property ŌĆö e.g., pressure, capacitance, ultrasonic echo, radiation absorption, weight. The level itself is *not directly* measured.
>
> Example classification for KTU:
> - Direct ŌåÆ Sight glass, Float, Dip stick, Tape/Chain float
> - Indirect ŌåÆ Capacitance, Ultrasonic, Radar, Differential pressure (when used to *infer* level), Conductivity

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Hydrostatic Pressure to Level Conversion

### Problem Statement
A closed cylindrical storage tank contains a liquid of **specific gravity 1.25**. A hydrostatic pressure transmitter is mounted at the **bottom of the tank**, flush with the base. The transmitter reads a **gauge pressure of 49.05 kPa**. The tank is **open to atmosphere** (i.e., the vapour space is at atmospheric pressure). Calculate:
1. The height of the liquid column above the transmitter.
2. The level as a percentage of the tank's total height if the tank is **4 m tall**.

### Given Data
- Specific gravity, $SG = 1.25$
- Density of water, $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Density of liquid, $\rho_{fluid} = SG \times \rho_{water} = 1.25 \times 1000 = 1250 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Gravitational acceleration, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- Measured gauge pressure, $P_{gauge} = 49.05 \text{ kPa} = 49050 \text{ Pa}$
- Total tank height, $H_{tank} = 4 \text{ m}$

### Step 1: Identify the Governing Equation
For a static, incompressible liquid in an open tank, the hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ below the surface is:

$$P_{gauge} = \rho_{fluid} \cdot g \cdot h$$

> The "gauge" qualifier is used because the tank is open to atmosphere ŌĆö atmospheric pressure acts on **both** the top of the liquid and the reference side of the pressure transmitter, and therefore **cancels out**.

### Step 2: Rearrange to Solve for Level $h$

$$h = \frac{P_{gauge}}{\rho_{fluid} \cdot g}$$

### Step 3: Substitute Numerical Values

$$h = \frac{49050 \text{ Pa}}{1250 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2}$$

### Step 4: Denominator Evaluation

$$1250 \times 9.81 = 12262.5$$

> The unit check: $\text{kg/m}^3 \times \text{m/s}^2 = \text{kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s}^2) = \text{N/m}^2 = \text{Pa}$ Ō£ō

### Step 5: Final Calculation

$$h = \frac{49050}{12262.5} = 4.00 \text{ m}$$

### Step 6: Percentage of Full Tank

$$\% \text{ Level} = \frac{h}{H_{tank}} \times 100 = \frac{4.00}{4.00} \times 100 = 100\%$$

### Final Answer
> **The liquid level is 4.00 m, and the tank is exactly 100% full.**
>
> *(This also tells us the transmitter is mounted at the very bottom of the tank, so $h$ equals the absolute liquid level. If the transmitter were mounted, say, 0.5 m above the base, we would subtract 0.5 m.)*

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Buoyancy Force on a Float

### Problem Statement
A **cylindrical hollow float** of outer diameter **15 cm** and height **12 cm** is used in a float-type level gauge to measure the level of a **petroleum oil** of specific gravity **0.82**. The float is fabricated from stainless steel of density **7850 kg/m┬│** and has a uniform wall thickness of **2 mm**. Determine whether the float will:
1. **Float partially submerged** (and compute the submerged depth), or
2. **Sink fully** (i.e., is it operationally usable?).

### Given Data
- Outer diameter, $D_o = 15 \text{ cm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$
- Height of float, $H_f = 12 \text{ cm} = 0.12 \text{ m}$
- Wall thickness, $t = 2 \text{ mm} = 0.002 \text{ m}$
- Density of steel, $\rho_{steel} = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Density of oil, $\rho_{oil} = 0.82 \times 1000 = 820 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

### Step 1: Compute the Outer Volume of the Float

$$V_{outer} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D_o^2 \cdot H_f = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.15)^2 \times 0.12$$

$$V_{outer} = 0.7854 \times 0.0225 \times 0.12 = 2.1206 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 2: Compute the Inner Diameter

$$D_i = D_o - 2t = 0.15 - 2(0.002) = 0.146 \text{ m}$$

### Step 3: Compute the Inner Volume (Hollow Cavity)

$$V_{inner} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D_i^2 \cdot H_f = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.146)^2 \times 0.12$$

$$V_{inner} = 0.7854 \times 0.02132 \times 0.12 = 2.0096 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 4: Compute the Steel Material Volume (Wall + Top + Bottom Caps)

> Since the float is closed at both ends, the **material volume** equals outer volume minus inner volume.

$$V_{material} = V_{outer} - V_{inner} = 2.1206 \times 10^{-3} - 2.0096 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$V_{material} = 1.110 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 5: Compute the Weight of the Float

$$W_{float} = \rho_{steel} \cdot V_{material} \cdot g$$

$$W_{float} = 7850 \times 1.110 \times 10^{-4} \times 9.81$$

$$W_{float} = 8.546 \text{ N}$$

### Step 6: Set Up the Buoyancy Equilibrium for Partial Submersion
For the float to operate correctly as a level sensor, it must **partially submerge**, and the submerged depth $h_{sub}$ must adjust such that:

$$\text{Buoyancy Force} = \text{Weight of Float}$$

$$\rho_{oil} \cdot g \cdot V_{submerged} = W_{float}$$

where $V_{submerged} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D_o^2 \cdot h_{sub}$

### Step 7: Solve for Submerged Depth

$$h_{sub} = \frac{W_{float}}{\rho_{oil} \cdot g \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D_o^2}$$

$$h_{sub} = \frac{8.546}{820 \times 9.81 \times 0.7854 \times (0.15)^2}$$

Denominator evaluation:

$$820 \times 9.81 = 8044.2$$

$$0.7854 \times 0.0225 = 0.01767$$

$$8044.2 \times 0.01767 = 142.14$$

$$h_{sub} = \frac{8.546}{142.14} = 0.0601 \text{ m} = 6.01 \text{ cm}$$

### Step 8: Validity Check
Since $h_{sub} = 6.01 \text{ cm} < H_f = 12 \text{ cm}$, the float is **partially submerged** at approximately **50% immersion**. The float will track the liquid surface linearly as the level changes.

### Final Answer
> **The float is operationally usable and will float with 6.01 cm of its 12 cm height submerged (i.e., 50.1% immersion).** It will rise and fall with the oil surface, providing a linear level measurement.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Direct Methods (KTU Table-Worthy)

| **Method** | **Measured Quantity** | **Output Type** | **Maximum Range (Typical)** | **Accuracy** | **Limitations** | **Best-Suited Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sight Glass (Transparent) | Visual level | Direct visual | 0ŌĆō3 m | ┬▒1ŌĆō2 mm | Fragile, needs cleaning, pressure/temp limited | Boiler drums, atmospheric tanks |
| Sight Glass (Reflex) | Visual level | Direct visual | 0ŌĆō3 m | ┬▒1ŌĆō2 mm | Same as above | Distillation columns, refinery vessels |
| Float-Operated | Mechanical displacement | Pointer/4ŌĆō20 mA | 0ŌĆō5 m | ┬▒0.5% FS | Affected by surface turbulence, density variation | Water tanks, fuel tanks |
| Tape Float | Tape length | Mechanical counter | 0ŌĆō20 m | ┬▒2ŌĆō5 mm | Mechanical wear of tape/pulley | Large storage tanks |
| Chain Float | Chain position | Graduated dial | 0ŌĆō10 m | ┬▒5 mm | Chain sag, density dependence | Open reservoirs, sumps |
| Dip Stick | Wetted length | Manual reading | 0ŌĆō10 m | ┬▒2ŌĆō5 mm | Not continuous, no remote read-out | Diesel tanks, small sumps |
| Hydrostatic (DP Cell) | Pressure | 4ŌĆō20 mA / 3ŌĆō15 psi | 0ŌĆō50 m (with correction) | ┬▒0.1% FS | Density change introduces error | Corrosive/pressurised chemical tanks |
| Bubbler Type | Bubbler back-pressure | 4ŌĆō20 mA / pneumatic | 0ŌĆō30 m | ┬▒0.5% FS | Plugging of dip tube, purge gas consumption | Slurries, dirty liquids, molten metals |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Always carry this comparative table in your memory. KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Compare float-type and sight glass level measurement"* or *"Justify which direct method is best for a corrosive chemical."*

## 4.1 Sight Glass ŌĆö Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD

    A1 -->|Hydrostatic Connection - Bottom| B1
```

**Interpretation:** The vessel and the sight glass chamber form a **U-tube communicating vessel system**. Hydrostatic equilibrium forces the liquid to the same level in both chambers. The operator reads the level off the graduated scale on the side of the sight glass.

## 4.2 Float-Type Level Measurement ŌĆö Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A2[Liquid Surface in Vessel] -->|Floats on Top| B2[Buoyant Float]

    D2 -->|Pointer Position| E2[Level Reading in cm or percent]

```

**Interpretation:** The float physically contacts the liquid surface. As the surface moves, the float moves, and the mechanical linkage converts the vertical motion into a rotary pointer deflection on a calibrated dial.

## 4.3 Tape Float Gauge ŌĆö Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A3[Liquid in Vessel] -->|Supports Float| B3[Float in Chamber]

    C3 -->|Tape Tension Maintained| F3[Counterweight on Other Side of Pulley]

    style F3 fill:#d62728,color:#fff
```

**Interpretation:** The float's vertical motion unwinds the tape over the pulley, whose rotation is read on a calibrated scale. The counterweight keeps the tape taut and balanced.

## 4.4 Hydrostatic Head / Bubbler Type ŌĆö Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A4[Liquid Column of Height h] -->|Hydrostatic Pressure P equals rho g h| B4[Pressure at Bottom]
    B4 -->|Measured by| C4[Pressure Transmitter or Manometer]
    C4 -->|Signal 4 to 20 mA or 3 to 15 psi| D4[Indicator / Controller / Recorder]
    A4 -.Bubbler Variant.-> E4[Purge Gas Supply]

```

**Interpretation:** The hydrostatic column generates a pressure at the bottom equal to $\rho g h$. A pressure transmitter converts this to a standard signal. In the **bubbler variant**, a regulated purge gas is forced through a dip tube; the back-pressure required to maintain a slow bubble rate equals the hydrostatic head ŌĆö the transmitter then measures this back-pressure.

```mermaid
graph TD
```

**Interpretation:** The simplest direct method ŌĆö purely manual. The rod is dipped, withdrawn, and the wetted boundary is read against a graduated scale.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2023, Set B]
**Define level measurement. List any four direct methods of level measurement.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Level measurement is the determination of the position of the interface between two fluids ŌĆö typically a liquid and a gas/vapour phase ŌĆö with respect to a fixed reference datum inside a process vessel. The output may be expressed as a linear distance (metres) or as a percentage of the full-scale range.

**Four Direct Methods (┬Į Mark Each, Total 2 Marks):**
1. **Sight Glass / Gauge Glass** ŌĆö direct visual reading of the liquid level through a transparent glass tube connected to the vessel.
2. **Float-Type Level Measurement** ŌĆö a buoyant float rests on the liquid surface and its vertical displacement is read mechanically or converted to an electrical signal.
3. **Dip Stick** ŌĆö a graduated rod is dipped into the liquid; the wetted length indicates the level.
4. **Hydrostatic Head Method** ŌĆö the pressure exerted by the liquid column at a fixed depth is measured and converted to level using $h = P/(\rho g)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuator's Note:** Students often confuse "tape float" and "float" as the same method. The examiner expects **distinct, named methods**, not a single method with sub-variants. Award full marks only if all four are different categories.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō December 2022]
**State Archimedes' principle and explain how it is applied in float-type level measurement.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Archimedes' Principle (1┬Į Marks):** When a body is wholly or partially immersed in a fluid, it experiences an **upward buoyant force** equal to the **weight of the fluid displaced** by the body. Mathematically:

$$F_b = \rho_{fluid} \cdot g \cdot V_{displaced}$$

where $\rho_{fluid}$ is the fluid density, $g$ is gravitational acceleration, and $V_{displaced}$ is the volume of the fluid displaced by the immersed portion of the body.

**Application to Float-Type Level Measurement (1┬Į Marks):** A buoyant float is placed on the liquid surface. The float is designed such that its weight is less than the maximum possible buoyancy force (i.e., it floats). As the liquid level rises, the float rises with it; as the level falls, the float descends. The vertical motion of the float is coupled mechanically (via a lever, torque tube, or tape) to a pointer or transducer, which provides a reading proportional to the liquid level. **The float is in equilibrium when the buoyancy force equals the weight of the float.**

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Students who write only the verbal definition of Archimedes' principle (1 mark) and skip the *application part* (1.5 marks) tend to score only 1 out of 3. Always explicitly link the principle to the **mechanism of level sensing**.

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B carries **14 marks** with **internal choice**. You must answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full.

### ­¤ōś Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **sight glass type level measurement** system. Compare the **transparent** and **reflex** types of sight glass. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A closed storage tank contains a liquid of **specific gravity 1.18**. A hydrostatic pressure transmitter is installed at a height of **0.4 m above the bottom** of the tank. The transmitter reads a **gauge pressure of 32.36 kPa**. The tank is **sealed** and the vapour space is pressurised to **15 kPa gauge**. Calculate the **true liquid level** in the tank. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) ŌĆö Sight Glass Working (7 Marks)

**Working Principle (2 Marks):**
A sight glass is a **transparent tube or chamber** fitted externally to the side of a process vessel. It is connected to the vessel via **two fittings** ŌĆö one at the top and one at the bottom. Because both the vessel and the sight glass form a **communicating vessel (U-tube) system**, the liquid level inside the sight glass is **always equal** to the level inside the vessel (by hydrostatic equilibrium). The operator reads the level directly off a **graduated scale** marked on the glass.

**Block Diagram (2 Marks):**
The diagram (see SECTION 4.1) shows:
- Vessel with process liquid
- Sight glass chamber externally mounted
- Liquid interface visible through the glass
- Graduated scale on the sight glass
- Operator reads the level

**Comparison Table (3 Marks):**

| **Feature** | **Transparent Sight Glass** | **Reflex Sight Glass** |
|---|---|---|
| Construction | Two flat parallel glass plates with a chamber between | Single glass plate with one face moulded into horizontal prismatic grooves |
| Working | Direct visual reading through clear glass | Light reflects above liquid (silvery) and refracts at liquid (dark), giving sharp contrast |
| Best For | Clear, colourless, non-corrosive liquids | Coloured, opaque, or slightly dirty liquids |
| Cost | Lower | Higher (precision glass moulding) |
| Maintenance | Easy to clean both sides | Cleaning the grooved side is harder |
| Maximum Pressure | Lower | Slightly higher (single piece) |

---

#### Model Solution to (b) ŌĆö Hydrostatic Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Specific gravity, $SG = 1.18$
- Density of liquid, $\rho_{fluid} = 1.18 \times 1000 = 1180 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $P_{gauge,transmitter} = 32.36 \text{ kPa} = 32360 \text{ Pa}$
- $P_{vessel-head} = 15 \text{ kPa} = 15000 \text{ Pa}$ (gauge)
- Transmitter mounted at height $h_{0} = 0.4 \text{ m}$ above tank bottom

**Step 1: Decompose the Transmitter Pressure**

The transmitter measures the **total pressure** at its location, which is the sum of the **vessel head pressure** and the **hydrostatic head** of the liquid column above the transmitter:

$$P_{gauge,transmitter} = P_{vessel-head} + \rho_{fluid} \cdot g \cdot h_{liquid-above-transmitter}$$

**Step 2: Substitute and Rearrange**

$$h_{liquid-above-transmitter} = \frac{P_{gauge,transmitter} - P_{vessel-head}}{\rho_{fluid} \cdot g}$$

**Step 3: Numerator Calculation**

$$P_{gauge,transmitter} - P_{vessel-head} = 32360 - 15000 = 17360 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 4: Denominator Calculation**

$$\rho_{fluid} \cdot g = 1180 \times 9.81 = 11575.8 \text{ N/m}^3$$

**Step 5: Submerged Depth of Transmitter from Liquid Surface**

$$h_{liquid-above-transmitter} = \frac{17360}{11575.8} = 1.500 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6: True Liquid Level above Tank Bottom**

The transmitter is mounted 0.4 m above the tank bottom, so the true liquid level above the bottom is:

$$H_{true} = h_{liquid-above-transmitter} + h_0 = 1.500 + 0.400 = 1.900 \text{ m}$$

### Final Answer (Part b)
> **The true liquid level in the tank is 1.90 m above the bottom.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract vessel head pressure** ŌĆö Many students directly use $h = P / (\rho g)$ without considering the sealed-tank pressurisation. This gives the wrong answer of 2.80 m instead of 1.90 m. **Deduct 2 marks.**
> 2. **Not adding the transmitter mounting height** at the end ŌĆö The hydrostatic head $h$ is the depth of liquid **above the transmitter**, not the level above the tank bottom. Always check what the calculated $h$ represents geometrically. **Deduct 1 mark.**
> 3. **Unit errors** ŌĆö Mixing up kPa and Pa, or forgetting to convert SG to density. **Deduct 0.5 mark per error.**

---

### ­¤ōŚ Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō December 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of a **float-type level measurement system**. Derive the **buoyancy equilibrium condition** for the float. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the **bubbler-type level measurement system**. State its advantages over direct hydrostatic methods. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) ŌĆö Float-Type Level Measurement (7 Marks)

**Working Principle (2 Marks):**
A float-type level measurement system uses a **buoyant float** that rests on the surface of the process liquid. As the liquid level changes, the float moves vertically. The float is mechanically linked (via a lever arm, torque tube, or rope) to a **pointer or transducer**, which provides a reading proportional to the level. The float is designed such that it is **partially submerged** in its normal operating condition.

**Block Diagram (2 Marks):**
See SECTION 4.2 ŌĆö Float rises with liquid, mechanical linkage to a lever/pulley, pointer deflects on a graduated dial, operator reads level.

**Buoyancy Equilibrium Derivation (3 Marks):**
The float has a weight $W_f$ acting downward. The buoyancy force $F_b$ acts upward. For the float to be in **static equilibrium**:

$$\sum F_{vertical} = 0$$

$$F_b - W_f = 0$$

$$\rho_{fluid} \cdot g \cdot V_{submerged} = m_{float} \cdot g$$

$$\rho_{fluid} \cdot V_{submerged} = m_{float}$$

If the float is **cylindrical** with cross-sectional area $A$ and submerged depth $h_{sub}$:

$$\rho_{fluid} \cdot A \cdot h_{sub} = m_{float}$$

$$h_{sub} = \frac{m_{float}}{\rho_{fluid} \cdot A}$$

This submerged depth is **constant** (as long as the float does not bottom out or emerge completely), meaning the **top of the float** tracks the liquid surface linearly.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) ŌĆö Bubbler-Type Level Measurement (7 Marks)

**Working Principle (3 Marks):**
In a bubbler-type level measurement system, a **regulated purge gas** (typically clean air or nitrogen) is supplied at a low, constant flow rate into a **dip tube** that extends into the process vessel. The gas escapes as a **slow stream of bubbles** from the bottom outlet of the tube, which must be submerged below the liquid surface. The **back-pressure** required to maintain this steady bubble flow is equal to the **hydrostatic pressure** of the liquid column above the dip tube outlet. This back-pressure is measured by a **pressure transmitter** (or a manometer in simpler systems) and is converted to a level reading.

**Governing Equation (2 Marks):**

$$P_{bubble} = \rho_{fluid} \cdot g \cdot h + P_{vessel-head}$$

Therefore, the level is:

$$h = \frac{P_{bubble} - P_{vessel-head}}{\rho_{fluid} \cdot g}$$

**Block Diagram (1 Mark):**
See SECTION 4.4 ŌĆö Bubbler variant. Purge gas ŌåÆ dip tube ŌåÆ bubbles at outlet ŌåÆ back-pressure measured by transmitter.

**Advantages of Bubbler over Direct Hydrostatic (1 Mark):**
1. **No direct contact between the transmitter and the process fluid** ŌĆö the transmitter only measures the gas pressure, so it is **immune to corrosion, fouling, and clogging** of the sensing line.
2. **Ideal for slurries, viscous fluids, and corrosive chemicals** ŌĆö the dip tube is the only wetted part, and it can be made of any compatible material (PTFE, SS316, Hastelloy).
3. **Safe for hazardous and explosive environments** ŌĆö only inert purge gas enters the vessel, with **no electrical components in contact with the process fluid**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. Students often draw a bubbler diagram showing the **pressure transmitter directly submerged** in the liquid ŌĆö this is **incorrect**. The transmitter is on the *supply side* of the gas, measuring the **back-pressure**, not the liquid directly. **Deduct 2 marks.**
> 2. Confusing the bubbler with a **dip tube manometer** ŌĆö they are different. In a bubbler, gas actively flows; in a manometer, it is static. **Deduct 1 mark.**
> 3. Missing the **"$\rho$" (density)** in the formula when stating the bubbler equation ŌĆö the level depends on density, so the formula must include it. **Deduct 0.5 mark.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **­¤ÜĆ Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö KTU Module 2: Direct Level Measurement**

### ­¤ö╣ Core Definitions
- **Direct Method:** Sensor **physically contacts** the liquid surface; output is **linearly proportional** to level.
- **Archimedes' Principle:** Buoyant force = weight of fluid displaced = $\rho g V_{disp}$.
- **Hydrostatic Head:** Pressure at depth = $\rho g h$.

### ­¤ö╣ Key Equations (Must Memorise)
- $P = \rho g h$ ŌĆö **THE** hydrostatic equation.
- $h = P / (\rho g)$ ŌĆö for an **open tank** only.
- $h = (P_{transmitter} - P_{head}) / (\rho g)$ ŌĆö for a **pressurised/closed tank** (DP cell needed).
- $F_b = \rho g V_{submerged}$ ŌĆö Archimedes' principle in action.
- $W_{float} = F_b$ ŌĆö Float equilibrium condition.
- $\theta = h / r$ ŌĆö Tape float pulley rotation.
- $P_{bubble} = \rho g h + P_{vessel-head}$ ŌĆö Bubbler equation.

### ­¤ö╣ Method-by-Method Summary
- **Sight Glass:** Communicating-vessel principle. **Two types** ŌĆö Transparent (clean liquids) and Reflex (coloured/dirty liquids). **Pressure- and temperature-limited.**
- **Float-Type:** Buoyant float + mechanical linkage. **Accurate, simple, but affected by surface turbulence and density changes.**
- **Tape Float:** Float + tape over pulley + counterweight. **Best for large tanks (up to 20 m).**
- **Chain Float:** Float + graduated chain. **Open reservoirs.**
- **Dip Stick:** Manual, wetted-rod method. **No remote read-out, no continuous monitoring.**
- **Hydrostatic Head:** Pressure transmitter at the bottom. **Direct method; requires density compensation.**
- **Bubbler:** Purge gas + back-pressure measurement. **Indirect but uses the same hydrostatic principle; ideal for corrosive/slurry service.**

### ­¤ö╣ Standard Constants
- $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (at 4 ┬░C)
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$

### ­¤ö╣ Common KTU Board Pitfalls to Avoid
- ŌØī Treating float-type as a "displacement" or "buoyancy" sensor only ŌĆö it is **level-specific** by design.
- ŌØī Forgetting the **vessel head pressure** correction in pressurised tanks.
- ŌØī Confusing **transmitter mounting height** with **true liquid level**.
- ŌØī Writing **$|x|$** inside a table ŌĆö use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown.
- ŌØī Using **$P = \rho h$** without $g$ ŌĆö always include $g = 9.81$ m/s┬▓.

### ­¤ö╣ Selection Checklist (For "Justify the Best Method" Type Questions)
- **Open tank, clear liquid, low pressure** ŌåÆ Sight Glass.
- **Open tank, dirty/coloured liquid, atmospheric** ŌåÆ Float-Type.
- **Large tank (10ŌĆō20 m)** ŌåÆ Tape Float.
- **Corrosive or pressurised** ŌåÆ Hydrostatic DP Cell.
- **Slurry, molten metal, very dirty** ŌåÆ Bubbler.
- **Manual, intermittent check** ŌåÆ Dip Stick.
- **Remote read-out required** ŌåÆ Float with 4ŌĆō20 mA transmitter, or Hydrostatic DP Cell.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Level in Open and Pressure Vessels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: MEASUREMENT OF LEVEL IN OPEN AND PRESSURE VESSELS

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

**Level Measurement** is defined as the determination of the position or height of the free surface (interface) of a process material ŌĆö typically a liquid, slurry, or bulk solid ŌĆö relative to a reference datum inside a process vessel, tank, or silo. The reference datum is usually the bottom of the vessel (true level) or an arbitrary process-defined zero mark.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Standard):** "Level is the height of the surface of a liquid or bulk solid in a vessel measured from a horizontal reference plane. The measurement may be expressed as a percentage of full scale (% FSD), in units of length (mm, m), or volume (litres, m┬│) when the vessel cross-section is known."

### Classification of Process Vessels

| Vessel Type | Description | Typical Examples |
|-------------|-------------|------------------|
| **Open Vessel (Atmospheric)** | Top exposed to atmosphere; pressure head = 0 gauge. Headspace is at ambient pressure. | Water reservoirs, sumps, open tanks, storage pools |
| **Pressure Vessel (Pressurized)** | Sealed container operating above or below atmospheric pressure; headspace is non-atmospheric. | Boiler drums, reactor vessels, LPG bullets, distillation columns |

> [!NOTE]
> The classification of the vessel is the **first design parameter** when selecting a level sensor because it directly determines whether the instrument must compensate for vapor pressure, temperature, and density variations of the gas/vapour space above the liquid.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine filling a **transparent water bottle** (open vessel) and a **pressurized LPG cylinder** (pressure vessel) with water.

- In the **water bottle**, you can simply dip a ruler from the top because air is freely exchanged with the atmosphere. A small tube (bubbler) at the bottom that you gently blow into will also work ŌĆö the harder you have to blow, the more water is in the bottle. This is **hydrostatic head** measurement.

- In the **LPG cylinder**, you cannot dip a ruler because the valve is sealed. The pressure of the gas above the liquid adds to the water pressure at the bottom. To know the liquid level, you must **subtract the gas pressure** from the total pressure at the bottom, or use a **non-invasive method** such as bouncing a sound wave (ultrasonic) off the liquid surface from the outside.

This single contrast ŌĆö *direct exposure vs sealed pressurization* ŌĆö governs the entire choice of level-measurement technology.

### 1.3 Importance of Level Measurement in Process Industries

Level measurement is the **second most measured variable** in process plants (after temperature) because it is fundamental to:
- **Inventory management** in storage tanks
- **Process safety** (preventing tank overflow and dry-running of pumps)
- **Mass/Volume balance** in batch and continuous reactors
- **Two-phase interface detection** (e.g., oil-water, foam-liquid)
- **Compliance** with statutory regulations (custody transfer, API 2350 overfill protection)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Unit:** The SI unit of level is **metres (m)**, though millimetres (mm) and percentage of full-scale deflection (% FSD) are common in industrial display standards such as **IEC 61511** and **ISA 5.1**.

### 1.4 Broad Classification of Level Measurement Methods

```
LEVEL MEASUREMENT
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ 1. DIRECT METHODS
Ōöé     Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Sight glass / Gauge glass
Ōöé     Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Float-type indicator
Ōöé     Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Dip stick / Tape
Ōöé     ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ Hook gauge
Ōöé
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ 2. INDIRECT METHODS (Inference-based)
Ōöé     Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Hydrostatic Pressure
Ōöé     Ōöé     Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Bubbler system
Ōöé     Ōöé     Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Differential Pressure (DP) transmitter
Ōöé     Ōöé     ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ Diaphragm seal with capillary
Ōöé     Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Buoyancy
Ōöé     Ōöé     Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Displacer-type
Ōöé     Ōöé     ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ Float-and-cable
Ōöé     Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Electrical
Ōöé     Ōöé     Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Capacitance / RF admittance
Ōöé     Ōöé     ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ Conductivity (resistance)
Ōöé     Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Acoustic
Ōöé     Ōöé     Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Ultrasonic (through-air)
Ōöé     Ōöé     ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ Sonic (vibrating fork)
Ōöé     Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Electromagnetic
Ōöé     Ōöé     Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Radar (FMCW & Pulsed)
Ōöé     Ōöé     Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Laser triangulation
Ōöé     Ōöé     ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ Guided Wave Radar (GWR)
Ōöé     ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ Radiation (Nuclear)
Ōöé           Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Gamma transmission
Ōöé           ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ Gamma backscatter
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Direct Methods of Level Measurement

### 2.1.1 Sight Glass (Gauge Glass)

A sight glass is a **transparent tube or pair of transparent panels** fitted vertically to the outside wall of a vessel so that the liquid level inside the vessel becomes visible from the outside.

**Operating Principle:** Communicating vessels principle ŌĆö the liquid in the sight glass settles at the same height as the liquid in the main vessel because the glass is hydraulically connected via top and bottom isolation valves.

**Components:**
- **Reflex gauge glass:** Uses internal prism grooves that reflect light in air spaces and transmit light in liquid spaces ŌåÆ dark band = liquid, bright band = vapour.
- **Transparent gauge glass:** Plain flat glass for clear liquids.
- **Tubular gauge glass:** Long cylindrical tube with toughened glass; used for moderate pressure/temperature.
- **Illuminated gauge glass:** Back-lit versions for low-light conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Reflex gauge glass is the **most common direct method** for steam drums and boiler water columns. It can operate up to **300 bar** and **540 ┬░C** with Klinger reflex glass.

**Advantages:** Simple, no power, intrinsically safe, very accurate (┬▒1 mm).
**Limitations:** Glass is fragile; not suitable for highly viscous, opaque, toxic, or corrosive fluids; requires regular cleaning.

### 2.1.2 Float-Type Level Indicator

A **float** ŌĆö a hollow, leak-proof, weighted ball ŌĆö rests on the liquid surface and moves up/down with the level. The float is mechanically linked (via lever, cable, or magnetic coupling) to a pointer or a transmitter.

**Working:** As the level changes, the float displaces vertically. The mechanical motion is converted to:
- A **pointer deflection** (local indication), or
- A **pneumatic signal** (3ŌĆō15 psi), or
- A **4ŌĆō20 mA electronic signal** via a magnetostrictive or reed-chain transducer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Float & Cable System:** A common industrial variant. A buoyant float hangs from a cable wound around a measuring drum. A servo motor keeps the cable taut; the drum rotation angle is proportional to level. Used in large storage tanks (up to **50 m** height).

### 2.1.3 Dip Stick and Hook Gauge

The **dip stick** is a graduated rod or tape lowered into the vessel and read directly. The **hook gauge** uses a pointed hook that is raised until the tip just breaks the liquid surface (meniscus). It offers **┬▒0.1 mm** accuracy and is used for laboratory calibration and stilling wells.

## 2.2 Indirect Methods ŌĆö Theoretical Framework

### 2.2.1 Hydrostatic Head Method (Open & Pressure Vessels)

The hydrostatic head method is the **most widely used industrial level technique**, exploiting Pascal's law: the pressure at the bottom of a liquid column is proportional to its height.

**For an Open Vessel:**

$$P_{bottom} = \rho \, g \, h$$

where $P_{bottom}$ is the gauge pressure at the bottom tap, $\rho$ is the liquid density (kg/m┬│), $g$ is gravitational acceleration (9.80665 m/s┬▓), and $h$ is the liquid height (m).

**For a Pressure Vessel (Closed Tank):**

When the gas/vapour space above the liquid exerts a pressure $P_{vessel}$ on the liquid surface, the pressure at the **bottom tap** becomes:

$$P_{bottom} = P_{vessel} + \rho \, g \, h$$

A **Differential Pressure (DP) transmitter** is used to cancel $P_{vessel}$:

$$\Delta P = P_{bottom} - P_{vessel} = \rho \, g \, h$$

This $\Delta P$ is proportional to the true level $h$, independent of the operating pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Always mention **density compensation** when the process fluid density $\rho$ changes with temperature. The corrected level is $h = \Delta P / (\rho \, g)$.

**Installation Configurations for Pressure Vessels:**

1. **Wet Leg (Condensate Leg):** The high-pressure (HP) tap is at the top of the vessel, the low-pressure (LP) tap is at the bottom. The HP line is filled with the condensate of the process vapour, providing a known reference column. Used for **vessels with steam or hot vapours**.
2. **Dry Leg (Dry Reference Leg):** The HP line is filled with an inert gas (typically nitrogen). Used when the process vapour is **corrosive, condensible, or when a wet leg is impractical**. The DP transmitter must be mounted **below the bottom tap** to avoid gas bubbling into the impulse line.
3. **Balanced Bellows / Diaphragm Seal:** A flexible diaphragm with capillary oil-filled connection isolates the DP transmitter from corrosive, hot, or viscous fluids. Used for **slurries and aggressive chemicals**.

### 2.2.2 Bubbler System (Open or Sealed Vessels)

A constant, low flow rate of air (or inert gas) is bubbled through a dip tube immersed in the tank. The back-pressure required to push the gas out of the tube equals the hydrostatic head of liquid above the tube outlet:

$$P_{bubble} = \rho \, g \, h + P_{headspace}$$

The bubble pressure is measured by a pressure transmitter. This method is **ideal for slurries, sewage, and corrosive liquids** because the sensor never contacts the process fluid.

> [!NOTE]
> The bubbling flow must be **laminar** (typically 1ŌĆō3 cm┬│/s) to avoid dynamic pressure errors. A constant-flow regulator with critical orifice ensures repeatability.

### 2.2.3 Displacer-Type (Buoyancy) Method

A **displacer** is a heavy, sealed cylinder (typically denser than the process liquid at its bottom position) suspended in the liquid. By Archimedes' principle, the buoyant force equals the weight of the displaced liquid:

$$F_{buoyancy} = \rho \, g \, V_{displacer}$$

As the level rises, more of the displacer is submerged, increasing $F_{buoyancy}$ and reducing the net downward force on the suspension. The net force is balanced by a **range tube (torque tube)** that converts it into a proportional pneumatic (3ŌĆō15 psi) or electronic (4ŌĆō20 mA) signal.

**Key advantage:** The displacer's apparent weight is determined by *how much* of it is submerged, not by the absolute liquid level. This makes it **immune to foam, turbulence, and surface waves** (since it integrates over a large vertical extent).

### 2.2.4 Capacitance Method

A **probe** (single rod, twin rod, or coaxial) is inserted vertically into the vessel. The probe and the vessel wall (ground) form the two plates of a cylindrical capacitor. The dielectric between them is partly **air/vapour** ($\varepsilon_{air} \approx 1$) and partly **process fluid** ($\varepsilon_{liquid} = 2$ to 80+).

**For a Coaxial (Concentric) Capacitor with Length $L$ Submerged in Liquid:**

$$C = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L}{\ln(R/r)}$$

**For a Single Rod in a Vessel (Rod-Wall Capacitor):**

The capacitance is the sum of two parts: (i) the air portion, and (ii) the liquid portion:

$$C = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0}{\ln(R/r)} \left[ \varepsilon_{air}(L - h) + \varepsilon_{liq} \, h \right]$$

where $R$ is the effective radius of the vessel wall, $r$ is the rod radius, and $h$ is the submerged length.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linearization:** Most capacitance probes use **active shielding (guarded electrode)** to eliminate the effect of coating build-up on the probe. RF admittance technique measures conductance and capacitance simultaneously, rejecting conductive coating errors.

### 2.2.5 Ultrasonic Level Measurement

A piezoelectric transducer emits a short burst of high-frequency sound (typically **20 kHz to 200 kHz**) that travels through the gas/vapour space, reflects off the liquid surface, and returns to the transducer. The round-trip time $t$ is measured:

$$h = \frac{c \, t}{2}$$

where $c$ is the velocity of sound in the gas (Ōēł 340 m/s in air at 20 ┬░C) and $h$ is the distance from the transducer to the surface. The total tank height $H$ minus $h$ gives the liquid level.

**Sources of Error:** Temperature gradients change $c$ (Ōēł 0.17 % per ┬░C); foam absorbs sound waves; heavy vapours deflect the beam; **dead zone** near the transducer (typically 0.25ŌĆō1 m blanking distance).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** Ultrasonic sensors are **non-contact** and ideal for corrosive, sticky, or hygienic fluids. They **cannot** measure in vacuum, and their accuracy degrades above **~30 m** height.

### 2.2.6 Radar Level Measurement (FMCW & Pulsed)

Radar uses electromagnetic waves in the microwave region (typically **6 GHz, 10 GHz, or 26 GHz**). It overcomes the foam/temperature limitations of ultrasonics.

**Pulsed Time-Domain Reflectometry (TDR) / Guided Wave Radar (GWR):**
A low-energy pulse propagates down a **coaxial probe, twin rod, or single rod**, and the time of the reflection at the liquid surface is measured:

$$h = \frac{c_{cable} \, t}{2}$$

where $c_{cable}$ is the velocity along the probe (about 80% of free-space light speed).

**FMCW (Frequency-Modulated Continuous Wave) Non-Contact Radar:**
The frequency of the transmitted wave is linearly swept over a band $\Delta f$ (typically 1 GHz). The received signal is mixed with the instantaneous transmitted signal, producing a difference (IF) frequency $f_{IF}$:

$$f_{IF} = \frac{2 \, \Delta f \, R}{c \, T_{sweep}}$$

where $R$ is the distance to the surface, $c$ is the speed of light in the gas, and $T_{sweep}$ is the sweep period. The IF frequency is converted to distance by an FFT.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Non-contact radar is unaffected by **temperature, pressure, dust, foam, and vapour** ŌĆö only by the **dielectric constant $\varepsilon_r$** of the liquid. For low-dielectric liquids (hydrocarbons, $\varepsilon_r < 3$), **stillwells / bypass chambers** are used to enhance reflection.

### 2.2.7 Conductivity (Resistance) Method

Two electrodes are mounted in the vessel at the desired "high" and "low" levels. When the conductive liquid contacts the electrode, it completes a low-resistance circuit and triggers a relay. Used in **boiler water-level alarms** and **sumps**.

### 2.2.8 Radiation (Nuclear) Method

A radioactive source (typically **Cs-137** or **Co-60**) is mounted on one side of the vessel, and a **scintillation detector** or Geiger-M├╝ller tube on the opposite side. The intensity of gamma radiation transmitted through the vessel is:

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu \, \rho \, x}$$

where $\mu$ is the mass attenuation coefficient. When the liquid level rises, the path length $x$ increases and $I$ decreases.

> [!WARNING]
> **HSE Caution:** Nuclear level measurement requires **ATOMIC ENERGY REGULATORY BOARD (AERB) licence** in India and is reserved for **extreme service** (molten metal, high-pressure reactor vessels, aggressive chemicals).

### 2.2.9 Laser Triangulation

A laser beam is projected onto the surface; the reflected spot is imaged onto a position-sensitive detector (PSD). The spot's position on the PSD relates geometrically to the surface height. Used for **solids (silos, hoppers)** and **short-range** applications due to eye-safety constraints.

### 2.2.10 Weight Method (Load Cells)

For very large tanks, the entire vessel is mounted on **strain-gauge load cells**. The mass of the tank plus its contents is converted to weight, and the level is derived from the mass change:

$$m_{liquid} = W_{total} - W_{empty}$$

$$h = \frac{m_{liquid}}{\rho \, A_{tank}}$$

Used in **custody transfer, LNG tanks**, and **large chemical storage**.

---

## KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (CHEAT SHEET)

| # | Method | Governing Equation | Key Variables | Typical Range / Units |
|---|--------|-------------------|---------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Hydrostatic (open) | $P = \rho g h$ | $P$: pressure, $\rho$: density, $g$: 9.80665 m/s┬▓, $h$: level | $h$ in m, $P$ in Pa |
| 2 | Hydrostatic (pressurized) | $\Delta P = P_{bottom} - P_{vessel} = \rho g h$ | $\Delta P$: differential pressure | Independent of $P_{vessel}$ |
| 3 | Bubbler | $P_{bubble} = \rho g h + P_{headspace}$ | $P_{bubble}$ measured | Flow Ōēł 1ŌĆō3 cm┬│/s |
| 4 | Buoyancy (displacer) | $F_{b} = \rho g V_{disp}$ | $V_{disp}$: displacer volume | Net force ŌåÆ torque tube |
| 5 | Capacitance (coaxial) | $C = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L}{\ln(R/r)}$ | $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m | C in Farads |
| 6 | Capacitance (rod) | $C = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0}{\ln(R/r)} \left[ \varepsilon_{air}(L - h) + \varepsilon_{liq} h \right]$ | $\varepsilon_{air}=1, \varepsilon_{liq}$: 2ŌĆō80 | Linear in $h$ |
| 7 | Ultrasonic | $h = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | $c$: speed of sound Ōēł 340 m/s | $c$ depends on T |
| 8 | Radar (FMCW) | $f_{IF} = \frac{2 \Delta f \cdot R}{c \cdot T_{sweep}}$ | $\Delta f$: sweep bandwidth | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| 9 | Radar (GWR/TDR) | $h = \frac{c_{cable} \cdot t}{2}$ | $c_{cable} \approx 0.8 \, c$ | Time-domain |
| 10 | Conductivity | $R = \frac{\rho_{liq} \cdot L}{A}$ | Liquids with $\sigma > 5 \, \mu S/cm$ | Point/limit switch |
| 11 | Radiation (BeerŌĆōLambert) | $I = I_0 e^{-\mu \rho x}$ | $\mu$: attenuation coefficient | Cs-137, Co-60 |
| 12 | Weight | $h = \frac{m_{liq}}{\rho \, A_{tank}}$ | $A$: cross-sectional area | Large tanks |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Conversion:** $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 10.197 \text{ mH}_2\text{O at 4 ┬░C}$. Many DP transmitters are calibrated in **mHŌééO** for level service.

## 2.3 Selection Criteria for Level Measurement

| Process Condition | Recommended Method |
|-------------------|--------------------|
| Open vessel, water, simple | Sight glass, hydrostatic DP, ultrasonic |
| Pressurized, steam environment | DP transmitter (wet leg) or radar |
| Corrosive, hazardous, slurry | Bubbler, radar (non-contact), GWR |
| High temperature, molten metal | Nuclear (radiation), laser |
| Foam, vapour, dust | Non-contact radar, GWR with stillwell |
| Interface (oil-water) | Capacitance (with $\varepsilon_{oil} \neq \varepsilon_{water}$), displacer |
| Hygienic / sanitary (pharma, food) | Radar with hygienic seal, ultrasonic |
| Point-level alarm only | Conductivity, vibrating fork, capacitance point probe |

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Hydrostatic Head in an Open Vessel

**Step 1 ŌĆö Start with the pressure definition.** Pressure at a depth $h$ in a static fluid of density $\rho$ is defined as the weight of the fluid column per unit area:

$$P = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{m \, g}{A} = \frac{\rho \, V \, g}{A}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute the volume of the column.** The volume of a fluid column of height $h$ and cross-sectional area $A$ is:

$$V = A \cdot h$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Simplify the expression:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{\rho \, (A h) \, g}{A} \\
  &= \rho \, g \, h
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö For a closed (pressurized) vessel** with a vapour space pressure $P_v$ above the liquid, the pressure at the bottom tap is the sum of the vapour pressure and the hydrostatic head:

$$P_{bottom} = P_v + \rho \, g \, h$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö The differential pressure** read by a DP transmitter connected between bottom and top taps is:

$$\Delta P = P_{bottom} - P_v = \rho \, g \, h$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Solving for the level:**

$$h = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho \, g}$$

> This is the **master equation** for DP-based level measurement in pressurized vessels. A change in $\Delta P$ of 1 mbar in water corresponds to a level change of **1.0197 cm**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1: Hydrostatic Level in a Pressurized Oil Tank

**Problem:** A cylindrical pressure vessel of diameter **2.5 m** contains lube oil (density **870 kg/m┬│**). The differential pressure transmitter reads **450 mbar** with the high-pressure tap at the bottom and the low-pressure tap connected to a wet leg (filled with oil) of height **1.2 m** above the bottom tap. The process temperature is **60 ┬░C**. Determine the actual liquid level above the bottom tap and the volume in litres.

**Given:**
- $D = 2.5$ m ŌåÆ $A = \pi D^2 / 4 = \pi (2.5)^2 / 4 = 4.9087$ m┬▓
- $\rho_{oil} = 870$ kg/m┬│
- $\Delta P_{transmitter} = 450$ mbar $= 45000$ Pa
- Wet leg height above bottom tap: $h_{leg} = 1.2$ m
- $g = 9.81$ m/s┬▓

**Step 1 ŌĆö Recognize the wet-leg correction.** In a wet leg configuration, the HP tap is at the bottom, the LP tap is at the top. The LP leg is **always filled with oil (condensate)**, which adds a **fixed hydrostatic offset** that must be zeroed out by the transmitter. The differential pressure is:

$$\Delta P = \rho \, g \, (h - h_{leg})$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Rearrange for the actual level $h$:**

$$h = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho \, g} + h_{leg}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $\Delta P / (\rho g)$:**

$$\frac{\Delta P}{\rho \, g} = \frac{45000}{870 \times 9.81} = \frac{45000}{8534.7} = 5.273 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Add the leg offset:**

$$h = 5.273 + 1.2 = 6.473 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute the volume:**

$$V = A \times h = 4.9087 \times 6.473 = 31.77 \text{ m}^3 = 31770 \text{ litres}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** Without the $+h_{leg}$ correction, the level would be **under-reported by 1.2 m** ŌĆö a critical safety error in overflow protection design.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Capacitance of a Partially Submerged Cylindrical Probe

**Step 1 ŌĆö Geometry.** Consider a vertical rod of radius $r$ at the centre of a cylindrical vessel of radius $R$. Total rod length is $L$, and a portion $h$ is submerged in a process liquid of dielectric constant $\varepsilon_{liq}$. The remaining $(L - h)$ is exposed to gas of dielectric constant $\varepsilon_{air} \approx 1$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Treat the system as two coaxial capacitors in parallel.** The total capacitance is the sum of the gas-section capacitance and the liquid-section capacitance:

$$C_{total} = C_{air} + C_{liq}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the coaxial capacitor formula** (rod of radius $r$, outer wall at radius $R$, length $L_i$, dielectric $\varepsilon_i$):

$$C_i = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_i L_i}{\ln(R/r)}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute the two lengths:**

$$C_{air} = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 \cdot 1 \cdot (L - h)}{\ln(R/r)}$$

$$C_{liq} = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{liq} \cdot h}{\ln(R/r)}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Add:**

$$C_{total} = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0}{\ln(R/r)} \left[ (L - h) + \varepsilon_{liq} h \right]$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Rearrange into a linear function of $h$:**

$$C_{total} = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0}{\ln(R/r)} \left[ L + (\varepsilon_{liq} - 1) h \right]$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Recognize the form** $C = C_0 + k \cdot h$, where:
- $C_0 = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 L}{\ln(R/r)}$ is the **zero-level capacitance** (vessel empty).
- $k = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 (\varepsilon_{liq} - 1)}{\ln(R/r)}$ is the **sensitivity (capacitance per metre of level)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical insight:** The sensitivity $k$ is proportional to $(\varepsilon_{liq} - 1)$. Liquids with $\varepsilon_{liq}$ close to 1 (e.g., liquid hydrocarbons with $\varepsilon_{liq} \approx 2$) produce a **very small $\Delta C$**, requiring **stillwells, twin-rod probes, or RF admittance techniques** to improve signal-to-noise ratio.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 2: Capacitance Probe Level Determination

**Problem:** A capacitance probe of diameter **10 mm** is mounted in a vessel of diameter **2.0 m**. The probe length is **3.0 m**. The process liquid (methanol) has a relative permittivity $\varepsilon_{liq} = 33$. The measured capacitance at empty condition is **82 pF** and at full condition is **645 pF**. Determine:
(a) The change in capacitance per metre of level (sensitivity).
(b) The measured capacitance when the level is at **1.8 m**.
(c) The percentage of full-scale reading for the level in (b).

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the empty and full capacitances' difference:**

$$\Delta C = C_{full} - C_{empty} = 645 - 82 = 563 \text{ pF over 3.0 m}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Sensitivity:**

$$k = \frac{\Delta C}{L} = \frac{563 \text{ pF}}{3.0 \text{ m}} = 187.67 \text{ pF/m}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Capacitance at $h = 1.8$ m:**

$$C = C_{empty} + k \cdot h = 82 + 187.67 \times 1.8 = 82 + 337.8 = 419.8 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Percentage of full scale:**

$$\% FSD = \frac{C - C_{empty}}{C_{full} - C_{empty}} \times 100 = \frac{419.8 - 82}{563} \times 100 = 60.0\%$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Ultrasonic Time-of-Flight

**Step 1 ŌĆö Emission.** At $t = 0$, the transducer emits a short ultrasonic pulse into the air/vapour space.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Propagation.** The pulse travels at speed $c$ (function of gas temperature) and reaches the surface after time $t_1$:

$$t_1 = \frac{D}{c}$$

where $D$ is the distance from the transducer to the surface.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Reflection.** The pulse reflects from the surface and travels back, arriving at the transducer at $t_2$:

$$t_2 = t_1 + \frac{D}{c} = \frac{2D}{c}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Total round-trip time:**

$$t = t_2 - 0 = \frac{2D}{c}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Solving for the distance:**

$$D = \frac{c \, t}{2}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Convert distance to level.** If the transducer is mounted at height $H$ above the tank bottom, the liquid level is:

$$h = H - D = H - \frac{c \, t}{2}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Temperature compensation for $c$:**

$$c = c_0 \sqrt{\frac{T_{gas}}{T_0}}$$

where $c_0 = 331.4$ m/s at $T_0 = 273.15$ K, and $T_{gas}$ is the absolute gas temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If the question gives a gas temperature different from 20 ┬░C, always **adjust $c$** before substituting. Use $c = 331.4 + 0.6 \times T(┬░C)$ m/s for dry air at 1 atm.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 3: Ultrasonic Level in a Heated Tank

**Problem:** An ultrasonic transducer is mounted **5.0 m** above the bottom of a tank. The round-trip time of the echo is **23.5 ms**. The gas temperature above the liquid is **80 ┬░C**. Find the liquid level in metres.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the speed of sound in the hot gas:**

$$c = 331.4 + 0.6 \times 80 = 331.4 + 48 = 379.4 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the distance from transducer to surface:**

$$D = \frac{c \, t}{2} = \frac{379.4 \times 0.0235}{2} = \frac{8.916}{2} = 4.458 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Liquid level:**

$$h = H - D = 5.0 - 4.458 = 0.542 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This example illustrates that **ignoring temperature compensation** would yield $D = (340)(0.0235)/2 = 3.995$ m, under-estimating the level by ~46 cm. Temperature compensation is **mandatory** in industrial ultrasonic transmitters (typically via a built-in PT100 RTD).

---

## 3.7 Derivation: FMCW Radar Beat Frequency

**Step 1 ŌĆö FMCW transmission.** The transmitted frequency is swept linearly from $f_1$ to $f_2$ over a sweep period $T_s$. The bandwidth is $\Delta f = f_2 - f_1$. The instantaneous transmitted frequency is:

$$f_{TX}(t) = f_1 + \frac{\Delta f}{T_s} \, t$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Received signal.** The reflected signal returns after a delay $\tau = 2R/c$:

$$f_{RX}(t) = f_1 + \frac{\Delta f}{T_s} (t - \tau)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Mixing in the receiver.** The IF (beat) frequency is the difference:

$$f_{IF} = f_{TX}(t) - f_{RX}(t) = \frac{\Delta f}{T_s} \cdot \tau = \frac{\Delta f}{T_s} \cdot \frac{2R}{c}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Final equation:**

$$f_{IF} = \frac{2 \Delta f \, R}{c \, T_s}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Distance computation:**

$$R = \frac{f_{IF} \cdot c \cdot T_s}{2 \, \Delta f}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The IF frequency is **directly proportional to distance**. A 6 GHz radar with $\Delta f = 1$ GHz and $T_s = 100$ ms gives $f_{IF} \approx 200$ Hz per metre of distance ŌĆö a very low frequency that can be processed accurately by DSP.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A ŌĆö SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**
**"Distinguish between the measurement of level in an open vessel and a pressure vessel. Why is a differential pressure transmitter preferred over a simple pressure gauge in pressurized level applications?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Open Vessel | Pressure Vessel |
|--------|-------------|-----------------|
| Headspace | Atmospheric ($P_v = 0$ gauge) | Non-atmospheric ($P_v \neq 0$) |
| Sensor | Single pressure gauge at bottom | DP transmitter (bottom vs top) |
| Equation | $h = P / (\rho g)$ | $h = (P_{bottom} - P_v) / (\rho g)$ |
| Compensation | Density only | Density and vessel pressure |

A **differential pressure transmitter** is preferred because it **automatically cancels the vessel pressure $P_v$** and gives an output proportional **only** to the true liquid head, ensuring measurement is independent of process pressure fluctuations. A single pressure gauge would give a reading that changes with both level and vessel pressure, leading to gross errors. (3 marks)

---

### Question A2: **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
**"Explain the working principle of a bubbler system for level measurement. State two situations where it is preferred over a DP transmitter."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A constant, low flow of air (or inert gas) is bubbled through a dip tube into the tank. The **back-pressure** required to push the gas out of the tube is the sum of the hydrostatic head of the liquid above the tube outlet and the headspace pressure. This pressure is measured by a remote transmitter that never contacts the process fluid.

**Bubbler is preferred when:**
1. The process fluid is **corrosive, abrasive, slurry, or hot** ŌĆö direct contact sensors would fail.
2. The process is **foaming, has suspended solids, or is chemically aggressive** ŌĆö bubbler is non-wetted except by gas.
3. The vessel has **no bottom tap** (nozzle available only at top) ŌĆö bubbler tube can be dropped from the top.

(3 marks)

---

## PART B ŌĆö LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B-A: **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024 / Module 2 Compulsory]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working of a **differential pressure transmitter for level measurement in a closed (pressurized) vessel using a wet leg**. Derive the relationship between the differential pressure and the level. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A closed cylindrical tank of diameter **1.8 m** contains water at **85 ┬░C** ($\rho = 965$ kg/m┬│). The bottom tap and the top tap of a DP transmitter are connected via a wet leg filled with water, the wet leg extending **0.8 m above the top tap**. The transmitter reads **820 mbar**. Determine:
1. The actual water level above the bottom tap.
2. The volume of water in the tank in litres.
3. The % full-scale reading if the maximum measurable level is **3.5 m**.

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B-A:

**(a) Working Principle and Derivation (7 marks):**

In a pressurized vessel, the gas/vapour space above the liquid exerts a pressure $P_v$ on the liquid surface. A **wet leg** is the high-pressure (HP) impulse line connecting the top of the vessel to the HP port of the DP transmitter, kept **continuously filled with the condensate of the process vapour** (or with the process liquid itself in low-temperature service). The low-pressure (LP) port is connected to the bottom tap of the vessel.

```
   ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌö┤ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ         ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌö┤ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
   Ōöé  Wet Leg  Ōöé         Ōöé Process   Ōöé
   Ōöé  height   Ōöé         Ōöé  line     Ōöé
   Ōöé  h_leg    Ōöé         Ōöé           Ōöé
```
