```
        ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌö¼ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś
                  Ō¢╝
        ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
        Ōöé  DP Transmitter  Ōöé
        Ōöé   4-20 mA        Ōöé
        ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś
```

**Derivation (4 marks) of content):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Pressure at the LP (bottom) tap:**

$$P_{LP} = P_v + \rho \, g \, h$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Pressure at the HP (top, wet leg) tap:** The wet leg is filled with liquid of density $\rho$, of height $h_{leg}$ measured from the top tap downward. The pressure at the HP port of the transmitter is the sum of the vessel vapour pressure and the hydrostatic head of the wet leg:

$$P_{HP} = P_v + \rho \, g \, h_{leg}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Differential pressure measured:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= P_{LP} - P_{HP} \\
         &= (P_v + \rho g h) - (P_v + \rho g h_{leg}) \\
         &= \rho \, g \, (h - h_{leg})
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Solving for the level:**

$$h = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho \, g} + h_{leg}$$

The term $h_{leg}$ is the **suppression** (or "zero elevation") and is set during transmitter calibration (LRV). (7 marks)

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $D = 1.8$ m ŌåÆ $A = \pi D^2 / 4 = \pi (1.8)^2 / 4 = 2.5447$ m┬▓
- $\rho = 965$ kg/m┬│ (water at 85 ┬░C)
- $h_{leg} = 0.8$ m
- $\Delta P = 820$ mbar $= 82000$ Pa
- $g = 9.81$ m/s┬▓
- $H_{max} = 3.5$ m

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the hydrostatic term $\Delta P / (\rho g)$:**

$$\frac{\Delta P}{\rho \, g} = \frac{82000}{965 \times 9.81} = \frac{82000}{9466.65} = 8.662 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Add the leg offset:**

$$h = 8.662 + 0.8 = 9.462 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö The reading exceeds the maximum tank height (which is implicitly limited by physical dimensions).** This indicates the wet leg height is being **subtracted** (a *suppressed zero* ŌĆö the transmitter's LRV corresponds to 0.8 m of liquid, not 0 m).

> For a typical 3.5 m tank, a reading of 8.66 m is non-physical. The student's interpretation must check whether the **leg is on the HP side (suppressed zero)** or the **LP side (elevated zero)**. Re-reading the problem with a standard wet-leg HP arrangement: when the tank is empty, the LP tap is at the bottom and reads $P_v + 0$, while the HP tap reads $P_v + \rho g h_{leg}$. So $\Delta P = -\rho g h_{leg}$ (negative). When full, $\Delta P = \rho g (H - h_{leg})$. The transmitter zero (LRV) is set to $\rho g h_{leg}$, and the transmitter span is $\rho g H$.

> **Corrected Approach (Suppressed Zero):** Let the transmitter be calibrated so that LRV = 0 mbar corresponds to tank empty ($\Delta P_0 = -\rho g h_{leg}$), and URV corresponds to tank full at $H$ ($\Delta P_H = \rho g (H - h_{leg})$). The actual reading 820 mbar therefore corresponds to:

$$h = \frac{\Delta P + \rho g h_{leg}}{\rho g} = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho g} + h_{leg}$$

> For a 3.5 m tank, the maximum $\Delta P$ is $\rho g (H - h_{leg}) = 965 \times 9.81 \times (3.5 - 0.8) = 25579$ Pa $\approx$ 256 mbar. A reading of 820 mbar **exceeds the URV** ŌåÆ the tank is **over-range** (overflowed).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Examination:** State clearly that the answer to (b) reveals a **process upset** ŌĆö the level is **off-scale high** and immediate operator action is required. The numerical answer is $h = 9.46$ m, but the **physical interpretation** is that the tank has overflowed and the alarm must be triggered.

**Volume (assuming $h = 3.5$ m full):** $V = A \times H = 2.5447 \times 3.5 = 8.907$ m┬│ = 8907 litres.

**% Full-Scale Reading:** $h / H_{max} = 3.5 / 3.5 = 100\%$ (tank is full at maximum scale).

(7 marks)

---

### Question B-B: **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 / Module 2 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **capacitance method of level measurement** for a conducting and a non-conducting liquid. Derive the capacitance expression in each case and discuss how the measurement is affected by the dielectric constant of the liquid. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical tank of diameter **2.5 m** contains a non-conducting liquid (oil) with $\varepsilon_{liq} = 2.4$. A single-rod capacitance probe of diameter **15 mm** and length **4 m** is mounted vertically at the centre. Determine:
1. The capacitance when the tank is empty.
2. The capacitance when the level is at **2.0 m**.
3. The sensitivity in pF per metre.

Take the effective vessel wall radius for a single-rod probe to be the radius of the tank. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B-B:

**(a) Capacitance Method ŌĆö Conducting vs Non-Conducting Liquids (7 marks):**

A vertical metallic probe is inserted into the vessel. The probe (one electrode) and the vessel wall (the other electrode) form a cylindrical capacitor. The dielectric between them is partly the gas/vapour (above the liquid) and partly the process liquid (below the surface).

**Case 1 ŌĆö Non-Conducting Liquid (e.g., oil, hydrocarbons, organic solvents):**

The liquid acts as a **dielectric**. The capacitance is the sum of the air-section and the liquid-section capacitances in parallel:

$$C = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0}{\ln(R/r)} \left[ (L - h) + \varepsilon_{liq} h \right] = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 L}{\ln(R/r)} + \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 (\varepsilon_{liq} - 1)}{\ln(R/r)} h$$

The empty-tank capacitance is $C_0 = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 L}{\ln(R/r)}$ and the sensitivity is $k = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 (\varepsilon_{liq} - 1)}{\ln(R/r)}$.

**Case 2 ŌĆö Conducting Liquid (e.g., water, acids, aqueous solutions):**

The liquid acts as a **conductor**, effectively extending the outer electrode (vessel wall) up to the liquid surface. The capacitor is **air-filled only above the liquid**, with the conducting liquid serving as the second plate. The capacitance is:

$$C = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 L}{\ln(R/r)} \cdot (1 - h/L) = C_0 \left(1 - \frac{h}{L}\right)$$

In this case, the capacitance **decreases** with rising level (the conducting liquid effectively "shorts out" the submerged portion). For conducting liquids, the probe must be **insulated** (e.g., PTFE-coated) to prevent a short circuit.

**Effect of dielectric constant:** Sensitivity is proportional to $(\varepsilon_{liq} - 1)$. Liquids with low $\varepsilon_{liq}$ (hydrocarbons, $\varepsilon_{liq} \approx 2$) produce small $\Delta C$, requiring stillwells or twin-rod probes. (7 marks)

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Tank diameter $D = 2.5$ m ŌåÆ effective wall radius $R = 1.25$ m
- Probe diameter $d = 15$ mm = 0.015 m ŌåÆ $r = 0.0075$ m
- Probe length $L = 4$ m
- $\varepsilon_{liq} = 2.4$ (non-conducting oil)
- $h = 2.0$ m
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the geometric factor:**

$$\ln(R/r) = \ln(1.25 / 0.0075) = \ln(166.67) = 5.116$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Empty-tank capacitance ($h = 0$):**

$$C_0 = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 L}{\ln(R/r)} = \frac{2 \pi (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \times 4}{5.116}$$

$$C_0 = \frac{2.224 \times 10^{-10}}{5.116} = 4.348 \times 10^{-11} \text{ F} = 43.48 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Capacitance at $h = 2.0$ m:**

$$C = C_0 + \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 (\varepsilon_{liq} - 1) h}{\ln(R/r)}$$

$$C = 43.48 + \frac{2 \pi (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) (2.4 - 1) (2.0)}{5.116}$$

$$\Delta C = \frac{2 \pi (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) (1.4) (2.0)}{5.116} = \frac{1.557 \times 10^{-10}}{5.116} = 3.043 \times 10^{-11} \text{ F} = 30.43 \text{ pF}$$

$$C = 43.48 + 30.43 = 73.91 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Sensitivity:**

$$k = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 (\varepsilon_{liq} - 1)}{\ln(R/r)} = \frac{2 \pi (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) (1.4)}{5.116} = 1.522 \times 10^{-11} \text{ F/m} = 15.22 \text{ pF/m}$$

**Verification:** $\Delta C = k \times h = 15.22 \times 2.0 = 30.43$ pF Ō£ō

(7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the wet-leg offset** in pressurized-vessel problems ŌĆö this is the single most common error, costing up to **3 marks** in Part B.
> 2. **Using the wrong density value** ŌĆö at elevated temperatures, $\rho$ of water decreases significantly (e.g., $\rho_{85} = 965$ kg/m┬│ vs $\rho_{20} = 998$ kg/m┬│). Always extract $\rho$ from the steam tables when the problem gives a temperature.
> 3. **Mixing up the conducting vs non-conducting capacitance equations** ŌĆö many students apply the non-conducting formula to water (which is conducting) and arrive at a *negative* or *inverted* response.
> 4. **Ignoring the speed-of-sound temperature correction** in ultrasonic problems ŌĆö yields errors of up to 10 % for high-temperature tanks.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert $\ln(R/r)$** when the probe and tank are in different units (mm vs m) ŌĆö a unit-conversion error in the geometric factor of the capacitance equation.
> 6. **Not writing the suppression/elevation explanation** in DP transmitter questions ŌĆö the LRV/URV calibration is a 2-mark item that examiners look for explicitly.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### Core Concepts to Memorize
- **Level measurement is the second most-measured process variable** after temperature in any process plant.
- **Open vessel**: $h = P / (\rho g)$ ŌĆö single pressure tap.
- **Pressure vessel**: $h = (\Delta P) / (\rho g)$ ŌĆö DP transmitter with suppression/elevation.
- **Wet leg** provides condensate-filled reference; **dry leg** uses inert gas; **diaphragm seals** isolate the DP from the process.

### Formula Sheet (One-Liners)
- Hydrostatic: $P = \rho g h$, $h = \Delta P / (\rho g)$.
- Capacitance: $C = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0 L}{\ln(R/r)}$, sensitivity $\propto (\varepsilon_{liq} - 1)$.
- Ultrasonic: $h = H - (c t / 2)$, with $c$ temperature-compensated.
- Radar FMCW: $f_{IF} = (2 \Delta f R) / (c T_s)$.
- Buoyancy: $F_b = \rho g V_{disp}$.
- Radiation: $I = I_0 e^{-\mu \rho x}$.

### Method Selection Cues (for Exam "Compare" Questions)
- **Best accuracy** ŌåÆ Radar FMCW, GWR, sight glass, load cell.
- **Best for corrosive/slurry** ŌåÆ Bubbler, radar (non-contact), nuclear.
- **Best for interface detection** ŌåÆ Displacer, capacitance (with two distinct $\varepsilon$).
- **Best for high pressure/temperature** ŌåÆ DP transmitter, sight glass (reflex), nuclear.
- **Best for sanitary/hygienic** ŌåÆ Radar with hygienic seal.
- **Best for low $\varepsilon$ (hydrocarbon)** ŌåÆ Stillwell, GWR.

### Key Definitions
- **Suppression (Zero Elevation)**: Setting LRV such that a non-zero physical level produces $\Delta P = 0$.
- **Elevation (Zero Suppression)**: Subtracting a fixed $\Delta P$ so that the transmitter span is reduced.
- **Dead Zone**: The minimum distance below an ultrasonic/radar transducer where measurement is unreliable (due to ring-down time).
- **Dielectric Constant $\varepsilon_r$**: The ratio of a material's permittivity to free-space permittivity; for water Ōēł 80, for oil Ōēł 2ŌĆō3, for air = 1.

### Quick Unit Conversions
- $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 10.197 \text{ mH}_2\text{O} = 750.06 \text{ mmHg}$.
- $1 \text{ inH}_2\text{O} = 249.09 \text{ Pa}$.
- Speed of sound in air at 20 ┬░C: **343 m/s**; at 0 ┬░C: **331.4 m/s**; linear approximation $c \approx 331.4 + 0.6 T$.

### Final Exam-Ready Mantra
> "Identify the vessel type ŌåÆ select the principle ŌåÆ write the governing equation ŌåÆ apply any suppression/elevation/compensation ŌåÆ solve for the level."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of pH and Humidity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT413 - PROCESS INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Measurement of pH and Humidity ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is pH? **pH** is a logarithmic measure (base 10) of the hydrogen ion concentration ($\text{H}^+$) in an aqueous solution. It quantifies the **acidity** or **alkalinity** of a solution on a scale from **0 to 14**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** > $$\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[\text{H}^+] = \log_{10}\frac{1}{[\text{H}^+]}$$ > where $[\text{H}^+]$ is the activity of hydrogen ions in moles per litre (mol/L). The standard reference temperature is **25 ┬░C (298.15 K)**, and pH is a **dimensionless quantity**. **Classification of pH Scale:** | Range | Nature | Example | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 0 ŌĆō 2 | Strongly Acidic | Hydrochloric Acid (HCl) | | 2 ŌĆō 6 | Weakly Acidic | Vinegar, Lemon Juice | | 7 | **Neutral** | Pure Water at 25 ┬░C | | 8 ŌĆō 12 | Weakly Alkaline / Basic | Baking Soda, Blood | | 12 ŌĆō 14 | Strongly Alkaline | Sodium Hydroxide (NaOH) | > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö pH as a "Concentration Thermometer":** > Imagine hydrogen ions ($\text{H}^+$) as loud music in a room. The pH scale is like a **decibel meter**. A pH of 0 means the music is deafening (concentrated acid), pH 7 is a calm room (neutral water), and pH 14 is silence (almost no $\text{H}^+$ ions, dominated by $\text{OH}^-$). The logarithmic nature means each step of 1 pH unit represents a **10├Ś change** in hydrogen ion concentration. ### 1.2 What is Humidity? **Humidity** is the measure of water vapour present in a gas mixture (typically air). In process industries, it is a **critical process variable** affecting product quality, drying efficiency, HVAC control, and corrosion behaviour. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three Primary Engineering Definitions of Humidity:** > 1. **Absolute Humidity (AH):** Mass of water vapour per unit volume of gas $\left[\text{g/m}^3\right]$. > 2. **Relative Humidity (RH):** Ratio of actual partial pressure of water vapour to its saturation pressure at the same temperature, expressed in **%**. > 3. **Dew Point (DP):** The temperature at which air becomes saturated, causing water vapour to condense into liquid droplets. > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Humidity as a "Wet Sponge":** > Picture a dry sponge (air at low temperature) that can soak up only a little water. A warm sponge can absorb much more. The **maximum water the sponge can hold** is the *saturation vapour pressure*; how much is actually absorbed is the *actual vapour pressure*. **Relative Humidity = (Water in Sponge) / (Max Capacity)** ŌĆö like a phone battery percentage! ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Reference Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Faraday's Constant ($F$):** $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}$ > - **Universal Gas Constant ($R$):** $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol┬ĘK)}$ > - **Standard pH Electrode Slope:** $\frac{2.303\,RT}{F} = 0.05916\ \text{V}$ at **25 ┬░C** (this is the *Nernst slope*). > - **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325\ \text{kPa}$ > - **Saturated Vapour Pressure of Water at 100 ┬░C:** $P_s = 101.325\ \text{kPa}$ ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The pH Electrochemical Cell A pH measurement system is fundamentally a **voltaic cell** composed of two half-cells: 1. **Measuring Electrode (Glass Electrode)** ŌĆö sensitive to $\text{H}^+$ activity. 2. **Reference Electrode** ŌĆö provides a stable, constant potential (e.g., Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE) or Ag/AgCl). > [!NOTE] > The **glass membrane** acts as a **selective ion exchanger**. When dipped into a solution, $\text{H}^+$ ions from the solution exchange with alkali ions ($\text{Na}^+$) in the hydrated gel layer of the glass, generating a **boundary potential** proportional to the $\text{H}^+$ activity. ### 2.2 The Nernst Equation (Heart of pH Measurement) The EMF ($E$) developed by the electrode pair is given by the **Nernst Equation**: $$E = E^\circ - \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF}\log_{10}\frac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$$ For a hydrogen-ion-selective electrode, the cell EMF is: $$E = K + \frac{2.303\,RT}{F}\,\text{pH}$$ where: - $E^\circ$ = standard electrode potential (V) - $K$ = asymmetry potential constant (V) - $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol┬ĘK)}$ - $T$ = absolute temperature (K) - $n = 1$ (number of electrons transferred per ion) - $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}$ ### 2.3 Components of a pH Measurement Loop - **Glass Electrode** ŌĆö sensing bulb of special Li-glass (low solubility). - **Reference Electrode** ŌĆö Ag/AgCl with KCl filling solution. - **Salt Bridge / Liquid Junction** ŌĆö porous frit maintaining ionic contact. - **High-Impedance pH Meter** ŌĆö amplifier with input impedance $> 10^{12}\ \Omega$. - **Temperature Compensation Probe (ATC)** ŌĆö for correcting Nernst slope. ### 2.4 Humidity: Psychrometric Theory The thermodynamic state of moist air is described by **psychrometrics**. Key psychrometric relationships: **Saturation Vapour Pressure (Antoine's Equation, water):** $$\ln P_s = A - \frac{B}{C + T}$$ For water ($P_s$ in mmHg, $T$ in ┬░C): $A = 18.3036,\ B = 3816.44,\ C = -46.13$ (valid 0ŌĆō60 ┬░C) **Relative Humidity:** $$\text{RH}\ (\%) = \frac{P_w}{P_s(T)} \times 100$$ where $P_w$ = actual partial pressure of water vapour (Pa), and $P_s(T)$ = saturation pressure at temperature $T$. **Dew Point Relationship:** $$P_w = P_s(T_{dp})$$ **Absolute Humidity:** $$\text{AH} = \frac{m_v}{V} = \frac{P_w \, M_w}{R\,T}$$ where $M_w = 18.015\ \text{g/mol}$ (molar mass of water). ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö Quick Reference > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE (MEMORIZE FOR ESE)** | # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | pH Definition | $\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[\text{H}^+]$ | Dimensionless | | 2 | pOH Relation | $\text{pH} + \text{pOH} = 14$ | At 25 ┬░C only | | 3 | Nernst Equation (pH) | $E = K + \frac{2.303\,RT}{F}\,\text{pH}$ | Volts | | 4 | Nernst Slope (25 ┬░C) | $\frac{2.303\,RT}{F} = 0.05916$ | V / pH unit | | 5 | Nernst Slope (general T) | $S = 0.05916 \cdot \frac{T}{298.15}$ | V / pH unit | | 6 | Relative Humidity | $\text{RH} = \frac{P_w}{P_s(T)} \times 100$ | Percent | | 7 | Dew Point | $P_w = P_s(T_{dp})$ | Pa / kPa | | 8 | Absolute Humidity | $\text{AH} = \frac{P_w\,M_w}{R\,T}$ | g/m┬│ | | 9 | Mixing Ratio | $W = 0.622\,\frac{P_w}{P_{atm} - P_w}$ | kg water/kg dry air | | 10 | Specific Humidity | $q = \frac{m_v}{m_{v} + m_a}$ | kg water/kg moist air | | 11 | Antoine (Water) | $\ln P_s = 18.3036 - \frac{3816.44}{T - 46.13}$ | $P_s$ in mmHg, $T$ in ┬░C | | 12 | Wet-Dry Bulb | $P_w = P_s(T_w) - A\,P_{atm}\,(T_d - T_w)$ | Psychrometric constant $A \approx 6.66 \times 10^{-4}\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ | | 13 | Faraday's Constant | $F = 96{,}485$ | C/mol | | 14 | Standard T | $T_{std} = 298.15$ | K (25 ┬░C) | | 15 | Ionic Product of Water | $K_w = [\text{H}^+][\text{OH}^-] = 10^{-14}$ | At 25 ┬░C | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility:** pH measurement is indispensable in **wastewater treatment**, **pharmaceutical manufacturing**, **fermentation reactors**, and **food & beverage** industries. Humidity measurement is critical in **HVAC**, **drying operations** (paper, textiles, food), **meteorology**, **clean rooms**, and **electronics fabrication**. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Nernst Slope at 25 ┬░C The Nernst slope for a single-electron ($n=1$) reaction at temperature $T$ is: $$S = \frac{2.303\,R\,T}{F}$$ **Step 1:** Substitute constants: $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol┬ĘK)}$, $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}$, $T = 298.15\ \text{K}$. $$S = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{96{,}485}$$ **Step 2:** Compute numerator. $$\begin{aligned} 2.303 \times 8.314 &= 19.1468\ \text{J/(mol┬ĘK)} \\ 19.1468 \times 298.15 &= 5708.42\ \text{J/mol} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3:** Divide by $F$. $$S = \frac{5708.42}{96{,}485} = 0.05916\ \text{V}$$ **Result:** $$\boxed{S_{25^\circ\text{C}} = 59.16\ \text{mV per pH unit}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** A change of **1 pH unit** corresponds to a **59.16 mV** change in electrode potential at 25 ┬░C. This is the foundation of all digital pH instruments and temperature compensation circuits. ### 3.2 Worked Example: pH from Measured EMF **Problem:** A glass-calomel electrode pair dipped in a buffer solution reads an EMF of $E = 0.414\ \text{V}$ at 25 ┬░C. The electrode is calibrated such that $E^\circ + E_{ref} = 0.250\ \text{V}$. Determine the pH. **Step 1:** Apply the modified Nernst equation. $$E = (E^\circ + E_{ref}) + 0.05916 \cdot \text{pH}$$ **Step 2:** Rearrange for pH. $$\text{pH} = \frac{E - (E^\circ + E_{ref})}{0.05916}$$ **Step 3:** Substitute values. $$\begin{aligned} \text{pH} &= \frac{0.414 - 0.250}{0.05916} \\ &= \frac{0.164}{0.05916} \\ &= 2.772 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4:** Final answer. $$\boxed{\text{pH} = 2.77 \quad (\text{strongly acidic solution})}$$ ### 3.3 Worked Example: pH at a Non-Standard Temperature **Problem:** The same electrode measures an EMF of 0.350 V at 50 ┬░C ($T = 323.15$ K). The reference constant $K = 0.250$ V. Find pH. **Step 1:** Compute Nernst slope at $T = 323.15$ K. $$S = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 323.15}{96{,}485}$$ **Step 2:** Evaluate numerator. $$\begin{aligned} 2.303 \times 8.314 &= 19.1468 \\ 19.1468 \times 323.15 &= 6187.30\ \text{J/mol} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3:** Divide by $F$. $$S = \frac{6187.30}{96{,}485} = 0.06412\ \text{V/pH unit}$$ **Step 4:** Solve for pH. $$\begin{aligned} \text{pH} &= \frac{E - K}{S} \\ &= \frac{0.350 - 0.250}{0.06412} \\ &= \frac{0.100}{0.06412} \\ &= 1.560 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5:** Final result. $$\boxed{\text{pH}_{50^\circ\text{C}} = 1.56}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Temperature Compensation is Mandatory:** Notice the slope increased from 59.16 mV to 64.12 mV (Ōēł 8.4% rise). Without **Automatic Temperature Compensation (ATC)**, an instrument calibrated at 25 ┬░C would give significant pH errors in hot process streams (e.g., fermentation broths at 50 ┬░C). ### 3.4 Worked Example: Relative Humidity from Wet-Dry Bulb Readings **Problem:** At atmospheric pressure $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa, the dry bulb temperature is $T_d = 30$ ┬░C and the wet bulb temperature is $T_w = 25$ ┬░C. Determine the relative humidity. **Step 1:** Calculate saturation pressure at wet bulb using Antoine's equation. $$\ln P_s(T_w) = 18.3036 - \frac{3816.44}{25 + 46.13}$$ **Step 2:** Evaluate the denominator. $$25 + 46.13 = 71.13$$ **Step 3:** Compute the fraction. $$\frac{3816.44}{71.13} = 53.66$$ **Step 4:** Subtract from $A$. $$18.3036 - 53.66 = -35.3564$$ **Step 5:** Exponentiate. $$P_s(25^\circ\text{C}) = e^{-35.3564} = 4.74\ \text{mmHg} = 631.4\ \text{Pa}$$ **Step 6:** Apply wet-dry bulb equation. $$P_w = P_s(T_w) - A \cdot P_{atm} \cdot (T_d - T_w)$$ $$P_w = 631.4 - (6.66 \times 10^{-4}) \times (101{,}325) \times (30 - 25)$$ **Step 7:** Compute the psychrometric term. $$6.66 \times 10^{-4} \times 101{,}325 \times 5 = 337.4\ \text{Pa}$$ **Step 8:** Subtract. $$P_w = 631.4 - 337.4 = 294.0\ \text{Pa}$$ **Step 9:** Compute $P_s(30^\circ\text{C})$ for RH calculation. $$\ln P_s(30^\circ\text{C}) = 18.3036 - \frac{3816.44}{30 + 46.13} = 18.3036 - 50.09 = -31.79$$ $$P_s(30^\circ\text{C}) = e^{-31.79} = 0.0314\ \text{atm} = 3181\ \text{Pa} = 23.85\ \text{mmHg}$$ **Step 10:** Compute relative humidity. $$\begin{aligned} \text{RH} &= \frac{P_w}{P_s(T_d)} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{294.0}{3181} \times 100 \\ &= 9.24\ \% \end{aligned}$$ **Step 11:** Final result. $$\boxed{\text{RH} \approx 9.24\ \% \quad (\text{very dry, desert-like air})}$$ ### 3.5 Worked Example: Absolute Humidity Using the previous problem's $P_w = 294.0$ Pa at $T = 30$ ┬░C $= 303.15$ K: **Step 1:** Apply the AH formula. $$\text{AH} = \frac{P_w \cdot M_w}{R \cdot T}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute values. $$\text{AH} = \frac{294.0 \times 0.018015}{8.314 \times 303.15}$$ **Step 3:** Evaluate numerator. $$294.0 \times 0.018015 = 5.296\ \text{(Pa┬Ękg/mol units converted: g/m}^3\text{)}$$ > More precisely: $P_w = 294.0\ \text{Pa} = 294.0\ \text{N/m}^2$; with $M_w = 18.015 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg/mol}$ and $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol┬ĘK)}$. $$\text{AH} = \frac{294.0 \times 0.018015}{8.314 \times 303.15} = \frac{5.296}{2520.0} = 2.10 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg/m}^3$$ **Step 4:** Final result. $$\boxed{\text{AH} = 2.10\ \text{g/m}^3}$$ ### 3.6 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Method Selection > [!NOTE] > **Decision Matrix: pH Electrode vs. Alternative Methods** | Method | Principle | Range | Pros | Cons | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Glass Electrode | Nernst EMF (HŌü║ selective) | 0 ŌĆō 14 | Continuous, accurate | Fragile, needs ATC | | Antimony Electrode | Metal-oxide redox | 1 ŌĆō 11 | Rugged, low cost | Sluggish response | | Hydrogen Electrode | HŌéé gas reaction | 0 ŌĆō 14 | Standard reference | Cumbersome | | Colorimetric (Indicators) | Visual dye change | 1 ŌĆō 12 | Cheap, portable | Subjective, narrow range | | ISFET (Ion-Selective FET) | MOSFET + ion membrane | 0 ŌĆō 14 | Solid-state, sterilizable | Temperature sensitive | > [!NOTE] > **Decision Matrix: Humidity Sensor Selection** | Method | Range (% RH) | Accuracy | Best Application | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Capacitive (Polymer) | 0 ŌĆō 100 | ┬▒2 % | HVAC, process industries | | Resistive (Salts) | 20 ŌĆō 95 | ┬▒5 % | Cheap consumer use | | Wet-Dry Psychrometer | 10 ŌĆō 100 | ┬▒3 % | Field meteorology | | Dew Point Mirror (Chilled Mirror) | 0 ŌĆō 100 | ┬▒0.2 ┬░C DP | Reference / calibration | | Hair Hygrometer | 0 ŌĆō 100 | ┬▒5 % | Old meteorological | | Infrared Absorption | 0 ŌĆō 100 | ┬▒0.5 % | Industrial drying | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 pH Measurement System ŌĆö Block Architecture ```mermaid graph LR A[Process Solution
pH Unknown] --> B[Glass Electrode

Saturated KCl] RefChamber --> PorousFrit[Porous Frit
