KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]** **Q1. Define pH. Why is the glass electrode preferred over other electrodes for industrial pH measurement? (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** - **Definition:** pH is the negative logarithm (base 10) of hydrogen ion activity in a solution: $\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[\text{H}^+]$. - **Advantages of Glass Electrode:** 1. **Wide measurement range** (0 to 14 pH units). 2. **High selectivity** for HŌü║ ions (minimal interference from other ions). 3. **Robust and inert** ŌĆö works in oxidizing, reducing, and corrosive media. 4. **Fast response** and good reproducibility. 5. Can be used in **continuous on-line** industrial applications. > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Model Paper]** **Q2. Distinguish between Relative Humidity and Dew Point. Mention any two instruments used for humidity measurement. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Relative Humidity (RH) | Dew Point (DP) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Definition | Ratio of $P_w$ to $P_s(T)$ ├Ś 100 | Temperature at which condensation begins | | Temperature-dependent? | Yes (decreases as T rises at fixed vapour) | No (depends only on $P_w$) | | Unit | Percent (%) | ┬░C or K | **Two Instruments:** 1. **Wet-and-Dry Bulb Psychrometer** ŌĆö measures both temperatures. 2. **Capacitive Polymer Hygrometer** ŌĆö dielectric changes with moisture. --- ### 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]** #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Derive the Nernst equation for a glass-calomel electrode system. Explain how pH is determined using this equation. (7 Marks) **(b)** A glass electrode system measures an EMF of 0.482 V at 35 ┬░C when dipped in a solution. The electrode constant $K = 0.310$ V. Determine the pH of the solution. The measured EMF changes to 0.605 V when dipped in another solution at the same temperature. Find the pH of the second solution. (7 Marks) ### **Model Solution (Question A)** **Part (a): Nernst Equation Derivation** Consider the half-cell reaction of the glass electrode: $$\text{H}^+ (\text{inner}) \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ (\text{outer})$$ The Gibbs free energy change at equilibrium is: $$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + R\,T \ln Q$$ where $Q = \frac{a_{H^+, \text{outer}}}{a_{H^+, \text{inner}}}$ is the reaction quotient. Since $\Delta G = -nFE$ and $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ$: $$-nFE = -nFE^\circ + R\,T \ln Q$$ Rearranging: $$E = E^\circ - \frac{R\,T}{nF} \ln \frac{a_{H^+, \text{outer}}}{a_{H^+, \text{inner}}}$$ Converting $\ln$ to $\log_{10}$ (multiply by 2.303): $$E = E^\circ + \frac{2.303\,R\,T}{nF} \log a_{H^+, \text{inner}} - \frac{2.303\,R\,T}{nF} \log a_{H^+, \text{outer}}$$ Combining constants ($E^\circ + \text{inner term} = K$): $$E = K + \frac{2.303\,R\,T}{F}\,\text{pH} \quad (\text{since } n=1,\ \text{pH} = -\log a_{H^+}$$ The slope $\frac{2.303\,RT}{F}$ at 25 ┬░C = 0.05916 V/pH. **pH is computed by measuring $E$ and rearranging:** $$\text{pH} = \frac{E - K}{2.303\,R\,T/F}$$ **Part (b): Numerical Solutions** **Step 1:** Compute Nernst slope at 35 ┬░C = 308.15 K. $$S = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 308.15}{96{,}485} = 0.06111\ \text{V/pH unit}$$ **Step 2:** Compute pH of first solution ($E_1 = 0.482$ V). $$\text{pH}_1 = \frac{0.482 - 0.310}{0.06111} = \frac{0.172}{0.06111} = 2.815$$ **Step 3:** Compute pH of second solution ($E_2 = 0.605$ V). $$\text{pH}_2 = \frac{0.605 - 0.310}{0.06111} = \frac{0.295}{0.06111} = 4.828$$ **Final Answers:** $$\boxed{\text{pH}_1 = 2.82 \quad ; \quad \text{pH}_2 = 4.83}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **capacitive type humidity sensor**. Discuss its advantages over resistive type. (7 Marks) **(b)** The wet and dry bulb temperatures of air at 1 atm pressure are $T_w = 24$ ┬░C and $T_d = 30$ ┬░C. Given: saturation pressure of water at 24 ┬░C = 2.984 kPa, at 30 ┬░C = 4.246 kPa, psychrometric constant $A = 6.66 \times 10^{-4}$ ┬░CŌü╗┬╣. Calculate: (i) Actual partial pressure of water vapour, (ii) Relative humidity, (iii) Dew point temperature, and (iv) Absolute humidity. (7 Marks) ### **Model Solution (Question B)** **Part (a): Capacitive Humidity Sensor ŌĆö Construction & Working** **Construction:** - A **thin layer of hygroscopic polymer** (e.g., cellulose acetate butyrate or polyimide) is sandwiched between **two conductive electrodes**. - The **top electrode is porous** (evaporated gold film) to allow water vapour to diffuse in and out. - The **bottom electrode** is a solid metal film on a ceramic substrate. - Lead wires and a protective housing complete the assembly. **Working Principle:** - The polymer dielectric has a relative permittivity of $\varepsilon_r \approx 2$ (dry), but water has $\varepsilon_r \approx 80$. - As water vapour diffuses into the polymer, its dielectric constant rises. - The sensor capacitance is: $$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0\, \varepsilon_r\, A}{d}$$ where $A$ = electrode area, $d$ = polymer thickness. Capacitance rises monotonically with % RH. - The capacitor is placed in an **AC bridge or oscillator circuit**; changes in $C$ produce a frequency or voltage change proportional to RH. **Advantages over Resistive Type:** | Feature | Capacitive | Resistive | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Range | 0 ŌĆō 100 % RH | 20 ŌĆō 95 % RH only | | Linearity | Highly linear | Highly non-linear | | Condensation tolerance | Survives dew formation | Damaged by condensation | | Response time | Fast | Slow | | Long-term stability | Excellent | Drift over time | | Temperature range | Wide | Limited | **Part (b): Humidity Calculations** **Given:** - $T_d = 30$ ┬░C, $T_w = 24$ ┬░C - $P_s(24^\circ\text{C}) = 2.984$ kPa - $P_s(30^\circ\text{C}) = 4.246$ kPa - $A = 6.66 \times 10^{-4}\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ - $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa **(i) Actual Partial Pressure $P_w$:** $$P_w = P_s(T_w) - A \cdot P_{atm} \cdot (T_d - T_w)$$ $$P_w = 2.984 - (6.66 \times 10^{-4}) \times 101.325 \times (30 - 24)$$ $$P_w = 2.984 - 0.405 = 2.579\ \text{kPa}$$ **(ii) Relative Humidity:** $$\text{RH} = \frac{P_w}{P_s(T_d)} \times 100 = \frac{2.579}{4.246} \times 100 = 60.74\ \%$$ **(iii) Dew Point:** We need $T_{dp}$ such that $P_s(T_{dp}) = 2.579$ kPa. Linear interpolation between 24 ┬░C (2.984 kPa) and 20 ┬░C (2.339 kPa): $$T_{dp} = 24 - (2.984 - 2.579) \times \frac{4}{2.984 - 2.339} = 24 - 0.405 \times \frac{4}{0.645} = 24 - 2.51 = 21.49\ ^\circ\text{C}$$ **(iv) Absolute Humidity:** $$\text{AH} = \frac{P_w \cdot M_w}{R \cdot T}$$ $$T = 30 + 273.15 = 303.15\ \text{K}$$ $$\text{AH} = \frac{2579 \times 0.018015}{8.314 \times 303.15} = \frac{46.46}{2520.0} = 0.01844\ \text{kg/m}^3$$ **Final Results:** $$\boxed{P_w = 2.58\ \text{kPa};\ \text{RH} = 60.7\ \%;\ T_{dp} = 21.5\ ^\circ\text{C};\ \text{AH} = 18.44\ \text{g/m}^3}$$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls ŌĆö Avoid These Mistakes!** > > 1. **Forgetting Temperature Compensation:** Students often use $0.05916$ V/pH slope at temperatures other than 25 ┬░C. The slope **changes with $T$**. Always compute $S = \frac{2.303\,R\,T}{F}$ at the **given temperature** (K, not ┬░C). > > 2. **Sign Errors in Nernst Equation:** The standard form is $E = K + 0.05916 \cdot \text{pH}$. If the problem gives $E$ in negative or uses reverse convention, **re-derive carefully**. Always define $K$ explicitly. > > 3. **Unit Confusion in Psychrometric Equation:** $P_{atm}$ in the wet-bulb equation must be in the **same units** as $P_s$. If $P_s$ is in kPa, $P_{atm}$ must also be in kPa. > > 4. **Mixing Dew Point with RH:** Dew point is a **temperature** (┬░C or K), RH is a **percentage**. They are NOT interchangeable. > > 5. **Glass Electrode Calibration:** In a 14-mark question, examiners expect at least a brief mention of **two-point calibration** (pH 7 and pH 4 or 10) and **ATC**. > > 6. **No Block Diagram = Lose Marks:** A 7-mark part without a labelled diagram will lose 2ŌĆō3 marks in KTU valuation. Always include one. > > 7. **Antoine Constants Validity:** State the **valid temperature range** of any Antoine equation used. For water 0ŌĆō60 ┬░C; for other liquids, ranges differ. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST ŌĆö Module 2: pH & Humidity** - [x] **pH = ŌłÆlog[HŌü║]**, dimensionless, range 0ŌĆō14. - [x] **Neutral pH = 7** at **25 ┬░C** only. - [x] **Nernst Equation:** $E = K + \frac{2.303\,RT}{F}\,\text{pH}$. - [x] **Nernst slope at 25 ┬░C = 59.16 mV/pH**; increases linearly with absolute temperature $T$. - [x] **Glass electrode** is the industrial workhorse; **ATC is mandatory** for accuracy. - [x] **Combination electrode** = glass + reference in one body. - [x] **Relative Humidity** depends on temperature; **Dew Point** is invariant for a given $P_w$. - [x] **Three primary humidity definitions:** Absolute Humidity, Relative Humidity, Dew Point. - [x] **Antoine Equation** gives $P_s$ as a function of temperature; constants must be in the correct units. - [x] **Psychrometric equation** links wet-bulb, dry-bulb, $P_w$, and $P_{atm}$ via psychrometric constant $A$. - [x] **Capacitive humidity sensors** are preferred industrially for **linearity, range, and stability**. - [x] **Dew Point Meters (Chilled Mirror)** are the **primary standard** for humidity calibration. - [x] **Faraday's constant $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol**; **Universal gas constant $R = 8.314$ J/(mol┬ĘK)**. - [x] **Molar mass of water $M_w = 18.015$ g/mol**. - [x] **$K_w = 10^{-14}$** at 25 ┬░C; $\text{pH} + \text{pOH} = 14$. - [x] **For 14-mark KTU questions:** always include a labelled block diagram, proper units, and the Nernst/psychrometric derivation steps. > [!NOTE] > **Closing Examiner's Note:** A high-scoring KTU answer in this topic must contain: > (1) A **labelled block diagram** (2 marks), > (2) **The relevant formula(s) with defined variables** (2 marks), > (3) **Step-by-step substitution with units** (3ŌĆō4 marks), > (4) **Final boxed answer with correct significant figures** (1ŌĆō2 marks), > (5) **A concluding remark on practical/industrial relevance** (1 mark). > Aim for **at least 4 of these 5 elements** in every long answer to secure Ōēź 11/14.

topics.title: Noise Pollution - Measurement of Sound
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT413 - PROCESS INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Noise Pollution - Measurement of Sound ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) **Sound** is a mechanical wave that propagates through an elastic medium (solid, liquid, or gas) as a longitudinal pressure variation, characterized by parameters of frequency, wavelength, amplitude, and propagation speed. In the context of **Process Instrumentation and Control Engineering**, sound is treated as an acoustic variable that requires precise transduction (microphones), signal conditioning (preamplifiers, weighting networks), and analysis (frequency spectrum, level metrics) for industrial hygiene, occupational safety, and environmental compliance monitoring. **Noise Pollution** is defined as the presence of unwanted, excessive, or harmful sound that disrupts human activity, animal life, or the proper functioning of industrial equipment. From an instrumentation perspective, noise is a measurable acoustic phenomenon quantified through standardized metrics such as **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)**, **Sound Power Level (PWL)**, and **Equivalent Continuous Sound Level (Leq)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 scheme requires students to understand the physical basis of sound generation, the decibel (dB) logarithmic scale, types of sound level meters, weighting networks (A, B, C, Z), octave band analysis, and Indian/International standards governing noise measurement (IS 9876, IEC 61672, OSHA standards). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine standing at the edge of a calm pond. When you drop a pebble, you see ripples traveling outward ŌĆö high-frequency (small) ripples when the pebble is small, and low-frequency (broad) waves when you toss a large stone. **Sound behaves identically**, but instead of water-surface ripples, it is **alternating compressions and rarefactions of air molecules** that travel from the source to your ear. A more practical analogy: think of a **car horn (80ŌĆō90 dB)** versus a **jet engine (140 dB)**. The human ear cannot linearly perceive such a vast range (10 trillion times in power). The **decibel scale** compresses this enormous range into manageable numbers ŌĆö like converting a billion-dollar economy into a logarithmic chart that is easy to read. > [!NOTE] > **Reference Pressure (pŌéĆ):** **20 ╬╝Pa (micropascals)** is the internationally accepted threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz. All sound pressure measurements are referenced to this value. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall) | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Reference Sound Pressure | $p_0$ | **20 ├Ś 10Ōü╗ŌüČ Pa** | Pascal (Pa) | | Reference Sound Intensity | $I_0$ | **10Ōü╗┬╣┬▓** | W/m┬▓ | | Reference Sound Power | $W_0$ | **10Ōü╗┬╣┬▓** | Watt (W) | | Speed of Sound in Air (20┬░C) | $c$ | **343** | m/s | | Density of Air (20┬░C) | $\rho$ | **1.204** | kg/m┬│ | | Threshold of Hearing | SPLŌéĆ | **0 dB** | dB re 20 ╬╝Pa | | Threshold of Pain | SPL_max | **120ŌĆō140** | dB re 20 ╬╝Pa | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Fundamental Acoustic Parameters #### A. Sound Pressure (p) The instantaneous local pressure deviation from ambient atmospheric pressure caused by a sound wave, measured in Pascals (Pa). Human hearing spans from **20 ╬╝Pa (rustle of leaves)** to **200 Pa (jet engine)** ŌĆö a factor of 10 million in linear terms. #### B. Sound Intensity (I) The acoustic power transmitted per unit area in a specified direction at a point: $$I = \frac{p^2}{\rho \cdot c}$$ where $\rho$ is the medium density and $c$ is the speed of sound. Intensity is a **vector quantity** indicating energy flow direction. #### C. Sound Power (W) The total acoustic energy radiated by a source per unit time. It is an **intrinsic property** of the source ŌĆö independent of the surrounding environment. #### D. Frequency (f) and Wavelength (╬╗) The relationship between wavelength, frequency, and propagation speed: $$\lambda = \frac{c}{f}$$ In air at 20┬░C, a **1 kHz** tone has a wavelength of approximately **0.343 m**. ### 2.2 The Decibel (dB) Scale ŌĆö Why Logarithmic? The human ear responds **logarithmically** to acoustic stimuli. A sound 10 times more intense is perceived as "twice as loud" ŌĆö not 10 times. The decibel scale elegantly captures this psychoacoustic behavior. > [!NOTE] > **Why log scale?** > 1. Compresses enormous linear range (10ŌüĘ Pa) into manageable numbers (0ŌĆō140 dB) > 2. Matches human hearing perception (Weber-Fechner Law) > 3. Simplifies multiplication/division of intensities into addition/subtraction ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Reference Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p}{p_0}\right)$ | $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ | dB | | 2 | Sound Intensity Level | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ | dB | | 3 | Sound Power Level | $L_W = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{W}{W_0}\right)$ | $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ | dB | | 4 | Wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | $c = 343$ m/s | m | | 5 | IntensityŌĆōPressure Relation | $I = \dfrac{p^2}{\rho c}$ | ŌĆö | W/m┬▓ | | 6 | Speed of Sound in Air | $c = \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma R T}{M}}$ | $\gamma = 1.4$ | m/s | | 7 | Addition of Two Levels | $L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{L_1/10} + 10^{L_2/10}\right)$ | ŌĆö | dB | | 8 | N Identical Sources | $L_{\text{total}} = L_1 + 10 \log_{10}(N)$ | ŌĆö | dB | | 9 | Doubling of Distance (Free Field) | $\Delta L = -6$ dB per doubling | ŌĆö | dB | | 10 | Equivalent Continuous Level | $L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T}\!\int_0^T \dfrac{p^2(t)}{p_0^2}\,dt\right)$ | ŌĆö | dB | | 11 | Octave Band Ratio | $f_2 / f_1 = 2$ | ŌĆö | ratio | | 12 | Sound Exposure Level | $L_{SE} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{t_0}\!\int_0^T \dfrac{p^2(t)}{p_0^2}\,dt\right)$ | $t_0 = 1$ s | dB | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:** > - **Sound Pressure Level** uses factor **20** (because pressure is amplitude-squared ŌåÆ $20 \log$). > - **Sound Intensity / Power Level** uses factor **10** (because these are already power-like ŌåÆ $10 \log$). > Examiners frequently test this distinction ŌĆö mark allocation is often 2 marks for this concept alone. ### 2.4 Frequency Weighting Networks (A, B, C, Z) | Weighting | Application | Frequency Response | |---|---|---| | **A-weighting (dBA)** | Environmental noise, occupational health | Approximates 40-phon equal-loudness contour | | **B-weighting (dBB)** | Intermediate loudness (rarely used today) | Approximates 70-phon contour | | **C-weighting (dBC)** | Peak/peakŌĆōpeak measurements, entertainment | Near-flat, emphasizes low frequencies | | **Z-weighting (dBZ)** | True unweighted (linear) response | Flat from 10 Hz to 20 kHz | > **Engineering Reality:** Nearly all modern regulatory limits (Indian CPCB, OSHA, WHO) specify **dBA** because it correlates well with the human ear's sensitivity to environmental noise and the risk of hearing damage. ### 2.5 Time Weighting (F, S, I) | Weighting | Time Constant (Žä) | Use Case | |---|---|---| | **Fast (F)** | 125 ms | Most general measurements | | **Slow (S)** | 1000 ms | Fluctuating signals, average level | | **Impulse (I)** | 35 ms (rise), 1.5 s (decay) | Short-duration impact noise | ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Occupational Health & Safety (OHS):** Prevents Noise-Induced Hearing Loss (NIHL) in industrial workers. - **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA):** Mandatory for any new plant, highway, or airport. - **Process Control:** Vibration/acoustic emission monitoring in rotating machinery, bearings, and valves. - **Building Acoustics:** HVAC noise control in hospitals, recording studios. - **Automotive NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness):** Cabin noise optimization. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Why 20 logŌéüŌéĆ for Sound Pressure? **Starting Point:** The decibel is defined as a ratio of powers: $$N_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$ **Step 1 ŌĆö Replace Power with Intensity.** For acoustic waves in the same medium under identical conditions, intensity $I \propto p^2$ (acoustic power is proportional to the square of RMS pressure). $$L_p = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p^2}{p_0^2}\right)$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Logarithm Power Rule** ($\log_{10}(a^n) = n \log_{10} a$): $$L_p = 10 \cdot 2 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right)$$ $$\boxed{\,L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right)\,}$$ This is the canonical sound pressure level formula ŌĆö **derivable in 2 minutes on an exam sheet**, scoring full marks. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Speed of Sound in Air (c = ŌłÜ(╬│RT/M)) **Starting Point ŌĆö Newton's equation for an adiabatic sound wave:** $$c = \sqrt{\frac{K_s}{\rho}}$$ where $K_s$ is the adiabatic bulk modulus and $\rho$ is the medium density. **Step 1 ŌĆö Use the Ideal Gas Relation.** For an adiabatic process, $PV^\gamma = \text{constant}$, which gives: $$K_s = \gamma P$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Use the Ideal Gas Law** to eliminate $\rho$: $$P = \rho \cdot \frac{RT}{M} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho = \frac{PM}{RT}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute** $K_s$ and $\rho$ into the wave speed equation: $$c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma P}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma P}{PM / RT}}$$ $$c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma R T}{M}}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Evaluate at 20┬░C (T = 293 K), M = 0.029 kg/mol, R = 8.314 J/(mol┬ĘK), ╬│ = 1.4:** $$c = \sqrt{\frac{1.4 \times 8.314 \times 293}{0.029}}$$ $$c = \sqrt{\frac{3410.5}{0.029}} = \sqrt{117{,}603} \approx 343\ \text{m/s}$$ This **matches the experimentally accepted value of 343 m/s** at 20┬░C. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example: Addition of Two Sound Sources **Problem:** Two machines operate simultaneously in a plant. Machine A produces 85 dBA, Machine B produces 88 dBA. What is the combined sound pressure level? **Step 1 ŌĆö Use the addition formula:** $$L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{L_1/10} + 10^{L_2/10}\right)$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute the values:** $$L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{85/10} + 10^{88/10}\right)$$ $$L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{8.5} + 10^{8.8}\right)$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the antilogarithms:** $$10^{8.5} = 3.162 \times 10^{8}$$ $$10^{8.8} = 6.310 \times 10^{8}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Sum and take the log:** $$L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left((3.162 + 6.310) \times 10^{8}\right)$$ $$L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(9.472 \times 10^{8}\right)$$ $$L_{\text{total}} = 10 \times 8.9766 = 89.77 \approx 89.8\ \text{dBA}$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Engineering Interpretation:** Adding an 85 dBA source to an 88 dBA source increases the level by only **1.8 dB**, because the louder source dominates. If both were equal at 88 dBA, the total would be **91 dBA** (a 3 dB increase ŌĆö the classic "doubling of acoustic power" rule). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rule of Thumb for KTU:** > - Two **equal** sources ŌåÆ **+3 dB** (doubling of intensity) > - One source is **Ōēź 10 dB higher** ŌåÆ it dominates; the other contributes < 0.5 dB and may be ignored. > - 10 dB difference ŌåÆ perceived as "twice as loud." --- ### 3.4 Derivation: Distance Attenuation in Free Field (Inverse Square Law) **Starting Point ŌĆö Point source radiates uniformly.** The intensity at distance $r$ from a point source: $$I = \frac{W}{4\pi r^2}$$ **Step 1 ŌĆö Ratio of intensities at two distances $r_1$ and $r_2$:** $$\frac{I_2}{I_1} = \left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right)^2$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Convert to dB:** $$\Delta L = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_1}{I_2}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)^2 = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö For doubling of distance** ($r_2 = 2 r_1$): $$\Delta L = 20 \log_{10}(2) = 20 \times 0.3010 = 6.02 \approx 6\ \text{dB}$$ $$\boxed{\,\text{Doubling the distance from a point source reduces SPL by 6 dB.}\,}$$ > **Note for Line Sources (e.g., highways):** The falloff is only **3 dB per doubling** because intensity falls as $1/r$ instead of $1/r^2$. --- ### 3.5 Equivalent Continuous Sound Level (Leq) ŌĆö Energy Average $$L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T \frac{p^2(t)}{p_0^2}\,dt\right)$$ **Discrete-Form Practical Use:** For sampled SPL data over $N$ intervals of duration $\Delta t$: $$L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} 10^{L_i/10}\right)$$ This is the metric used by **CPCB (India)**, **OSHA**, and **WHO** for daily exposure assessment. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Sound Level Meter (SLM) Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Sound Source] --> B[Microphone
Transducer] B --> C[Preamplifier
Impedance Matching] C --> D[Frequency Weighting
A, B, C, Z Networks] D --> E[Time Weighting
Fast, Slow, Impulse] E --> F[Signal Processing
RMS, Leq, Statistics] F --> G[Display Unit
dB Readout] F --> H[Data Storage
Memory Card] F --> I[Output Ports
USB RS232] J[Calibrator 94 dB 1 kHz] -.->|Reference Check| B K[Octave Band Filter
1 or 1/3 Octave] --> D ``` > **Operational Sequence (Reading the Diagram):** > 1. Acoustic pressure ŌåÆ **microphone** (capacitive/electret) converts pressure to voltage. > 2. **Preamplifier** boosts the weak signal and matches high-impedance output. > 3. **Weighting network** applies A/B/C/Z frequency response. > 4. **Time weighting** smooths the signal using exponential averaging. > 5. **Signal processor** computes RMS, peak, Leq, statistical percentiles (L10, L50, L90). > 6. Result displayed in dB and optionally stored/exported. --- ### 4.2 Octave Band Analyzer Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Audio Signal] --> B[Anti-Aliasing Filter] B --> C[Bandpass Filter Bank
31.5 Hz to 16 kHz] C --> D1[31.5 Hz Band] C --> D2[63 Hz Band] C --> D3[125 Hz Band] C --> D4[250 Hz Band] C --> D5[500 Hz Band] C --> D6[1 kHz Band] C --> D7[2 kHz Band] C --> D8[4 kHz Band] C --> D9[8 kHz Band] D1 --> E[RMS Detector per Band] D2 --> E D3 --> E D4 --> E D5 --> E D6 --> E D7 --> E D8 --> E D9 --> E E --> F[Display SPL per Band] ``` > **Why Octave Bands?** A single dBA value hides spectral information. An 80 dBA reading might be a high-frequency hiss (easy to control) or a low-frequency rumble (requires structural damping). Octave band analysis pinpoints the dominant frequencies for targeted noise control. --- ### 4.3 Noise Measurement Workflow (Field Procedure) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Define Measurement Objective] --> B[Select Site and Time] B --> C[Choose Equipment Type 1 or 2] C --> D[Field Calibration 94 dB] D --> E[Mount Microphone on Tripod 1.2 to 1.5 m] E --> F[Record SPL Over Sampling Period] F --> G[Apply Corrections Background Wind Temp] G --> H[Compute Leq Ln Percentiles] H --> I[Compare with Regulatory Limits] I --> J[Generate Compliance Report] ``` > **Key Standards Referenced:** > - **IEC 61672-1** ŌĆö Specification for sound level meters > - **IEC 61260** ŌĆö Octave-band and fractional-octave-band filters > - **IS 9876 (Part 1)** ŌĆö Indian Standard for SLM > - **ISO 1996** ŌĆö Description, measurement, and assessment of environmental noise ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]** Define Sound Pressure Level (SPL). State its mathematical expression and the standard reference pressure used. **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Sound Pressure Level is the logarithmic measure of the RMS sound pressure of a sound wave, expressed in decibels (dB) relative to a standard reference pressure. **Mathematical Expression (1 mark):** $$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right)\ \text{dB}$$ **Reference Pressure (1 mark):** The standard reference pressure is $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ (i.e., $20 \times 10^{-6}$ Pa), which corresponds to the threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]** Differentiate between **A-weighting** and **C-weighting** networks used in sound level meters. State one application of each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | A-Weighting (dBA) | C-Weighting (dBC) | |---|---|---| | **Frequency Response** | Approximates 40-phon equal-loudness curve; attenuates low frequencies heavily | Near-flat across 31.5 HzŌĆō8 kHz; minor attenuation below 31.5 Hz and above 8 kHz | | **Application** | Environmental noise, occupational exposure assessment, regulatory compliance | Entertainment noise, peak level measurement of impulsive sounds | **A-weighting (1.5 marks):** Used in most regulatory measurements because it correlates with human hearing sensitivity and the risk of hearing damage at moderate levels. **C-weighting (1.5 marks):** Used where low-frequency content is significant, such as measuring peak levels of machinery or for aircraft/blast noise where low frequencies dominate. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice]** **(a)** Derive the mathematical expression for Sound Pressure Level. Explain why a logarithmic scale is used for representing sound levels. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A factory floor has three identical machines, each producing a sound pressure level of 80 dB. Calculate: (i) the total SPL when all three operate simultaneously, and (ii) the percentage increase in sound intensity. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(a) **Derivation (5 marks):** The decibel scale is fundamentally a power ratio expressed logarithmically. For acoustic power $P$: $$N_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right)$$ For plane and spherical sound waves, acoustic intensity $I$ is proportional to the square of the RMS sound pressure $p$: $$I \propto p^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{I}{I_0} = \frac{p^2}{p_0^2}$$ Substituting: $$L_p = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p^2}{p_0^2}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right)\ \text{dB}$$ where $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ is the reference sound pressure (threshold of hearing at 1 kHz). **Why Logarithmic Scale (2 marks):** 1. The human ear responds logarithmically to acoustic stimuli (Weber-Fechner Law). A 10-fold increase in intensity is perceived as a doubling of loudness. 2. The range of human hearing spans from 20 ╬╝Pa to 200 Pa ŌĆö a 10-million-fold linear ratio. The log scale compresses this to a manageable 0ŌĆō140 dB range. 3. Multiplication/division of intensities becomes simple addition/subtraction in dB. 4. The scale matches equal-loudness contours in psychoacoustics. --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(b) **Given:** Three identical machines, each producing $L_1 = 80$ dB. **(i) Total SPL of 3 identical sources (4 marks):** Using the formula $L_{\text{total}} = L_1 + 10 \log_{10}(N)$, where $N = 3$: $$L_{\text{total}} = 80 + 10 \log_{10}(3)$$ $$L_{\text{total}} = 80 + 10 \times 0.4771$$ $$L_{\text{total}} = 80 + 4.77 = 84.77\ \text{dB} \approx 84.8\ \text{dB}$$ **(ii) Percentage increase in intensity (3 marks):** First, find the original intensity of one source: $$L_1 = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_1}{I_0}\right) = 80\ \text{dB} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{I_1}{I_0} = 10^{8}$$ Three identical machines give three times the intensity: $$\frac{I_{\text{total}}}{I_0} = 3 \times 10^{8}$$ Percentage increase: $$\%\text{ increase} = \frac{I_{\text{total}} - I_1}{I_1} \times 100 = \frac{(3 \times 10^8) - 10^8}{10^8} \times 100 = 200\%$$ **Final Answer:** $L_{\text{total}} = 84.8$ dB; **intensity increase = 200%** (despite only a 4.77 dB rise ŌĆö illustrating the dB scale's compression). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Module 2 Choice]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Sound Level Meter. State the functions of frequency weighting and time weighting networks. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A point source produces a sound pressure level of 90 dB at a distance of 5 m. Calculate the SPL at: (i) 10 m, (ii) 20 m, and (iii) 40 m. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(a) **Block Diagram (3 marks):** ``` Sound ŌåÆ Microphone ŌåÆ Preamplifier ŌåÆ Weighting Network ŌåÆ RMS Detector ŌåÆ Time Averaging ŌåÆ Display ``` The microphone (capacitive/electret type) converts acoustic pressure to an electrical signal. The preamplifier provides impedance matching and signal gain. The frequency weighting network (A/B/C/Z) applies standardized response curves. The RMS detector computes the true root-mean-square value, and the time-weighting circuit (Fast/Slow/Impulse) provides exponential averaging. The processed DC voltage drives the analog or digital display in dB. **Frequency Weighting (2 marks):** - **A-weighting (dBA):** Filters the signal to mimic the human ear's response at low sound levels. Used for environmental and occupational noise. - **C-weighting (dBC):** Near-flat response, used for peak measurements of impulsive or low-frequency-heavy noise. - **Z-weighting:** Linear, unweighted ŌĆö for laboratory-grade measurements. **Time Weighting (2 marks):** - **Fast (F):** 125 ms time constant ŌĆö used for most general measurements. - **Slow (S):** 1000 ms time constant ŌĆö used for fluctuating signals to obtain smoothed average. - **Impulse (I):** 35 ms rise / 1.5 s decay ŌĆö used for transient impact sounds. --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(b) **Given:** Point source, $L_5 = 90$ dB at $r_1 = 5$ m. Find SPL at $r_2 = 10, 20, 40$ m. **Formula for free-field point source:** $$L_2 = L_1 - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$$ **(i) At 10 m (2 marks):** $$L_{10} = 90 - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10}{5}\right) = 90 - 20 \log_{10}(2) = 90 - 6.02 = 83.98 \approx 84\ \text{dB}$$ **(ii) At 20 m (2 marks):** $$L_{20} = 90 - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{20}{5}\right) = 90 - 20 \log_{10}(4) = 90 - 12.04 = 77.96 \approx 78\ \text{dB}$$ **(iii) At 40 m (3 marks):** $$L_{40} = 90 - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{40}{5}\right) = 90 - 20 \log_{10}(8) = 90 - 18.06 = 71.94 \approx 72\ \text{dB}$$ **Comment (Bonus, 1 mark):** Each doubling of distance reduces SPL by 6 dB, consistent with the inverse-square law for point sources in a free field. The intensity falls to one-fourth per doubling. | Distance (m) | SPL (dB) | Drop (dB) | |---|---|---| | 5 | 90 | ŌĆö | | 10 | 84 | 6 | | 20 | 78 | 6 | | 40 | 72 | 6 | --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Mixing 20 log and 10 log:** Students frequently use 10 logŌéüŌéĆ for SPL. **Mark loss: 1ŌĆō2 marks** per occurrence. > 2. **Wrong reference pressure:** Writing $p_0 = 10\ \mu\text{Pa}$ instead of 20 ╬╝Pa is a frequent error. **Mark loss: 1 mark.** > 3. **Confusing dBA with dB:** "A" weighting is not just a unit ŌĆö it is a frequency-dependent correction. Always mention A-weighting in occupational/environmental answers. > 4. **Forgetting the doubling rule:** When asked "two equal sources," many students answer 2 ├Ś dB (linear) instead of +3 dB. **Mark loss: 2 marks.** > 5. **Missing log base:** Some write $\log$ without base 10. Examiners expect $\log_{10}$ explicitly. > 6. **Octave band ratio:** Some students write $f_2/f_1 = 1.5$ for octave bands. Correct ratio is **2** (1/3-octave uses $2^{1/3} \approx 1.26$). > 7. **Unit inconsistency:** Intensity in W/m┬▓ but power in W ŌĆö keep the unit specified when reporting. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Sound Pressure Level:** $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_0)$, with $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$. - **Sound Intensity / Power Level:** Uses **10** as the multiplier, with reference $10^{-12}$ W (or W/m┬▓). - **Doubling rule (equal sources):** Total SPL increases by **+3 dB**. - **Distance rule (point source):** SPL drops by **ŌłÆ6 dB per doubling of distance**. - **Speed of sound in air at 20┬░C:** $c \approx 343$ m/s; in water $\approx 1480$ m/s; in steel $\approx 5000$ m/s. - **Octave band:** Center frequency ratio of 2 (e.g., 500 Hz, 1 kHz, 2 kHz). - **1/3-octave band:** Ratio of $2^{1/3} \approx 1.26$. - **A-weighting (dBA):** Default metric for environmental and occupational noise. - **Leq:** Energy-equivalent continuous SPL ŌĆö the standard for time-varying noise. - **L10, L50, L90:** Statistical percentile levels exceeded 10%, 50%, 90% of the measurement time. - **SLM classes (IEC 61672):** Class 1 (precision, ┬▒0.7 dB), Class 2 (general, ┬▒1.5 dB). - **Calibration:** Always perform pre- and post-measurement calibration with **94 dB, 1 kHz** acoustic calibrator. - **Microphone height:** **1.2ŌĆō1.5 m** above ground for free-field environmental measurements. - **Indian Standards:** IS 9876 (SLM), IS 4954 (industrial noise), CPCB ambient noise rules. - **Threshold limits:** OSHA PEL 90 dBA (8-hr TWA); WHO recommends 70 dBA outdoor average. > **Final Exam Tip:** Always mention the **standard** (IEC/IS), the **weighting** (A/C/Z), the **time weighting** (F/S/I), and the **distance** of measurement in your answer to score full marks in instrumentation questions.

topics.title: Tolerable Levels of Sound
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT413 - PROCESS INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Tolerable Levels of Sound ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition **Tolerable Levels of Sound** refer to the maximum permissible intensities (or pressures) of acoustic energy to which human beings can be exposed over specific durations without suffering temporary or permanent physiological damage, particularly to the auditory system. These levels are mathematically expressed on a **logarithmic decibel (dB) scale** because the human ear responds to acoustic stimuli in a logarithmic, not linear, manner. Regulatory bodies such as **OSHA (Occupational Safety and Health Administration)**, **NIOSH (National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health)**, and **ISO (International Organization for Standardization)** define these limits in terms of **A-weighted decibels (dBA)**, **Time-Weighted Average (TWA)**, **Equivalent Continuous Sound Level (Leq)**, and **Noise Dose (%)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The module specifically tests the student's ability to (1) define dB scales, (2) compute permissible exposure durations for given SPLs, (3) explain frequency weighting curves, and (4) relate industrial noise sources to occupational health standards. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a light switch with a smooth dimmer. As you slide the dimmer, the perceived brightness does not increase linearly with the voltage applied ŌĆö your eyes perceive a logarithmic progression. The human ear behaves the same way with sound. The faintest whisper you can hear in a quiet room corresponds to a sound pressure of about **20 microPascals (┬ĄPa)**, while a jet engine at close range pushes the pressure to **200 Pascals (Pa)** ŌĆö that is a 10-million-fold increase in pressure! Because handling such a massive range linearly is impractical, engineers use the **decibel** ŌĆö a compact logarithmic unit ŌĆö to "compress" this huge range into a manageable scale from **0 dB** (threshold of hearing) to about **140 dB** (threshold of pain). "Tolerable" simply means: *at what point on this compressed scale does the ear (or the brain) start screaming "enough!"?* > [!NOTE] > **Key Reference Constants Used Globally:** > - Reference sound pressure: **$p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa} = 20 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{Pa}$** > - Reference sound intensity: **$I_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{W/m}^2$** > - Threshold of hearing: **0 dB** > - Threshold of pain: **120 ŌĆō 140 dB** > - OSHA PEL: **90 dBA for 8 hours** > - NIOSH REL: **85 dBA for 8 hours** ### Why Logarithmic? The decibel was not invented arbitrarily. Three biological realities demand a logarithmic scale: 1. **Huge dynamic range** of human hearing (10ŌüĘ in pressure, 10┬╣Ōü┤ in intensity). 2. The ear's response follows approximately the **Weber-Fechner Law** ŌĆö perceived loudness is proportional to the logarithm of stimulus intensity. 3. Multiplication of acoustic energies (in cascaded systems) becomes simple **addition** in dB. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Decibel ŌĆö Mathematical Foundation The **Bel** was originally named in honour of Alexander Graham Bell. One Bel represents a 10:1 power ratio. Because the Bel is a rather large step for everyday acoustics, the **decibel (dB)**, which is 1/10 of a Bel, is universally used. For **sound pressure level (SPL)**, since acoustic power is proportional to the square of pressure, the factor of 20 appears in the formula: $$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right) \quad [\text{dB}]$$ For **sound intensity level (SIL)**, the factor of 10 appears because intensity itself is a power-like quantity: $$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) \quad [\text{dB}]$$ For **sound power level (PWL)** radiated by a source: $$L_W = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{W_0}\right) \quad [\text{dB}]$$ where the reference power is **$W_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{W}$**. ### 2.2 Combining Sound Sources A very common KTU-style question is: *"If two machines produce 80 dB and 83 dB individually, what is the combined SPL?"* **Logic Steps:** 1. Convert dB values back to linear intensities using the inverse formula: $I = I_0 \cdot 10^{L/10}$. 2. Add the linear intensities. 3. Convert the sum back to dB. 4. **Shortcut Rule of Thumb:** If two sources differ by **Ōēż 3 dB**, add 3 dB. If they differ by **> 10 dB**, the louder source dominates (add 0 dB). In between, use interpolation tables. ### 2.3 Frequency Weighting (A, B, C) The human ear is not equally sensitive to all frequencies. To make sound level meters replicate the ear's response, **standardized weighting filters** are applied: | Weighting | Application | Attenuation at 1 kHz | |---|---|---| | **A-weighting (dBA)** | Industrial / environmental noise (most common) | 0 dB | | **B-weighting (dBB)** | Intermediate loudness (rarely used) | 0 dB | | **C-weighting (dBC)** | Peak / impact noise measurement | 0 dB | | **Z-weighting (dBZ)** | Linear (no weighting) | 0 dB | > [!NOTE] > **A-weighting** is mandated by virtually every occupational safety regulation worldwide because it correlates best with **noise-induced hearing loss (NIHL)**. ### 2.4 Equivalent Continuous Sound Level ($L_{eq}$) Many industrial processes are not steady. To compare a varying noise against a constant standard, we use: $$L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} 10^{L(t)/10}\, dt\right] \quad [\text{dBA}]$$ For discrete time intervals (the practical case): $$L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\frac{1}{T}\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i \cdot 10^{L_i/10}\right]$$ ### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_0)$ | dB | $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ | | 2 | Sound Intensity Level | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I/I_0)$ | dB | $I_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{W/m}^2$ | | 3 | Sound Power Level | $L_W = 10 \log_{10}(W/W_0)$ | dB | $W_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{W}$ | | 4 | Permissible Exposure Time (OSHA) | $T = \dfrac{8}{2^{(L-90)/5}}$ | hours | 90 dBA criterion, 5 dB exchange | | 5 | Permissible Exposure Time (NIOSH) | $T = \dfrac{8}{2^{(L-85)/3}}$ | hours | 85 dBA criterion, 3 dB exchange | | 6 | Daily Noise Dose (D) | $D = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n}\dfrac{C_i}{T_i} \times 100$ | % | $C_i$ = time at level $i$, $T_i$ = reference duration | | 7 | Time-Weighted Average (TWA) | $\text{TWA} = 16.61 \log_{10}(D/100) + 90$ | dBA | OSHA 8-hour reference | | 8 | Equivalent SPL | $L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\frac{1}{T}\sum t_i 10^{L_i/10}\right]$ | dBA | Varying noise | | 9 | Sound Exposure Level | $\text{SEL} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\int 10^{L(t)/10} dt\right]$ | dB | Normalized to 1 s | | 10 | Inverse Conversion (p) | $p = p_0 \cdot 10^{L_p/20}$ | Pa | From dB to pressure | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exchange Rate** is the increase in dB that halves the permissible exposure time. OSHA uses **5 dB**, NIOSH and most of the world use **3 dB**. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Understanding tolerable levels is critical in: - **Hearing conservation programs** in heavy industries (steel, mining, aviation). - **Process plant design** ŌĆö sizing silencers, mufflers, and acoustic enclosures. - **Environmental impact assessments** for community noise compliance. - **Product design** ŌĆö electric vehicles, HVAC systems, and home appliances must meet noise emission norms (e.g., EU Directive 2003/10/EC). - **Medical audiology** ŌĆö calibrating audiometers and hearing aids. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the OSHA Permissible Exposure Time **Statement:** *A worker can be exposed to 90 dBA for 8 hours. For every 5 dB increase, the permissible exposure time halves.* **Starting Point ŌĆö The 5 dB Doubling Rule:** Let $L$ = measured SPL in dBA. We seek a permissible exposure time $T(L)$ such that: $$T(L+5) = \frac{1}{2}\,T(L) \quad \text{(1)}$$ and the boundary condition: $$T(90) = 8\ \text{hours} \quad \text{(2)}$$ **Step 1 ŌĆö Assume an exponential model.** Since doubling follows a constant increment of 5 dB, the most natural model is: $$T(L) = A \cdot b^{L} \quad \text{(3)}$$ for some constants $A$ and base $b$. **Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the doubling condition (1):** $$ \begin{aligned} T(L+5) &= A \cdot b^{L+5} = A \cdot b^{L} \cdot b^{5} \\ \frac{T(L+5)}{T(L)} &= b^{5} = \frac{1}{2} \\ \therefore\quad b^{5} &= \frac{1}{2} \\ \therefore\quad b &= \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{1/5} = 2^{-1/5} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the boundary condition (2):** $$ \begin{aligned} 8 &= A \cdot (2^{-1/5})^{90} = A \cdot 2^{-90/5} = A \cdot 2^{-18} \\ A &= 8 \cdot 2^{18} = 8 \cdot 262144 = 2097152 = 2^{21} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Write the final compact formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} T(L) &= 2^{21} \cdot \left(2^{-1/5}\right)^{L} = 2^{21} \cdot 2^{-L/5} = 2^{21 - L/5} \\ T(L) &= 2^{(105 - L)/5} \\ T(L) &= \frac{2^{21}}{2^{L/5}} = \frac{2^{21}}{2^{(L-90)/5} \cdot 2^{18}} \\ \therefore\quad T(L) &= \frac{8}{2^{(L-90)/5}} \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Verification (Numerical Check):** - $L = 90\ \text{dBA}$: $T = 8/2^0 = 8$ hours Ō£ō - $L = 95\ \text{dBA}$: $T = 8/2^1 = 4$ hours Ō£ō - $L = 100\ \text{dBA}$: $T = 8/2^2 = 2$ hours Ō£ō - $L = 85\ \text{dBA}$: $T = 8/2^{-1} = 16$ hours (capped at 8 by OSHA). --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Daily Noise Dose ($D$) **Statement:** *The noise dose is the percentage of an employee's actual noise exposure relative to the permissible exposure.* **Starting Point ŌĆö Definition of Dose:** For a single noise level $L_i$ lasting time $C_i$, the partial dose is $C_i / T_i$, where $T_i$ is the OSHA reference duration at that level: $$ \begin{aligned} D_i &= \frac{C_i}{T_i} = \frac{C_i}{\dfrac{8}{2^{(L_i - 90)/5}}} = \frac{C_i \cdot 2^{(L_i - 90)/5}}{8} \end{aligned} $$ Summing over all intervals of the day: $$ D = \sum_{i=1}^{n} D_i = \frac{1}{8}\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \cdot 2^{(L_i - 90)/5} $$ Expressed as a percentage (multiplying by 100): $$ D(\%) = \frac{100}{8}\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \cdot 2^{(L_i - 90)/5} = 12.5 \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \cdot 2^{(L_i - 90)/5} $$ **KTU Quick Table (OSHA Standard):** | $L$ (dBA) | Permissible $T$ (h) | |---|---| | 80 | 32 (capped at 8) | | 85 | 16 (capped at 8) | | 90 | 8 | | 95 | 4 | | 100 | 2 | | 105 | 1 | | 110 | 0.5 | | 115 | 0.25 | --- ### 3.3 Derivation of TWA from Dose Given dose $D$ (as a decimal, not %), the Time-Weighted Average over 8 hours is: $$ \begin{aligned} D &= \frac{C_1}{T_1} \quad (\text{for an 8-hour shift at constant } L) \\ \frac{C_1}{T_1} &= \frac{8}{\dfrac{8}{2^{(L-90)/5}}} = 2^{(L-90)/5} \\ D &= 2^{(L-90)/5} \\ \log_2 D &= \frac{L - 90}{5} \\ L &= 90 + 5 \log_2 D \end{aligned} $$ Converting $\log_2$ to $\log_{10}$ using $\log_2 x = \log_{10} x / \log_{10} 2 \approx \log_{10} x / 0.30103$: $$ L = 90 + \frac{5}{0.30103}\log_{10} D \approx 90 + 16.61 \log_{10} D \quad \blacksquare $$ In terms of percentage dose $D_\% = 100D$: $$\text{TWA} = 90 + 16.61 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{D_\%}{100}\right)$$ --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Style) **Problem:** A worker is exposed to the following noise levels in a single shift: - 4 hours at **95 dBA** - 3 hours at **100 dBA** - 1 hour at **85 dBA** Calculate: (a) the daily noise dose, (b) the 8-hour TWA, and (c) whether the exposure is within OSHA limits. **Solution ŌĆö Step 1: Permissible durations (from OSHA table)** $$ T_{95} = 4\ \text{h},\quad T_{100} = 2\ \text{h},\quad T_{85} = 16\ \text{h (capped at 8)} $$ **Step 2: Compute partial doses** $$ \begin{aligned} D_{95} &= \frac{4}{4} = 1.000 \\ D_{100} &= \frac{3}{2} = 1.500 \\ D_{85} &= \frac{1}{16} = 0.0625 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Total Dose** $$ D = 1.000 + 1.500 + 0.0625 = 2.5625 \quad\Rightarrow\quad D_\% = 256.25\% $$ **Step 4: TWA** $$ \text{TWA} = 90 + 16.61 \log_{10}(2.5625) = 90 + 16.61 \times 0.4086 = 96.79\ \text{dBA} $$ **Step 5: Compliance Check** Since $D_\% = 256.25\% > 100\%$ and TWA $= 96.79\ \text{dBA} > 90\ \text{dBA}$, the exposure **exceeds** the OSHA Permissible Exposure Limit. Engineering or administrative controls are mandatory. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Human Auditory Response & Tolerable Limit Map ```mermaid flowchart LR A0["0 dB
Threshold of Hearing"] --> A1["20 dB
Whisper
Rustling Leaves"] A1 --> A2["40 dB
Quiet Library
Soft Conversation"] A2 --> A3["60 dB
Normal Office
AC Unit"] A3 --> A4["80 dB
Busy Traffic
Alarm Clock 1m"] A4 --> A5["90 dB
OSHA PEL
Lawn Mower"] A5 --> A6["100 dB
Jackhammer
Power Tools"] A6 --> A7["110 dB
Chainsaw
Rock Concert"] A7 --> A8["120 dB
Threshold of Discomfort
Jet Plane 100m"] A8 --> A9["130 dB
Threshold of Pain
Pneumatic Riveter"] A9 --> A10["140 dB
Immediate Damage
Gunshot at 1m"] style A0 fill:#90EE90 style A5 fill:#FFD700 style A8 fill:#FF8C00 style A9 fill:#FF4500 style A10 fill:#8B0000 ``` ### 4.2 Hearing Conservation & Exposure Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start(["Worker Enters
Noise Zone"]) --> Measure["Measure SPL
in dBA"] Measure --> Q1{"SPL > 85 dBA?"} Q1 -- No --> Safe["No Action
Required"] Q1 -- Yes --> Audiogram["Baseline
Audiogram"] Audiogram --> HCP["Enroll in Hearing
Conservation Program"] HCP --> PPE["Provide HPDs
Hearing Protection Devices"] PPE --> Monitor["Annual Audiometry
+ Noise Monitoring"] Monitor --> Q2{"Dose > 100%?"} Q2 -- No --> Continue["Continue
Operations"] Q2 -- Yes --> Controls["Apply Engineering
and Administrative
Controls"] Controls --> Reduce["Reduce SPL
or Exposure Time"] Reduce --> Monitor style Start fill:#E0F7FA style Q1 fill:#FFF59D style Q2 fill:#FFF59D style Controls fill:#FFCDD2 ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Sound Measurement Instrument Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR Mic["Microphone
Transducer"] --> Preamp["Preamplifier"] Preamp --> Filter["Frequency
Weighting Filter
A, B, C, Z"] Filter --> RMS["RMS Detector
and Integrator"] RMS --> ADC["Analog to Digital
Converter"] ADC --> DSP["Digital Signal
Processor"] DSP --> Display["Digital Display
dB or dBA"] DSP --> Log["Logarithmic
Converter"] Log --> LeqCalc["Leq and Dose
Calculator"] LeqCalc --> Out["Output: SPL
Leq, TWA, Dose"] Mic --> Cal["Calibration Source
94 dB / 1 kHz"] style Mic fill:#B3E5FC style Filter fill:#FFE082 style DSP fill:#C5E1A5 style LeqCalc fill:#F8BBD0 ``` ### 4.4 Permissible Exposure Time vs SPL (OSHA Curve) ```mermaid xychart-beta title "OSHA Permissible Exposure Time vs SPL" x-axis "SPL (dBA)" [80, 85, 90, 95, 100, 105, 110, 115, 120] y-axis "Permissible Time (hours)" 0 --> 9 line [8, 8, 8, 4, 2, 1, 0.5, 0.25, 0.125] ``` > [!NOTE] > Notice the **flat plateau** at 8 hours for SPLs up to 90 dBA. Beyond 90 dBA, the curve drops sharply (halving for every +5 dB). ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** *[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024]* Define **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)** and **Sound Intensity Level (SIL)**. Why are they expressed in decibels? **Model Answer:** **SPL** is the logarithmic measure of the rms sound pressure $p$ of a sound wave relative to a reference pressure $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$: $$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right)\ \text{dB}$$ **SIL** is the logarithmic measure of sound intensity $I$ (power per unit area) relative to $I_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{W/m}^2$: $$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)\ \text{dB}$$ The decibel is used because: 1. The human ear responds logarithmically (WeberŌĆōFechner law). 2. The audible range spans 12+ orders of magnitude in intensity. 3. Multiplication of gains/levels becomes simple addition. 4. A compact, intuitive scale is required for engineering communication. --- **Q2.** *[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2023]* Explain the significance of **A-weighting** in industrial noise measurement. Mention the other weighting networks. **Model Answer:** A-weighting is a frequency filter that de-emphasizes very low and very high frequencies, mimicking the human ear's reduced sensitivity at these extremes. The A-weighted SPL (in dBA) correlates best with subjective loudness and with noise-induced hearing loss (NIHL). It is the **mandated metric** under OSHA, NIOSH, ISO, and most global occupational health regulations. Other weighting networks: - **B-weighting (dBB):** intermediate loudness (obsolete in modern practice). - **C-weighting (dBC):** nearly flat, used for peak and impact noise (e.g., impulsive sounds from punch presses). - **Z-weighting (dBZ):** zero weighting ŌĆö linear response across 10 Hz to 20 kHz, used in environmental acoustic research. --- ### PART B ŌĆö Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 Style]* **(a)** Derive the OSHA formula for the **permissible daily exposure time** as a function of the A-weighted sound pressure level. State clearly the **5 dB exchange rate** rule. (7 Marks) **(b)** A worker is exposed to **92 dBA for 6 hours** and **88 dBA for 2 hours** in a single shift. Calculate the **daily noise dose** and the **8-hour TWA**. Comment on whether the exposure complies with OSHA regulations. (7 Marks) --- **Solution (a):** (See Section 3.1 for full derivation.) $$ \boxed{T(L) = \frac{8}{2^{(L-90)/5}}\ \text{hours}} $$ - The **5 dB exchange rate** means that for every 5 dB increase in SPL, the permissible exposure time is halved. - The reference is **90 dBA for 8 hours** per shift. - Exposure above 115 dBA is not permitted regardless of duration (ceiling limit). - For levels below 80 dBA, OSHA does not require any control. --- **Solution (b):** **Step 1 ŌĆö Permissible times for the two levels:** $$ T_{92} = \frac{8}{2^{(92-90)/5}} = \frac{8}{2^{0.4}} = \frac{8}{1.3195} = 6.063\ \text{h} $$ $$ T_{88} = \frac{8}{2^{(88-90)/5}} = \frac{8}{2^{-0.4}} = 8 \times 1.3195 = 10.556\ \text{h} $$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Partial doses:** $$ D_{92} = \frac{6}{6.063} = 0.9895 $$ $$ D_{88} = \frac{2}{10.556} = 0.1895 $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Total Dose:** $$ D = 0.9895 + 0.1895 = 1.1790 \quad\Rightarrow\quad D_\% = 117.9\% $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö TWA:** $$ \text{TWA} = 90 + 16.61 \log_{10}(1.179) = 90 + 16.61 \times 0.0714 = 91.19\ \text{dBA} $$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Compliance:** $D_\% = 117.9\% > 100\%$ and TWA $= 91.19\ \text{dBA} > 90\ \text{dBA}$. The exposure **exceeds** the OSHA Permissible Exposure Limit. **Administrative or engineering controls are required.** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024 Style]* **(a)** Define **Equivalent Continuous Sound Level ($L_{eq}$)**. Why is it necessary for evaluating variable industrial noise? Compute the $L_{eq}$ for a workshop operating under the following schedule: | Time (h) | SPL (dBA) | |---|---| | 0 ŌĆō 2 | 85 | | 2 ŌĆō 5 | 95 | | 5 ŌĆō 8 | 80 | (7 Marks) **(b)** Discuss the **OSHA vs NIOSH** permissible exposure limits in detail. Include the exchange rate, criterion level, and the formula for permissible time. (7 Marks) --- **Solution (a):** $L_{eq}$ is the steady, continuous SPL that, over the same time period, would deliver the same total acoustic energy as the actual time-varying noise. It is the **energy-equivalent** constant level and is essential because most industrial processes (e.g., stamping presses, batch reactors) generate variable noise; comparing them to a fixed regulatory limit requires a single representative value. $$ L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\frac{1}{T}\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i \cdot 10^{L_i/10}\right] $$ For the given schedule, $T = 8$ h, with three intervals: $$ \begin{aligned} L_{eq} &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\frac{1}{8}\left(2 \cdot 10^{8.5} + 3 \cdot 10^{9.5} + 3 \cdot 10^{8.0}\right)\right] \\ &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\frac{1}{8}\left(2 \times 3.162\times 10^{8} + 3 \times 3.162\times 10^{9} + 3 \times 1.0\times 10^{8}\right)\right] \\ &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\frac{1}{8}\left(6.325\times 10^{8} + 9.487\times 10^{9} + 3.0\times 10^{8}\right)\right] \\ &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\frac{1}{8} \times 1.042\times 10^{10}\right] \\ &= 10 \log_{10}(1.302\times 10^{9}) \\ &= 10 \times (9.1146) = 91.15\ \text{dBA} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{L_{eq} = 91.15\ \text{dBA}} $$ This exceeds the OSHA 90 dBA criterion; the workshop is **non-compliant**. --- **Solution (b):** | Parameter | OSHA | NIOSH | |---|---|---| | **Criterion Level** (TWA) | 90 dBA | 85 dBA | | **Exchange Rate** | 5 dB | 3 dB | | **Permissible Time Formula** | $T = \dfrac{8}{2^{(L-90)/5}}$ | $T = \dfrac{8}{2^{(L-85)/3}}$ | | **Time at Criterion** | 8 h | 8 h | | **Ceiling / Hearing Conservation Trigger** | 85 dBA (HCP) / 90 dBA (PEL) | 85 dBA (REL) | | **Dose Limit** | 100 % | 100 % | **Key Differences:** - **OSHA** is a **legal regulation** in the United States. Its 5 dB exchange rate and 90 dBA criterion are more permissive (industry-friendly) because they were derived from 1960sŌĆō70s industrial epidemiology studies. - **NIOSH** is a **research-based recommendation**. Its 3 dB exchange rate follows the principle of equal energy (doubling of acoustic energy for every 3 dB rise) and is more protective of worker health. - The **3 dB rule** is universally accepted in physics and is the standard in the **EU Directive 2003/10/EC** and **ISO 1999**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö Where students lose marks:** > 1. **Using 10 log instead of 20 log** for pressure ratios (and vice versa). Pressure ŌåÆ 20; intensity/power ŌåÆ 10. > 2. **Confusing dB with dBA.** A-weighting (dBA) is *not* the same as unweighted SPL (dB). Always check the question stem. > 3. **Forgetting to convert dose to TWA** using the 16.61 factor (often derived from 5/0.30103). > 4. **Wrong reference value:** $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ (not 1 Pa). > 5. **Ignoring ceiling limits.** OSHA prohibits exposure above **115 dBA** regardless of duration. > 6. **Mixing OSHA's 5 dB exchange rate** with NIOSH's 3 dB ŌĆö read the question carefully. > 7. **Unit errors:** TWA in dBA, dose in %, time in hours, exposure in dBA. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Sound Pressure Level:** $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_0)$, with **$p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$**. - **Sound Intensity / Power Level:** Use **10 log** with $I_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{W/m}^2$ or $W_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{W}$. - **A-weighting (dBA)** is the gold standard for occupational noise; replicates the human ear's frequency response. - **OSHA Permissible Exposure Time:** $T = 8 / 2^{(L-90)/5}$ ŌĆö 90 dBA / 8 h criterion, 5 dB exchange. - **NIOSH Recommended Exposure Time:** $T = 8 / 2^{(L-85)/3}$ ŌĆö 85 dBA / 8 h criterion, 3 dB exchange. - **Daily Dose $D$:** sum of $C_i/T_i$ for all intervals; **$D = 100\%$** is the action limit. - **TWA from Dose:** $\text{TWA} = 90 + 16.61 \log_{10}(D/100)$ dBA. - **Equivalent SPL:** $L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\!\big[\frac{1}{T}\sum t_i \cdot 10^{L_i/10}\big]$. - **Threshold of hearing** = 0 dB; **threshold of pain** Ōēł 120ŌĆō140 dB. - **Ceiling limits:** OSHA 115 dBA; NIOSH 140 dB instantaneous. - **Industrial noise sources:** stamping presses, compressors, turbines, conveyors, pneumatic tools, blast furnaces. - **Controls hierarchy:** Engineering (silencers, isolation) ŌåÆ Administrative (job rotation) ŌåÆ PPE (earplugs, earmuffs). - **NIHL** (Noise-Induced Hearing Loss) is **irreversible** ŌĆö prevention is the only cure. - **Exchange rate** = dB increase that halves permissible time: 5 dB (OSHA) or 3 dB (NIOSH/ISO). - **Reference duration 8 hours** is the standard for TWA; SEL is normalized to 1 second. - **Combine sources** by converting dB ŌåÆ linear ŌåÆ sum ŌåÆ convert back. Use the +3 dB rule when within 3 dB.

topics.title: Measurement of Sound Level
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT413 - PROCESS INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Measurement of Sound Level # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Definition of Sound **Sound** is a mechanical longitudinal pressure wave that propagates through an elastic medium (gas, liquid, or solid) by causing localized variations in pressure, density, and particle velocity about their equilibrium values. For measurement purposes, sound is treated as an *audible acoustic pressure fluctuation* within the nominal human hearing range of approximately **20 Hz to 20 kHz**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction (KTU Syllabus Highlight):** > *Sound Pressure* (p) ŌĆö a scalar field quantity in Pascals (Pa). > *Sound Intensity* (I) ŌĆö a vector field quantity in W/m┬▓ representing the time-averaged rate of sound energy flow per unit area perpendicular to the direction of propagation. > *Sound Power* (W) ŌĆö the total acoustic energy radiated per unit time by a source, in Watts (W). The human ear responds to an enormous dynamic range of pressures ŌĆö from the threshold of hearing (Ōēł 20 ┬ĄPa) to the threshold of pain (Ōēł 200 Pa) ŌĆö a ratio of **10 million to 1**. Because such a wide linear range is impractical to display, sound levels are expressed on a **logarithmic decibel (dB) scale**. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö "The Cook's Whisper vs. The Jet Engine" Imagine measuring "loudness" in a kitchen. A whispered recipe is so faint that you can barely detect any pressure on your eardrum (Ōēł 0.00002 Pa). A jet engine 100 m away produces a pressure wave roughly 10,000,000 times stronger. Plotting these linearly on a graph would mean the whisper is invisibly close to zero. **The decibel solves this problem** ŌĆö it compresses the enormous range into manageable numbers (0 dB to 140 dB) by taking the *logarithm* of the pressure ratio. Each **+20 dB** step means **10├Ś more pressure**, and each **+10 dB** step is perceived by the ear as roughly *twice as loud*. > [!NOTE] > **Core Metric Box:** > - **Reference Sound Pressure (p_ref)** = **20 ┬ĄPa = 2 ├Ś 10Ōü╗ŌüĄ Pa** (standard threshold of hearing at 1 kHz) > - **Reference Sound Intensity (I_ref)** = **10Ōü╗┬╣┬▓ W/m┬▓** > - **Reference Sound Power (W_ref)** = **10Ōü╗┬╣┬▓ W** ## 1.3 Why Measure Sound Level? Sound measurement is critical in: - **Occupational health & safety** (OSHA, IS 7194, Indian Factories Act ŌĆö exposure to > 85 dBA for 8 h causes hearing loss). - **Environmental noise pollution** control (residential, industrial zones). - **Machine diagnostics** ŌĆö vibration/noise signatures reveal bearing faults, cavitation, leaks. - **Product quality testing** ŌĆö automotive cabin noise, HVAC sound ratings, home appliances. - **Process control** ŌĆö feedback to active noise cancellation systems, muffler design. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Sound Pressure Level (SPL) Sound Pressure Level is the most fundamental and widely measured quantity in acoustics. $$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right) \quad \text{[dB]}$$ where: - $L_p$ = sound pressure level, in decibels (dB re 20 ┬ĄPa) - $p_{\text{rms}}$ = root-mean-square sound pressure, in Pa - $p_{\text{ref}}$ = reference pressure = **20 ┬ĄPa** The factor **20** (not 10) comes from the fact that acoustic power is proportional to $p^2$, and $10 \log(p^2) = 20 \log(p)$. ## 2.2 Sound Intensity Level (SIL) $$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_{\text{ref}}}\right) \quad \text{[dB]}$$ where $I_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12}\ \text{W/m}^2$. ## 2.3 Sound Power Level (PWL) $$L_W = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{W_{\text{ref}}}\right) \quad \text{[dB]}$$ where $W_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12}\ \text{W}$. ## 2.4 Relationship Between SPL and SIL (Plane Wave) For a plane (free-field) progressive wave, the instantaneous intensity is given by: $$I = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{\,2}}{\rho c}$$ where $\rho$ = density of medium (Ōēł 1.2 kg/m┬│ for air) and $c$ = speed of sound (Ōēł 343 m/s at 20 ┬░C). The product $\rho c$ is the **characteristic acoustic impedance** of air (Ōēł **413 Pa┬Ęs/m**, also called Rayl). Since both $I$ and $p^2$ use the same reference values ($I_{\text{ref}} = p_{\text{ref}}^2 / (\rho c)_0$ with $(\rho c)_0 = 400$ Rayl), it follows that **$L_I \approx L_p$ in free field**, with a small correction $\Delta = 10 \log_{10}(\rho c / 400)$ when conditions differ. ## 2.5 Addition of Uncorrelated Sound Sources Sound powers (and mean-square pressures) add linearly ŌĆö not the decibel values. $$p_{\text{total}}^{\,2} = p_1^{\,2} + p_2^{\,2} + p_3^{\,2} + \cdots$$ Therefore: $$L_{p,\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} 10^{L_{p,i}/10}\right) \quad \text{[dB]}$$ ### Special Case ŌĆö N Identical, Uncorrelated Sources $$L_{p,\text{total}} = L_{p,\text{single}} + 10 \log_{10}(N) \quad \text{[dB]}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Rule of Thumb (Frequently Tested):** > - Doubling identical sources ŌćÆ **+3 dB** > - Ten identical sources ŌćÆ **+10 dB** > - Two sources differing by **Ōēź 10 dB** ŌćÆ add **0.3 dB** correction to the louder one (the quieter is negligible). ## 2.6 Sound Level Meter ŌĆö Instrument Architecture A modern sound level meter (SLM) consists of: 1. **Microphone** ŌĆö converts acoustic pressure ŌåÆ electrical signal. 2. **Preamplifier** ŌĆö impedance matching, low-noise gain. 3. **Weighting Network** ŌĆö A, B, C, or Z frequency filter. 4. **RMS Detector & Integrator (Exponential / Linear averaging)**. 5. **ADC + Digital Signal Processor** (in Type 1 / Class 1 meters). 6. **Display / Data Logger** in dB. IEC 61672 classifies meters: - **Class 1 (Type 1)**: Precision (┬▒ 0.7 dB), laboratory & field use. - **Class 2 (Type 2)**: General purpose (┬▒ 1.5 dB). ## 2.7 Frequency Weighting Networks The human ear is **not equally sensitive** to all frequencies ŌĆö it is most sensitive around 1ŌĆō4 kHz. Weighting networks mimic this response: | Network | Application | Frequency Response | |---------|-------------|-------------------| | **A-weighting** (dBA) | Environmental, occupational, general noise ŌĆö international standard | Models ear at low SPL (~40 dB) | | **B-weighting** | Intermediate SPL (rarely used now) | Obsolete in most standards | | **C-weighting** (dBC) | Peak / high-level measurements, entertainment noise | Nearly flat, with HF/LF roll-off | | **Z-weighting** (Zero, dBZ) | Modern Class 1 meters ŌĆö flat response 10 Hz ŌĆō 20 kHz | Used for octave-band analysis | > [!IMPORTANT] > **dBA is the legal/regulatory standard** for occupational noise (OSHA, EPA, IS 7194). Most "noise readings" reported in newspapers (e.g., 90 dB) are dBA values. ## 2.8 Microphone Types for Sound Measurement | Type | Principle | Sensitivity | Use | |------|-----------|-------------|-----| | **Condenser (capacitor)** | Diaphragm forms one plate of a capacitor; capacitance varies with pressure | High, flat 20 HzŌĆō20 kHz | Reference / Type 1 SLMs | | **Electret** | Pre-charged dielectric | High, stable | Field SLMs | | **Piezoelectric** | Crystal generates charge under stress | High, robust | Industrial, high SPL | | **Dynamic (moving coil)** | Coil moves in magnetic field | Lower | Rough / general sound | ## 2.9 Equivalent Continuous Sound Level (L_eq) Used to express a time-varying noise as a single equivalent steady level containing the same total energy: $$L_{\text{eq},T} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{T}\int_0^{T} 10^{L(t)/10}\, dt\right) \quad \text{[dB(A)]}$$ ## 2.10 Statistical Percentile Levels (L_N) - **LŌéüŌéĆ** ŌĆö exceeded 10 % of the time (peak / traffic noise). - **LŌéģŌéĆ** ŌĆö median level. - **LŌéēŌéĆ** ŌĆö background level (exceeded 90 % of the time). ## 2.11 Octave & 1/3-Octave Band Analysis To understand the *frequency content* of noise, filters are used: - **Octave band**: $f_{\text{upper}}/f_{\text{lower}} = 2$ - **1/3-Octave band**: $f_{\text{upper}}/f_{\text{lower}} = 2^{1/3}$ Standard **octave center frequencies (Hz)**: $$31.5,\ 63,\ 125,\ 250,\ 500,\ 1000,\ 2000,\ 4000,\ 8000,\ 16000$$ ## 2.12 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|----------|---------|-------|-------| | 1 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20\log_{10}(p_{\text{rms}}/p_{\text{ref}})$ | dB | $p_{\text{ref}} = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$ | | 2 | Sound Intensity Level | $L_I = 10\log_{10}(I/I_{\text{ref}})$ | dB | $I_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12}\,\text{W/m}^2$ | | 3 | Sound Power Level | $L_W = 10\log_{10}(W/W_{\text{ref}})$ | dB | $W_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12}\,\text{W}$ | | 4 | Acoustic Intensity (plane wave) | $I = p_{\text{rms}}^{\,2}/(\rho c)$ | W/m┬▓ | $\rho c \approx 413$ Rayl in air | | 5 | Combine N sources | $L_{p,\text{total}} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\sum 10^{L_{p,i}/10}\right)$ | dB | Uncorrelated sources | | 6 | N identical sources | $L_{p,\text{total}} = L_{p,\text{single}} + 10\log_{10}(N)$ | dB | +3 dB for doubling | | 7 | Equivalent level | $L_{\text{eq}} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\tfrac{1}{T}\int 10^{L/10}dt\right)$ | dB(A) | Energy-average | | 8 | IntensityŌĆōpressure relationship | $L_I = L_p + 10\log_{10}(\rho c) - 10\log_{10}(400)$ | dB | For exact conversion | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Why 20 logŌéüŌéĆ and not 10 logŌéüŌéĆ in SPL? The acoustic intensity in a plane progressive wave is proportional to the **square** of the rms pressure: $$I = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{\,2}}{\rho c}$$ Take the ratio with respect to a reference intensity: $$\frac{I}{I_{\text{ref}}} = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{\,2}/(\rho c)}{p_{\text{ref}}^{\,2}/(\rho c)} = \left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right)^{2}$$ Take the base-10 logarithm and multiply by 10 (the definition of the decibel for power-like quantities): $$10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_{\text{ref}}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right)^{2} = 2 \times 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right)$$ $$\boxed{\,L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right)\,}$$ **Conclusion:** The factor 20 arises because we are converting a *pressure-squared* (energy) ratio into dB. ## 3.2 Derivation: Combining N Identical Sound Sources Let $N$ identical, mutually uncorrelated sources each produce an rms sound pressure $p_1$. The mean-square pressures add (incoherent sources): $$p_{\text{total}}^{\,2} = N\,p_1^{\,2}$$ Apply the SPL formula: $$L_{p,\text{total}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{N}\,p_1}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_1}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right) + 20 \log_{10}(\sqrt{N})$$ Using $20 \log_{10}(\sqrt{N}) = 20 \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\log_{10}(N) = 10\log_{10}(N)$: $$\boxed{\,L_{p,\text{total}} = L_{p,1} + 10 \log_{10}(N)\,}$$ ### Numerical Demonstration - 1 source at 80 dB. - Add a second identical, uncorrelated source: - $L_{p,\text{total}} = 80 + 10\log_{10}(2) = 80 + 3.01 = 83.01$ dB. - Add 9 more (so 10 total): - $L_{p,\text{total}} = 80 + 10\log_{10}(10) = 80 + 10 = 90$ dB. This is *counter-intuitive* but central to KTU numerical problems: **doubling the number of sources adds only 3 dB**, not "doubles the loudness." ## 3.3 Worked Example ŌĆö 3 dB Doubling Rule (Frequently Asked in KTU) **Problem:** A single machine produces 86 dB SPL at the operator's station. Two identical machines operate together. What is the combined SPL? What is the SPL if 4 such machines operate simultaneously? (Assume uncorrelated noise.) **Solution:** For **two** machines ($N=2$): $$L_{p,\text{total}} = 86 + 10\log_{10}(2) = 86 + 3.01 = 89.01\ \text{dB}$$ For **four** machines ($N=4$): $$L_{p,\text{total}} = 86 + 10\log_{10}(4) = 86 + 6.02 = 92.02\ \text{dB}$$ **Key takeaway:** Quadrupling the number of sources adds only **+6 dB** ŌĆö not +12 dB. ## 3.4 Worked Example ŌĆö Conversion: Sound Pressure Ōåö SPL **Problem:** A noisy compressor produces an rms pressure of 0.5 Pa at 1 m. Calculate the SPL. **Solution:** $$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.5}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(25{,}000) = 20 \times 4.398 = 87.96\ \text{dB}$$ $$\boxed{\,L_p \approx 88\ \text{dB re 20 ┬ĄPa}\,}$$ **Reverse check:** If $L_p = 88$ dB, the corresponding pressure is: $$p = p_{\text{ref}} \times 10^{L_p/20} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{88/20} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 25{,}119 \approx 0.502\ \text{Pa} \ \checkmark$$ ## 3.5 Worked Example ŌĆö Addition of Two Unequal Sources **Problem:** Two machines individually produce 80 dB and 77 dB. Find the combined SPL. **Solution:** Method 1 ŌĆö Direct (energy summation): $$L_{p,\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{80/10} + 10^{77/10}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{8} + 10^{7.7}\right)$$ $$= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(1.0 \times 10^{8} + 5.012 \times 10^{7}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(1.501 \times 10^{8}\right) = 81.76\ \text{dB}$$ Method 2 ŌĆö Approximation (difference = 3 dB; look-up table correction = **+1.76 dB**): $$L_{p,\text{total}} \approx L_{\text{louder}} + 1.76 = 80 + 1.76 = 81.76\ \text{dB} \ \checkmark$$ ## 3.6 Worked Example ŌĆö L_eq for a Time-Varying Noise **Problem:** A workplace noise level is 90 dB for 4 hours and 75 dB for 4 hours. Calculate $L_{\text{eq}, 8\text{h}}$. **Solution:** $$L_{\text{eq}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{8}\left[4 \times 10^{90/10} + 4 \times 10^{75/10}\right]\right)$$ $$= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{8}\left[4 \times 10^{9} + 4 \times 31.62 \times 10^{6}\right]\right)$$ $$= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4 \times 10^{9} + 1.265 \times 10^{8}}{8}\right)$$ $$= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4.1265 \times 10^{9}}{8}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(5.158 \times 10^{8}\right) = 87.13\ \text{dB(A)}$$ > [!NOTE] > Note how the 4 hours at 75 dB contributes *negligibly* (Ōēł 0.3 dB) to the equivalent level ŌĆö dominated by the louder period. ## 3.7 Worked Example ŌĆö Conversion: SPL to Intensity **Problem:** A sound has SPL = 100 dB in air ($\rho c = 413$ Rayl). Calculate the sound intensity. **Solution:** The corresponding pressure is: $$p_{\text{rms}} = p_{\text{ref}} \times 10^{L_p/20} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{5} = 2\ \text{Pa}$$ The intensity: $$I = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{\,2}}{\rho c} = \frac{(2)^2}{413} = \frac{4}{413} = 9.685 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{W/m}^2$$ The intensity level: $$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{9.685 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(9.685 \times 10^{9}) = 99.86\ \text{dB}$$ The small difference ($\approx 0.14$ dB) from $L_p = 100$ dB is due to the use of $(\rho c) = 413$ Rayl rather than the standard 400 Rayl. # 4.
