[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023] **Define Sound Pressure Level. Why is it expressed in decibels using a logarithmic scale?** **Model Answer:** Sound Pressure Level (SPL) is defined as twenty times the logarithm to the base 10 of the ratio of the rms sound pressure to a reference pressure of **20 ┬ĄPa**: $$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right) \quad \text{dB}$$ The decibel scale is used because the human ear responds to a very wide range of sound pressures (from **20 ┬ĄPa to 200 Pa**, a ratio of 10ŌüĘ:1). A linear scale would be impractical for display and calculation. The logarithmic dB scale compresses this huge range into manageable numbers (0ŌĆō140 dB), and it also matches the approximately logarithmic loudness perception of the human ear. Each 10 dB increase corresponds to a 10├Ś increase in sound pressure (or 100├Ś in intensity) but is perceived as roughly *twice as loud*. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024] **List the standard reference values for Sound Pressure, Sound Intensity, and Sound Power. Differentiate between A, B, and C weighting networks.** **Model Answer:** | Quantity | Reference Value | |----------|-----------------| | Sound Pressure ($p_{\text{ref}}$) | **20 ┬ĄPa = 2 ├Ś 10Ōü╗ŌüĄ Pa** | | Sound Intensity ($I_{\text{ref}}$) | **10Ōü╗┬╣┬▓ W/m┬▓** | | Sound Power ($W_{\text{ref}}$) | **10Ōü╗┬╣┬▓ W** | **Differences between weighting networks:** | Network | Response | Use | |---------|----------|-----| | **A-weighting (dBA)** | Models 40-phon Fletcher-Munson curve (low-level ear response); rolls off at 50 Hz and 10 kHz | Universal standard for environmental & occupational noise | | **B-weighting (dBB)** | Models 70-phon ear response | Largely obsolete | | **C-weighting (dBC)** | Nearly flat, with 31.5 Hz and 16 kHz roll-off | High-level / peak / entertainment noise | | **Z-weighting (dBZ)** | Flat, 10 Hz ŌĆō 20 kHz | Modern octave-band analysis | --- ## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (Choice 1) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024] **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **Sound Level Meter**. List any four specifications of a Type 1 / Class 1 SLM. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Two identical machines operating independently produce 84 dB SPL each. (i) What is the SPL when both operate together? (ii) What is the SPL when four such identical machines operate together? (iii) Comment on the result. **[7 Marks]** --- #### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) A Sound Level Meter (SLM) measures sound pressure level in accordance with IEC 61672. The block diagram is as follows: **Block Diagram:** ``` [Microphone] ŌåÆ [Preamplifier] ŌåÆ [Weighting Network (A/B/C/Z)] ŌåÆ [RMS Detector] ŌåÆ [Logarithmic Amplifier] ŌåÆ [ADC] ŌåÆ [Display] ŌåÆ [Data Logger] ``` **Construction and Working:** 1. **Microphone (Condenser / Electret type):** Converts acoustic pressure variations into a proportional electrical voltage. Must be omnidirectional, with flat frequency response (20 Hz ŌĆō 20 kHz for Class 1). 2. **Preamplifier:** Provides high input impedance and low noise gain close to the microphone, preventing cable losses and noise pickup. 3. **Weighting Network:** Electronic filters that shape the frequency response to mimic human hearing. A-weighting is the universal default. 4. **RMS Detector & Integrator:** Computes the true rms value of the filtered signal over a defined averaging time. Modes: Fast (125 ms), Slow (1 s), Impulse (35 ms rise, 1.5 s decay). 5. **Logarithmic Amplifier:** Implements the $20 \log_{10}$ relationship, converting the linear voltage to dB. 6. **ADC + DSP:** Digitizes the signal; computes $L_{\text{max}}, L_{\text{min}}, L_{\text{eq}}, L_{10}, L_{50}, L_{90}$ in real time. 7. **Display & Logger:** Shows SPL in dB (or dBA, dBC) and stores data for later analysis. **Specifications of a Type 1 / Class 1 SLM (any four):** - Frequency range: 20 Hz ŌĆō 20 kHz (┬▒ 0.5 dB tolerance). - Dynamic range: typically 25 ŌĆō 140 dB. - Accuracy: ┬▒ 0.7 dB at reference conditions. - Time weighting: Fast, Slow, Impulse. - Frequency weighting: A, C, Z (linear). - Microphone: ┬Į-inch free-field condenser, polarization voltage 200 V. - Compliance: IEC 61672-1, IEC 60942 (calibrator), ANSI S1.4. --- #### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) Given: $L_{p,\text{single}} = 84$ dB. **(i) Two machines together** ($N = 2$): $$L_{p,\text{total}} = L_{p,\text{single}} + 10\log_{10}(2)$$ $$= 84 + 3.01 = 87.01\ \text{dB}$$ **(ii) Four machines together** ($N = 4$): $$L_{p,\text{total}} = 84 + 10\log_{10}(4) = 84 + 6.02 = 90.02\ \text{dB}$$ **(iii) Comment:** - Doubling the number of identical, uncorrelated sources increases the SPL by only **3 dB**, not 6 dB. - Quadrupling the sources increases SPL by only **6 dB**. - Although pressure adds in quadrature ($\propto \sqrt{N}$), the *logarithmic* dB scale makes the increase appear small. - Loudness perception roughly doubles for every 10 dB increase; therefore, doubling the number of sources is **not** perceived as "twice as loud." --- ### Question B (Choice 2) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024] **(a)** Define Sound Pressure Level, Sound Intensity Level, and Sound Power Level. Derive the relationship between SPL and SIL for a plane progressive wave in air. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A factory floor has a noise level of 92 dB(A) for 6 hours, 86 dB(A) for 2 hours, and the rest of the 8-hour shift is at 70 dB(A). Calculate the equivalent continuous level $L_{\text{eq}, 8\text{h}}$ and comment on occupational exposure according to OSHA / IS 7194. **[7 Marks]** --- #### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) **Definitions:** - **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)**: $$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right)\ \text{dB},\quad p_{\text{ref}} = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$$ - **Sound Intensity Level (SIL)**: $$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_{\text{ref}}}\right)\ \text{dB},\quad I_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12}\ \text{W/m}^2$$ - **Sound Power Level (PWL or $L_W$)**: $$L_W = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{W_{\text{ref}}}\right)\ \text{dB},\quad W_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12}\ \text{W}$$ **Derivation ŌĆö Relationship between SPL and SIL:** For a plane progressive wave in a lossless medium, the instantaneous intensity $I$ is: $$I = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{\,2}}{\rho c}$$ Divide by the reference intensity $I_{\text{ref}}$: $$\frac{I}{I_{\text{ref}}} = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{\,2}/(\rho c)}{p_{\text{ref}}^{\,2}/(\rho c)_0}$$ Using the standard reference $(\rho c)_0 = 400$ Rayl (Pa┬Ęs/m) so that $I_{\text{ref}} = p_{\text{ref}}^2/(\rho c)_0$: $$\frac{I}{I_{\text{ref}}} = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{\,2}}{p_{\text{ref}}^{\,2}} \times \frac{(\rho c)_0}{\rho c}$$ Take 10 $\log_{10}$: $$L_I = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right) + 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{400}{\rho c}\right)$$ $$\boxed{\,L_I = L_p + 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{400}{\rho c}\right)\,}$$ For air at standard conditions ($\rho c \approx 413$ Rayl), the correction is $-0.14$ dB, so $L_I \approx L_p$ to a very good approximation. --- #### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) Given: - $L_1 = 92$ dB(A) for $t_1 = 6$ h. - $L_2 = 86$ dB(A) for $t_2 = 2$ h. - $L_3 = 70$ dB(A) for $t_3 = 8 - 6 - 2 = 0$ h (rest is silent, so we omit). **Equivalent continuous level (energy basis):** $$L_{\text{eq},8\text{h}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\frac{1}{8}\left(6 \times 10^{92/10} + 2 \times 10^{86/10}\right)\right]$$ Calculate the antilog terms: $$6 \times 10^{9.2} = 6 \times 1.585 \times 10^{9} = 9.51 \times 10^{9}$$ $$2 \times 10^{8.6} = 2 \times 3.981 \times 10^{8} = 7.96 \times 10^{8}$$ Sum: $9.51 \times 10^{9} + 0.796 \times 10^{9} = 10.31 \times 10^{9}$ Average over 8 h: $\dfrac{10.31 \times 10^{9}}{8} = 1.289 \times 10^{9}$ $$L_{\text{eq},8\text{h}} = 10 \log_{10}(1.289 \times 10^{9}) = 10 \times 9.110 = 91.10\ \text{dB(A)}$$ **Comment on occupational exposure (OSHA / IS 7194):** - OSHA Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL) = **90 dB(A) for 8 h**. - IS 7194 / Indian Factories Act recommends **Ōēż 85 dB(A) for 8 h**. - The computed $L_{\text{eq}} = 91.1$ dB(A) **exceeds both** the OSHA and IS 7194 limits. - Required action: hearing-protection devices (ear muffs/plugs), engineering noise control, reduced exposure time, audiometric testing for workers, and signage indicating a noise-hazard zone. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 20 in SPL.** Many students write $L_p = 10\log_{10}(p/p_{\text{ref}})$. This is wrong ŌĆö 20 is essential because intensity is proportional to $p^2$. > 2. **Adding decibel values directly** (e.g., 80 dB + 80 dB = 160 dB). Decibels are logarithmic ŌĆö they do **not** add; the *underlying powers* add. > 3. **Writing "doubling the source doubles the SPL"** ŌĆö the correct answer is +3 dB. > 4. **Confusing dBA with dB** ŌĆö dBA is A-weighted (frequency filtered); dB SPL is unweighted. > 5. **Forgetting the reference value (20 ┬ĄPa)** in SPL definitions. Examiners *always* check for this. > 6. **Mixing up Sound Power and Sound Pressure** ŌĆö they are different physical quantities with different reference values. > 7. **Not specifying the weighting network** when reporting measurements. > 8. **Neglecting the integration time** when discussing $L_{\text{eq}}$ or $L_N$ percentile levels. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Sound** = mechanical longitudinal pressure wave (20 Hz ŌĆō 20 kHz audible band). - **Reference pressure** = 20 ┬ĄPa; **reference intensity** = 10Ōü╗┬╣┬▓ W/m┬▓; **reference power** = 10Ōü╗┬╣┬▓ W. - **SPL formula:** $L_p = 20\log_{10}(p_{\text{rms}}/p_{\text{ref}})$ ŌĆö the **20** comes from $I \propto p^2$. - **SIL formula:** $L_I = 10\log_{10}(I/I_{\text{ref}})$. - **PWL formula:** $L_W = 10\log_{10}(W/W_{\text{ref}})$. - **Acoustic impedance of air** $\rho c \approx 413$ Rayl; intensity $I = p_{\text{rms}}^2/(\rho c)$. - **For N identical, uncorrelated sources:** $L_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{single}} + 10\log_{10}(N)$. - Doubling ŌćÆ +3 dB. - Ten identical ŌćÆ +10 dB. - **Energy summation** for unequal sources: $L_{\text{total}} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\sum 10^{L_i/10}\right)$. - **Weighting networks:** A (universal standard), B (obsolete), C (peaks), Z (flat/modern). - **$L_{\text{eq}}$** is the energy-averaged level over time T. - **$L_{10}, L_{50}, L_{90}$** are statistical percentile levels (peak, median, background). - **Octave band** $f_{\text{high}}/f_{\text{low}} = 2$; standard center frequencies from 31.5 Hz to 16 kHz. - **Sound level meter** chain: Microphone ŌåÆ Preamplifier ŌåÆ Weighting ŌåÆ RMS ŌåÆ Log ŌåÆ ADC ŌåÆ Display. - **Microphone types:** Condenser (most accurate), Electret (field use), Piezoelectric, Dynamic. - **Class 1** (precision, ┬▒ 0.7 dB) vs **Class 2** (general, ┬▒ 1.5 dB) per IEC 61672. - **Occupational limit** (IS 7194 / OSHA) = 85 ŌĆō 90 dB(A) for 8 hours; 3 dB exchange rate halves permissible time. - **Logarithmic perception:** +10 dB Ōēł doubling of perceived loudness. - **Always quote the weighting** (dBA, dBC, dBZ) and **time constant** (Fast/Slow/Impulse) when reporting sound levels.

topics.title: Recording Instruments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT413 - PROCESS INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Recording Instruments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ­¤ōś MODULE 2: RECORDING INSTRUMENTS ŌĆö SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recording Instrument:** A recording instrument is a measurement device that provides a permanent, continuous, and visible trace (graphical record) of a measured variable as a function of time, or as a function of another independent variable, on a suitable recording medium such as paper, magnetic tape, or electronic memory.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PESFT413 - Module 2)** terminology, recording instruments are classified as **indicating-recording** or **recording-only** devices whose primary function is to create a **time-stamped historical archive** of process variables for analysis, legal compliance, and operational control.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education, students must demonstrate the ability to:
> - **CO2:** "Identify and describe various recording devices used in process industries."
> - **CO3:** "Analyze the working principles of strip, circular, X-Y, magnetic, and data loggers."

The output of a recording instrument is a **continuous, permanent record** called a **chart** or **tracing**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Memory of a Process"

Imagine you are measuring a patient's body temperature every hour. If you just glance at the thermometer, you know the temperature *now*, but you forget what it was 3 hours ago. However, if you plot each reading on a graph with time on the X-axis and temperature on the Y-axis, you now have a **permanent "memory" of the patient's fever pattern**. This memory lets the doctor analyze whether the patient is improving, worsening, or having fever spikes at night.

**A recording instrument does exactly this for an industrial process:**

| Human Scenario | Industrial Equivalent |
|----------------|----------------------|
| Patient's body temperature | Reactor temperature |
| Doctor writing the readings in a notebook | Pen drawing on chart paper |
| Time axis of the graph | Moving chart paper driven at constant speed |
| Doctor's memory | The chart record (permanent memory) |

> [!TIP]
> **Why recording is essential in process industries:**
> 1. **Trend Analysis** ŌĆö Detect slow drifts or oscillations in the process.
> 2. **Quality Control** ŌĆö Provide documented evidence of product quality.
> 3. **Legal Compliance** ŌĆö Statutory requirement in pharmaceutical, food, and nuclear industries.
> 4. **Operator Decision-Making** ŌĆö Past data helps predict future behavior.
> 5. **Post-Incident Diagnosis** ŌĆö If a plant trips, the chart tells the engineer *what happened* just before the failure.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Units used in Recording Instruments (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - **Chart Speed:** $\text{mm/hr}$ or $\text{cm/min}$ (typical: **60 mm/hr**, **600 mm/hr**)
> - **Chart Width:** $\text{mm}$ (standard: **100 mm**, **250 mm**)
> - **Pen Response Time:** $\text{seconds}$ (typically **1 s** for full-scale deflection)
> - **Dead Band:** $\%\text{ of full scale}$ (standard: **0.1% to 0.5%**)
> - **Recording Accuracy Class:** $\text{Class 0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5}$ (as per **IS 3194 / IEC 61187**)

The fundamental relationship of any time-based recorder is captured by:

$$L = v \cdot t$$

where $L$ is the length of chart paper consumed, $v$ is the chart speed, and $t$ is the elapsed time.

---

## 1.4 Broad Classification of Recording Instruments

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification Basis 1: By Chart Geometry**
> - **Strip Chart Recorders** ŌĆö Paper moves linearly; pen moves perpendicular to it.
> - **Circular Chart Recorders** ŌĆö Paper is a rotating disc; pen moves radially.
> - **X-Y Recorders** ŌĆö Two pens plot two independent variables against each other; no time axis.

**Classification Basis 2: By Operating Principle**
> - **Galvanometric (Direct-Acting)** Recorders
> - **Null-Balance (Servo-Operated)** Recorders
> - **Potentiometric** Recorders
> - **Magnetic / Electronic / Digital Data Loggers**

**Classification Basis 3: By Recording Medium**
> - **Paper-based** (inked, thermal, pressure-sensitive)
> - **Magnetic Tape**
> - **Electronic Memory** (solid-state storage)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ŌÜÖ’ĖÅ SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Strip Chart Recorders ŌĆö Working Principle (Detailed)

The **strip chart recorder** is the most widely used industrial recorder. Its core operational philosophy is based on a **two-coordinate plotting system**:

> **The paper moves along the time axis (X-axis) at a precise, constant speed.**
> **The pen moves along the value axis (Y-axis) in proportion to the measured variable.**

The intersection of these two motions creates a **continuous trace** of the variable versus time.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Operation

1. The **primary sensing element** (e.g., thermocouple, RTD, pressure capsule) converts the process variable into a proportional **electrical signal** (mV, mA, or voltage).
2. This signal is conditioned by the **measuring network** (Wheatstone bridge, amplifier, attenuator) to bring it to a level suitable for the recording mechanism.
3. The **recording mechanism** (galvanometer or servo motor) drives the **pen assembly** across the chart paper.
4. The **chart drive mechanism** (spring-wound clock, synchronous motor, or stepper motor) moves the paper at a preset constant speed.
5. The **inking system** continuously feeds ink to the pen tip, producing a visible trace.
6. The **reference grid** printed on the chart provides calibrated scale markings.

---

## 2.2 Circular Chart Recorders ŌĆö Working Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Circular Chart Recorders** are used when the process is to be monitored over a **fixed period** (typically 24 hours, 7 days, or 30 days).

The paper is a **circular disc** mounted on a turntable that rotates at a constant speed (e.g., 1 revolution per 24 hours). The pen moves **radially inward** as the measured variable changes, drawing a continuous spiral trace on the disc. The trace is read as a polar plot, where the angular position represents time and the radial position represents the value.

**Engineering Use Case:** Power plant boiler drum level monitoring, where a 24-hour circular chart is reviewed every shift.

---

## 2.3 X-Y Recorders ŌĆö Working Principle

> [!NOTE]
> **X-Y Recorders** are used when the relationship between **two independent variables** is to be studied ŌĆö for example, *voltage vs. current* (V-I characteristics), *stress vs. strain*, or *B-H curve* of a magnetic material.

- One variable is plotted along the **X-axis** (driven by one servo).
- The other variable is plotted along the **Y-axis** (driven by another servo).
- **There is no time axis.** The recorder shows the *functional relationship* between the two variables.
- The pen rests on stationary paper, which is moved manually only to start a new recording.

**Working Principle:** Each axis has an independent **null-balance servo system**. A slidewire detects the imbalance, an amplifier drives a DC servo motor, which positions the pen until the system is balanced.

---

## 2.4 Null-Balance (Servo) Recorders ŌĆö The Engineering Heart of Industrial Recording

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Null-Balance Recorder** is the **most accurate** class of industrial recorders, with typical accuracy of **┬▒0.25% of full scale** or better.

**Operating Philosophy:** The instrument continuously maintains a **null (zero) deflection** condition by automatically adjusting a balancing element. The position of the balancing element is proportional to the input signal, and this position is mechanically linked to the pen.

### Detailed Operational Sequence

1. The input signal (e.g., thermocouple EMF) is compared against a **reference voltage from a precision slidewire**.
2. The **difference (error) voltage** is amplified by a high-gain DC amplifier.
3. The amplified error drives a **two-phase AC servo motor**.
4. The servo motor repositions the **wiper on the slidewire** *and* the **pen** simultaneously.
5. As the wiper moves, the reference voltage changes until it **exactly balances** the input signal.
6. At null, the error is zero, and the pen position is now an accurate representation of the input.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is "null-balance" so accurate?**
> Because the system operates at **zero error most of the time**, the linearity of the slidewire, amplifier gain, and motor torque become the only limiting factors. This eliminates the linearity errors that plague direct-acting galvanometric recorders.

---

## 2.5 Galvanometric (Direct-Acting) Recorders

These are the simplest and fastest recorders. A **D'Arsonval galvanometer** converts the input current into angular displacement of a coil, and a pen attached to the coil traces the value on the chart.

| Parameter | Galvanometric | Null-Balance |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|
| Accuracy | ┬▒2% to ┬▒5% FS | ┬▒0.25% to ┬▒0.5% FS |
| Frequency Response | High (up to 100 Hz) | Limited (1ŌĆō10 Hz) |
| Torque Required | High input power | Negligible input power |
| Cost | Low | High |

---

## 2.6 Magnetic Tape Recorders and Data Loggers

- **Magnetic Tape Recorders** use **Frequency Modulation (FM)** or **Direct Recording (DR)** techniques. They offer very high bandwidth and are used for transient analysis (e.g., vibration monitoring, seismic studies).
- **Data Loggers** are modern digital devices. They sample multiple input channels, perform A/D conversion, store data in solid-state memory, and can transfer data via USB/Ethernet to a computer.

> [!NOTE]
> A typical industrial data logger can scan **16 to 64 channels** at programmable intervals (1 second to 1 hour), with **16-bit resolution** and storage up to several GB.

---

## 2.7 Drive Mechanisms for Chart Paper

| Drive Type | Speed Range | Application |
|------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Spring-Wound Clock** | 1 rev/hour to 1 rev/day | Portable recorders, field use |
| **Synchronous AC Motor** | 20 to 600 mm/hr | Lab & industrial panels |
| **Stepper Motor + Gearbox** | 1 mm/hr to 1000 mm/hr | Modern digital recorders |
| **Pneumatic Drive** | Programmable | Hazardous (explosive) areas |

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **frequently tested** in KTU University Examinations (Dec 2023, May 2024 patterns). Master these for full marks.

| # | Concept | Equation | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|----------|-------------------|
| 1 | Chart Length Consumed | $L = v \cdot t$ | $L$ in mm, $v$ in mm/hr, $t$ in hr |
| 2 | Angular Velocity of Circular Chart | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi}{T}$ | $\omega$ in rad/hr, $T$ = time for 1 revolution |
| 3 | Time for Chart Revolve | $T = \dfrac{60}{N}$ | $N$ = rev/hr, $T$ in min |
| 4 | Resolution of Recorder | $R = \dfrac{\Delta s}{S_{FS}} \times 100\,\%$ | $\Delta s$ = smallest scale division, $S_{FS}$ = full scale |
| 5 | Natural Frequency (Galvanometer) | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{K}{J}}$ | $K$ = spring constant (N┬Ęm/rad), $J$ = moment of inertia (kg┬Ęm┬▓) |
| 6 | Time Constant of Galvanometer | $\tau = \dfrac{2\pi J}{P_0}$ | $P_0$ = restoring torque per radian |
| 7 | Damping Ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{C}{2\sqrt{KJ}}$ | $C$ = damping coefficient |
| 8 | Magnification (2nd Order) | $M = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | $r = \omega/\omega_n$ |
| 9 | Bandwidth (Null-Balance) | $BW = 0.707 \cdot f_n$ | At $\zeta = 0.707$ |
| 10 | Slidewire Sensitivity | $S_{sw} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{R_{sw}}$ | $V_{FS}$ = full-scale voltage, $R_{sw}$ = slidewire resistance |
| 11 | Dead Band | $DB = \dfrac{I_{min,\,detectable}}{I_{FS}} \times 100\,\%$ | In % of full-scale input |
| 12 | Pen Travel Speed | $v_{pen} = \dfrac{dy}{dt}$ | mm/s; relates to frequency response |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** The frequency-response formulas are critical in **electromagnetic flowmeter recorders**, **vibration monitoring** in turbomachinery, and **seismic data acquisition** ŌĆö all real industrial applications.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# ­¤ö¼ SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Chart Length Consumed for a Given Time Interval

> **Problem Statement:** A strip chart recorder has a chart speed of **60 mm/hr**. Calculate the length of chart paper consumed in **8 hours** of continuous recording.

### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the fundamental relationship.**
For a strip chart recorder, the length of paper consumed is directly proportional to the chart speed and the time of recording.

$$L = v \cdot t$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute the known values.**
The chart speed is given as $v = 60 \, \text{mm/hr}$ and the recording time is $t = 8 \, \text{hr}$.

$$L = 60 \, \text{mm/hr} \times 8 \, \text{hr}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Cancel the unit 'hour' and compute the result.**

$$L = 480 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Convert to centimeters for practical readability.**

$$L = 480 \, \text{mm} = 48 \, \text{cm}$$

> **Answer:** The recorder consumes **480 mm (or 48 cm)** of chart paper in 8 hours.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Angular Velocity of a Circular Chart

> **Problem Statement:** A circular chart recorder makes **1 revolution every 24 hours**. Find (a) the angular velocity in rad/hr, and (b) the linear speed of a point on the chart paper at a radius of 120 mm.

### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

**Step 1 ŌĆö Recall the relationship between angular velocity and time period.**
For uniform circular motion, one complete revolution equals $2\pi$ radians. Therefore:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the time period in hours.**
The chart makes one revolution in 24 hours.

$$T = 24 \, \text{hr}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute and calculate the angular velocity.**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{24}$$

$$\omega = \frac{\pi}{12} \, \text{rad/hr}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the numerical value.**

$$\omega \approx 0.2618 \, \text{rad/hr}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute the linear (tangential) speed of a point at $r = 120 \, \text{mm}$.**
The linear speed is the product of the angular velocity and the radius.

$$v = \omega \cdot r$$

$$v = 0.2618 \, \text{rad/hr} \times 120 \, \text{mm}$$

$$v = 31.416 \, \text{mm/hr}$$

> **Answer:** The chart rotates at **$\omega \approx 0.2618$ rad/hr**, and a point on the chart at radius 120 mm moves linearly at **31.42 mm/hr**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Natural Frequency and Time Constant of a Galvanometer Recorder

> **Problem Statement:** A D'Arsonval galvanometer used in a direct-acting recorder has a coil moment of inertia $J = 5 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{kg┬Ęm}^2$ and a spring constant $K = 2 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{N┬Ęm/rad}$. Calculate (a) the natural frequency $f_n$ and (b) the time constant $\tau$.

### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

**Step 1 ŌĆö Recall the formula for the natural frequency of a torsional system.**

$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{K}{J}}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute the given values.**

$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 10^{-9}}}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the ratio inside the square root.**

$$\frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{2}{5} \times 10^{3} = 0.4 \times 10^{3} = 400$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Take the square root.**

$$\sqrt{400} = 20$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Divide by $2\pi$.**

$$f_n = \frac{20}{2\pi} = \frac{10}{\pi} \approx 3.183 \, \text{Hz}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Calculate the time constant using $\tau = \frac{2\pi J}{P_0}$.**
Here, the restoring torque per radian $P_0$ is equivalent to the spring constant $K$.

$$\tau = \frac{2\pi J}{K}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Substitute values.**

$$\tau = \frac{2\pi \times 5 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 10^{-6}}$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Simplify.**

$$\tau = \pi \times \frac{5 \times 10^{-9}}{10^{-6}}$$

$$\tau = \pi \times 5 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\tau \approx 15.708 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{s}$$

$$\tau \approx 15.71 \, \text{ms}$$

> **Answer:** The natural frequency is **$f_n \approx 3.18 \, \text{Hz}$** and the time constant is **$\tau \approx 15.71 \, \text{ms}$**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Resolution of a Strip Chart Recorder

> **Problem Statement:** A strip chart recorder has a full-scale pen deflection of 100 mm and the smallest grid division on the chart is 1 mm. Calculate the resolution of the recorder.

### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

**Step 1 ŌĆö Recall the resolution formula.**

$$R = \frac{\Delta s}{S_{FS}} \times 100\,\%$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Identify the given values.**
Smallest scale division: $\Delta s = 1 \, \text{mm}$.
Full-scale deflection: $S_{FS} = 100 \, \text{mm}$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute and compute.**

$$R = \frac{1}{100} \times 100\,\% = 1\,\%$$

> **Answer:** The resolution of the recorder is **1% of full scale**.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Frequency Response ŌĆö Magnification Factor for a Second-Order Galvanometer

> **Problem Statement:** A galvanometer-type recorder has a natural frequency $f_n = 10 \, \text{Hz}$ and damping ratio $\zeta = 0.7$. Find the magnification factor $M$ when a signal of frequency $f = 15 \, \text{Hz}$ is applied.

### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the frequency ratio.**

$$r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{f}{f_n} = \frac{15}{10} = 1.5$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Recall the magnification formula for a second-order system.**

$$M = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $r^2$.**

$$r^2 = (1.5)^2 = 2.25$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute $1 - r^2$.**

$$1 - r^2 = 1 - 2.25 = -1.25$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute $(1 - r^2)^2$.**

$$(1 - r^2)^2 = (-1.25)^2 = 1.5625$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Compute $2\zeta r$.**

$$2\zeta r = 2 \times 0.7 \times 1.5 = 2.1$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Compute $(2\zeta r)^2$.**

$$(2\zeta r)^2 = (2.1)^2 = 4.41$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Compute the denominator.**

$$(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2 = 1.5625 + 4.41 = 5.9725$$

**Step 9 ŌĆö Take the square root and compute $M$.**

$$M = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5.9725}} = \frac{1}{2.4439} \approx 0.409$$

> **Answer:** The magnification factor is **$M \approx 0.409$**, meaning the recorder attenuates a 15 Hz signal to about **40.9%** of its true amplitude.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **high-yield comparison table** frequently asked in KTU Part B questions (Compare-type).

| Feature | Strip Chart | Circular Chart | X-Y Recorder |
|---------|-------------|----------------|--------------|
| Paper shape | Long rectangular strip | Circular disc | Rectangular sheet (stationary) |
| Paper motion | Linear (continuous) | Rotational (1 rev/period) | Stationary |
| Axes | Time (X) vs Value (Y) | Angle (time) vs Radius (value) | Var1 (X) vs Var2 (Y) |
| Time axis | Yes | Yes (angular) | **No** |
| Recording duration | Unlimited (paper roll) | Limited to 1 revolution | Manual restart |
| Typical use | Trend monitoring | Shift/daily logs | Functional relationships |
| Cost | Moderate | Low | High |
| Accuracy | ┬▒0.5% to ┬▒1% FS | ┬▒1% to ┬▒2% FS | ┬▒0.25% to ┬▒0.5% FS |

---

## 3.7 Decision Matrix ŌĆö When to Use Which Recorder

| Process Need | Recommended Recorder |
|--------------|---------------------|
| Long-term trend of a single variable | **Strip Chart Recorder** |
| 24-hour shift summary in a plant | **Circular Chart Recorder** |
| Plotting V-I characteristics of a diode | **X-Y Recorder** |
| High-frequency transient (vibration, shock) | **Magnetic Tape / Digital Data Logger** |
| Hazardous area (explosive atmosphere) | **Pneumatic Strip Chart** |
| Multi-channel monitoring (16+ inputs) | **Digital Data Logger with SCADA** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# ­¤¦® SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Null-Balance Strip Chart Recorder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Process Variable<br>Temperature / Pressure] --> B[Primary Sensor<br>Thermocouple / RTD]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioner<br>Bridge / Amplifier]
    C --> D[Error Detector<br>Comparator]
    D --> E[DC Amplifier<br>High Gain]
    E --> F[Two-Phase<br>Servo Motor]
    F --> G[Mechanical Linkage]
    G --> H[Pen Position]
    G --> I[Slidewire Wiper]
    I --> D
    J[Chart Drive Motor<br>Synchronous] --> K[Chart Paper<br>Linear Motion]
    H --> K
    L[Ink Reservoir] --> H
    style A fill:#FFE4B5
    style K fill:#E0FFE0
    style F fill:#FFB6C1
    style I fill:#B0E0E6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The pen position and slidewire wiper are mechanically linked (ganged) to the servo motor. As the servo moves them, the wiper reduces the error voltage to zero. This is the **closed-loop null-balance principle**.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Classification Tree: Types of Recording Instruments

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RI[Recording Instruments]
    RI --> A1[Paper Based]
    RI --> A2[Magnetic Tape]
    RI --> A3[Digital Electronic]

    A1 --> B1[Strip Chart]
    A1 --> B2[Circular Chart]
    A1 --> B3[X-Y Recorder]

    A2 --> C1[FM Recording]
    A2 --> C2[Direct Recording]

    A3 --> D1[Data Loggers]
    A3 --> D2[Paperless Recorders]
    A3 --> D3[SCADA Archives]

    B1 --> E1[Galvanometric]
    B1 --> E2[Null Balance]
    B1 --> E3[Potentiometric]

    B3 --> F1[Two Servo XY]
    B3 --> F2[Single Servo XY]

    style RI fill:#FFD700
    style A1 fill:#FFA07A
    style A2 fill:#87CEEB
    style A3 fill:#90EE90
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Working of a Galvanometric Strip Chart Recorder

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PV as Process Variable
    participant SEN as Sensor
    participant COIL as D'Arsonval Coil
    participant PEN as Pen Tip
    participant PAPER as Chart Paper
    participant DRV as Drive Motor

    PV->>SEN: Pressure / Temp / Flow acts on sensor
    SEN->>COIL: Converts to proportional current
    COIL->>COIL: Coil rotates in magnetic field
    COIL->>PEN: Mechanical coupling moves pen
    DRV->>PAPER: Constant chart speed
    PEN->>PAPER: Pen draws continuous trace
    PAPER-->>PAPER: Permanent record formed
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: X-Y Recorder Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph XAxis [X-Axis Channel]
        X1[X Input Variable] --> X2[Amplifier X]
        X2 --> X3[Error Detector X]
        X3 --> X4[Servo Motor X]
        X4 --> X5[Slidewire X]
        X5 --> X3
        X4 --> X6[Pen X Carriage]
    end

    subgraph YAxis [Y-Axis Channel]
        Y1[Y Input Variable] --> Y2[Amplifier Y]
        Y2 --> Y3[Error Detector Y]
        Y3 --> Y4[Servo Motor Y]
        Y4 --> Y5[Slidewire Y]
        Y5 --> Y3
        Y4 --> Y6[Pen Y Carriage]
    end

    X6 --> PEN[Pen Tip on Stationary Paper]
    Y6 --> PEN
    PEN --> TRACE[2D Trace of Var1 vs Var2]

    style XAxis fill:#E6F3FF
    style YAxis fill:#FFF0E6
    style PEN fill:#FFD700
```

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Comparison Flowchart: Selecting a Recorder for Industry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Need to Record Process Data] --> Q1{Is Time the X-Axis?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Multiple Hours Continuous?}
    Q1 -->|No| XY[Use X-Y Recorder]
    Q2 -->|Yes| STRIP[Use Strip Chart Recorder]
    Q2 -->|No, Fixed Period| CIRC[Use Circular Chart Recorder]
    XY --> Q3{High Frequency Signal?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| TAPE[Use Magnetic Tape / Data Logger]
    Q3 -->|No| XYCONF[Use X-Y Null Balance]
    STRIP --> Q4{Accuracy Required?}
    Q4 -->|High >0.25% FS| NULL[Use Null-Balance Servo]
    Q4 -->|Moderate| GALV[Use Galvanometric]

    style START fill:#FFD700
    style XY fill:#90EE90

```

# ­¤ōØ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Part A carries **3 marks** with no choice. Answers should be **concise, definition-based, and to the point** (typically **30ŌĆō60 words**).

### **Q1. Define a recording instrument. State any two reasons why recording instruments are necessary in process industries.** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Model]`

**Model Answer:**

> A **recording instrument** is a device that produces a **continuous, permanent graphical record** of a measured variable as a function of time or another independent variable, on a recording medium such as chart paper or magnetic tape.
>
> **Two reasons for necessity:**
> 1. **Historical Record** ŌĆö A permanent trace is available for trend analysis, fault diagnosis, and process optimization.
> 2. **Legal and Quality Compliance** ŌĆö Many industries (pharmaceutical, food, power) require documented evidence of process conditions for regulatory audits.

*(Expected word count: 50ŌĆō60 words | Marks: 3)*

---

### **Q2. List and briefly distinguish between strip chart, circular chart, and X-Y recorders.** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model]`

**Model Answer:**

> | Type | Paper Format | Time Axis | Typical Use |
> |------|--------------|-----------|-------------|
> | **Strip Chart** | Long continuous strip moving linearly | Yes (X-axis) | Long-term trend monitoring |
> | **Circular Chart** | Rotating circular disc | Yes (angular) | 24-hour shift logs |
> | **X-Y Recorder** | Stationary rectangular sheet | **No** (variable vs variable) | Plotting V-I curves, stress-strain |
>
> The key distinction is that **strip and circular chart recorders plot variable vs time**, while an **X-Y recorder plots one variable against another independent variable**.

*(Expected word count: 60ŌĆō70 words | Marks: 3)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Part B questions carry **14 marks each**, with **internal choice** (two alternative questions). Each question has sub-parts (typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**). Examiners expect **neat sketches**, **labelled diagrams**, and **step-by-step derivations**.

---

### Ō£Å’ĖÅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Pattern]`

#### **Q.A (a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a **null-balance strip chart recorder**. Discuss its advantages over a galvanometric recorder. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**

The null-balance strip chart recorder consists of the following main parts:

1. **Measuring System (Input Network):** A Wheatstone bridge or potentiometric circuit that compares the input signal (e.g., thermocouple EMF) with a reference voltage from the slidewire.
2. **DC Amplifier:** A high-gain, chopper-stabilized DC amplifier that amplifies the error signal.
3. **Two-Phase Servo Motor:** A reversible AC servo motor that drives the pen and slidewire wiper in response to the amplified error.
4. **Slidewire Potentiometer:** A precision wire-wound resistance element across which a stable reference voltage is applied. The wiper taps off a voltage proportional to its position.
5. **Chart Drive System:** A synchronous AC motor with a gear train that moves the strip chart paper at a constant speed (e.g., 20 mm/hr to 600 mm/hr).
6. **Pen and Inking System:** A capillary or V-type pen fed from an ink reservoir, mounted on a carriage driven by the servo motor.
7. **Chart Paper:** A calibrated strip with printed grid lines for time (X) and value (Y).

**Working (2 marks):**

The input signal $E_{in}$ is compared with the slidewire voltage $E_{sw}$. The difference $E_{in} - E_{sw}$ (the error voltage) is amplified and fed to the servo motor. The motor drives both the pen and the slidewire wiper. The wiper moves until $E_{sw} = E_{in}$, at which point the error is zero. The pen position is then an accurate representation of the input.

**Advantages over Galvanometric Recorder (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Null-Balance | Galvanometric |
|--------|--------------|---------------|
| Accuracy | ┬▒0.25% FS (high) | ┬▒2% to ┬▒5% FS (low) |
| Input power required | Negligible | High (drives coil) |
| Linearity | Excellent (depends on slidewire) | Limited (coil geometry) |
| Sensitivity | High (depends on amplifier gain) | Limited |
| Drift | Low (slidewire is stable) | Affected by spring aging |

> **Conclusion:** The null-balance principle provides higher accuracy, better linearity, and requires no input power, making it the preferred choice for industrial process recording.

---

#### **Q.A (b)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of an **X-Y recorder**. List its **four major industrial applications**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working (4 marks):**

The X-Y recorder has two independent servo systems, one for each axis.

- The **X-axis** receives the first input variable. The servo drives the pen carriage horizontally along the X-direction.
- The **Y-axis** receives the second input variable. The servo drives the pen vertically.
- Each axis has its own **slidewire, amplifier, error detector, and servo motor**.
- The pen is mounted on a carriage that combines the X and Y motions. The resulting pen position is the cartesian plot $(X, Y)$ of the two inputs.
- The paper is held stationary on a flat plotting board. The operator manually advances the paper only when a new recording is needed.

The functional architecture is summarized in the mermaid block diagram shown in **Section 4.4** of these notes.

**Four Industrial Applications (3 marks):**

1. **Plotting V-I Characteristics of Semiconductor Diodes, Transistors, and Zeners** ŌĆö to study forward and reverse bias behavior.
2. **Plotting B-H Curves of Ferromagnetic Materials** ŌĆö to determine hysteresis loss and permeability.
3. **Recording Stress-Strain Diagrams** in materials testing laboratories.
4. **Plotting Pressure-Volume (P-V) Diagrams** of internal combustion engines and compressor performance.
5. **Plotting Frequency Response (Bode Plots)** of amplifiers and control systems.

> **Conclusion:** The X-Y recorder is indispensable wherever a **functional relationship between two variables** must be captured without reference to time.

---

### Ō£Å’ĖÅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Pattern]`

#### **Q.B (a)** Explain the construction and working of a **galvanometric strip chart recorder** with a neat labelled diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**

A galvanometric (direct-acting) strip chart recorder consists of:

1. **D'Arsonval Movement:** A moving coil suspended in a permanent magnetic field. When current flows, the coil experiences a torque proportional to the current.
2. **Spiral Hairspring (Torsion Spring):** Provides the restoring torque. The equilibrium angle $\theta$ of the coil is given by $\theta = I \cdot (K_b / K_s)$, where $K_b$ is the motor constant and $K_s$ is the spring constant.
3. **Pen Assembly:** A lightweight capillary or V-pen attached to the coil via a pointer. As the coil rotates, the pen draws a trace on the paper.
4. **Chart Paper Drive:** A synchronous motor with gear reduction moves the paper at constant speed.
5. **Damping System:** A vane immersed in oil (or eddy current damping) prevents overshoot and oscillations.
6. **Inking System:** A reservoir feeds ink to the pen tip continuously.

**Working (2 marks):**

The input current $I$ (from a sensor like a thermocouple or pressure transducer) flows through the moving coil. The coil deflects by angle $\theta$ proportional to $I$. The pen traces a vertical line on the moving paper. The X-coordinate is time (from paper motion), and the Y-coordinate is the value (from pen deflection). The combination creates a time-history graph of the input.

**Frequency Response Consideration (2 marks):**

The natural frequency is given by $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{K}{J}}$. The damping ratio is $\zeta = \dfrac{C}{2\sqrt{KJ}}$. For a faithful recording without distortion, the signal frequency should be **well below $f_n$**, and the damping should be near **critical ($\zeta \approx 0.7$)** to avoid overshoot.

> **Conclusion:** The galvanometric recorder is simple, fast, and low-cost, but its accuracy is limited compared to the null-balance type.

---

#### **Q.B (b)** Compare **strip chart, circular chart, and X-Y recorders** in a tabular form based on **eight parameters**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Tabular Comparison):**

| # | Parameter | Strip Chart | Circular Chart | X-Y Recorder |
|---|-----------|-------------|----------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Paper shape** | Long continuous strip | Circular disc | Rectangular sheet (stationary) |
| 2 | **Paper motion** | Linear, continuous | Rotational (1 rev/period) | None (manual) |
| 3 | **Time axis** | Yes (linear X) | Yes (angular) | **No** |
| 4 | **Independent variables** | Time only | Time only | Two independent variables |
| 5 | **Recording duration** | Unlimited (paper roll) | Limited to 1 revolution | Per plot basis |
| 6 | **Operating principle** | Galvanometric / Null-balance | Galvanometric / Pneumatic | Dual servo null-balance |
| 7 | **Accuracy** | ┬▒0.5% to ┬▒1% FS | ┬▒1% to ┬▒2% FS | ┬▒0.25% to ┬▒0.5% FS |
| 8 | **Typical application** | Long-term trend | Daily shift logs | V-I curves, B-H curves |
| 9 | **Cost** | Moderate | Low | High |
| 10 | **Pen mechanism** | Single pen, linear motion | Single pen, radial motion | Two-axis pen carriage |

> **Conclusion:** The choice of recorder depends on whether the application requires **time-domain recording** (strip/circular) or **variable-vs-variable plotting** (X-Y). The strip chart is the most versatile for industrial trend monitoring.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing strip chart with circular chart** ŌĆö Students often describe a circular chart's pen as moving linearly. **The pen moves radially; the paper rotates.** (Lose 2 marks if missed.)
> 2. **Forgetting to label the slidewire in a null-balance diagram** ŌĆö The slidewire wiper is the heart of the null-balance mechanism. Its mechanical linkage to the pen is the key feature. (Lose 1ŌĆō2 marks.)
> 3. **Writing "X-Y recorder has a time axis"** ŌĆö This is **WRONG**. The X-Y recorder has **no time axis**; it plots one variable against another. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 4. **Forgetting units in derivations** ŌĆö KTU examiners **strictly deduct** for missing units like mm/hr, Hz, or %. Always write units.
> 5. **Not drawing a neat labelled diagram** ŌĆö A question worth 7 marks typically carries **2ŌĆō3 marks for the diagram alone**. Always draw a block diagram or schematic, even if rough.
> 6. **Mixing up potentiometric and galvanometric** ŌĆö A potentiometric recorder is **null-balance type**; a galvanometric recorder is **direct-acting**. This distinction is a KTU favourite.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ­¤ōŗ

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Read this the night before the exam!**

- Ō£ģ A **recording instrument** provides a **permanent, continuous trace** of a variable vs. time (or another variable).
- Ō£ģ **Strip chart** ŌåÆ linear paper motion, time on X-axis, used for long-term trends.
- Ō£ģ **Circular chart** ŌåÆ rotating disc, time represented as angle, used for fixed-period logs (24 hr typical).
- Ō£ģ **X-Y recorder** ŌåÆ stationary paper, **no time axis**, plots Var1 vs Var2 (e.g., V-I, B-H, stress-strain).
- Ō£ģ **Null-balance recorder** = highest accuracy (┬▒0.25% FS), uses slidewire + servo motor, principle: balance error to zero.
- Ō£ģ **Galvanometric recorder** = direct-acting, lower accuracy (┬▒2ŌĆō5% FS), but **highest frequency response** (up to 100 Hz).
- Ō£ģ **Potentiometric recorder** is a synonym for the self-balancing null-balance recorder.
- Ō£ģ **Natural frequency** of galvanometer: $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{K}{J}}$ ŌĆö higher K and lower J = higher $f_n$.
- Ō£ģ **Damping ratio** $\zeta \approx 0.7$ gives the **best compromise** between speed and overshoot.
- Ō£ģ **Chart length formula:** $L = v \cdot t$ ŌĆö the most-tested numerical in KTU papers.
- Ō£ģ **Circular chart angular velocity:** $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi}{T}$ where $T$ is hours per revolution.
- Ō£ģ **Resolution** is the smallest readable fraction of full scale: $R = \dfrac{\Delta s}{S_{FS}} \times 100\%$.
- Ō£ģ **Drive mechanisms:** Spring-wound (portable), synchronous motor (industrial), stepper motor (digital), pneumatic (hazardous areas).
- Ō£ģ **Magnetic tape recorders** are used for **high-frequency transient** signals (vibration, seismic).
- Ō£ģ **Data loggers** are the modern digital replacement for paper recorders ŌĆö multi-channel, programmable, computer-interfaced.
- Ō£ģ **KTU 2024 CO mapping:** This topic primarily maps to **CO2** (Identify recording devices) and **CO3** (Analyze working principles).
- Ō£ģ **Examiner tip:** Always **draw a labelled block diagram** ŌĆö it earns you 2ŌĆō3 free marks.

---
