# ­¤ōØ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define frequency response of a closed-loop control system. List any three advantages of frequency response analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** The frequency response of a closed-loop control system is the **steady-state output** of the system when subjected to a **sinusoidal input** of constant amplitude and variable frequency $\omega$, with the response characterized by the magnitude ratio $M = \vert T(j\omega) \vert$ and phase angle $\phi = \angle T(j\omega)$.

**Three Advantages:**
1. **Stability assessment** is straightforward using Nyquist and Bode plots (gain and phase margins).
2. **Experimental determination** is possible by applying sinusoidal test signalsŌĆöno analytical model required.
3. **Design simplification** ŌĆö specifications like bandwidth and resonance peak can be directly read from Bode plots.
4. **Robustness evaluation** ŌĆö sensitivity to parameter variations and noise rejection can be analyzed.
5. **Filter design** ŌĆö anti-aliasing and noise filtering characteristics are clearly visible.

*(Any three well-explained points fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**What is resonance peak $M_r$? How is it related to the damping ratio $\zeta$ of a second-order system?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Resonance Peak ($M_r$):** It is the **maximum value** of the magnitude ratio $\vert T(j\omega) \vert$ of a closed-loop system as the frequency $\omega$ varies. It indicates the relative stability of the system.

**Mathematical Relation:**

$$
M_r = \frac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:**
- **Higher $M_r$** ŌåÆ lower damping ŌåÆ more oscillatory response ŌåÆ less stable.
- **Lower $M_r$** ŌåÆ higher damping ŌåÆ smoother response ŌåÆ more stable.
- For $\zeta \geq \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$, $M_r = 1$ (no resonance peak).

**Example:** For $\zeta = 0.5$, $M_r = \dfrac{1}{2(0.5)\sqrt{1-0.25}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{0.75}} \approx 1.155$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

#### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks
**Derive the expression for the closed-loop frequency response $T(j\omega)$ of a unity feedback system. Explain how the magnitude and phase are obtained from the open-loop frequency response.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** The closed-loop transfer function for a unity feedback system is:

$$
T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $s = j\omega$:

$$
T(j\omega) = \frac{G(j\omega)}{1 + G(j\omega)}
$$

**Step 3:** Let $G(j\omega) = X(\omega) + jY(\omega) = \vert G(j\omega) \vert e^{j\phi(\omega)}$

**Step 4:** Magnitude:

$$
\vert T(j\omega) \vert = \frac{\sqrt{X^2 + Y^2}}{\sqrt{(1+X)^2 + Y^2}}
$$

**Step 5:** Phase:

$$
\angle T(j\omega) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{Y}{X}\right) - \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{Y}{1+X}\right)
$$

**Step 6:** Explanation ŌĆö The closed-loop magnitude is the **ratio of open-loop magnitude to the magnitude of the feedback path vector** $1 + G(j\omega)$, which geometrically represents the **distance from the point $-1+j0$** to the open-loop frequency response point $G(j\omega)$ in the Nyquist plot.

> **Significance:** This forms the basis of the **inverse polar plot (Nichols chart)** where M-circles and N-circles are constructed in the G-plane to read $\vert T \vert$ and $\angle T$ directly.

---

#### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks
**For a unity feedback system with $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$, calculate the closed-loop frequency response at $\omega = 1$ rad/s. Also determine the resonant frequency and bandwidth approximately.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Substitute $s = j\omega$ with $\omega = 1$:

$$
G(j1) = \frac{10}{(j1)(j1+2)(j1+5)} = \frac{10}{(j)(2+j)(5+j)}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute denominator product:

$$
(2+j)(5+j) = 10 + 2j + 5j + j^2 = 10 + 7j - 1 = 9 + 7j
$$

$$
j \times (9 + 7j) = 9j + 7j^2 = -7 + 9j
$$

**Step 3:** So:

$$
G(j1) = \frac{10}{-7 + 9j} = \frac{10(-7 - 9j)}{(-7)^2 + 9^2} = \frac{-70 - 90j}{130} = -0.538 - 0.692j
$$

**Step 4:** Closed-loop frequency response:

$$
T(j1) = \frac{G(j1)}{1 + G(j1)} = \frac{-0.538 - 0.692j}{0.462 - 0.692j}
$$

**Step 5:** Multiply numerator and denominator by complex conjugate of denominator:

$$
T(j1) = \frac{(-0.538 - 0.692j)(0.462 + 0.692j)}{0.462^2 + 0.692^2}
$$

Numerator:
$$
(-0.538)(0.462) + (-0.538)(0.692j) + (-0.692j)(0.462) + (-0.692j)(0.692j)
$$
$$
= -0.2486 - 0.3723j - 0.3197j - 0.4789j^2
$$
$$
= -0.2486 + 0.4789 - 0.692j = 0.2303 - 0.692j
$$

Denominator:
$$
0.2134 + 0.4789 = 0.6923
$$

**Step 6:** Final result:

$$
T(j1) = 0.3327 - 0.9996j
$$

$$
\vert T(j1) \vert = \sqrt{0.3327^2 + 0.9996^2} = \sqrt{0.1107 + 0.9992} = \sqrt{1.1099} \approx 1.053
$$

$$
\angle T(j1) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{-0.9996}{0.3327}\right) = -71.6┬░
$$

**Step 7:** Approximate $\omega_r$ and $\omega_b$ ŌĆö Since this is a third-order system, exact closed-form expressions are not simple. Using dominant second-order approximation (if applicable), or via numerical Bode plot:

- Approximate $\omega_r \approx 1.2$ rad/s
- Approximate $\omega_b \approx 2.5$ rad/s
- $M_r \approx 1.4$ (moderately damped)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks
**What are M-circles and N-circles? Derive the equation of the M-circles in the G-plane.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Definition:**

> [!NOTE]
> **M-Circles:** Locus of all points $G(j\omega) = X + jY$ in the G-plane for which the closed-loop magnitude $\vert T(j\omega) \vert = M$ is constant.
>
> **N-Circles:** Locus of all points in the G-plane for which the closed-loop phase $\angle T(j\omega) = N$ is constant.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Derivation of M-Circles:**

Starting from:

$$
M = \left\vert \frac{G(j\omega)}{1 + G(j\omega)} \right\vert
$$

Let $G(j\omega) = X + jY$:

$$
M^2 = \frac{X^2 + Y^2}{(1+X)^2 + Y^2}
$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$
M^2[(1+X)^2 + Y^2] = X^2 + Y^2
$$

Expanding:

$$
M^2(1 + 2X + X^2 + Y^2) = X^2 + Y^2
$$

$$
M^2 + 2M^2 X + M^2 X^2 + M^2 Y^2 - X^2 - Y^2 = 0
$$

$$
X^2(M^2 - 1) + Y^2(M^2 - 1) + 2M^2 X + M^2 = 0
$$

Dividing by $(M^2 - 1)$:

$$
X^2 + Y^2 + \frac{2M^2}{M^2 - 1} X + \frac{M^2}{M^2 - 1} = 0
$$

Completing the square:

$$
\left(X + \frac{M^2}{M^2 - 1}\right)^2 + Y^2 = \left(\frac{M}{M^2 - 1}\right)^2
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Final Equation:**

$$
\boxed{\left[X - \frac{M^2}{1 - M^2}\right]^2 + Y^2 = \left[\frac{M}{1 - M^2}\right]^2}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Properties:**

| M value | Center | Radius | Location |
|---|---|---|---|
| $M = 0.5$ | $\frac{0.25}{0.75} = 0.333$ | $\frac{0.5}{0.75} = 0.667$ | Right half-plane |
| $M = 1$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | Vertical line $X = -0.5$ |
| $M = 2$ | $\frac{4}{-3} = -1.333$ | $\frac{2}{3} = 0.667$ | Left half-plane |
| $M = \infty$ | $(0, 0)$ | $\to 0$ at $-1$ | Passes through $-1$ |

> **Significance:** M-circles allow direct reading of closed-loop magnitude from open-loop Nyquist plot. The Nyquist plot is overlaid on M-circles; the point of tangency gives $M_r$.

---

#### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks
**Explain how the closed-loop frequency response specifications ($M_r$, $\omega_b$) are related to the time-domain specifications (overshoot $M_p$, settling time $t_s$).**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Relationship between $M_r$ and Overshoot $M_p$:**

Both $M_r$ and $M_p$ depend on the **damping ratio** $\zeta$:

$$
M_p = e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \quad \text{(for 2nd order underdamped)}
$$

$$
M_r = \frac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \quad \text{(for 2nd order)}
$$

| Damping $\zeta$ | $M_r$ | $M_p$ (%) |
|---|---|---|
| 0.3 | 1.75 | 37.2 |
| 0.5 | 1.155 | 16.3 |
| 0.7 | 1.017 | 4.6 |
| 1.0 | 1.0 | 0.0 |

> [!TIP]
> **Empirical Approximation:** $M_p \approx 0.01 \times M_r$ (in %) for lightly damped systems. So $M_r = 1.25$ gives roughly 25% overshoot.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Relationship between $\omega_b$ and Settling Time $t_s$:**

$$
t_s \approx \frac{4}{\zeta\omega_n}
$$

From bandwidth formula:

$$
\omega_b \approx \omega_n \quad \text{(for moderate damping)}
$$

Therefore:

$$
t_s \approx \frac{4\zeta}{\omega_b \cdot \zeta^2} = \frac{4}{\zeta\omega_b}
$$

For a typical $\zeta = 0.7$:

$$
t_s \approx \frac{4}{0.7 \cdot \omega_b} \approx \frac{5.7}{\omega_b}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Design Trade-off Summary:**

| Goal | Increase $M_r$ | Increase $\omega_b$ |
|---|---|---|
| Reduce overshoot | Decrease $M_r$ | ŌĆö |
| Reduce settling time | ŌĆö | Increase $\omega_b$ |
| Improve noise rejection | ŌĆö | Decrease $\omega_b$ |
| Increase speed | ŌĆö | Increase $\omega_b$ |

> **Conclusion:** Frequency domain specifications provide an **intuitive, graphical** way to design for time-domain performance. Engineers typically design in the **frequency domain** and verify with **time-domain simulations**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **­¤ö┤ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Skipping the $s = j\omega$ substitution step** ŌĆö Examiners specifically check for this. Always state it explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting to use $\vert \cdot \vert$ (modulus) in magnitude** ŌĆö Students often write phase directly without magnitude.
> 3. **M-circle sign error** ŌĆö The center is $\dfrac{M^2}{1-M^2}$, not $\dfrac{M^2}{M^2-1}$. The sign convention matters.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions** ŌĆö Always mention "assuming $M \neq 1$" and "for $\zeta < 1/\sqrt{2}$".
> 5. **Confusing open-loop and closed-loop $M_r$** ŌĆö Resonance peak is a **closed-loop** property; the open-loop plot has no $M_r$ in the same sense.
> 6. **Units missing** ŌĆö Always state rad/s for frequency, dB for gain, degrees for phase.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **­¤ÜĆ Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Module 3: Frequency Response of Closed-Loop Systems**

### Ō£ģ Core Concepts
- [x] **Frequency Response** = steady-state response to sinusoidal input ŌåÆ characterized by $M$ and $\phi$.
- [x] **Substitution Rule:** $s = j\omega$ is the entry point to the frequency domain.
- [x] **Closed-Loop Response:** $T(j\omega) = \dfrac{G(j\omega)}{1 + G(j\omega)H(j\omega)}$

### Ō£ģ Key Formulas
- [x] Magnitude: $\vert T(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{\vert G \vert}{\vert 1 + GH \vert}$
- [x] Phase: $\angle T = \angle G - \angle(1 + GH)$
- [x] M-circles: $\left[X - \dfrac{M^2}{1-M^2}\right]^2 + Y^2 = \left[\dfrac{M}{1-M^2}\right]^2$
- [x] $\omega_r = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$ (for $\zeta < 1/\sqrt{2}$)
- [x] $M_r = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$
- [x] $\omega_b = \omega_n\sqrt{(1-2\zeta^2) + \sqrt{4\zeta^4 - 4\zeta^2 + 2}}$

### Ō£ģ Performance Metrics
- [x] $M_r$ (resonance peak) ŌĆö indicates **relative stability** / damping
- [x] $\omega_b$ (bandwidth) ŌĆö indicates **speed of response** / noise rejection
- [x] $\omega_r$ (resonant frequency) ŌĆö frequency at which $M_r$ occurs

### Ō£ģ Plotting Tools
- [x] **Bode Plot:** log-magnitude (dB) and phase vs $\log \omega$
- [x] **Nyquist Plot:** $\text{Im}[G(j\omega)]$ vs $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)]$ in complex plane
- [x] **Nichols Chart:** phase vs magnitude in dB ŌĆö overlays M and N contours
- [x] **M-Circles:** constant $\vert T \vert$ loci in G-plane
- [x] **N-Circles:** constant $\angle T$ loci in G-plane

### Ō£ģ Frequency Ōåö Time Domain
- [x] $M_r \leftrightarrow M_p$ (overshoot) ŌĆö both decrease with higher $\zeta$
- [x] $\omega_b \leftrightarrow t_s$ (settling time) ŌĆö larger $\omega_b$ gives faster response
- [x] Phase margin $\leftrightarrow$ overshoot ŌĆö higher $PM$ gives lower $M_p$

### Ō£ģ Design Thumb Rules
- [x] $M_r$ between **1.0 and 1.5** for good performance
- [x] Phase margin between **30┬░ and 60┬░**
- [x] Gain margin **> 6 dB**
- [x] Bandwidth should be **10├Ś signal bandwidth** for tracking
- [x] Bandwidth should be **< 0.1├Ś noise frequency** for rejection

### Ō£ģ Typical KTU Question Patterns
- [x] Derivation of $T(j\omega)$ from $G(s)$ ŌĆö **3 to 7 marks**
- [x] M-circle equation derivation ŌĆö **5 to 7 marks**
- [x] Numerical calculation of $T(j\omega)$ at given $\omega$ ŌĆö **5 to 7 marks**
- [x] Explanation of $M_r$, $\omega_b$, $\omega_r$ ŌĆö **3 marks**
- [x] Frequency-time domain relationship ŌĆö **5 to 7 marks**
- [x] Sketching/identifying Bode or Nyquist plot ŌĆö **3 to 5 marks**

> **Final Tip:** Always carry the **substitution $s = j\omega$** as your first line in any frequency-domain derivation ŌĆö it is the KTU board's most-checked foundational step! ­¤Ä»

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control System Design by Frequency Response Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT413 - PROCESS INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Control System Design by Frequency Response Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Control System Design by Frequency Response Techniques** is a classical control engineering methodology that shapes the open-loop transfer function of a system in the frequency domain by adjusting magnitude (gain) and phase characteristics at strategic frequencies, in order to meet prescribed **Gain Margin (GM)**, **Phase Margin (PM)**, **Bandwidth**, **Resonant Peak (Mr)**, and **Resonant Frequency (Žēr)** specifications, while simultaneously satisfying transient response requirements like rise time, settling time, and overshoot.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, this design paradigm translates transient-domain specifications (like % overshoot and settling time) into equivalent frequency-domain indices, after which **compensators** (Lead, Lag, Lead-Lag) are cascaded with the plant to reshape the Bode/Nichols/Nyquist plots. The compensator transfer function $G_c(s)$ is chosen such that:

$$G_{compensated}(s) = G_c(s) \cdot G_p(s)$$

satisfies the new stability and performance criteria without violating physical realizability (proper transfer function, no pole-zero cancellations in RHP, etc.).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy ŌĆö The Ship's Steering Wheel**
> Imagine you are steering a large cargo ship. The ship's natural response to a rudder turn is sluggish and oscillatory ŌĆö it takes a long time to settle, and it oversteers before correcting. Now, the **ship's dynamics** represent your plant $G_p(s)$. The **rudder, the steering linkage, and the autopilot** together form a compensator $G_c(s)$. The **sea conditions, weather, and desired heading** are your specifications (PM, GM, bandwidth).
> - A **Lead compensator** is like a *sharper, more responsive rudder* ŌĆö it gives you faster reaction and a tighter turn (improves transient response, increases bandwidth, increases phase margin).
> - A **Lag compensator** is like a *gentle trim tab* ŌĆö it adds steadiness in calm waters, reducing steady-state error (improves low-frequency gain without disturbing high-frequency phase).
> - A **Lead-Lag compensator** is the *complete autopilot* ŌĆö both responsive and steady, designed to track the heading accurately without overshooting.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Benchmarks Used in KTU Board Examinations:**
> - **Gain Crossover Frequency (Žēgc):** Frequency at which $\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$ (i.e., $0\,dB$)
> - **Phase Crossover Frequency (Žēpc):** Frequency at which $\angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -180^\circ$
> - **Gain Margin (GM in dB):** $GM = -20\log_{10}\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$
> - **Phase Margin (PM in degrees):** $PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$
> - **Resonant Peak (Mr):** $M_r = \dfrac{1}{\vert \sin(PM) \vert}$ (approximate relation for 2nd-order systems)
> - **Bandwidth (ŽēB):** Frequency at which magnitude drops to $-3\,dB$ of its low-frequency value
> - **Standard Target Specs:** $PM = 45^\circ$ to $60^\circ$, $GM \geq 6\,dB$ (often $10$ to $20\,dB$)

## 1.4 Why Frequency Response Design?

| Aspect | Time Domain | Frequency Domain |
|---|---|---|
| **Mathematical Tool** | Differential equations | Algebraic complex functions |
| **Visualization** | Step/impulse responses | Bode, Nyquist, Nichols plots |
| **Specification Translation** | Direct (rise time, overshoot) | Indirect (PM, GM, ŽēB) |
| **Compensator Tuning** | Trial-and-error root locus | Systematic slope/phase shaping |
| **Noise Handling** | Difficult | Easy (high-frequency roll-off visible) |
| **Experimental Validation** | Impulse tests | Sine-wave frequency sweep |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 focuses on Bode-plot-based compensator synthesis, with strong emphasis on the translation of time-domain specs to frequency-domain specs and vice versa. Students must master the *art of slope shaping* around the gain crossover frequency.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Frequency Response Design Philosophy ŌĆö Step by Step

The complete design procedure consists of the following structured phases:

### **Phase 1: Translation of Specifications**

Given transient specifications, derive equivalent frequency-domain metrics:

**Step A ŌĆö From % Overshoot ($Mp$) to Phase Margin (PM):**
For a second-order prototype system, the relationship between peak overshoot and phase margin is given by:

$$PM = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2\zeta}{\sqrt{\sqrt{1+4\zeta^4}-2\zeta^2}}\right)$$

where $\zeta$ is the damping ratio. An approximate, widely-used shortcut in KTU problems is:

$$\zeta \approx \frac{PM}{100} \quad \text{(valid for } PM \approx 30^\circ \text{ to } 70^\circ\text{)}$$

**Step B ŌĆö From Settling Time ($ts$) to Gain Crossover Frequency:**
A common engineering approximation:

$$\omega_{gc} \approx \frac{4}{t_s \cdot \zeta} \quad \text{(for 2\% settling criterion)}$$

or more conservatively:

$$\omega_{gc} \approx \frac{1.5}{t_r}$$

where $t_r$ is the rise time.

**Step C ŌĆö From Damping Ratio to Resonant Peak:**

$$M_r = \frac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \quad \text{for } 0 \le \zeta \le \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

### **Phase 2: Bode Plot Analysis of the Uncompensated System**

- Plot $|G(j\omega)H(j\omega)|_{dB}$ vs $\log_{10}\omega$ and $\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$ vs $\log_{10}\omega$.
- Identify the **uncompensated phase margin** $PM_u$ and **uncompensated gain margin** $GM_u$.
- Compare against specifications. The required additional phase $\phi_m$ is:

$$\phi_m = PM_{spec} - PM_u + 5^\circ \text{ to } 12^\circ \text{ (safety margin)}$$

### **Phase 3: Compensator Selection and Synthesis**

| If the system needs... | Use... | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Higher PM, faster response, larger bandwidth | **Lead Compensator** | Adds positive phase (phase lead) |
| Lower steady-state error without changing transient response | **Lag Compensator** | Boosts low-frequency gain |
| Both improved transient and steady-state | **Lead-Lag Compensator** | Combines both advantages |

### **Phase 4: Verification**

Plot the compensated Bode diagram and confirm PM, GM, and bandwidth meet all specs. Simulate step response to cross-validate.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Bode Design)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **definitive cheat sheet** for compensator design derivations in KTU 2024 ESE papers.

### **A. Lead Compensator**

$$G_c(s) = K_c \cdot \frac{1 + \alpha \tau s}{1 + \tau s}, \quad \alpha > 1$$

Maximum phase contribution frequency:

$$\omega_m = \frac{1}{\tau\sqrt{\alpha}}$$

Maximum phase lead:

$$\phi_m = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha + 1}\right)$$

Solving for $\alpha$ from required $\phi_m$:

$$\alpha = \frac{1 + \sin\phi_m}{1 - \sin\phi_m}$$

Magnitude boost at $\omega_m$ (in dB):

$$A_m = 10\log_{10}\alpha = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha}}\right)^{-1}$$

Zero and pole placement:

$$\omega_z = \frac{1}{\alpha\tau} = \omega_m\sqrt{\alpha}, \quad \omega_p = \frac{1}{\tau} = \frac{\omega_m}{\sqrt{\alpha}}$$

### **B. Lag Compensator**

$$G_c(s) = K_c \cdot \frac{1 + \beta \tau s}{1 + \tau s}, \quad \beta < 1$$

(Note: $K_c$ chosen to compensate for attenuation; pole-zero ratio is $\beta$.)

Phase lag at design frequency is small (typically $-5^\circ$ to $-15^\circ$, acceptable). The compensator primarily provides attenuation:

$$\text{Attenuation} = 20\log_{10}\beta \quad (\text{negative dB value})$$

Zero and pole:

$$\omega_z = \frac{1}{\tau}, \quad \omega_p = \frac{1}{\beta\tau}$$

### **C. Lead-Lag Compensator**

$$G_c(s) = K_c \cdot \underbrace{\frac{1 + \alpha\tau_1 s}{1 + \tau_1 s}}_{\text{Lead part, } \alpha > 1} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{1 + \beta\tau_2 s}{1 + \tau_2 s}}_{\text{Lag part, } \beta < 1}$$

### **D. Critical Conversion Formulas**

Time ŌåÆ Frequency Spec Mapping:

$$t_s \approx \frac{4}{\zeta\omega_n}, \quad t_p = \frac{\pi}{\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}, \quad M_p = e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \times 100\%$$

$$PM \approx 100\zeta, \quad \omega_{BW} \approx \omega_n\sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2 + \sqrt{4\zeta^4 - 4\zeta^2 + 2}}$$

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Process Instrumentation Applications of Frequency Response Design:**
> - **Temperature Control Loops (Jacketed CSTR):** Lead compensation accelerates sluggish thermal dynamics.
> - **Pressure Control in Compressors:** Lag compensation reduces steady-state offset caused by load disturbances.
> - **Flow Control in Pipelines:** Lead-Lag compensators handle both fast hydraulic transients and slow demand variations.
> - **Level Control in Distillation Columns:** Lead compensators are used to dampen oscillatory column dynamics.
> - **pH Control in Neutralization Reactors:** Lag compensators are essential due to the severe nonlinearity and integrator-like behavior at the equivalence point.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Phase Lead Frequency $\omega_m$ for a Lead Compensator

Consider the lead compensator:

$$G_c(j\omega) = \frac{1 + j\alpha\tau\omega}{1 + j\tau\omega}$$

We need to find the frequency at which the phase contribution is maximum.

**Step 1: Write the phase of the compensator.**

The phase angle $\phi(\omega)$ contributed by the lead compensator is the angle of the numerator minus the angle of the denominator:

$$\phi(\omega) = \tan^{-1}(\alpha\tau\omega) - \tan^{-1}(\tau\omega)$$

**Step 2: Differentiate $\phi(\omega)$ with respect to $\omega$ and set to zero.**

$$\frac{d\phi}{d\omega} = \frac{\alpha\tau}{1 + \alpha^2\tau^2\omega^2} - \frac{\tau}{1 + \tau^2\omega^2} = 0$$

**Step 3: Cross-multiply and simplify.**

$$\alpha\tau(1 + \tau^2\omega^2) = \tau(1 + \alpha^2\tau^2\omega^2)$$

$$\alpha + \alpha\tau^2\omega^2 = 1 + \alpha^2\tau^2\omega^2$$

**Step 4: Collect $\omega^2$ terms on one side.**

$$\alpha - 1 = \alpha^2\tau^2\omega^2 - \alpha\tau^2\omega^2$$

$$\alpha - 1 = \tau^2\omega^2(\alpha^2 - \alpha) = \alpha\tau^2\omega^2(\alpha - 1)$$

**Step 5: Cancel $(\alpha - 1)$ on both sides (valid since $\alpha > 1$):**

$$1 = \alpha\tau^2\omega^2 \implies \omega^2 = \frac{1}{\alpha\tau^2}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_m = \frac{1}{\tau\sqrt{\alpha}}}$$

This is the geometric mean of the pole and zero frequencies, which is a key insight: **at $\omega_m$, the zero and pole are equidistant on a logarithmic scale.**

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Phase Lead $\phi_m$ in Terms of $\alpha$

Substitute $\omega_m = \dfrac{1}{\tau\sqrt{\alpha}}$ back into the phase expression:

**Step 1: Compute $\tan(\phi_m)$.**

$$\tan(\phi_m) = \tan\left[\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\alpha\tau\omega_m}{1}\right) - \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\tau\omega_m}{1}\right)\right]$$

Using the identity $\tan(A - B) = \dfrac{\tan A - \tan B}{1 + \tan A \tan B}$:

$$\tan(\phi_m) = \frac{\alpha\tau\omega_m - \tau\omega_m}{1 + \alpha\tau^2\omega_m^2}$$

**Step 2: Substitute $\tau\omega_m = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha}}$:**

$$\tan(\phi_m) = \frac{\alpha \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha}}}{1 + \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{\alpha}} = \frac{\sqrt{\alpha} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha}}}{2} = \frac{\alpha - 1}{2\sqrt{\alpha}}$$

**Step 3: Use the double-angle identity.**

Recall: $\sin\phi_m = \dfrac{\tan\phi_m}{\sqrt{1 + \tan^2\phi_m}}$. After algebra (or via half-angle manipulation), the canonical result is:

$$\sin\phi_m = \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha + 1}$$

**Step 4: Invert to solve for $\alpha$:**

$$\alpha\sin\phi_m + \sin\phi_m = \alpha - 1$$

$$\alpha(1 - \sin\phi_m) = 1 + \sin\phi_m$$

$$\boxed{\alpha = \frac{1 + \sin\phi_m}{1 - \sin\phi_m}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Magnitude Boost at $\omega_m$

At $\omega_m$, the magnitude of the lead compensator is:

$$|G_c(j\omega_m)| = \frac{\sqrt{1 + \alpha^2\tau^2\omega_m^2}}{\sqrt{1 + \tau^2\omega_m^2}}$$

Substitute $\tau\omega_m = 1/\sqrt{\alpha}$:

$$|G_c(j\omega_m)| = \frac{\sqrt{1 + \alpha^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\alpha}}}{\sqrt{1 + \frac{1}{\alpha}}} = \frac{\sqrt{1 + \alpha}}{\sqrt{1 + \frac{1}{\alpha}}} = \frac{\sqrt{1 + \alpha}}{\sqrt{\frac{\alpha + 1}{\alpha}}} = \sqrt{\alpha}$$

In decibels:

$$\boxed{A_m = 20\log_{10}\sqrt{\alpha} = 10\log_{10}\alpha \quad (dB)}$$

This is the *attenuation* the system suffers at $\omega_m$ due to the lead network. To compensate, the new gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}^{new}$ must be placed **higher** than $\omega_m$, specifically:

$$\omega_{gc}^{new} = \omega_m \cdot \sqrt{\alpha}$$

This is the KTU 2024 standard iterative procedure:

> 1. Find $\omega_m$ such that at $\omega_m$, the uncompensated magnitude is $-10\log_{10}\alpha\,dB$.
> 2. This $\omega_m$ becomes the new gain crossover frequency.

## 3.4 Complete Worked Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

**Problem:** A unity feedback system has open-loop transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+2)(s+8)}$. Design a lead compensator to achieve $PM = 45^\circ$ and $K_v = 40\,s^{-1}$.

**Step 1: Determine $K$ from the steady-state velocity error coefficient.**

$$K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} sG(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{K \cdot s}{s(s+2)(s+8)} = \frac{K}{2 \cdot 8} = \frac{K}{16}$$

Setting $K_v = 40$:

$$\frac{K}{16} = 40 \implies K = 640$$

**Step 2: Write the uncompensated frequency response.**

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{640}{j\omega(j\omega+2)(j\omega+8)}$$

$$|G(j\omega)| = \frac{640}{\omega\sqrt{\omega^2+4}\sqrt{\omega^2+64}}$$

$$\angle G(j\omega) = -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right) - \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega}{8}\right)$$

**Step 3: Compute the uncompensated PM at the gain crossover.**

Find $\omega_{gc}$ such that $|G(j\omega_{gc})| = 1$:

$$\frac{640}{\omega_{gc}\sqrt{\omega_{gc}^2+4}\sqrt{\omega_{gc}^2+64}} = 1$$

For large $\omega_{gc}$ (where $\omega^2 \gg 4, 64$):

$$\omega_{gc}^3 \approx 640 \implies \omega_{gc} \approx 8.6\,rad/s$$

A more accurate iterative solution: $\omega_{gc} \approx 8.6\,rad/s$.

**Step 4: Calculate uncompensated phase margin.**

$$PM_u = 180^\circ - 90^\circ - \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{8.6}{2}\right) - \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{8.6}{8}\right)$$

$$PM_u = 90^\circ - 76.9^\circ - 47.1^\circ = -33.9^\circ$$

The uncompensated system is **unstable** (negative PM).

**Step 5: Determine required phase lead.**

$$\phi_m = PM_{spec} - PM_u + 10^\circ = 45^\circ - (-33.9^\circ) + 10^\circ = 88.9^\circ$$

This is physically impossible (max lead from a single-stage is $\sim 70^\circ$). So we either relax specs or use a two-stage lead. KTU examiners often give smaller required leads (15┬░ŌĆō40┬░) for single-stage compensators. Let us use a relaxed spec: $PM = 30^\circ$.

$$\phi_m = 30^\circ - (-33.9^\circ) + 8^\circ = 71.9^\circ$$

Still large. Reconsidering with $PM = 20^\circ$:

$$\phi_m = 20^\circ + 33.9^\circ + 5^\circ = 58.9^\circ$$

**Step 6: Compute $\alpha$.**

$$\alpha = \frac{1 + \sin(58.9^\circ)}{1 - \sin(58.9^\circ)} = \frac{1 + 0.857}{1 - 0.857} = \frac{1.857}{0.143} = 12.99 \approx 13$$

**Step 7: Magnitude attenuation by the lead network.**

$$A_m = 10\log_{10}(13) = 11.14\,dB$$

**Step 8: Find new gain crossover frequency.**

We need the uncompensated $|G(j\omega)| = -11.14\,dB$ (i.e., $0.277$ in magnitude).

$$\frac{640}{\omega_{gc}^{new}\sqrt{(\omega_{gc}^{new})^2+4}\sqrt{(\omega_{gc}^{new})^2+64}} = 0.277$$

Iterating: $\omega_{gc}^{new} \approx 12.8\,rad/s$.

**Step 9: Compensator parameters.**

$$\omega_m = \omega_{gc}^{new} = 12.8\,rad/s$$

$$\tau = \frac{1}{\omega_m\sqrt{\alpha}} = \frac{1}{12.8 \times \sqrt{13}} = \frac{1}{46.14} = 0.0217\,s$$

$$\alpha\tau = 13 \times 0.0217 = 0.282\,s$$

**Step 10: Final Lead Compensator.**

$$G_c(s) = \frac{1 + 0.282s}{1 + 0.0217s}$$

**Step 11: Verification.**

At $\omega = 12.8\,rad/s$, the uncompensated phase is approximately $-130^\circ$, and the compensator adds $+58.9^\circ$, giving a total phase of $-71.1^\circ$, hence $PM = 180^\circ - 130^\circ - 71.1^\circ$... wait, let me recompute:

$$PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc}^{new}) = 180^\circ + (-90^\circ - 76.5^\circ - 56.4^\circ + 58.9^\circ) = 16^\circ$$

A small deviation due to the approximations. KTU examiners accept ┬▒5┬░ tolerance. The procedure is what earns marks.

## 3.5 Lag Compensator Design Procedure (Complete Analytical Walk-through)

Suppose the uncompensated system has $PM = 60^\circ$ (good transient) but $K_v$ is too low (poor steady-state).

**Step 1:** Compute the uncompensated $K_v = K_b / \text{(open-loop constant)}$, where $K_b$ is the gain at the uncompensated $\omega_{gc}$.

**Step 2:** Find the ratio $\gamma = K_{v,spec} / K_{v,old}$. This equals $\beta$ in the lag compensator:

$$\beta = \frac{K_{v,spec}}{K_{v,old}} < 1 \quad \text{(but as a ratio, often expressed as } \beta = 1/\gamma \text{)}$$

**Step 3:** Choose the upper zero of the lag compensator to be one decade *below* the new $\omega_{gc}$:

$$\omega_z = \frac{\omega_{gc}^{new}}{10}$$

**Step 4:** Place the pole at $\omega_p = \omega_z / \beta$ (much lower than $\omega_z$).

**Step 5:** The lag network contributes a small phase lag (typically $-5^\circ$ to $-12^\circ$) at $\omega_{gc}^{new}$, which the engineer accounts for by reducing target PM by that amount initially.

**Why the pole must be one decade below $\omega_{gc}$?** Because at that frequency, the magnitude of the lag network is approximately $0\,dB$ (since the pole-zero pair has both rolled in), and the phase contribution is nearly $-5^\circ$, negligible for transient behavior.

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Design Step | Lead Focus | Lag Focus | Lead-Lag Focus |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Goal** | Improve transient response | Reduce steady-state error | Improve both |
| **PM Effect** | Increase significantly | Slight decrease ($-5┬░$ to $-12┬░$) | Net increase |
| **Bandwidth Effect** | Increase | Slight decrease | Moderate increase |
| **$K_v$ / $K_p$ Effect** | Slight (depends on $K_c$) | Significant increase | Significant increase |
| **High-Freq Noise** | Amplified (problematic) | Attenuated (good) | Depends on design |
| **Typical $\alpha$** | 5 to 20 | 0.05 to 0.2 | Lead: 5ŌĆō15, Lag: 0.05ŌĆō0.2 |
| **Design Frequency** | $\omega_m = $ new $\omega_{gc}$ | $\omega_z = \omega_{gc}/10$ | Combination |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of Frequency Response Compensator Design Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the iterative KTU-approved workflow for compensator synthesis:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Plant Transfer Function Gp s"] --> B["Translate Time Specs to Frequency Specs"]
    B --> B1["Mp to PM: PM approx 100 zeta"]
    B --> B2["ts to omega gc: omega gc approx 4 divided by ts zeta"]
    B --> B3["Ess to Kv or Kp requirement"]
    B --> C["Plot Bode of Uncompensated Gp s"]
    C --> D["Measure PMu and GMu"]
    D --> E{"PMu meets spec?"}
    E -- "No, PMu too low" --> F["Check Kv: does steady-state meet spec?"]
    E -- "Yes" --> G["Check Kv requirement"]
    F -- "Kv also insufficient" --> H["Use Lead-Lag Compensator"]
    F -- "Kv is sufficient" --> I["Use Lead Compensator only"]
    G -- "Kv insufficient" --> J["Use Lag Compensator"]
    G -- "Kv sufficient" --> K["No compensator needed: System OK"]
    I --> L["Compute alpha from phi m = PM spec - PMu + 5 to 12 deg"]
    J --> M["Compute beta = Kv old divided by Kv new"]
    H --> N["Design Lead section first, then Lag section"]
    L --> O["Find new omega gc where mag = -10 log alpha"]
    M --> P["Place lag zero one decade below omega gc"]
    N --> Q["Cascade both compensators"]
    O --> R["Compute tau and alpha tau"]
    P --> S["Compute tau lag and beta tau lag"]
    Q --> T["Form Gc s Gp s"]
    R --> T
    S --> T
    T --> U["Verify Bode plot: PM, GM, omega BW"]
    U --> V{"All specs met?"}
    V -- "Yes" --> W["End: Final Compensator Gc s"]
    V -- "No" --> X["Iterate: Adjust alpha or beta"]
    X --> L
```

## 4.2 Bode Plot Compensation Effect (Conceptual Magnitude-Phase Diagram)

The following diagram shows the *before* and *after* effects of lead compensation on the Bode plot:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ORIG["Uncompensated System"]
        O1["Magnitude: Slope -20 to -60 dB per decade"]
        O2["Phase: -90 to -270 deg"]
        O3["PMu: Negative or low"]
    end
    subgraph COMP["Compensated System with Lead"]
        C1["Magnitude: Same at low freq, lifted at high freq"]
        C2["Phase: Boosted by phi m near omega m"]
        C3["PM: Improved to 45 to 60 deg"]
    end
    subgraph NET["Net Effect"]
        N1["Bandwidth: Increased"]
        N2["Transient: Faster, less overshoot"]
        N3["Stability: Robust"]
    end
    ORIG --> COMP
    COMP --> NET
```

## 4.3 Lead, Lag, and Lead-Lag Compensator Pole-Zero Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LEAD["Lead Compensator: alpha greater than 1"]
        LZ["Zero at omega z = 1 over alpha tau on negative real axis"]
        LP["Pole at omega p = 1 over tau further left on negative real axis"]
    end
    subgraph LAG["Lag Compensator: beta less than 1"]
        GZ["Zero at omega z = 1 over tau on negative real axis"]
        GP["Pole at omega p = 1 over beta tau closer to origin"]
    end
    subgraph LL["Lead-Lag Compensator"]
        LLZ1["Lead Zero"]
        LLP1["Lead Pole left of lead zero"]
        LLZ2["Lag Zero further right"]
        LLP2["Lag Pole closest to origin"]
    end
    LEAD --> LL
    LAG --> LL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Pole-Zero Map:** In a Lead compensator, the *zero is closer to the origin* than the *pole*. In a Lag compensator, the *pole is closer to the origin* than the *zero*. This fundamental inversion of pole-zero order is what produces phase lead versus phase lag.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Specifications: PM, GM, omega BW, Kv, Mp, ts"] --> B["Specification Translation Block"]
```
