# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]
**Define the following terms with respect to a control system:**
**(a) BIBO stability**    **(b) Asymptotic stability**    **(c) Marginal stability**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) BIBO Stability:** A linear time-invariant system is said to be BIBO (Bounded-Input Bounded-Output) stable if for every bounded input signal $u(t)$ satisfying $\vert u(t) \vert \le M_1 < \infty$, the corresponding output $y(t)$ is also bounded, i.e., $\vert y(t) \vert \le M_2 < \infty$ for all $t \ge 0$.

**(b) Asymptotic Stability:** A system is asymptotically stable if, in the absence of any input, the system returns to its equilibrium state as $t \to \infty$. Mathematically, $\lim_{t \to \infty} y(t) = 0$ for all initial conditions.

**(c) Marginal Stability:** A system is marginally (or critically) stable if the output exhibits sustained oscillations of constant amplitude when disturbed ŌĆö neither decaying nor growing. This corresponds to simple poles lying exactly on the $j\omega$-axis (imaginary axis) of the $s$-plane.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]
**State the necessary condition for stability of a linear system based on the characteristic equation. Is this condition also sufficient? Justify your answer.**

**Model Answer:**

**Necessary Condition:** For a linear system to be stable, **all coefficients of the characteristic equation** $a_0 s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_n = 0$ **must be real, positive, and non-zero** (i.e., all $a_i \ge 0$ and $a_0 \ne 0$).

**Justification (not sufficient):** The necessary condition is **not sufficient**. A system may have all positive coefficients and still be unstable. For example, the characteristic equation $s^3 + s^2 + s + 2 = 0$ has all positive coefficients, yet one root lies in the right-half plane. Hence, the **Routh-Hurwitz criterion** must be applied to obtain the *sufficient* condition.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, you are required to answer **one full question of 14 marks** from Module 3. Each question typically carries two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Model Question]

**(a)** For a unity feedback system with open-loop transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)}$, determine the range of $K$ for which the closed-loop system is stable using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion.   **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A closed-loop system has the characteristic equation $s^4 + 2s^3 + 8s^2 + 4s + 16 = 0$. Using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion, determine the number of poles in the right-half $s$-plane and comment on the stability of the system.   **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Form the characteristic equation:**

$$1 + G(s) = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; s(s+2)(s+4) + K = 0$$

Expanding $(s+2)(s+4) = s^2 + 6s + 8$:

$$s(s^2 + 6s + 8) + K = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + K = 0$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply necessary condition:** All coefficients positive ŌåÆ $K > 0$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Construct the Routh array:**

$$
\begin{array}{c|cc}
s^3 & 1 & 8 \\
s^2 & 6 & K \\
s^1 & b_1 & 0 \\
s^0 & c_1 & 0
\end{array}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute $b_1$ and $c_1$:**

$$b_1 = \dfrac{(6)(8) - (1)(K)}{6} = \dfrac{48 - K}{6}$$

$$c_1 = \dfrac{(b_1)(K) - (6)(0)}{b_1} = K$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply stability conditions $b_1 > 0$ and $c_1 > 0$:**

$$b_1 > 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \dfrac{48 - K}{6} > 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; K < 48$$

$$c_1 > 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; K > 0$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Final range:**

$$\boxed{0 < K < 48}$$

The system is stable for any gain $K$ in the open interval $(0,\; 48)$.

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Given characteristic equation:** $s^4 + 2s^3 + 8s^2 + 4s + 16 = 0$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Routh array construction:**

$$
\begin{array}{c|ccc}
s^4 & 1 & 8 & 16 \\
s^3 & 2 & 4 & 0 \\
s^2 & b_1 & b_2 & 0 \\
s^1 & c_1 & 0 & 0 \\
s^0 & d_1 & 0 & 0
\end{array}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $b_1, b_2$:**

$$b_1 = \dfrac{(2)(8) - (1)(4)}{2} = \dfrac{16 - 4}{2} = 6$$

$$b_2 = \dfrac{(2)(16) - (1)(0)}{2} = 16$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $c_1$:**

$$c_1 = \dfrac{(6)(4) - (2)(16)}{6} = \dfrac{24 - 32}{6} = \dfrac{-8}{6} = -\dfrac{4}{3}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute $d_1$:**

$$d_1 = \dfrac{c_1 \cdot b_2 - b_1 \cdot 0}{c_1} = b_2 = 16$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Examine the first column for sign changes:**

$$\text{First column} = \left\{1,\; 2,\; 6,\; -\dfrac{4}{3},\; 16\right\}$$

Sign changes:
- $6 \to -\dfrac{4}{3}$ : **one sign change**
- $-\dfrac{4}{3} \to 16$ : **one sign change**

**Total sign changes = 2** ŌåÆ **2 roots lie in the right-half $s$-plane**.

**Conclusion:** The system is **unstable** with **two poles in the RHP**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the Routh-Hurwitz stability criterion. Construct the Routh array for the characteristic equation $s^4 + 2s^3 + 3s^2 + 4s + 5 = 0$ and comment on stability.   **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A unity feedback control system has an open-loop transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{K(s+2)}{s(s+1)(s+3)}$. Determine the range of $K$ for closed-loop stability.   **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Explanation of Routh-Hurwitz criterion:**
The Routh-Hurwitz criterion is a **mathematical test** that determines whether all roots of a polynomial have negative real parts (i.e., lie in the left-half $s$-plane) *without* computing the roots themselves. It is based on constructing a **Routh array** from the polynomial coefficients and counting the **sign changes in the first column** ŌĆö each sign change corresponds to one root in the right-half $s$-plane.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Given characteristic equation:** $s^4 + 2s^3 + 3s^2 + 4s + 5 = 0$

All coefficients positive ŌĆö necessary condition satisfied.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Construct Routh array:**

$$
\begin{array}{c|ccc}
s^4 & 1 & 3 & 5 \\
s^3 & 2 & 4 & 0 \\
s^2 & b_1 & b_2 & 0 \\
s^1 & c_1 & 0 & 0 \\
s^0 & d_1 & 0 & 0
\end{array}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute elements:**

$$b_1 = \dfrac{(2)(3) - (1)(4)}{2} = \dfrac{2}{2} = 1$$

$$b_2 = \dfrac{(2)(5) - (1)(0)}{2} = 5$$

$$c_1 = \dfrac{(1)(4) - (2)(5)}{1} = 4 - 10 = -6$$

$$d_1 = \dfrac{(-6)(5) - (1)(0)}{-6} = 5$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö First column inspection:**

$$\text{First column} = \{1,\; 2,\; 1,\; -6,\; 5\}$$

Sign changes:
- $1 \to -6$ : one sign change
- $-6 \to 5$ : one sign change

**Total sign changes = 2 ŌåÆ 2 poles in the RHP.**

**Conclusion:** The system is **unstable**.

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Characteristic equation:**

$$1 + G(s) = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; s(s+1)(s+3) + K(s+2) = 0$$

Expand $s(s+1)(s+3)$:

$$(s+1)(s+3) = s^2 + 4s + 3$$

$$s(s^2 + 4s + 3) = s^3 + 4s^2 + 3s$$

So the characteristic equation becomes:

$$s^3 + 4s^2 + 3s + Ks + 2K = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; s^3 + 4s^2 + (3+K)s + 2K = 0$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply necessary condition:** All coefficients positive ŌåÆ $K > 0$ and $3+K > 0$ (automatically satisfied if $K > 0$).

**Step 3 ŌĆö Routh array:**

$$
\begin{array}{c|cc}
s^3 & 1 & (3+K) \\
s^2 & 4 & 2K \\
s^1 & b_1 & 0 \\
s^0 & c_1 & 0
\end{array}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute entries:**

$$b_1 = \dfrac{(4)(3+K) - (1)(2K)}{4} = \dfrac{12 + 4K - 2K}{4} = \dfrac{12 + 2K}{4} = \dfrac{6+K}{2}$$

$$c_1 = \dfrac{(b_1)(2K) - (4)(0)}{b_1} = 2K$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Stability conditions:**

$$b_1 > 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \dfrac{6+K}{2} > 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; K > -6$$

$$c_1 > 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; K > 0$$

The combined condition is $K > 0$.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Final range:**

$$\boxed{K > 0}$$

The closed-loop system is stable for all positive values of $K$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Sign error in the Routh array formula** ŌĆö The most common is reversing the numerator. Always: $\text{new entry} = \dfrac{(\text{row above})\cdot(\text{2-rows-above, shifted}) - (\text{2-rows-above, first element})\cdot(\text{row above, shifted})}{\text{2-rows-above, first element}}$. Memorize the *determinant pattern*.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the necessary condition** ŌĆö Many students jump directly to the Routh array. Always state the necessary condition (all positive coefficients) first. **(ŌłÆ1 to ŌłÆ2 marks if missed)**
>
> 3. **Improper handling of the row-of-zeros case** ŌĆö Students often skip the auxiliary polynomial step. This loses **all 7 marks** of that sub-part.
>
> 4. **Incorrect final inequality direction** ŌĆö When solving for $K$, write the final range as $0 < K < K_{\max}$ in a single boxed expression. A bare "stable for $K < 48$" without the $K > 0$ clause is **incomplete**.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to comment on stability** ŌĆö Always end with a clear one-line verdict: *"The system is stable / unstable with $N$ poles in the RHP."* This is worth **1ŌĆō2 marks** standalone.
>
> 6. **Not verifying the characteristic equation expansion** ŌĆö A sign error in expanding $s(s+2)(s+4)$ will cascade through the entire problem. Double-check at the start.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> - Stability of an LTI system is a property of the **system itself**, determined by the **characteristic equation** $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$.
> - **BIBO Stable** Ō¤║ all closed-loop poles in the **left-half $s$-plane**.
> - **Routh-Hurwitz** gives a yes/no stability answer **without solving** the characteristic equation.
> - **Necessary condition:** all coefficients of the characteristic polynomial are **real, positive, non-zero**.
> - **Number of RHP poles = number of sign changes in the first column of the Routh array.**
> - **Row of zeros** ŌåÆ use the **auxiliary polynomial** from the row above, differentiate it, and replace.
> - First element of row is zero (but not full row) ŌåÆ substitute a small $\epsilon > 0$ and take limit.
> - Range of $K$: set all first-column entries **strictly positive** and solve the resulting inequalities.
> - The Routh array determinant formula is a **signed 2├Ś2 determinant divided by the previous first-column entry**.
> - For **second-order** systems, the only stability condition is: **all coefficients > 0**.
> - For **third-order** systems: $a_2 > 0$ and $a_1 a_2 > a_0 a_3$.
> - For **marginal stability**: poles lie on the $j\omega$-axis but are **not repeated**; the system produces **sustained oscillations** of constant amplitude.
> - Routh-Hurwitz is **purely algebraic** ŌĆö applicable only to **linear** systems with **real coefficients**.
> - In KTU exams, **always box the final range of $K$** and **always write the stability verdict** in words.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Controller Tuning** is the systematic procedure of adjusting the internal parameters of a feedback controller ŌĆö specifically the **Proportional Band/Gain ($K_c$)**, the **Integral Time / Reset Time ($T_i$)**, and the **Derivative Time / Rate Time ($T_d$)** ŌĆö to achieve a desired closed-loop dynamic response. The objective is to obtain an optimal trade-off between three competing performance criteria: **rapid setpoint tracking (speed)**, **minimal steady-state error (accuracy)**, and **adequate stability margins (damping)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** A student must be able to (a) explain the philosophy of controller tuning, (b) apply Ziegler-Nichols open-loop and closed-loop tuning correlations, and (c) compute P, PI, and PID settings for first-order-plus-dead-time (FOPDT) processes.

The mathematical representation of a generic **PID controller output** in parallel form is:

$$u(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i} \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

where $e(t)$ is the error signal and $u(t)$ is the controller output. Tuning, in essence, is finding the "best" numerical values for $K_c$, $T_i$, and $T_d$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Car Driver (Cruise Control)**

Imagine you are driving a car and want to maintain **100 km/h** (the setpoint). The speed error is the difference between 100 km/h and your current speed. Your foot on the accelerator is the **controller output**, and the gas pedal, brake, and steering represent the **three control modes**:

- **Proportional (P):** The harder the error (farther from 100 km/h), the harder you press the accelerator. If you are at 80, you push more; if you are at 95, you ease off. This is intuitive but a pure P-controller will leave you stuck at 97 km/h (steady-state error / offset) because you are not pushing the pedal hard enough to gain that last 3 km/h.

- **Integral (I):** Realising you have been stuck at 97 for 30 seconds, you slowly press the accelerator harder and harder until the car finally reaches 100. The I-action **accumulates past errors** and eliminates the offset, but if too aggressive, it makes you **overshoot** and oscillate (like flooring the pedal after waiting too long).

- **Derivative (D):** You see the speed climbing fast and approaching 100, so you preemptively lift your foot to avoid overshooting. D-action **anticipates future error** based on the rate of change, adding damping.

**Tuning** is the art of deciding *how hard* (P), *how persistently* (I), and *how anticipatory* (D) your control actions should be. Without tuning, the car either crawls, overshoots wildly, or oscillates between 90 and 110.

## 1.3 Why Controller Tuning is Critical

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance Metrics Used as Tuning Objectives:**
> - **IAE** ŌĆö Integral of Absolute Error: $\int_0^\infty \vert e(t) \vert \, dt$ (penalises sustained error)
> - **ISE** ŌĆö Integral of Squared Error: $\int_0^\infty e(t)^2 \, dt$ (penalises large errors heavily)
> - **ITAE** ŌĆö Integral of Time-weighted Absolute Error: $\int_0^\infty t \cdot \vert e(t) \vert \, dt$ (penalises long-duration errors)
> - **Quarter Decay Ratio (QDR):** A classic Ziegler-Nichols objective where each successive peak is **1/4** of the previous one.

## 1.4 Types of Tuning Methods (Classification)

| Method Type | Information Required | Applicability |
|---|---|---|
| **Open-Loop / Trial-and-Error** | Step test on plant alone | Slow processes, offline tuning |
| **Process Reaction Curve (Z-N Open)** | FOPDT parameters ($K_p$, $L$, $T$) | Stable, non-integrating processes |
| **Cohen-Coon** | FOPDT parameters ($K_p$, $L$, $T$) | Processes with $0.1 \leq L/T \leq 1$ |
| **Ultimate-Cycle (Z-N Closed)** | $K_u$ and $P_u$ from sustained oscillation | Closed-loop stable processes |
| **Tyreus-Luyben** | $K_u$ and $P_u$ | Better than Z-N for noisy processes |
| **Model-Based / Analytical** | Full process transfer function | Advanced tuning, MIMO systems |
| **Auto-Tuning / Intelligent** | Relay-feedback or fuzzy logic | Modern DCS / PLC systems |

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Need for a FOPDT Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Most industrial processes can be approximated by a **First-Order Plus Dead Time (FOPDT)** model because:
> 1. Higher-order dynamics decay quickly and are well-represented by a single dominant time constant.
> 2. Dead time (transport delay, measurement delay) is the most destabilising element and must be captured explicitly.
> 3. The model has only **three parameters**, making tuning correlations easy to apply.

The FOPDT transfer function is:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K_p \, e^{-Ls}}{Ts + 1}$$

where:
- $K_p$ = **Process Gain** (dimensionless or with units) ŌĆö steady-state change in PV per unit change in MV.
- $L$ = **Dead Time / Transport Delay** (time units) ŌĆö time before the PV starts responding.
- $T$ = **Process Time Constant** (time units) ŌĆö time to reach **63.2%** of the final change.

## 2.2 Open-Loop Tuning ŌĆö Ziegler-Nichols Process Reaction Curve Method

**Procedure:**
1. Bring the plant to a steady state at a normal operating point.
2. Apply a small **step change** $\Delta M$ in the manipulated variable (controller in **MANUAL** mode).
3. Record the open-loop response of the process variable (PV).
4. The response will look like an **S-shaped curve** (delayed first-order response).
5. From the recorded curve, determine:
   - The slope of the tangent at the inflection point: $R = \dfrac{\Delta PV / \Delta t_{max}}{}$
   - The dead time $L$ (where the tangent intersects the initial steady-state value).
   - The process gain $K_p = \dfrac{\Delta PV_{ss}}{\Delta M}$.

**Graphical Construction:**
- $L$ is the time axis intercept of the steepest tangent line drawn at the inflection point of the S-curve.
- $T$ is determined by drawing the tangent until it reaches the final steady-state value of PV; the time corresponding to this intersection gives $L + T$.

## 2.3 Closed-Loop Tuning ŌĆö Ziegler-Nichols Ultimate Gain Method

**Procedure:**
1. With the controller in **AUTOMATIC** mode, set $T_i = \infty$ and $T_d = 0$ (pure P-controller).
2. Gradually increase the proportional gain $K_c$ until the closed-loop system exhibits **sustained oscillations** (neither growing nor decaying).
3. Record this critical gain as the **Ultimate Gain $K_u$** and the period of oscillation as the **Ultimate Period $P_u$**.
4. Use empirical correlations to compute controller settings.

> [!WARNING]
> **Practical Pitfall:** This method pushes the system to the **stability limit**. It is not recommended for processes that cannot tolerate sustained oscillations (e.g., exothermic chemical reactors, mechanical systems prone to fatigue). Use the **Relay Feedback / ├ģstr├Čm-H├żgglund method** as a safer alternative for industrial applications.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these tables for KTU 2024 examinations. Memorise the formula structure; the numerical values (0.5, 0.45, 0.6, etc.) are repeatedly asked in numerical problems.**

### 2.4.1 Ziegler-Nichols Open-Loop (Process Reaction Curve) Correlations

| Controller | $K_c$ | $T_i$ | $T_d$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P** | $\dfrac{T}{K_p \cdot L}$ | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |
| **PI** | $\dfrac{0.9 \, T}{K_p \cdot L}$ | $3L$ | ŌĆö |
| **PID** | $\dfrac{1.2 \, T}{K_p \cdot L}$ | $2L$ | $0.5L$ |

> **Note on notation:** In the Z-N open-loop method, $K_p$, $L$, $T$ come from the FOPDT fit.

### 2.4.2 Ziegler-Nichols Closed-Loop (Ultimate Cycle) Correlations

| Controller | $K_c$ | $T_i$ | $T_d$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P** | $0.5 \, K_u$ | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |
| **PI** | $0.45 \, K_u$ | $\dfrac{P_u}{1.2}$ | ŌĆö |
| **PID** | $0.6 \, K_u$ | $\dfrac{P_u}{2}$ | $\dfrac{P_u}{8}$ |

### 2.4.3 Cohen-Coon Tuning Correlations

Let $x = L/T$ (the dead-time-to-time-constant ratio). Cohen-Coon settings are:

| Controller | $K_c$ | $T_i$ | $T_d$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P** | $\dfrac{1}{K_p x} \left(1 + \dfrac{x}{3}\right)$ | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |
| **PI** | $\dfrac{1}{K_p x} \left(0.9 + \dfrac{x}{12}\right)$ | $L \cdot \dfrac{30 + 3x}{9 + 20x}$ | ŌĆö |
| **PD** | $\dfrac{1}{K_p x} \left(1.25 + x\right)$ | ŌĆö | $L \cdot \dfrac{6 - 2x}{22 + 3x}$ |
| **PID** | $\dfrac{1}{K_p x} \left(\dfrac{4}{3} + \dfrac{x}{4}\right)$ | $L \cdot \dfrac{32 + 6x}{13 + 8x}$ | $L \cdot \dfrac{4}{11 + 2x}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cohen-Coon Advantage:** Produces faster response than Z-N but is **more sensitive to process noise** and is **not recommended** when $L/T < 0.1$ (where the dead time is negligible).

### 2.4.4 Tyreus-Luyben Correlations (Improved Z-N Closed-Loop)

| Controller | $K_c$ | $T_i$ | $T_d$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PI** | $\dfrac{K_u}{3.2}$ | $2.2 \, P_u$ | ŌĆö |
| **PID** | $\dfrac{K_u}{2.2}$ | $2.2 \, P_u$ | $\dfrac{P_u}{6.3}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Rule of Thumb:** Tyreus-Luyben produces **less oscillatory** and **more robust** settings than Z-N closed-loop. It is the preferred method for **distillation column** tuning in the chemical process industry.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Controller tuning is the **backbone of process control** in every industry:
- **Petrochemical:** Temperature and composition control in distillation columns.
- **Power Generation:** Boiler drum level and steam temperature regulation.
- **Pharmaceutical:** pH control in bioreactors (highly nonlinear, requires careful tuning).
- **HVAC:** Chilled water temperature and airflow regulation.
- **Robotics / Mechatronics:** Servo motor position and velocity control loops.

Poorly tuned controllers cause **production losses**, **quality variability**, **equipment damage**, and **safety hazards**.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Slope and Dead-Time Extraction from Process Reaction Curve

Given an open-loop step response of a FOPDT process, we need to extract $K_p$, $L$, and $T$ graphically.

**Step 1: Record steady-state values.**
Let the initial PV be $PV_0$ and the final steady-state PV be $PV_{ss}$.
The step change in MV is $\Delta M$ (the bump introduced manually).

**Step 2: Compute Process Gain.**
The process gain is the ratio of steady-state PV change to MV change:

$$K_p = \frac{PV_{ss} - PV_0}{\Delta M} = \frac{\Delta PV_{ss}}{\Delta M}$$

**Step 3: Locate the inflection point.**
For an S-shaped response, the inflection point is where the curve transitions from concave-up to concave-down (the steepest point of the curve). Mathematically, this is where $\dfrac{d^2 PV}{dt^2} = 0$.

**Step 4: Draw the tangent line at the inflection point.**
The slope of this tangent is:

$$R = \left. \frac{d PV}{dt} \right|_{\text{inflection}}$$

**Step 5: Determine dead time $L$.**
Project the tangent line backward in time until it intersects the line $PV = PV_0$. The time coordinate of this intersection is the **apparent dead time** $L$.

**Step 6: Determine time constant $T$.**
Project the tangent line forward until it intersects the line $PV = PV_{ss}$. The time coordinate minus the dead time $L$ gives the **apparent time constant** $T$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example #1 ŌĆö Z-N Open-Loop Method (PI Controller)

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A temperature process is modelled as FOPDT with $K_p = 2.5$ ┬░C/%CO, $T = 8$ min, and $L = 2$ min. Design a PI controller using the Ziegler-Nichols open-loop (process reaction curve) method. Compute $K_c$, $T_i$, and the integral gain $K_i$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the controller type and the relevant Z-N correlation.**
For a PI controller using Z-N open-loop:

$$K_c = \frac{0.9 \, T}{K_p \cdot L}, \quad T_i = 3L$$

**Step 2: Substitute the given values into the $K_c$ formula.**

$$K_c = \frac{0.9 \times 8}{2.5 \times 2}$$

$$K_c = \frac{7.2}{5.0}$$

$$K_c = 1.44 \; \%CO/^{\circ}C$$

**Step 3: Substitute the given values into the $T_i$ formula.**

$$T_i = 3 \times 2 = 6 \; \text{min}$$

**Step 4: Compute the integral gain $K_i$ (alternative form).**
In the parallel PID representation, the integral term is $\dfrac{K_c}{T_i} \int e \, dt$, so the integral gain is:

$$K_i = \frac{K_c}{T_i} = \frac{1.44}{6} = 0.24 \; \%CO/^{\circ}C\text{-min}$$

**Step 5: Express the final PI controller law.**

$$u(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i} \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau \right] = 1.44 \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{6} \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau \right]$$

**Step 6: Verify the controller's $K_c T_i / L$ product (a common check).**

$$K_c \cdot T_i = 1.44 \times 6 = 8.64 \approx 0.9 \times T = 7.2 \; \text{(within rounding, consistent)}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example #2 ŌĆö Z-N Closed-Loop Method (PID Controller)

**Problem Statement:**
A level control loop is tuned in closed loop. The ultimate gain is found to be $K_u = 9.2$ and the ultimate period is $P_u = 4.5$ min. Compute the PID controller settings using the Ziegler-Nichols closed-loop (ultimate cycle) method.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Recall the Z-N closed-loop PID correlations.**

$$K_c = 0.6 \, K_u, \quad T_i = \frac{P_u}{2}, \quad T_d = \frac{P_u}{8}$$

**Step 2: Compute $K_c$.**

$$K_c = 0.6 \times 9.2 = 5.52$$

**Step 3: Compute $T_i$.**

$$T_i = \frac{4.5}{2} = 2.25 \; \text{min}$$

**Step 4: Compute $T_d$.**

$$T_d = \frac{4.5}{8} = 0.5625 \; \text{min}$$

**Step 5: Express the PID controller law.**

$$u(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i} \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

$$u(t) = 5.52 \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{2.25} \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + 0.5625 \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

**Step 6: Compute derivative and integral gains for validation.**

$$K_i = \frac{K_c}{T_i} = \frac{5.52}{2.25} = 2.453 \; \text{min}^{-1}$$

$$K_d = K_c \times T_d = 5.52 \times 0.5625 = 3.105 \; \text{min}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example #3 ŌĆö Cohen-Coon Method (PI Controller)

**Problem Statement:**
A mixing tank exhibits FOPDT behaviour with $K_p = 1.8$ ┬░C/(kg/s), $T = 10$ min, $L = 3$ min. Compute PI controller settings using the Cohen-Coon method.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the dead-time ratio $x$.**

$$x = \frac{L}{T} = \frac{3}{10} = 0.3$$

**Step 2: Recall Cohen-Coon PI correlations.**

$$K_c = \frac{1}{K_p x} \left(0.9 + \frac{x}{12}\right), \quad T_i = L \cdot \frac{30 + 3x}{9 + 20x}$$

**Step 3: Substitute values in the $K_c$ formula.**

$$K_c = \frac{1}{1.8 \times 0.3} \left(0.9 + \frac{0.3}{12}\right)$$

$$K_c = \frac{1}{0.54} \left(0.9 + 0.025\right)$$

$$K_c = 1.852 \times 0.925$$

$$K_c = 1.713 \; \text{(kg/s)/}^{\circ}C$$

**Step 4: Substitute values in the $T_i$ formula.**

$$T_i = 3 \times \frac{30 + 3(0.3)}{9 + 20(0.3)} = 3 \times \frac{30.9}{15.0} = 3 \times 2.06 = 6.18 \; \text{min}$$

**Step 5: Express the PI controller law.**

$$u(t) = 1.713 \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{6.18} \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau \right]$$

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (P, PI, PID Settings)

| Method | $K_c$ Behaviour | $T_i$ Behaviour | Response Character |
|---|---|---|---|
| Z-N Open-Loop | Moderate | $3L$ | Quarter-decay, fast but oscillatory |
| Z-N Closed-Loop | Aggressive ($0.6 K_u$) | $P_u/2$ | Quarter-decay, very fast, high overshoot |
| Cohen-Coon | Slightly more aggressive | Slightly less than $3L$ | Faster than Z-N open, similar oscillation |
| Tyreus-Luyben | Conservative ($K_u/2.2$) | $2.2 P_u$ (much larger) | Slower but more robust, lower overshoot |
| Direct Synthesis | Model-based | Model-based | User-defined; closed-loop pole placement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fine-Tuning Rule of Thumb (used in industry after applying the above correlations):**
> - Increase $K_c$ by 10ŌĆō20% ŌåÆ **faster response**, but more overshoot and oscillation.
> - Decrease $K_c$ by 10ŌĆō20% ŌåÆ **slower response**, more stable.
> - Decrease $T_i$ ŌåÆ **stronger integral action**, faster offset removal, but more overshoot.
> - Increase $T_i$ ŌåÆ **weaker integral action**, slower offset removal, more stable.
> - Increase $T_d$ ŌåÆ **more damping**, less overshoot, but amplifies measurement noise.
> - Decrease $T_d$ or set $T_d = 0$ ŌåÆ **less damping**, more oscillation possible.

## 3.6 Derivation of the Stability Limit (Background for $K_u$)

The closed-loop characteristic equation for a P-controlled FOPDT process is:

$$1 + K_c \cdot \frac{K_p \, e^{-Ls}}{Ts + 1} = 0$$

Using the approximation $e^{-Ls} \approx 1 - Ls$ (first-order Pad├®), we get:

$$Ts + 1 + K_c K_p (1 - Ls) = 0$$

$$(T - K_c K_p L) s + (1 + K_c K_p) = 0$$

For the system to be on the **stability boundary** (sustained oscillation at $\omega = \omega_u$), the characteristic equation evaluated at $s = j\omega_u$ must have a real and an imaginary part of zero. Solving the resulting two equations simultaneously:

$$K_u = \frac{1}{K_p} \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{T}{L}\right)^2}, \quad P_u = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_u} = 2\pi \sqrt{L^2 + \left(\frac{LT}{T}\right)^2 \cdot \text{...}}$$

A simpler exact result for FOPDT processes (using higher-order Pad├® approximations) yields:

$$\omega_u = \frac{1}{L} \sqrt{\frac{T}{L} - 1} \quad \text{(valid only when } T > L \text{)}$$

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Closed-Loop PID Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint R] --> SUM1((+))
    SUM1 --> E[Error e t]
    E --> P[Proportional Kc]
    E --> I[Integral Kc over Ti]
    E --> D[Derivative Kc times Td]
    P --> SUM2((+))
    I --> SUM2
    D --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> U[Controller Output u t]
    U --> ACT[Final Control Element]
    ACT --> PROC[Process Gp of s]
    PROC --> PV[Process Variable Y s]
    PV --> FB[Feedback H s equals 1]
    FB --> SUM1
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Open-Loop Step Test for Process Reaction Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    STEP[Step Input Delta M] --> CTRL[Controller in MANUAL]
    CTRL --> VALVE[Control Valve]
    VALVE --> PROCESS[Plant Gp of s]
    PROCESS --> REC[Recorder or DCS Trend]
    REC --> SCURVE[S shaped Response]
    SCURVE --> ANALYZE[Graphical Analysis]
    ANALYZE --> TANGENT[Draw Tangent at Inflection]
    TANGENT --> EXTRACT[Extract Kp, L, T]
    EXTRACT --> CORREL[Apply Z N Correlations]
    CORREL --> SETTINGS[PID Settings]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Closed-Loop Ultimate Cycle Test Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INIT[Set Ti to Infinity, Td to 0] --> INC[Increase Kc Gradually]
    INC --> OBS[Observe Closed Loop Response]
    OBS --> OSC{Sustained Oscillation?}
    OSC -- No --> INC
    OSC -- Yes --> REC[Record Ku and Pu]
    REC --> CORR[Apply Z N Closed Loop Correlations]
    CORR --> KcCalc[Compute Kc equals 0.6 Ku]
    KcCalc --> TiCalc[Compute Ti equals Pu over 2]
    TiCalc --> TdCalc[Compute Td equals Pu over 8]
    TdCalc --> IMPLEMENT[Implement PID Settings]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Tuning Workflow Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start: Tuning Required] --> Q1{Plant Available Offline?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> OL[Open Loop Step Test]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Can Tolerate Oscillation?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> CL[Closed Loop Ultimate Cycle]
    Q2 -- No --> RF[Relay Feedback Auto Tune]
    OL --> FIT[Fit FOPDT Model]
    FIT --> Q3{Dead Time Ratio L over T}
    Q3 -- Greater than 0.1 --> CC[Cohen Coon Correlations]
    Q3 -- Less than 0.1 --> ZNOL[Z N Open Loop Correlations]
    CL --> ZNCL[Z N Closed Loop Correlations]
    RF --> ZNCL
    CC --> IMPL[Implement Settings]
    ZNOL --> IMPL
    ZNCL --> IMPL
    IMPL --> FINE[Fine Tune on Plant]
    FINE --> DONE[Loop in Service]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Tuning Iteration Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initial Settings from Correlation] --> B[Implement on Controller]
    B --> C[Monitor Step Response]
    C --> D{Meets Performance?}
    D -- No --> E[Adjust Parameters]
    E --> F{Response Too Oscillatory?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Reduce Kc, Increase Ti]
    F -- No --> H{Response Too Sluggish?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Increase Kc, Reduce Ti]
    G --> B
    I --> B
    D -- Yes --> J[Lock Settings]
```
